EDCS-09700726.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, the Dolichene, for the safety of our lord Marcus Antonius Gordianus Pius Felix Augustus and Sabinia Tranquillina Augusta, Solinus Tertius? gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow for himself and his family.
EDCS-09700726.summary: A votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Dolichenus for the health of Emperor Gordian III and his empress, Sabina Tranquillina, around the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-09700728.text_edition: \n GI Aurel Ver sororis dolentis SA \n
EDCS-09700728.translation: Gaius Aurelius Ver, grieving, to his sister.
EDCS-09700728.summary: A funerary epitaph for an unknown individual, likely erected by a grieving brother named Gaius Aurelius Ver.

EDCS-09800287.text_edition: \n Aur<el>ius Victor librarius legionis V Macedonicae votum solvit\n
EDCS-09800287.translation: Aurelius Victor, scribe of the Fifth Macedonian Legion, fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-09800287.summary: A votive inscription by Aurelius Victor, a scribe serving in the Legio V Macedonica, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10200172.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publio Aelio Malcho veterano vixit nnos XXXXII vivo se fecit sibi et coniugi carissimae et filio pientissimo\n
EDCS-10200172.translation: To the Divine Manes. Publius Aelius Malchus, veteran, lived 42 years. He made this for himself, his dearest wife, and his most dutiful son.
EDCS-10200172.summary: A funerary monument prepared by a veteran, Publius Aelius Malchus, for himself and his family, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10200174.summary: A brief inscription identifying the Thirteenth Twin Legion, likely marking property or a monument associated with the unit.

EDCS-10200175.summary: A brief inscription identifying the Thirteenth Twin Legion, likely marking property or a monument associated with the unit.

EDCS-10200177.summary: A brief inscription identifying the Thirteenth Twin Legion, likely marking property or a monument associated with the unit.

EDCS-10200178.summary: A brief inscription identifying the Thirteenth Twin Legion, likely marking property or a monument associated with the unit.

EDCS-10200181.translation: The Thirteenth Twin Legion, dutiful and faithful.
EDCS-10200181.summary: An inscription proudly stating the epithet 'dutiful and faithful' for the Thirteenth Twin Legion, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10200183.translation: The Thirteenth Twin Legion of Flavius Martinus.
EDCS-10200183.summary: An inscription indicating ownership or association of the Thirteenth Twin Legion with an individual named Flavius Martinus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10200186.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Antonio Capitoni veterano legionis IIII Flaviae felicis Antonius Paternus filius et Antonia Primitiva liberta posuerunt\n
EDCS-10200186.translation: To the divine spirits. Antonius Paternus, his son, and Antonia Primitiva, freedwoman, erected this to Gaius Antonius Capito, a veteran of the Fourth Flavian Felix legion.
EDCS-10200186.summary: A funerary epitaph honoring Gaius Antonius Capito, a veteran, erected by his son and freedwoman.

EDCS-10701843.text_edition: \n Libero Patri deo Publius Aelius Marcianus decurio coloniae Napocensium pro sua et suorum votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-10701843.translation: To Liber Pater, the god. Publius Aelius Marcianus, a decurion of the colony of Napoca, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow for himself and his family.
EDCS-10701843.summary: A votive inscription dedicated to Liber Pater by Publius Aelius Marcianus, a decurion of Napoca, for fulfilling a vow.

EDCS-10701850.summary: A fragmentary inscription that likely records a personal name and title, possibly late Roman.

EDCS-10701869.summary: A very fragmentary inscription that begins with the imperial title, likely commemorating an emperor.

EDCS-11200045.text_edition: \n Ex permissu splendidissimi ordinis coloniae Sarmizegetusae metropolis er Augustalis coloniae eiusdem aedem collegarum vetustate dilapsam sumptibus suis restituit\n
EDCS-11200045.translation: With the permission of the most distinguished council of the colony of Sarmizegetusa, its metropolis, Augustalis of the same colony restored the temple of the collegia, which had fallen into ruin through age, at his own expense.
EDCS-11200045.summary: An inscription recording the restoration of a temple by Augustalis of Sarmizegetusa, undertaken with the council's permission and at his own expense.

EDCS-11200046.text_edition: \n Pro salute Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Aureli Commodi Pii Augusti Marcus Pomponius Severus decurio coloniae quaestor patronus collegii fabrum et Marcus Urbius Valerianus patronus collegii eiusdem aedem pecunia sua fecerunt\n
EDCS-11200046.translation: For the health of Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Commodus Pius Augustus. Marcus Pomponius Severus, decurion of the colony, quaestor, and patron of the guild of smiths, and Marcus Urbius Valerianus, patron of the same guild, built the temple with their own money.
EDCS-11200046.summary: An inscription recording the construction of a temple by two patrons of a smiths' guild, Marcus Pomponius Severus and Marcus Urbius Valerianus, for the health of Emperor Commodus.

EDCS-11200048.text_edition: \n Titus Ancharius Octavius decurio coloniae Sarmizegetusae metropolis patronus decuriae XV porticum per pedes XXXXV ob honorem patronatus ex suo fecit\n
EDCS-11200048.translation: Titus Ancharius Octavius, decurion of the colony of Sarmizegetusa, its metropolis, and patron of the fifteenth subgroup, built a portico thirty-five feet long at his own expense in honor of his patronage.
EDCS-11200048.summary: An inscription recording the construction of a portico by Titus Ancharius Octavius, a decurion and patron in Sarmizegetusa, in honor of his patronage.

EDCS-11200049.text_edition: \n Libero Patri Augusto sacrum Lucius Apuleius Marcus decurio coloniae quaestor porticus cum cubiculis a vii sic hostium exustos pecunia sua ob scribatum restituit permittente ordine\n
EDCS-11200049.translation: Sacred to Liber Pater Augustus. Lucius Apuleius Marcus, decurion of the colony, quaestor, restored the portico with seven rooms, after the enemy had been routed, with his own money, with the permission of the council.
EDCS-11200049.summary: A dedicatory inscription by Lucius Apuleius Marcus, a decurion and quaestor of the colony, who restored a portico with private funds after routing enemies.

EDCS-11200051.text_edition: \n Caelesti Virgini Augustae sacrum Nemesianus Caesaris nostri servos sic librarius templum a solo <p=R>ecunia sua ex voto fecit \n
EDCS-11200051.translation: Sacred to the Virgin Goddess Augusta. Nemesianus, servant of our Emperor, scribe, built the temple from its foundations at his own expense, in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-11200051.summary: A dedicatory inscription by Nemesianus, a scribe and servant of the emperor, who built a temple from scratch at his own expense.

EDCS-11200053.text_edition: \n In honorem domus divinae Lucius Ophonius Papiria Domitius Priscus IIvir coloniae Dacicae pecunia sua fecit locus datus decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-11200053.translation: In honor of the divine house. Lucius Ophonius Papirius Domitius Priscus, IIvir of the Dacian colony, built this at his own expense; the site was granted by a decree of the decurions.
EDCS-11200053.summary: An inscription recording the construction of a monument by Lucius Ophonius Papirius Domitius Priscus, a IIvir of a Dacian colony, built at his own expense with land granted by the council.

EDCS-11200087.text_edition: \n Publius Aelius Caius Publicia Eu Publius Aelius Iulianus Publius Aelius Statorius Aelia Mo Publius Aelius Caianus Claudia Et\n
EDCS-11200087.translation: Publius Aelius Caius, from the Publicia tribe, Eu Publius Aelius Iulianus, Publius Aelius Statorius, and Aelia Mo, from the Claudia tribe, and Et.
EDCS-11200087.summary: This inscription lists multiple names, likely belonging to a family or group, with tribal affiliations, possibly as part of a funerary monument or dedication.

EDCS-11200093.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Iulio Philippo Pio Felici Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate V consuli III patri patriae proconsuli concilium III provinciarum Daciarum devotum numini maiestatique eius\n
EDCS-11200093.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Iulius Philippus, Pious, Fortunate, Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 5th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the fatherland, proconsul, the council of the three Dacian provinces, devoted to his divine majesty.
EDCS-11200093.summary: This is a dedication by the council of the Dacian provinces to Emperor Philip the Arab, likely dating to his reign (AD 244-249).

EDCS-11200094.text_edition: \n Publio Aelio Hammonio viro egregio I\n
EDCS-11200094.translation: To Publius Aelius Hammonius, a distinguished man.
EDCS-11200094.summary: This is a dedication to Publius Aelius Hammonius, described as a distinguished man, likely on a tombstone or a monument.

EDCS-11200096.text_edition: \n Caio Arrio Antonino filio Cai Arri Antonini legato Augusti pro praetore colonia Ulpia Traiana Augusta Dacica Sarmizegetusa\n
EDCS-11200096.translation: To Gaius Arrius Antoninus, son of Gaius Arrius Antoninus, imperial legate with the power of a proconsul, from the colony Ulpia Traiana Augusta Dacica Sarmizegetusa.
EDCS-11200096.summary: This is a dedication from the colony of Sarmizegetusa to Gaius Arrius Antoninus, son of a former governor, likely dating to the 2nd century AD.

EDCS-11200097.text_edition: \n Caio Arrio Quadrato filio Cai Arri Antonini legato Augusti pro praetore colonia Ulpia Traiana Augusta Dacica Sarmizegetusa\n
EDCS-11200097.translation: To Gaius Arrius Quadratus, son of Gaius Arrius Antoninus, imperial legate with the power of a proconsul, from the colony Ulpia Traiana Augusta Dacica Sarmizegetusa.
EDCS-11200097.summary: This is a dedication from the colony of Sarmizegetusa to Gaius Arrius Quadratus, son of a former governor, likely dating to the 2nd century AD.

EDCS-11200100.text_edition: \n Marco Sedatio Cai filio Quirina Severiano Iulio Acri Metilio Nepoti Rufino Tiberio Rutiliano Censori tribuno plebis quaestori provinciae Siciliae praetori legato Augusti legionis V Macedonicae curatori viae Flaminiae legato Augusti pro praetore provinciae Daciae consuli colonia Ulpia Traiana Augusta Dacica Sarmizegetusa patrono\n
EDCS-11200100.translation: To Marcus Sedatius Severianus, son of Gaius, of the Quirina tribe, nephew of Rufinus Tiberius Rutilianus, censor, tribune of the plebs, quaestor of the province of Sicily, praetor, imperial legate of the legion V Macedonica, curator of the Via Flaminia, imperial legate with the power of a proconsul of the province of Dacia, consul, patron of the colony Ulpia Traiana Augusta Dacica Sarmizegetusa.
EDCS-11200100.summary: This is a lengthy dedication from the colony of Sarmizegetusa honoring Marcus Sedatius Severianus for his many public offices, including his patronage of the colony, likely dating to the late 2nd or early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-11200101.text_edition: \n Marco Sedatio Cai filio Quirina Severiano tribuno plebis quaestori provinciae Siciliae praetori legato Augusti legionis V Macedonicae curatori viae Flaminiae legato Augusti pro praetore provinciae Daciae consuli colonia Ulpia Traiana Augusta Dacica Sarmizegetusa patrono\n
EDCS-11200101.translation: To Marcus Sedatius Severianus, son of Gaius, of the Quirina tribe, tribune of the plebs, quaestor of the province of Sicily, praetor, imperial legate of the legion V Macedonica, curator of the Via Flaminia, imperial legate with the power of a proconsul of the province of Dacia, consul, patron of the colony Ulpia Traiana Augusta Dacica Sarmizegetusa.
EDCS-11200101.summary: This is a dedication from the colony of Sarmizegetusa honoring Marcus Sedatius Severianus for his public offices and patronage, likely dating to the late 2nd or early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-11200102.text_edition: \n N AE legato Augusti pro praetore consulari Daciarum III colonia Ulpia Traiana Augusta Dacica Sarmizegetusa patrono optimo\n
EDCS-11200102.translation: To N. AE, imperial legate with consular power of the three Dacian provinces, to the most excellent patron of the colony Ulpia Traiana Augusta Dacica Sarmizegetusa.
EDCS-11200102.summary: This is a dedication from the colony of Sarmizegetusa to an unnamed imperial legate, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-11200103.text_edition: \n Aeliae Publi filiae Lucillae Aelii Theodorus et Firminus alumni loco dato decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-11200103.translation: To Aelia Lucilla, daughter of Publius, from alumni Theodorus and Firminus, a place granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-11200103.summary: This inscription records a burial plot granted by local officials to Aelia Lucilla, likely for her freedmen or dependents, from the colony of Sarmizegetusa.

EDCS-11200105.text_edition: \n Firmo veterano legionis IIII Flaviae felicis decurioni coloniae Daciae Ulpiae Traianae Augustae Sarmizegetusae\n
EDCS-11200105.translation: To Firminus, veteran of the legion IIII Flavia Felix, decurion of the colony Dacia Ulpia Traiana Augusta Sarmizegetusa.
EDCS-11200105.summary: This inscription identifies Firminus as a veteran and decurion from the colony of Sarmizegetusa, likely dating to the late 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-11200106.text_edition: \n Caio Iulio Cai filio Papiria Valerio veterano legionis XIII geminae Severianae ex beneficiario consularis decurioni et IIvirali coloniae Sarmizegetusae metropolis CCai Iulii Valerianus beneficiarius consularis Carus frumentarius et decurio coloniae supra scriptae Fronto miles cohortis I praetoriae scriniarius praeffectorum praetorio et decurio coloniae eiusdem Valeria et Carissima filii memoriae patris locus datus decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-11200106.translation: To Gaius Julius Valerianus, son of Gaius Julius, of the Papiria tribe, a veteran of the Thirteenth Gemina Severiana legion, formerly a *beneficiarius consularis*, a decurion, and a *duumvir* of the colony of Sarmizegetusa, the metropolis; Carus Frumentarius, son of Gaius Julius, of the Papiria tribe, a *beneficiarius consularis* and decurion of the aforementioned colony, and Fronton Miles, a soldier of the I Praetorian cohort, a clerk in the office of the praetorian prefects and a decurion of the same colony, and Valeria and Carissima, daughters, have given this place in memory of their father, by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-11200106.summary: This funerary inscription records the granting of burial space to Gaius Julius Valerianus by his sons, dedicating it by decree of the local council.

EDCS-11200109.text_edition: \n Marco Opellio Marci filio Papiria Adiutori decurioni coloniae IIviro iuris dicundi praefecto collegii fabrum plebs aere conlato locus datus decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-11200109.translation: To Marcus Opellius Adiutor, son of Marcus, of the Papiria tribe, a decurion of the colony, a *duumvir* with judicial authority, and prefect of the guild of builders, the common people have granted this place, by decree of the decurions, through a collection of money.
EDCS-11200109.summary: This inscription records the granting of burial space to Marcus Opellius Adiutor by the plebs of the colony, funded by a collection, by decree of the local council.

EDCS-11200111.text_edition: \n Marco Procilio Iuliano decurioni coloniae equo publico\n
EDCS-11200111.translation: To Marcus Procilius Julianus, a decurion of the colony, with public horse.
EDCS-11200111.summary: This inscription records Marcus Procilius Julianus as a decurion of the colony, provided with a public horse.

EDCS-11200113.text_edition: \n Ulpio Domitio Ulpi filio Papiria Rufino decurioni coloniae Sarmizegetusae Ulpius Domitius Hermes libertus locus datus decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-11200113.translation: To Ulpius Domitius Rufinus, son of Ulpius, of the Papiria tribe, a decurion of the colony of Sarmizegetusa; Ulpius Domitius Hermes, his freedman, has granted this place, by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-11200113.summary: This funerary inscription records the granting of burial space to Ulpius Domitius Rufinus by his freedman, by decree of the local council.

EDCS-11200115.text_edition: \n Caio Valerio Cai filio Papiria Suro decurioni coloniae Sarmizegetusae metropolis aedilicio praefecto collegii fabrum et IIvirali coloniae eiusdem Titus Aurelius Emeritus |(centurio) legionis VI victricis Severianae socero locus datus decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-11200115.translation: To Gaius Valerius Suro, son of Gaius, of the Papiria tribe, a decurion of the colony of Sarmizegetusa, the metropolis, a former *aedile* and prefect of the guild of builders, and a *duumvir* of the same colony; Titus Aurelius Emeritus, centurion of the VI Victrix Severiana legion, his father-in-law, has granted this place, by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-11200115.summary: This funerary inscription records the granting of burial space to Gaius Valerius Suro by his father-in-law, by decree of the local council.

EDCS-11200119.text_edition: \n Ordini Augustalium Publius Antonius Super decurio coloniae Sarmizegetusae metropolis dono dedit\n
EDCS-11200119.translation: To the Council of Augustales, Publius Antonius Super, a decurion of the colony of Sarmizegetusa, the metropolis, has given as a gift.
EDCS-11200119.summary: This inscription records a gift made by Publius Antonius Super, a decurion of the colony of Sarmizegetusa, to the Council of Augustales.

EDCS-11200129.translation: [This inscription is too fragmentary to translate or summarize].
EDCS-11200129.summary: This inscription is too fragmentary to translate or summarize.

EDCS-11200138.text_edition: \n Aesculapio et Hygiae pro salute Aeliae Florae et Mettiorum Proteni Cassiani Florae filiae Caius Mettius Cassianus IIvir coloniae votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-11200138.translation: To Aesculapius and Hygieia, for the health of Aelia Flora and the Mettii, children of Protenus Cassianus and Flora; Gaius Mettius Cassianus, *duumvir* of the colony, has gladly fulfilled his vow, deservedly.
EDCS-11200138.summary: This votive inscription records Gaius Mettius Cassianus, a *duumvir* of the colony, fulfilling a vow to Aesculapius and Hygieia for the health of his family.

EDCS-11200140.text_edition: \n Numini Aesculapio et Hygiae Ulpius Avillius Florentinus ex voto\n
EDCS-11200140.translation: To the divine Aesculapius and Hygieia, Ulpius Avillius Florentinus, in accordance with a vision, gladly.
EDCS-11200140.summary: This votive inscription records Ulpius Avillius Florentinus dedicating something to Aesculapius and Hygieia following a vision.

EDCS-11200141.text_edition: \n Numini Augusto Aesculapi sacrum Lucius Bononius Saturninus ex viso libens\n
EDCS-11200141.translation: To the divine Augustus, sacred to Aesculapius; Lucius Bononius Saturninus, in accordance with a vision, gladly.
EDCS-11200141.summary: This votive inscription records Lucius Bononius Saturninus dedicating something to the divine Augustus and Aesculapius following a vision.

EDCS-11200142.translation: For the well-being of A. Claudius Gaianus.
EDCS-11200142.summary: This inscription records a dedication for the health of an individual named A. Claudius Gaianus.

EDCS-11200143.text_edition: \n Euprepes Luci Valeri Eutychi Asclepio et Hygiae dono dedit\n
EDCS-11200143.translation: Euprepes gave this as a gift to Asclepius and Hygieia, of Lucius Valerius Eutychus.
EDCS-11200143.summary: This inscription records a gift to the gods Asclepius and Hygieia by Euprepes on behalf of Lucius Valerius Eutychus.

EDCS-11200144.text_edition: \n Aesculapio et Hygiae Publius Flaccinius Felix ex voto\n
EDCS-11200144.translation: Publius Flaccinius Felix to Asclepius and Hygieia, in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-11200144.summary: This inscription records a votive offering to Asclepius and Hygieia by Publius Flaccinius Felix.

EDCS-11200145.text_edition: \n Aesculapio et Hygiae pro salute Lucillae filiae Publius Flaccinius Felix decurio collegii fabrum ex voto\n
EDCS-11200145.translation: Publius Flaccinius Felix, decurion of the guild of craftsmen, to Asclepius and Hygieia for the well-being of his daughter Lucilla, in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-11200145.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Publius Flaccinius Felix, a decurion of the craftsmen's guild, for the health of his daughter Lucilla.

EDCS-11200146.text_edition: \n Aesculapio et Hygiae Augustis Caius Titius Agathopus Augustalis coloniae Sisciae et Sarmizegetusae ex voto\n
EDCS-11200146.translation: Caius Titius Agathopus, Augustalis of the colony of Siscia and Sarmizegetusa, to the August Emperors Asclepius and Hygieia, in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-11200146.summary: This inscription records a votive offering by Caius Titius Agathopus, an Augustalis in the colony of Siscia and Sarmizegetusa, to Asclepius and Hygieia.

EDCS-11200159.text_edition: \n ISI mis fonte V nis est CE ens Marcus Ulpius \n
EDCS-11200159.translation: From the spring of Isis, [belonging to] Venus Est.
EDCS-11200159.summary: This fragmentary inscription likely records something related to a spring dedicated to Isis and Venus.

EDCS-11200160.text_edition: \n Deo Aeterno et Iunoni et Angelis Marcus Procilius Aphrodisius Augustalis coloniae metropolis et Seximia Hermione et Procilia filia colitoribus dono dederunt posuerunt\n
EDCS-11200160.translation: Marcus Procilius Aphrodisius, Augustalis of the metropolis colony, and Seximia Hermione, and their daughter Procilia, gave and dedicated this as a gift to the Eternal God, Juno, and the Angels.
EDCS-11200160.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Marcus Procilius Aphrodisius, his wife Seximia Hermione, and daughter Procilia to the Eternal God, Juno, and the Angels.

EDCS-11200161.text_edition: \n Deae Caelesti sacrum Liberalis Augusti nostri verna adiutor tabularii votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-11200161.translation: To the Sky-Born Goddess, sacred. Liberalis, a freedman and helper in the registry office of our emperor, fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-11200161.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Liberalis, an imperial freedman and registry office assistant, to the goddess Caelestis.

EDCS-11200162.text_edition: \n Cautopati sacrum Synethus adiutor tabularii votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-11200162.translation: To Cautopates, sacred. Synethus, helper in the registry office, fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-11200162.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Synethus, an imperial registry office assistant, to the god Cautopates.

EDCS-11200167.text_edition: \n Dito Patri et Proserpinae sacrum Caius Valerius Zeno decurio et IIvir coloniae votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-11200167.translation: Sacred to the Underworld Father and Proserpina. Caius Valerius Zeno, decurion and former duumvir of the colony, fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-11200167.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Caius Valerius Zeno, a decurion and former duumvir of the colony, to Pluto and Proserpina.

EDCS-11200170.text_edition: \n Fortunae Daciarum Caius Sempronius Urbanus procurator Augusti\n
EDCS-11200170.translation: To the goddess Fortuna of Dacia, Gaius Sempronius Urbanus, imperial procurator, [dedicated this].
EDCS-11200170.summary: This dedicates an altar or shrine to Fortuna Daciarum by an imperial procurator, likely in the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-11200172.text_edition: \n Priscus ex iusso posuit libens merito\n
EDCS-11200172.translation: Priscus gladly set this up as commanded, deservedly.
EDCS-11200172.summary: This records a private dedication, likely a votive offering, set up by Priscus, possibly in the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-11200173.text_edition: \n Daciae Felicis et Genio domus divinae Diogenes Eutychetis Augg[g](ustorum) nn[n](ostrorum) dispensatoris vikarius sic votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-11200173.translation: To Dacia Felix and the Genius of the divine house of our Emperors, Diogenes, freedman of the freedman Eutyches, freedman of the emperors, an overseer, gladly fulfilled his vow as commanded, deservedly.
EDCS-11200173.summary: This records a vow fulfilled by Diogenes, an overseer, to Dacia Felix and the Genius of the imperial family, likely in the late 2nd or early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11200175.text_edition: \n Deae Haechatae sic pro salutae sic Aeliae Saturninae coniugis suae Haerennius sic Gemellinus tribunus statum coniugis redemit ex visu et votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-11200175.translation: To the goddess Haechata, for the health of Aelia Saturnina, his wife, Haerennius Gemellinus, tribune, redeemed his wife's status from a vision and gladly fulfilled his vow as commanded, deservedly.
EDCS-11200175.summary: This records the redemption of a wife's status from a divine vision by her husband, a tribune, to the goddess Haechata, likely in the late 2nd or early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11200177.text_edition: \n Dis immortalibus et numini sanctissimorum Augustorum Lucius Octavius Felix procurator promotus ad ducenariam provinciae Delmatiae sic\n
EDCS-11200177.translation: To the immortal gods and the divine spirit of the most holy Emperors, Lucius Octavius Felix, procurator promoted to the ducenarian rank in the province of Dalmatia, [dedicated this].
EDCS-11200177.summary: This records a promotion in rank by Lucius Octavius Felix, an imperial procurator in Dalmatia, to the immortal gods and the imperial numen, likely in the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-11200179.text_edition: \n Iunoni Regini sic Marcia Cara votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-11200179.translation: To Juno Regina, Marcia Cara gladly fulfilled her vow as commanded, deservedly.
EDCS-11200179.summary: This records a private votive offering to Juno Regina by Marcia Cara, likely in the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-11200180.text_edition: \n Iunoni Reginae Caius Sempronius Urbanus procurator Augusti\n
EDCS-11200180.translation: To Juno Regina, Gaius Sempronius Urbanus, imperial procurator, [dedicated this].
EDCS-11200180.summary: This dedicates an altar or shrine to Juno Regina by an imperial procurator, likely in the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-11200182.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Terentius Primus pro salute sua et suorum \n
EDCS-11200182.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Terentius Primus, for his own health and that of his family.
EDCS-11200182.summary: This records a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Terentius Primus for the health of himself and his family, likely in the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-11200184.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Ulpius Maximianus beneficiarius consularis votum libens posuit\n
EDCS-11200184.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Ulpius Maximianus, a beneficiarius of a consular governor, gladly set up this vow.
EDCS-11200184.summary: This records a vow dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Ulpius Maximianus, a military subordinate, likely in the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-11200188.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Marti Augusto pro salute Iuli Flaccini legati Augusti pro praetore translati in legionem XIII geminam posuerunt\n
EDCS-11200188.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest and Mars Augustus, for the health of Iulius Flaccinus, legate of the emperor with praetorian power, who was transferred to the Thirteenth Legion Gemina, they dedicated [this].
EDCS-11200188.summary: This records a dedication to Jupiter and Mars by officers for the health of Iulius Flaccinus, a legate, upon his transfer to a legion, likely in the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-11200189.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Iunoni Minervae Diis Consentibus Saluti Fortunae Reduci Apollini Dianae Victrici Nemesi Mercurio Herculi Soli Invicto Aesculapio Hygiae diis deabusque immortalibus Publius Aelius Hammonius vir egregius procurator Auggustorum\n
EDCS-11200189.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Juno, Minerva, the Gods Who Agree, Health, Fortune Returned, Apollo, Diana the Victorious, Nemesis, Mercury, Hercules the Unconquered Sun, Aesculapius, Hygeia, and all the immortal gods and goddesses, Publius Aelius Hammonius, a distinguished man, procurator of the Emperors.
EDCS-11200189.summary: This inscription is a dedication to a multitude of deities by Publius Aelius Hammonius, a procurator, likely on a public monument.

EDCS-11200191.text_edition: \n Deo numini Libero Patri ex voto Claudius Anicetus Augustalis coloniae\n
EDCS-11200191.translation: To the divine spirit of Liber Pater, Claudius Anicetus, Augustalis of the colony, fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-11200191.summary: This inscription records a votive offering to Liber Pater by Claudius Anicetus, an Augustalis in a colony.

EDCS-11200195.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription naming Gaius Spedius, likely the beginning of a dedication or dedication.

EDCS-11200201.text_edition: \n Deo Malalag(beli) Epipodius Augusti nostri verna vilicus votum solvit\n
EDCS-11200201.translation: To the god Malagabel, Epipodius, a household slave of our emperor, fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-11200201.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled to the god Malagabel by Epipodius, an imperial slave and vilicus.

EDCS-11200202.text_edition: \n Meni Cilvastiano Gaius Petronius Iustus cornicularius procuratoris ex voto\n
EDCS-11200202.translation: To Meni Cilvastianus, Gaius Petronius Iustus, a cornicularius of a procurator, fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-11200202.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Gaius Petronius Iustus, a cornicularius assisting a procurator.

EDCS-11200203.text_edition: \n Minervae Augustae Marcus Aurelius Valens magister kampi actarius l pa\n
EDCS-11200203.translation: To Minerva Augusta, Marcus Aurelius Valens, magister of the kampus, actarius, legio... [perhaps pa = prima?]
EDCS-11200203.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Minerva Augusta by Marcus Aurelius Valens, likely an official of a local organization or military unit.

EDCS-11200205.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by someone named Primus.

EDCS-11200217.text_edition: \n Invicto deo Serapidi Caesidius Respectus procurator Augusti nostri et Apronilla eius\n
EDCS-11200217.translation: To the Unconquered god Serapis, Caesidius Respectus, procurator of our emperor, and his wife Apronilla.
EDCS-11200217.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the god Serapis by Caesidius Respectus, an imperial procurator, and his wife.

EDCS-11200218.text_edition: \n Marcus Aurelius Valens decurio coloniae ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-11200218.translation: Marcus Aurelius Valens, decurio of the colony, erected by vow.
EDCS-11200218.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Marcus Aurelius Valens, a decurio in a colony.

EDCS-11200219.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico Titus Corbulius Priscianus decurio coloniae votum solvit\n
EDCS-11200219.translation: To Silvanus Domestus, Titus Corbulius Priscianus, decurio of the colony, fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-11200219.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled to Silvanus Domestus by Titus Corbulius Priscianus, a decurio in a colony.

EDCS-11200221.text_edition: \n ------ Aur]elius Dexter donum de suo posuit loco dato decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-11200221.translation: Aurelius Dexter placed this gift from his own resources, on land granted by decree of the town council.
EDCS-11200221.summary: This is a dedication by Aurelius Dexter, likely for a public purpose, as land was granted by decree of the decurions.

EDCS-11200222.text_edition: \n Euhemerus pro salute Antonini fili sui\n
EDCS-11200222.translation: Euhemerus for the health of his son Antoninus.
EDCS-11200222.summary: This inscription records a vow or dedication made by Euhemerus for the well-being of his son, Antoninus.

EDCS-11200226.text_edition: \n ------] ? August- NT mnis Augustalis coloniae patri cultorum ex visu fecit locus datus decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-11200226.translation: Augustalis of the colony, father of the worshippers, made this from a vision, on land granted by decree of the town council.
EDCS-11200226.summary: This is a dedication made by Augustalis, a prominent citizen of the colony, who acted upon a divine vision and with official land grant.

EDCS-11200227.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription, likely recording the names of two individuals, Julius Rufinus and another person whose name is lost.

EDCS-11200228.text_edition: \n - Sarmizegetusae Caius Aurelius Primus CIV PM-\n
EDCS-11200228.translation: Caius Aurelius Primus, honored man, governor of the province, [from] Sarmizegetusa...
EDCS-11200228.summary: This inscription likely honors Caius Aurelius Primus, a notable figure and governor, possibly from Sarmizegetusa.

EDCS-11200230.summary: This dedication was made by Venconia Severina in response to a divine vision.

EDCS-11200234.translation: Flavia Revocata placed this in fulfillment of a vow to the most present and placid goddess.
EDCS-11200234.summary: This is a votive offering by Flavia Revocata to a powerful and benevolent goddess.

EDCS-11200235.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelio Macrino GETTI decurioni coloniae Aelius Macrinius Epidianus qui et Epidius \n
EDCS-11200235.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Aelius Macrinius Epidianus, who is also called Epidius, dedicated this to Aelius Macrinus, decurion of the colony.
EDCS-11200235.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicating a monument to Aelius Macrinus, a decurion, by his son Aelius Macrinius Epidianus.

EDCS-11200239.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus his tegitur terris Antonia quam generavit Pergamos excelso monte superposita pascuis Nisonis titulum Antonius \n
EDCS-11200239.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Antonia, whom Pergamos bore, lies buried under these lands, situated on a high mountain above the pastures of Niso; Antonius [erected] the monument.
EDCS-11200239.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Antonia, whose burial place is described, with the monument erected by Antonius.

EDCS-11200240.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Aquilio Domitiano vixit nnos \n
EDCS-11200240.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Gaius Aquilius Domitianus lived for... years.
EDCS-11200240.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the death of Gaius Aquilius Domitianus and his lifespan, which is incomplete.

EDCS-11200241.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelia Hygia Iunior vixit annos VII menses VI Aelia Venusta neptiae fecit\n
EDCS-11200241.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aurelia Hygia the Younger lived 7 years and 6 months. Aelia Venusta made this for her granddaughter.
EDCS-11200241.summary: A tombstone for Aurelia Hygia the Younger, dedicated by her grandmother Aelia Venusta, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11200242.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caecilia Cai filia Valentina vixit annos VXEMAIVS \n
EDCS-11200242.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Caecilia Valentina, daughter of Caius, lived for [unknown duration, indicated by garbled letters VXEMAIVS].
EDCS-11200242.summary: A tombstone fragment for Caecilia Valentina, daughter of Caius, with a damaged lifespan, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11200243.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Claudiae Turpillae vixit annos item Caro Caesaris nostri vernae vixit annnos Herculanus Aug(usti) lib(ertus) adiutor tabularii uxori et filio bene merentibus fecit\n
EDCS-11200243.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Claudia Turpilla, and also for Caro, a homeborn slave of our Caesar. Herculanus, freedman of the Emperor and assistant to the registry office, made this for his deserving wife and son.
EDCS-11200243.summary: A tombstone for Claudia Turpilla and Caro, dedicated by Herculanus, an imperial freedman and registry assistant, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11200244.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marci Domiti Primi vixit annos LX collegium fabrum\n
EDCS-11200244.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Marcus Domitius Primus lived for 60 years. The guild of craftsmen [dedicated this].
EDCS-11200244.summary: A tombstone for Marcus Domitius Primus, who lived 60 years, dedicated by the guild of craftsmen, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11200246.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flavio Fortunato Augustali coloniae ex decuria VII collegii fabrum \n
EDCS-11200246.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Flavius Fortunatus, Augustalis of the colony, from the seventh decuria of the guild of craftsmen.
EDCS-11200246.summary: A tombstone for Flavius Fortunatus, an Augustalis and member of a craftsmen's guild decuria in a colony, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11200247.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulius C numeri Palmyrenorum Optatianensium militavit vixit nnos vivus fecit\n
EDCS-11200247.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Iulius C., a soldier of the Palmyrenes of Optatiania, served and lived [unknown duration]; he made this himself while alive.
EDCS-11200247.summary: A tombstone for Iulius C., a soldier of Palmyrene origin, who served and made his own memorial while alive, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11200249.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Quintus Iulius Secundinus veteranus legionis XIII geminae vixit annos LX Quintus Iulius Secundus filius patri pientissimo\n
EDCS-11200249.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Quintus Iulius Secundinus, a veteran of the 13th Gemina legion, lived for 60 years. Quintus Iulius Secundus, his most devoted son, [made this] for his father.
EDCS-11200249.summary: A tombstone for Quintus Iulius Secundinus, a 60-year-old veteran of the 13th Gemina legion, dedicated by his son, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11200251.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Macrino civi Trevero Attius Valens et Carantius Germanus faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-11200251.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Macrinus, a citizen of Trier. Attius Valens and Carantius Germanus arranged for this to be made.
EDCS-11200251.summary: A tombstone for Macrinus, a citizen of Trier, commissioned by Attius Valens and Carantius Germanus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11200252.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Saturninae Iacchius coniugi et Timotheus bene merenti fecerunt\n
EDCS-11200252.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Saturnina. Iacchius made this for his deserving wife, and Timotheus [also].
EDCS-11200252.summary: A tombstone for Saturnina, dedicated by her husband Iacchius and Timotheus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11200254.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius Valerius Epigonus vixit annos XXXX hic situs est\n
EDCS-11200254.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Lucius Valerius Epigonus lived for 40 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-11200254.summary: A tombstone for Lucius Valerius Epigonus, who lived 40 years, marking his burial place, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11200255.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valeri Philini vixit annos LXX collegium fa[brum ------\n
EDCS-11200255.translation: To the divine spirits of Valeria Philini, who lived 70 years; the guild of [craftsmen ---
EDCS-11200255.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Valeria Philini, mentioning her age and a guild, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11200262.text_edition: \n T laboribus aegrum memor esto fratri \n
EDCS-11200262.summary: This inscription is a personal admonition, likely part of a funerary monument or a tombstone, urging remembrance of a brother.

EDCS-11200268.translation: Convertina? To her husband Plotinus, Palma
EDCS-11200268.summary: This inscription appears to be a dedication or memorial, possibly to Plotinus, mentioning a woman named Convertina and a person named Palma.

EDCS-11200271.translation: Saturninus, decurio of the colony, a decurio.
EDCS-11200271.summary: This inscription records the name and position of Saturninus, a decurio (member of the town council) in a Roman colony, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11200323.summary: This is a dedication or funerary inscription honoring Publius Antonius Superus.

EDCS-11200324.summary: This inscription contains the initials of a Roman name, Marcus Cornelius (?), likely part of a larger inscription.

EDCS-11200325.summary: This inscription contains the initials of Roman names, Quintus Cornelius (?) and Sextus (?), likely part of a larger inscription.

EDCS-11200331.summary: This inscription records the name Aelius Iulianus, possibly as a dedication or a name on a tombstone.

EDCS-11200335.summary: This inscription records the name Figlina Victor Fortis, likely a personal name or part of a dedication.

EDCS-11200350.text_edition: \n Dianae Augustae Quintus Vibius Donatus praefectus cohortis donum dedit libens\n
EDCS-11200350.translation: Quintus Vibius Donatus, prefect of the cohort, gladly gave this gift to Diana Augusta.
EDCS-11200350.summary: This is a votive inscription recording a gift made by Quintus Vibius Donatus, a cohort prefect, to the goddess Diana Augusta, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11200352.summary: This is a fragmentary dedication to a personal deity or individual, likely from a tomb, with an incomplete name and patronymic.

EDCS-11200356.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Glavicida annorum XXX Valeria Vellavia coniugi bene merenti\n
EDCS-11200356.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Glavicida, aged 30, a devoted wife to her well-deserving husband.
EDCS-11200356.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Glavicida, who died at 30, set up by her husband, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11200365.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publio Aelio Aeliano vixit nnos III dies XXX Piperas Timostrati dispensatoris vikarius sic et Aelia Epictesis parentes filio bene merenti fecerunt\n
EDCS-11200365.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Publius Aelius Aelianus lived for 3 years and 30 days. Piperas Timostratus, a clerk's assistant, and his deputy, as well as Aelia Epictesis, his parents, made this for their well-deserving son.
EDCS-11200365.summary: This tombstone commemorates Publius Aelius Aelianus, who died at age 3 years and 30 days, and was erected by his parents, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11200366.text_edition: \n Agnam dono dedit Oppius Iulianus miles\n
EDCS-11200366.translation: Agnam was given as a gift by Oppius Iulianus, a soldier.
EDCS-11200366.summary: This inscription records a soldier, Oppius Iulianus, dedicating an 'agnam' (likely a type of offering or animal), probably from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11200378.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Bellinus Titi Iuli Saturnini conductoris publici portorii servus |contrascriptor votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-11200378.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest. Bellinus, a slave of Titus Iulius Saturninus, a public contractor of the customs duties, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow as a counter-scribe.
EDCS-11200378.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow by Bellinus, a slave of a public contractor, to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, likely from a provincial setting.

EDCS-11200380.text_edition: \n vixit annos XV et Iuliae Provincialis vixit annos XXV Provi[ncialis ------\n
EDCS-11200380.translation: lived for 15 years and Iulia Provencialis lived for 25 years. Provencialis [died]...
EDCS-11200380.summary: This fragmentary inscription seems to be a tombstone for two individuals, Iulia Provencialis and another person, recording their ages at death.

EDCS-11200401.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelius Cornelius veteranus vixit nnos LXXX Aelia Primitiva coniugi bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-11200401.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aelius Cornelius, a veteran, lived for 80 years. Aelia Primitiva set this up for her well-deserving husband.
EDCS-11200401.summary: This tombstone records Aelius Cornelius, a veteran who lived to 80, and was erected by his wife Aelia Primitiva, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11200402.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Surus mil(es) cohortis III Delmatarum vixit annos XXXX Aurelius Proculinus signifer et heres <e=F>t Quintina coniux et Surilleo sic et Quintinus et Sura pii patri bene merenti posuerunt \n
EDCS-11200402.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aurelius Surus, soldier of the 3rd Cohort of Dalmatians, lived for 40 years. Aurelius Proculinus, a standard-bearer, and his heir Quintina, his wife, and Surilleo, as well as Quintinus and Sura, set this up for their devoted and well-deserving father.
EDCS-11200402.summary: This tombstone commemorates Aurelius Surus, a soldier, who died at 40, and was erected by his children and wife, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11200403.summary: This is a highly fragmented inscription, possibly part of a name or dedication, with the beginning of a nomen Aurelius visible.

EDCS-11200404.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ulpius Vitalio vixit annos XVII Fabius Hatius annorum LX Valentina tlitulum fecet sic --\n
EDCS-11200404.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Ulpius Vitalio lived for 17 years. Fabius Hatius, aged 60, and Valentina made this epitaph...
EDCS-11200404.summary: This tombstone records the deaths of Ulpius Vitalio (age 17) and Fabius Hatius (age 60), with Valentina also involved in creating the monument, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11200413.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Antestius Valentinus miles cohortis III Delmatarum \n
EDCS-11200413.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Antestius Valentinus, soldier of the 3rd Cohort of Dalmatians.
EDCS-11200413.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Antestius Valentinus, a soldier of the 3rd Cohort of Dalmatians.

EDCS-11200422.text_edition: \n Cohortis I Vindelicorum Iuli Martialis centuria Clementis\n
EDCS-11200422.translation: Of the 1st Cohort of Vindelicorum, the century of Clementis, belonging to Iulius Martialis.
EDCS-11200422.summary: This inscription identifies a specific unit and commander within the 1st Cohort of Vindelicorum, likely related to a military standard or building.

EDCS-11200442.text_edition: \n Apollini conservatori maximi sanctissimique Imperatoris nostri Marci Aureli Antonini Pii Felicis Augusti Lucius Marius Perpetuus legatus devotus numini eius per Publium Aelium Claudia Sentino Gemellum tribunum\n
EDCS-11200442.translation: To Apollo, the greatest and most sacred preserver of our Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus. Lucius Marius Perpetuus, legate, devoted to his divine power through Publius Aelius Claudius Sentinus Gemellus, tribune.
EDCS-11200442.summary: This dedication honors Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus by Lucius Marius Perpetuus, a legate, and was overseen by the tribune Publius Aelius Claudius Sentinus Gemellus, likely dedicated to the emperor's health and power.

EDCS-11200443.text_edition: \n decurio ulus decurio anus decurio decurio us |(centurio) decurio Lucius Verus Litua Marcianus Severus Bo o Ianuarius Marcianus genus decurio \n
EDCS-11200443.translation: Decurio ... decurio ... decurio ... decurio ... (centurion) decurio Lucius Verus Litua Marcianus Severus Boius Ianuarius Marcianus, member of the curia, decurio.
EDCS-11200443.summary: This inscription lists several individuals identified as decuriones, including Lucius Verus Litua Marcianus Severus Boius Ianuarius Marcianus, likely indicating their roles within the local council or administration.

EDCS-11200446.text_edition: \n Dis Patriis Marcellus L DEL numeri Palmyrenorum Tibiscensium <v=I>(otum) solvit libens merito \n
EDCS-11200446.translation: To the Father Gods. Marcellus L. [son of ?] of the numbers of the Palmyrenes of Tibiscum, fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-11200446.summary: This votive inscription records Marcellus fulfilling a vow to the Father Gods for the Palmyrene and Tibiscensian numeri, possibly a military unit.

EDCS-11200447.text_edition: \n Genio numeri Palmyrenorum Tibiscensium etet horrei dis patriis et pro salute Auggustorum nnostrorum Publius Aelius Servius veteranus ex optione n(umeri)? s(upra) scripti ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-11200447.translation: To the Genius of the numbers of the Palmyrenes of Tibiscum, and to the Father Gods of the granary, and for the health of our Emperors Augustus. Publius Aelius Servius, a veteran, formerly optio of the aforementioned unit (?), set this up as a votive offering.
EDCS-11200447.summary: This inscription is a votive dedication for the health of the emperors by Publius Aelius Servius, a veteran and former optio of the Palmyrene and Tibiscensian numeri, also honoring the granary's tutelary deities.

EDCS-11200448.text_edition: \n Deo Soli Ierhaboli pro salutem sic dd(ominorum) nn(ostrorum) Augg(ustorum) Aurelius Laecanius Paulinus veteranus ex custode armorum cohortis I Vindelicorum et decurio coloniae Sarmizegetusae votum libens solvit\n
EDCS-11200448.translation: To the god Sol Ierabolus, for the safety of our Lords Emperors Augustus. Aurelius Laecanius Paulinus, a veteran, formerly armorum custos of the 1st Cohort of Vindelicorum and a decurio of the colony of Sarmizegetusa, willingly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-11200448.summary: This inscription records Aurelius Laecanius Paulinus, a veteran, former armorum custos of the 1st Cohort of Vindelicorum, and decurio of Sarmizegetusa, fulfilling a vow to the god Sol Ierabolus for the emperors' safety.

EDCS-11200449.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Laecanius Paulinus veteranus votum solvit\n
EDCS-11200449.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus. Laecanius Paulinus, a veteran, fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-11200449.summary: This is a votive inscription by Laecanius Paulinus, a veteran, dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus.

EDCS-11200450.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Conservatori Publius is Antoninus quod effugerit periculum votum libens solvit\n
EDCS-11200450.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Conservator. Publius Antoninus willingly fulfilled his vow, because he escaped danger.
EDCS-11200450.summary: This votive inscription records Publius Antoninus fulfilling a vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Conservator after surviving a dangerous event.

EDCS-11200451.text_edition: \n Aelius Victorinus decurio quaestoricius coloniae Apulensis pro salute sua et suorum ex voto libens posuit merito\n
EDCS-11200451.translation: Aelius Victorinus, decurio and quaestor of the colony of Apulensis, set this up as a votive offering willingly and deservedly for his own safety and that of his family.
EDCS-11200451.summary: This votive inscription records Aelius Victorinus, a decurio and quaestor of Apulensis, dedicating something for his and his family's well-being.

EDCS-11200455.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico sacrum pro salute M\n
EDCS-11200455.translation: Sacred to Silvanus Domestus for the health of M.
EDCS-11200455.summary: This is a dedication to the god Silvanus Domestus, likely by a soldier or official concerned with health, potentially for himself or another person whose name is lost.

EDCS-11200459.summary: This is a brief inscription recording that Sici made or erected something.

EDCS-11200460.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publius Aelius Claudianus miles numeri Palmyrenorum Tibiscensium vixit annos SC A MP M\n
EDCS-11200460.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Publius Aelius Claudianus, a soldier of the cohort of Palmyrenes at Tibiscum, lived SC A MP M years.
EDCS-11200460.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Publius Aelius Claudianus, a soldier in the Palmyrene cohort at Tibiscum, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11200461.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus lius Male E miles ex numero Palmyrenorum vixit annos ZI \n
EDCS-11200461.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed.lius Male E, a soldier from the Palmyrenes, lived ZI years.
EDCS-11200461.summary: This is a funerary inscription forlius Male E, a soldier from the Palmyrene unit, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11200462.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelius Sebler ex numero Maurorum Tibiscensium vixit nns VL et Aeliae SaTVS vixit annis XXX et Ael(ius) Valens filius eorum AT I heres ponendum curavit\n
EDCS-11200462.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aelius Sebler from the cohort of Moors at Tibiscum lived VL and Aelia SaTVS lived XXX years, and their son Aelius Valens, the heir, arranged for the burial.
EDCS-11200462.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aelius Sebler and Aelia SaTVS, with their son Aelius Valens arranging the burial; Aelius Sebler was a soldier in the Moorish cohort at Tibiscum, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11200463.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Publius Aelius.
EDCS-11200463.summary: This is a fragment of a funerary inscription mentioning Publius Aelius, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11200464.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publia Aelia In<g=C>enua vixit annos II et Publia Ingenua Florentina vixit annos II parentes posuerunt \n
EDCS-11200464.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Publia Aelia Ingenua lived II years and Publia Ingenua Florentina lived II years; the parents set them up.
EDCS-11200464.summary: This is a funerary inscription for two young girls, Publia Aelia Ingenua and Publia Ingenua Florentina, set up by their parents, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11200465.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Antonio Marco et Valerio Iuliano militibus ex Palmyrenis vixerunt annos XXX posuit Aelius Priscus bene merentibus\n
EDCS-11200465.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Antonio Marco and Valerio Iuliano, soldiers from Palmyra, who lived XXX years; Aelius Priscus set this up for them as deserving.
EDCS-11200465.summary: This is a funerary inscription for two soldiers from Palmyra, Antonio Marco and Valerio Iuliano, set up by Aelius Priscus, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11200466.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Barsemia vixit annos XV Aelia Artem<e=I>dora et Aurelius Barbarcas filio pusuerunt sic et fratri Menio \n
EDCS-11200466.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aurelius Barsemia lived XV years. Aelia Artemidora and Aurelius Barbarcas set this up for their son, and also for their brother Menius.
EDCS-11200466.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aurelius Barsemia, set up by his parents Aelia Artemidora and Aurelius Barbarcas, and also mentioning their brother Menius, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11200467.text_edition: \n Bana Gaius MAEIN optio patri et filio bene merentibus AKHFS\n
EDCS-11200467.translation: To Bana Gaius MAEIN, optio, for his father and son, deserving men.
EDCS-11200467.summary: This inscription is a dedication by Gaius MAEIN, an optio, to his father and son, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11200468.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Brisanus Aulusani miles co[hortis ------\n
EDCS-11200468.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Brisanus, son of Aulusanus, a soldier of the [cohort]...
EDCS-11200468.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for a soldier named Brisanus, son of Aulusanus, who served in an unknown cohort, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11200469.text_edition: \n S M D Claudius M veteranus ex numero Palmyrenorum Tibiscensium vixit annos XXXV militavit annos? CRA vixit annos XXV Au et MaD B V B filio bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-11200469.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Claudius M., a veteran from the contingent of Palmyrenes at Tibiscum, lived 35 years, served [duration unspecified] years, lived 25 years. Au and MaD erected this for their well-deserving son B.V.B.
EDCS-11200469.summary: This funerary inscription records the life and service of Claudius M., a veteran from the Palmyrene contingent at Tibiscum, erected by his parents, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11200470.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flavius Quintus PRI ex MARO vixit annos XXX Iulia Maxima coniugi piissimo faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-11200470.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Flavius Quintus, from MARO, lived 30 years. Iulia Maxima had this made for her most devoted husband.
EDCS-11200470.summary: This tombstone inscription commemorates Flavius Quintus from MARO, who lived 30 years, with his wife Iulia Maxima commissioning the monument, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11200472.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Procula Batava vixit annos XXVI A \n
EDCS-11200472.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Procula Batava lived 26 years. A.
EDCS-11200472.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Procula Batava, who died at the age of 26, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11200473.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Temai Dassi filio annorum XX \n
EDCS-11200473.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. To Temai, son of Dassi, aged 20 years.
EDCS-11200473.summary: This tombstone records the death of Temai, son of Dassi, at the age of 20, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11200475.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valeriae Crescentillae vixit nnos XXXV Caius Caecilius Felix Augustalis coloniae coniugi bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-11200475.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Caius Caecilius Felix, Augustalis of the colony, placed this for his well-deserving wife Valeriae Crescentillae, who lived 35 years.
EDCS-11200475.summary: This funerary inscription was set up by Caius Caecilius Felix, an Augustalis of the colony, for his wife Valeriae Crescentillae, who died at 35, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11200476.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus DISLARIIA s librarius numeri vixit annos XXXV ENNI BE vixit annos XX posuit bene merenti Dis Manibus TXEMA VLA SE V ID EILIVS bene merenti \n
EDCS-11200476.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. DISLARIIA s, a scribe of the [military] contingent, lived 35 years. ENNI BE lived 20 years. TXEMA VLA SE V ID EILIVS erected this for the well-deserving.
EDCS-11200476.summary: This tombstone records the lives of DISLARIIA s, a scribe, and ENNI BE, with TXEMA VLA SE V ID EILIVS commissioning the monument, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11200478.translation: EIE from the contingent of Palmyrenes lived...
EDCS-11200478.summary: This is a fragmentary tombstone inscription for EIE, who was part of the Palmyrene contingent, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11200479.translation: The father-in-law erected [this monument] for the well-deserving.
EDCS-11200479.summary: This is a funerary inscription where a father-in-law dedicated a monument to a well-deserving individual, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11200490.summary: This is a very brief funerary inscription recording the death of a child at the age of one, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11200492.translation: Of the cohort and M. Eivd
EDCS-11200492.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely recording something related to a cohort, possibly a dedication or ownership mark, date unknown.

EDCS-11200564.summary: This is a personal name, Marcus Syros, likely a freedman, with his patron's initial, purpose and date unknown.

EDCS-11200569.summary: This inscription records the name Aurelius Alexander, likely a tombstone or dedication, date unknown.

EDCS-11200577.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Aurelio Timoni veterano legionis XIII T V Aurelius Firminus tubicen heres posuit\n
EDCS-11200577.translation: To the departed spirits. Aurelius Firminus, a horn blower, his heir, erected this for Marcus Aurelius Timon, a veteran of the 13th Legion, and his wife.
EDCS-11200577.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by a horn blower and heir to a veteran of the 13th Legion and his wife, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11200591.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription mentioning Sarmizegetusa, a Roman city in Dacia, context and date unknown.

EDCS-11200618.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Luci Septimi Severi Pii Pertinacis Augusti Arabici Adiabenici Parthici maximi filio divi Marci Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici nepoti divi Hadriani abnepoti divi Traiani Parthici et divi Nervae adnepoti Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto res publica coloniae Septimiae Drobetensium\n
EDCS-11200618.translation: For the Emperor, Caesar Lucius Septimius Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, son of the divine Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, grandson of the divine Hadrian, great-grandson of the divine Trajan Parthicus and the divine Nerva, Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus. The state of the colony of Septimia Drobeta [dedicated this].
EDCS-11200618.summary: This is a formal dedication by the city of Septimia Drobeta to Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus (Caracalla), likely dating to his reign (c. 198-217 AD).

EDCS-11200621.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari [Marco Iulio] [Philippo] Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate consuli patri patriae proconsuli cohors I sagittariorum [Philip][piana] |(miliaria) equitata devota numini maiestatique eius\n
EDCS-11200621.translation: For the Emperor, Caesar [Marcus Julius] Philippus Augustus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power, consul, father of the country, proconsul. The First Cohort of Archers, the Philippian, one thousand strong and cavalry, devoted to his divine majesty, [dedicated this].
EDCS-11200621.summary: This inscription is a dedication by an auxiliary cohort (1st Archers, Philippian, equitate) to Emperor Philip the Arab, likely dating to his reign (244-249 AD).

EDCS-11200627.text_edition: \n Pro salute Augustorum Luci Septimi Severi et Marci Aureli Antonini et Publi Septimio sic Getae Caesaris sub cura Aureli Heracliti procuratoris Auggustorum Eutyches et Apulensis servi vilici tabularium a solo fecerunt\n
EDCS-11200627.translation: For the safety of the Emperors Lucius Septimius Severus, Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, and Publius Septimius Geta Caesar, under the supervision of Aurelius Heraclitus, procurator of the Emperors. Eutyches and Apulensis, slaves of the steward, built the record office from the ground up.
EDCS-11200627.summary: This inscription records the construction of a record office by slaves under the supervision of a procurator, for the welfare of the Severan dynasty emperors, likely early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-11200631.text_edition: \n Aram Iovi Cohortali sacrum PPOO Lupus tribunus\n
EDCS-11200631.translation: An altar sacred to Jupiter Cohors. Lupus, tribune, [dedicated this].
EDCS-11200631.summary: This is a dedication of an altar to Jupiter Cohors by a tribune named Lupus, date unknown but likely Roman Imperial.

EDCS-11200639.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio? Titio Cai filio Victricensi Ianuario |(centurioni) legionis IIII Flaviae frumentario vixit nnos LVI C(aius) Titius Epipodius libertus et heres optimo patrono bene merenti faciendum curavit hic situs est\n
EDCS-11200639.translation: To the departed spirits. Gaius Titius Epipodius, freedman and heir, had this made for his excellent patron Gaius? Titius Victricensis Januarius, a centurion of the 4th Flavian Legion and a grain official. He lived 56 years. He lies here.
EDCS-11200639.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a centurion and grain official of the 4th Flavian Legion, erected by his freedman and heir, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-11200640.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flaviae Valentinae Sirmiensi vixit nnos XL dies XVII coniugi carissimae et pientissimae et dignissimae ob principium castitatis Maecius Domitius |(centurio) legionis V Macedonicae\n
EDCS-11200640.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Flaviae Valentina of Sirmium lived 40 years and 17 days. For a most dear, most dutiful, and most worthy wife, Maecius Domitius, centurion of the Fifth Macedonian Legion, set this up.
EDCS-11200640.summary: A funerary inscription set up by a centurion for his wife, Flaviae Valentina, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE given the legion.

EDCS-11200641.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publius Aelius Iada Publio Aelio Viatori filio vixit annos III bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-11200641.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Publius Aelius Iada, son of Publius Aelius Viator, lived 3 years. Erected for a well-deserving son.
EDCS-11200641.summary: A funerary inscription for a young child, Publius Aelius Iada, set up by his father, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11200643.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcus Arrius Saturninus decurio municipii Hadriani Drobetensium vixit nnos XVI Arria Saturnina soror et heres iuxta voluntatem testamenti fac<i=E>undum curavit \n
EDCS-11200643.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Marcus Arrius Saturninus, a decurion of the municipium Hadriani Drobetensium, lived 16 years. Arria Saturnina, sister and heir, arranged for this to be done in accordance with the will.
EDCS-11200643.summary: A funerary inscription for a young decurion, Marcus Arrius Saturninus, set up by his sister and heir, indicating the inscription dates to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11200644.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulia Kalligenia vixit annos XL Iulius Daciscus vixit annos Valerius Vivibius vixit annos L Iulius Paet(inus) Vibenus Augustalis coloniae Drubetensium coniugi pientissimae et amicis bene merentibus posuit\n
EDCS-11200644.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Iulia Kalligenia lived 40 years. Iulius Daciscus lived [age missing]. Valerius Vivibius lived 50 years. Iulius Paet(inus) Vibenus Augustalis of the colony of Drubetensium set this up for a most dutiful wife and well-deserving friends.
EDCS-11200644.summary: A funerary inscription set up by Iulius Paetinus Vibenus, an Augustalis of Drubetensium, for his wife Iulia Kalligenia and other friends, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11200645.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Minicio Simphoro sic Augustali municipii Hadriani Dobretensium Minicia \n
EDCS-11200645.translation: To the Divine Spirits. For Marcus Minicius Simphorus, who held the position of Augustalis of the municipium Hadriani Dobretensium. Minicia [her relationship and the rest of the inscription are missing].
EDCS-11200645.summary: A fragmentary funerary inscription for Marcus Minicius Simphorus, an Augustalis of the municipium Hadriani Dobretensium, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11200646.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Aelius P. lived [age missing].
EDCS-11200646.summary: A very fragmentary funerary inscription mentioning Aelius P., likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11200648.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Laudice sic Syrae Collina Philadelphia vixit annis XXXC Hera coniugi bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-11200648.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Laudice, a Syra of Collina Philadelphia, lived 30 years. Hera set this up for a well-deserving wife.
EDCS-11200648.summary: A funerary inscription for Laudice, an enslaved woman or freedwoman of Syrian origin, set up by her wife Hera, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11200649.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Antio Herculano beneficiario consularis \n
EDCS-11200649.translation: To the Divine Spirits. For Marcus Antio Herculanus, a beneficiarius consularis.
EDCS-11200649.summary: A funerary inscription for Marcus Antio Herculanus, a military officer (beneficiarius consularis), likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11200650.translation: To the Divine Spirits. For Grata Vitalis.
EDCS-11200650.summary: A fragmentary funerary inscription commemorating Grata Vitalis, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11200652.translation: To the Divine Spirits. For Iulia Fortunata. [Rest of inscription missing].
EDCS-11200652.summary: A fragmentary funerary inscription for Iulia Fortunata, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11200654.summary: This is a funerary inscription from a mother to her son, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11200657.text_edition: \n IL Aurelius Atellanus veteranus candidatus legionis V Macedonicae \n
EDCS-11200657.translation: Gaius Aurelius Atellanus, veteran, candidate of the Fifth Macedonian Legion.
EDCS-11200657.summary: This inscription identifies a veteran soldier, Gaius Aurelius Atellanus, from the Fifth Macedonian Legion, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11200669.summary: This is a fragmented inscription mentioning a woman named Aelia and possibly a military cohort, with an uncertain date.

EDCS-11200672.text_edition: \n Quadrubiis sic Aurelius Marci filius Philiscus votum solvit libens \n
EDCS-11200672.translation: At Quadrubiis, Aurelius Philiscus, son of Marcus, willingly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-11200672.summary: This is a votive inscription recording Aurelius Philiscus fulfilling a vow at a location called Quadrubiis, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11200675.summary: This is a fragmented inscription mentioning a name ending in -ianus and Veri-, with an uncertain date and purpose.

EDCS-11200698.translation: The Seventh Claudia Pia Fidelis Legion.
EDCS-11200698.summary: This is an inscription identifying the Seventh Claudia Pia Fidelis Legion, a well-known Roman legion, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11200706.text_edition: \n Aurelius Mercurius milis sic cohortis I sagittariorum in figlinis magister super milites LX scripsit Aurelius Iulianus milis sic cohortis primae\n
EDCS-11200706.translation: Aurelius Mercurius, soldier, foreman over 60 soldiers in the potteries, wrote this; Aurelius Iulianus, soldier of the First Cohort.
EDCS-11200706.summary: This inscription records Aurelius Mercurius, a soldier and foreman in the potteries, along with Aurelius Iulianus, a soldier of the First Cohort, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11200721.text_edition: \n Aurelius Gratinianus pro immunitate votum vovit\n
EDCS-11200721.translation: Aurelius Gratinianus vowed his vow for immunity.
EDCS-11200721.summary: This is a votive inscription by Aurelius Gratinianus seeking or fulfilling a vow related to immunity, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11200727.translation: Castor set this up in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-11200727.summary: This is a votive inscription indicating that Castor erected a monument in fulfillment of a vow, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11200728.translation: Domina Placida Valerius Mexy, a vow.
EDCS-11200728.summary: This is a fragmented votive inscription mentioning Domina Placida and Valerius Mexy, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11200729.text_edition: \n Soli Invicto Mithrae pro salute sua suorumque Caius Antonius Iulianus votum libens merito solvit\n
EDCS-11200729.translation: Caius Antonius Iulianus willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Sol Invictus Mithras for the well-being of himself and his family.
EDCS-11200729.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Sol Invictus Mithras by Caius Antonius Iulianus.

EDCS-11200731.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld for Marcus Cassius Herculanus.
EDCS-11200731.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Marcus Cassius Herculanus.

EDCS-11200741.text_edition: \n Dianae Regina<e> Dioscorus Ianuari et [] []I contubernali<s> <e=T>x votum sic posuerunt \n
EDCS-11200741.translation: Dioscorus and [] I, comrades in the barracks, set up this vow to Diana Regina.
EDCS-11200741.summary: This is a votive inscription to Diana Regina, likely set up by soldiers or members of a barracks community.

EDCS-11200743.summary: This is a simple dedication or maker's inscription by Ambiurus.

EDCS-11200763.translation: To the Unconquered Sun God Mithras.
EDCS-11200763.summary: This is a dedication to the god Sol Invictus Mithras.

EDCS-11200770.text_edition: \n Septimius decurio coloniae Drubetae vixit annis LXV C<l=I>odii Septimius et Peregrinus R \n
EDCS-11200770.translation: Septimius, a decurion of the colony of Drubetae, lived for 65 years; Clodius Septimius and Peregrinus R [dedicated this?].
EDCS-11200770.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the life and death of Septimius, a decurion from the colony of Drubetae.

EDCS-11200781.text_edition: \n Reginae Aurelius Antoninus ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-11200781.translation: Aurelius Antoninus set this up in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-11200781.summary: This is a votive inscription by Aurelius Antoninus.

EDCS-11200784.text_edition: \n Deae placidae pro salute Marcianae et Quintiliani filiorum \n
EDCS-11200784.translation: To the tranquil goddess for the well-being of Marciana and her sons Quintilianus.
EDCS-11200784.summary: This is a prayer inscription to a goddess for the health of Marciana and her sons.

EDCS-11200785.summary: This is a votive inscription by Lucius Aurelius to the deity Eronus.

EDCS-11200787.text_edition: \n Herculi Invicto pro salute rinus et Iulianus posuerunt\n
EDCS-11200787.translation: Rinus and Iulianus set this up to the Unconquered Hercules for [their] well-being.
EDCS-11200787.summary: This is a votive inscription to Hercules Invictus by Rin us and Iulianus.

EDCS-11200807.summary: This is a simple funerary inscription recording the maker of a mold, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11200808.summary: This is a simple funerary inscription recording the maker of a mold, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11200883.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Publius Iulius Vitalianus ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-11200883.translation: Publius Iulius Vitalianus placed this in accordance with his vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus.
EDCS-11200883.summary: A votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Publius Iulius Vitalianus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11200902.text_edition: \n Soli Invicto Mithrae librarii cum Antonio Zoilo actuario praepositi numeri Surorum posuerunt\n
EDCS-11200902.translation: The scribes of the unit of Suri, along with Antonio Zoilo, the acting secretary, dedicated this to Sol Invictus Mithras.
EDCS-11200902.summary: This is a dedication to Sol Invictus Mithras made by the scribes and an acting secretary of a military unit, probably from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11200904.text_edition: \n Dardanicae us decurio coloniae et Aelius decurio? coloniae ex voto posuerunt\n
EDCS-11200904.translation: The decurion of the colony of Dardanica and Aelius, a decurion of the colony, placed this in accordance with their vow.
EDCS-11200904.summary: A votive inscription by two decurions of the colony of Dardanica, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11200905.summary: A personal votive inscription by Aurelius Dardanus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11200910.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelio Iulio Iuliano decurioni quaestoricio aedilicio coloniae Romulae Valeria Gemellina marito bene merenti posuit coniugi pro meritis quondam karissimo coniunx hanc Iuliano domum flendo fabricavi perennem frigida qua membra possint requiescere morti quattuor hic denos vixit sine culpa per annos et sua perfunctus vidit cum gloria honores ecce Gemellina pietate ducta marito struxi dolens digno sedem cum liberis una inter pampinea virgulta et gramina laeta umbra super rami virides ubi densa ministrat qui legis hos versus opta leve terra viator\n
EDCS-11200910.translation: To the departed spirits. Valeria Gemellina placed this for her husband, Aelius Iulius Iulianus, a quaestorian and aedilician decurion of the colony of Romula, a husband who deserved well; out of conjugal affection for his merits, most beloved husband, I, Gemellina, built this eternal home for Julian, that my cold limbs might rest there. He lived four times ten years without fault and saw his honors with glory. Behold, Gemellina, moved by piety, built a dwelling for her worthy husband with children, amid leafy shrubs and joyful green grass, where dense shade is provided by green branches; traveler who reads these verses, wish for light earth.
EDCS-11200910.summary: A complex funerary epitaph for Aelius Iulius Iulianus, a decurion of Romula, set up by his devoted wife Valeria Gemellina, with poetic verses, likely dating to the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11200926.text_edition: \n Titi Pii Prisci Titi Pii Prisci Vitalis turma Crispini\n
EDCS-11200926.translation: The troop of Titius Pius Priscus Vitalis, son of Titius Pius Priscus.
EDCS-11200926.summary: This inscription likely records a military troop or unit, possibly a cavalry squadron, with the names of its members or patron, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11200936.translation: Marinus Summus in front of the brick.
EDCS-11200936.summary: This is a very short inscription that seems to record a name and a location, possibly indicating ownership or a marker, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11200941.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription that mentions 'Invictus', possibly referring to Sol Invictus, and some illegible text, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11200943.summary: This inscription records a single name, possibly a dedication or part of a longer text.

EDCS-11200958.summary: This inscription records a partial name, Sextus Cornelius Ta, likely the beginning of a longer text, possibly a funerary or dedicatory monument.

EDCS-11201016.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Iulio Philippo Pio Felici Invicto Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate consuli proconsuli patri patriae et Imperatori Caesari Marco Iulio Philippo Pio Felici Invicto Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate consuli proconsuli patri patriae ala I Hispanorum devota numini et maiestati eorum Impperatoribus Philippo III et Philippo II consulibus\n
EDCS-11201016.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, supreme pontiff, holder of tribunician power, consul, proconsul, father of the fatherland, and to the Emperor Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, supreme pontiff, holder of tribunician power, consul, proconsul, father of the fatherland, the First Spanish Cavalry, devoted to their divine will and majesty. With Philippus III and Philippus II as consuls.
EDCS-11201016.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription to the emperors Philip the Arab and his son Philip the Younger, recording the devotion of the First Spanish Cavalry, likely from the mid-3rd century AD.

EDCS-11201021.text_edition: \n Optimum collegium duplariorum Iulius Marinus posuit\n
EDCS-11201021.translation: Julius Marinus erected this for the best guild of duplarii.
EDCS-11201021.summary: This inscription records the erection of a monument by Julius Marinus for the guild of duplarii, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201022.summary: This inscription records the name and rank (sesquiplicarius) of Aurelius Aurelianus, likely a soldier, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201023.text_edition: \n t sescuplucarius sic TPL votum sic posuit\n
EDCS-11201023.translation: sescuplucarius sic TPL he set up the vow in this manner
EDCS-11201023.summary: This inscription, though fragmentary, records the vow or dedication of a sescuplucarius, likely a military rank, during the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201025.text_edition: \n Ara Solis pro salutem alae primae Hispanorum piae fidelis principales et pro\n
EDCS-11201025.translation: Altar of the Sun, for the safety of the First Spanish Cavalry, the pious and faithful principals and for
EDCS-11201025.summary: This is the beginning of an inscription dedicated to the Sun for the well-being of the First Spanish Cavalry, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201144.translation: It has been copied and verified from the document which was displayed at Alburnus Maior near the Resculum station, in which was written what is written below.
