Artemidorus, son of Apollonius, master of the Guild of Jupiter Cernenus, and Valerius, son of Nicon, and Offas, son of Menophilus, quaestors of the same guild, by setting up this document publicly attest that from the aforesaid guild, where there were 54 men, no more than 17 men remained at Alburnus, and that Julius Iulus, their fellow magistrate, did not attend at Alburnus from the day of his magistracy, nor did he render an account to the guild and to those who were present, and if he had had anything, he would have returned it, nor were their funeral contributions sufficient, nor did he have a burial place, nor did anyone for such a long time, on the days when reading is contained, wish to convene or contribute funeral contributions or gifts, and for this reason they publicly attest through this document that if anyone should die, he should not think that he has the guild or that anyone will have a claim for a funeral from them. Displayed at Alburnus Maior on the 5th day before the Ides of February in the year when Lucius Aurelius Verus III and Quadratus were consuls. Done at Alburnus Maior. Copied and verified from the document which was displayed at Alburnus Maior near the Resculum station, in which was written what is written below. Artemidorus, son of Apollonius, master of the Guild of Jupiter Cernenus, and Valerius, son of Nicon, and Offas, son of Menophilus, quaestors of the same guild, by setting up this document publicly attest that from the aforesaid guild, where there were 54 men, no more than 17 men remained at Alburnus. Lucius Vasidius Victor, Gaius Secundinius Legitimus, Stertinius Rusticus, Aelius Plator, Geldon, Ulpius Felix, Septimius Plator. And that Julius Iulus, their fellow magistrate, did not attend at Alburnus from the day of his magistracy, nor did he render an account to the guild and to those who were present, and if he had had anything, he would have returned it, nor were their funeral contributions sufficient, nor did he have a burial place, nor did anyone for such a long time, on the days when reading is contained, wish to convene or contribute funeral contributions or gifts, and for this reason they publicly attest through this document that if anyone should die, he should not think that he has the guild or that anyone will have a claim for a funeral from them. Displayed at Alburnus Maior on the 5th day before the Ides of February in the year when Lucius Aurelius Verus III and Quadratus were consuls. Done at Alburnus Maior.
EDCS-11201144.summary: This lengthy document, dated to 147 AD, is a public declaration from the Guild of Jupiter Cernenus at Alburnus Maior concerning the depleted membership and financial issues related to funeral contributions, detailing the absence of a magistrate and the subsequent lack of provision for deceased members.

EDCS-11201145.translation: Epicadus [died?]... Cerdonis(?) until all... done at Alburnus Maior the 15th day before the Kalends of [October?], when Quintillus and Priscus were consuls. 15th day before the Kalends [of October?]. Andueia Batonis by pledge(?)... ceded(?) ... to be ready(?) ... Apulus Cerialis... Augebis... Helcius... Epicius (Felix?)... Maxsimus Veranis... Servilius Cassi(?) of Epicadus... of the debtor... Julius Macedon, the guarantor.
EDCS-11201145.summary: This fragmented inscription, likely dating to the late 2nd or early 3rd century AD, lists names of individuals and possibly refers to a debt or legal agreement involving a guarantor, related to the location Alburnus Maior.

EDCS-11201146.translation: One hundred and forty denarii, the principal and their interest, at the rate of one percent per month from that day onwards, as long as he abstains. That both be paid correctly, he requested on pledge. Andueina Batonis promised to pay on her pledge. Julius Alexander, which they should repay on the day requested, with the aforementioned interest. That both principal and interest be paid correctly, he requested on pledge. Andueina [aforementioned] promised to pay on her pledge. Julius Alexander. Done at Deusara on the 12th day before the Kalends of July, when Rusticus II and Aquilinus were consuls. One hundred and forty denarii, the principal and their interest, from that day onwards at the rate of one percent per month. That both be paid correctly, he requested on pledge. Andueina Batonis promised to pay on her pledge. Julius Alexander, which they should repay on the day requested, with the aforementioned interest. That both principal and interest be paid correctly, he requested on pledge. Andueina [aforementioned] promised to pay on her pledge. Julius Alexander. Done at Deusara on the 12th day before the Kalends of July, when Rusticus II and Aquilinus were consuls. Iulius Max[...]. Lucius [...]. Aurelius S[...]. Veri[...]. Vasi[...]. Nicom[...]. One hundred and forty denarii. Julius Alexander [...]. 140 denarii to Julius Alexander.
EDCS-11201146.summary: This inscription, dated to 138 AD, records a financial transaction, specifically a loan of 140 denarii with monthly interest, involving Andueina Batonis and Julius Alexander, with the transaction taking place at Deusara.

EDCS-11201148.translation: Julius Alexander promised that he would pay back sixty denarii on the day requested, and Alexander Caricci stated that he had received and owed sixty denarii, the aforementioned loan, and their interest for thirty days from this day to Julius Alexander or to whoever the matter pertains. Titius Primitius ordered this to be done on his own responsibility on the aforementioned day, that the principal with interest be paid back properly. This was done in Alburnus Maior on the 13th day before the Kalends of November in the consulship of Rusticus II and Aquilinus.
EDCS-11201148.summary: This inscription is a loan contract detailing the repayment of sixty denarii with interest, dated to the consulship of Rusticus II and Aquilinus (likely 162 CE) and enacted in Alburnus Maior.

EDCS-11201149.translation: Maximus Batonis bought and took possession by mancipation of a slave girl named Passia, or by whatever other name she is known, approximately six years old, a vendor's item, from Dasius Verzonis Pirusta of Kavieretum, for two hundred and five denarii, and he promised on his word that the girl is healthy, free from faults and crimes, and not a runaway or a wanderer, and that if anyone should evict this girl or any part of her from Maximus Batonis or from whoever the matter pertains, so that he cannot properly have and possess her, then he promised on his word that the money for which the girl was bought and as much again would be paid. Dasius Verzonis Pirusta of Kavieretum promised on his word and stated that he had received and has two hundred and five denarii from Maximus Batonis for the aforementioned girl. This was done in Karto on the 16th day before the Kalends of April in the consulship of Titus Aelius Caesar Antoninus Pius II and Bruttio Praesente II (139 CE).
EDCS-11201149.summary: This inscription records the sale of a slave girl named Passia, approximately six years old, for 205 denarii, with guarantees of her health and status, dated to 139 CE.

EDCS-11201150.translation: Dasius Breucus bought and took possession by mancipation of a boy named Apalaustus, or by whatever other name he is known, a Greek freedman, for two unciae (of silver) worth 600 denarii, from Bellicus Alexandri, with Marcus Vibius Longus acting as guarantor. He promised on his word that the boy, having been delivered healthy, free from faults and crimes, and not a runaway, fugitive, or subject to forfeiture, would be guaranteed, and if anyone should evict this boy or any part of him, so that the buyer above-mentioned, or whoever the matter pertains to, cannot properly enjoy, have, and possess him, then he promised on his word that the amount by which he is thus evicted, double the price, would be paid properly. Bellicus Alexandri also ordered this to be done on his own responsibility. And he stated that he had received and has 600 denarii, the price for the aforementioned boy, from Dasius Breucus. This was done in the camp of Legio XIII Gemina on the 17th day before the Kalends of June in the consulship of Rufinus and Quadratus (161 CE).
EDCS-11201150.summary: This inscription records the purchase of a Greek freedman boy for 600 denarii, with guarantees regarding his status and health, dated to 161 CE.

EDCS-11201152.translation: Andueia Batonis bought and took possession by mancipation of half of a house, with the right side being in Alburnus Maior in the street of the Pirustae, between the neighbors Plator Acceptianus and Ingenuus Callisti, for three hundred denarii from Vetur[ius] Valens. She promised on her word that she would have half of the house, which is the subject of this transaction, with its fences, enclosures, boundaries, entrances, and barred windows, in the best and most complete way possible, and that if anyone should evict this house or any part of it from Andueia Batonis or whoever the matter pertains to, so that she cannot properly have, possess, and enjoy it, then she promised on her word that the amount by which it is thus permitted not to have, double the price, would be paid properly. Vetur[ius] Valens promised on his word and stated that he had received and has 300 denarii, the price for half of the aforementioned house, from Andueia Batonis. It was agreed between them that Vetur[ius] Valens would pay the taxes for this house until the census. This was done in Alburnus Maior on the day before the Nones of May in the consulship of Quintillus and Priscus (166 CE).
EDCS-11201152.summary: This inscription records the sale of half a house in Alburnus Maior for 300 denarii, with a stipulation for the seller to continue paying taxes, dated to 166 CE.

EDCS-11201154.translation: In the consulship of Macrinus and Celsus, on the 13th day before the Kalends of June. I, Flavius Secundinus, wrote at the request of Memmius Asclepius, because he said he does not know how to write. He stated that he had contracted and contracted his labor to Aurelius Adiutor, a gold worker, from today until the upcoming Ides of November, and he will have to receive seventy denarii and [HS 3] denarii for his children as payment over time. He will have to perform healthy and strong labor for the aforementioned contractor. If, however, he wishes to leave or cease work against the contractor's will, he will have to pay 5 HS (sestertii) per day in coined money. The contractor, if he hinders the work, will have to calculate his share proportionally. If, at the end of the period, the contractor delays payment of the wages, he will be subject to the same penalty, except for three days of cessation. This was done in Immensum Maior.
EDCS-11201154.summary: This inscription is a labor contract where Flavius Secundinus, on behalf of Memmius Asclepius, hires Aurelius Adiutor for aurary work for a period and a specified wage, with penalties for breach of contract, dated to the consulship of Macrinus and Celsus (likely 205 or 207 CE).

EDCS-11201156.translation: In the consulship of Verus III and Quadratus II, on the 4th day before the Kalends of June. Lupus Carentis stated that he had received and accepted fifty denarii entrusted to him by Iulius Alexander, which he must return to him without any dispute. This was done in Alburnus Maior.
EDCS-11201156.summary: This inscription records a deposit of fifty denarii made by Iulius Alexander to Lupus Ca rentis, dated to the consulship of Verus III and Quadratus II (likely 199 CE).

EDCS-11201157.translation: Between Cassius Frontinus and Julius Alexander, a partnership for the sale of dates, from the next X Kalends of January, which was in the consulship of Pudens and Polio, until the upcoming pridie Idus Aprilis, it has been agreed that whatever profit or loss arises in this partnership from the business will be shared equally. In this partnership, Julius Alexander contributed five hundred denarii, paid in cash, and Secundus, the slave of Cassius Palumbus, the manager, contributed two hundred and sixty-seven denarii. If anyone in this partnership is found to have committed fraud through malice, they will have to pay one denarius for each asse (of damage) and one denarius for twenty (asses), and bring another. And at the end of the period, after deducting debts, they will either receive their sum mentioned above or, if anything remains, they will divide it. Cassius Frontinus stipulated that this be done, paid, and guaranteed. Julius Alexander promised. Two signed copies of this document have been made. Furthermore, Losa owes 50 denarii, which she must receive from the aforementioned partners. This was done in Deusarae on the 5th day before the Kalends of April in the consulship of Verus III and Quadratus (199 CE).
EDCS-11201157.summary: This inscription details a partnership agreement for the sale of dates between Cassius Frontinus and Julius Alexander, including contributions and profit/loss sharing, dated to 199 CE.

EDCS-11201161.translation: In the consulship of Pontianus and Rufinus, on the 7th day before the Ides of February. Valerius Firmus wrote at the request of Verzo Beusantis, because [...] Beusantis [...] land [...] twenty denarii [...] Alburnus Maior.
EDCS-11201161.summary: This fragmented inscription likely records a transaction involving land or property and twenty denarii, with parties named Valerius Firmus and Verzo Beusantis, enacted in Alburnus Maior.

EDCS-11300110.text_edition: \n Deae Nemesi pro salute Auggustorum curiales territorii \xce\xa3ucidavensis templum a solo restituerunt\n
EDCS-11300110.translation: The curiales of the territory of Ucensis restored the temple to the Goddess Nemesis from the ground up for the health of the Augusti.
EDCS-11300110.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a temple to the Goddess Nemesis by the curiales of the Ucensis territory for the health of the emperors.

EDCS-11300180.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Umbria Africana vixit annis LXV Umbrius Villanus matri pientissimae ponendum curavit\n
EDCS-11300180.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. Umbria Africana lived for 65 years. Umbrius Villanus had this erected for his most devoted mother.
EDCS-11300180.summary: This is a tombstone inscription recording the death of Umbria Africana at age 65, dedicated by her son Umbrius Villanus.

EDCS-11300181.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Ianius Ianuarius vixit annis XLI Ianius Marcianus et Ianiae Ianuariae ipsi patri bene merenti et C Marcia coniux pia cum FILIVIO suam posuerunt ex C\n
EDCS-11300181.translation: To the Divine Shades. Caius Ianius Ianuarius lived for 41 years. Ianius Marcianus and Iania Ianuaria, his devoted wife, with their son, set this up for their much-deserving father and husband and for themselves. His devoted wife with her son.
EDCS-11300181.summary: A funerary epitaph for Caius Ianius Ianuarius, set up by his wife and son.

EDCS-11300184.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno pro salute Imperatorum Luci Septimi Severi Pii Pertinacis et Marci Aureli Antonini Augusti et Publi Septimi Getaes sic S Caius Valerius Ingenuus signifer legionis XIII geminae ex voto\n
EDCS-11300184.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest Dolichenus, for the safety of the Emperors Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus and Publius Septimius Geta, Caius Valerius Ingenuus, standard-bearer of the Thirteenth Gemina Legion, [erected this] in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-11300184.summary: A votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Dolichenus for the safety of the emperors Severus, Caracalla, and Geta, by a soldier of the Thirteenth Legion, likely from the late 2nd or early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-11300185.translation: To Silvanus Domesticus, Aurelius Praesentinus [set this up].
EDCS-11300185.summary: A dedication to the god Silvanus Domesticus, likely set up by Aurelius Praesentinus, the rest of which is lost.

EDCS-11300191.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Primin ONCA vixit annos V item filia VX CO et IPT AVD\n
EDCS-11300191.translation: To the Divine Shades. Prima Onca lived for 5 years. Likewise [her] daughter VX CO et IPT AVD.
EDCS-11300191.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the short lives of Prima Onca and her daughter, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11300193.text_edition: \n Numini domini nostri Marci Aureli Antonini Pii Felicis Augusti Caius Gaurius Gaurianus sacerdos coloniae Apulensis et Flavius Sotericus Augustalis coloniae Sarmizegetusae conductores ferrariarum\n
EDCS-11300193.translation: To the divine numen of our lord Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, Caius Gaurius Gaurianus, priest of the colony of Apulum, and Flavius Sotericus, Augustalis of the colony of Sarmizegetusa, supervisors of the mines, [set this up].
EDCS-11300193.summary: An inscription honoring Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, erected by officials from the colonies of Apulum and Sarmizegetusa, likely from the mid-3rd century AD.

EDCS-11300201.text_edition: \n Pro salute ddominorum nnostrorum invictissimorum Impperatorum Severi et Antonini et Getae Caesaris Augggustorum sic et Iuliae et Plautillae Auggustarum et Plautiani clarissimi viri praefecti praetorio patris Augustae sub Pomponio Liberale consulari Mauri Micienses et Iulius Euangelianus praefectus templum deorum patriorum vetustate conlapsum sua pecunia et opera restituerunt Cilone II et Libone consulibus\n
EDCS-11300201.translation: For the safety of our lords, the most invincible Emperors Severus and Antoninus and Caesar Geta Augusti, and of Julia and Plautilla Augustae, and of Plautianus, most distinguished man, praetorian prefect, father of Augusta. Under the consulship of Cilon II and Libo, Mauri Micienses and Iulius Euangelianus, prefect, restored at their own expense and labor a temple of the ancestral gods that had collapsed from age.
EDCS-11300201.summary: An inscription recording the restoration of a temple of ancestral gods for the imperial family, including Severus, Caracalla, Geta, Julia Domna, Plautilla, and Plautianus, during the consulship of Cilo II and Libo (likely 205 AD).

EDCS-11300202.text_edition: \n Deo Invicto Publius Aelius Euphorus pro salute sua et suorum templum a solo fecit\n
EDCS-11300202.translation: To the Unconquered God. Publius Aelius Euphorus built this temple from the ground up for his own safety and that of his family.
EDCS-11300202.summary: A dedication to the Unconquered God, recording the construction of a temple by Publius Aelius Euphorus for his family's well-being.

EDCS-11300207.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno Commageno Iulius Trophimus vovit\n
EDCS-11300207.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest Dolichenus Commagenus, Iulius Trophimus vowed.
EDCS-11300207.summary: A votive inscription to Jupiter Dolichenus Commagenus, dedicated by Iulius Trophimus.

EDCS-11300208.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno aram Miciae M praefectus cohortis II Flaviae Commagenorum bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-11300208.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest Dolichenus, [something was dedicated] by Micia, prefect of the Second Cohort of Commagenian Flavians, for him well-deserving.
EDCS-11300208.summary: An altar dedication to Jupiter Dolichenus, set up by a prefect of the Second Cohort of Commagenians, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11300210.text_edition: \n In honorem domus divinae Genio Miciae Marcus Cornelius Stratonicus Augustalis coloniae Dacicae Sarmizegetusae pecunia sua dedit\n
EDCS-11300210.translation: In honor of the divine house, Marcus Cornelius Stratonicus, Augustalis of the Dacian colony of Sarmizegetusa, dedicated this with his own funds to the Genius of Micia.
EDCS-11300210.summary: An inscription honoring the imperial family and the Genius of Micia, dedicated by Marcus Cornelius Stratonicus, an official from Sarmizegetusa.

EDCS-11300211.text_edition: \n Pro salute domus divinae sacrum Genium sic Miciae Marcus Cornelius Stratonicus Augustalis coloniae et ante frontem lapidibus stravit\n
EDCS-11300211.translation: Sacred to the welfare of the divine household, Marcus Cornelius Stratonicus, Augustalis of the colony, paved the approach and the area before the entrance with stones for the Genius, as he did for Micia.
EDCS-11300211.summary: This is a votive paving inscription dedicated to the Genius of the imperial family by Marcus Cornelius Stratonicus, an Augustalis, likely in the 1st-2nd century CE.

EDCS-11300213.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo veterani et cives Romani per Caium Antonium Crispinum magistrum pagi Miciensis\n
EDCS-11300213.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, veterans and Roman citizens of the Miciensian pagus, through Caius Antonius Crispinus, the magistrate.
EDCS-11300213.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus made by veterans and citizens of the Miciensian pagus, through their magistrate Caius Antonius Crispinus, likely in the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-11300214.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo veterani et cives Romani Micienses per Aurelium Alpinum et Claudium Nicomaedem sic magistros votum solverunt libentes merito\n
EDCS-11300214.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, the veterans and Roman citizens of Miciensium, through Aurelius Alpinus and Claudius Nicomedes, their magistrates, gladly and deservedly fulfilled their vow.
EDCS-11300214.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by the veterans and citizens of Miciensium, through their magistrates Aurelius Alpinus and Claudius Nicomedes, likely in the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-11300218.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Claudius Saecularis beneficiarius consularis pro se et Priscino collega votum solvit\n
EDCS-11300218.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Claudius Saecularis, a beneficiarius consularis, fulfilled his vow for himself and his colleague Priscinus.
EDCS-11300218.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus made by Claudius Saecularis, a beneficiarius consularis, for himself and his colleague, likely in the 2nd-3rd century CE.

EDCS-11300219.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Caius Iulius Zoticus ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-11300219.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Caius Julius Zoticus erected this in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-11300219.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Caius Julius Zoticus, likely in the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-11300220.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Marcus Mateus Victor veteranus\n
EDCS-11300220.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Marcus Mateus Victor, a veteran.
EDCS-11300220.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Marcus Mateus Victor, a veteran, likely in the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-11300221.text_edition: \n Iovi Hierapolitano fecit Cassius Rufus votum dedit\n
EDCS-11300221.translation: To Jupiter Hierapolitanus, Cassius Rufus made this and gave his vow.
EDCS-11300221.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Hierapolitanus by Cassius Rufus, likely in the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-11300226.text_edition: \n Libero Patri et Liberae Ulpius Abascantus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-11300226.translation: To Liber Pater and Libera, Ulpius Abascantus gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-11300226.summary: This is a dedication to the deities Liber Pater and Libera by Ulpius Abascantus, likely in the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-11300229.text_edition: \n Minervae Augustae et Genio cohortis II Flaviae Commagenorum Ianuarius actarius votum libens merito s(olvit)\n
EDCS-11300229.translation: To Minerva Augusta and the Genius of the Second Cohort of Flavia Commagenorum, Ianuarius, an actarius, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-11300229.summary: This is a dedication to Minerva Augusta and the Genius of a military cohort by Ianuarius, an actarius, likely in the 2nd-3rd century CE.

EDCS-11300231.text_edition: \n Plutoni Rufus praefectus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-11300231.translation: To Pluto, Rufus, the prefect, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-11300231.summary: This is a dedication to Pluto by Rufus, a prefect, likely in the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-11300233.text_edition: \n Silvano Rufus praefectus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-11300233.translation: To Silvanus, Rufus the prefect fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-11300233.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled to the god Silvanus by Rufus, a prefect, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-11300234.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico Aelius Verus armorum custos votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-11300234.translation: To Silvanus Domesticus, Aelius Verus, guardian of arms, fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-11300234.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled to Silvanus Domesticus by Aelius Verus, identified as an 'armorum custos' (guardian of arms), likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-11300235.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico Aurelia Valentina votum solvit libens\n
EDCS-11300235.translation: To Silvanus Domesticus, Aurelia Valentina fulfilled her vow willingly.
EDCS-11300235.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled to Silvanus Domesticus by Aurelia Valentina, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-11300236.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Silvanus Domesticus by Calpurnius Philomusus, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-11300237.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Silvanus Domesticus by Iulius Crescens, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-11300238.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico Ulpius Mare ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-11300238.translation: Ulpius Mare placed this in fulfillment of a vow to Silvanus Domesticus.
EDCS-11300238.summary: This inscription records a votive offering to Silvanus Domesticus by Ulpius Mare, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-11300239.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the god Silvanus Domesticus, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-11300240.text_edition: \n Herculi sacrum Dom cohortis II Flaviae Commagenorum \n
EDCS-11300240.translation: Sacred to Hercules, by the cohort of the 2nd Flavian Cohort of Commageni.
EDCS-11300240.summary: This inscription records a sacred dedication to Hercules by the 2nd Flavian Cohort of Commageni, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11300241.text_edition: \n Silvano deo aeterno Quintianus decurio alae votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-11300241.translation: To Silvanus, the eternal god, Quintianus the decurion of the ala fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-11300241.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled to Silvanus, identified as an eternal god, by Quintianus, a decurion of an ala, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11300242.text_edition: \n Marti ala I Bosporanorum cui praeest Claudius Sosius praefectus Silvano Domestico et Mercurio sacrum Aelius Ianuarius posuit\n
EDCS-11300242.translation: Sacred to Mars of the 1st Ala of Bosporani, commanded by Claudius Sosius the prefect, and to Silvanus Domesticus and Mercury; Aelius Ianuarius placed this.
EDCS-11300242.summary: This inscription records a sacred dedication to Mars, Silvanus Domesticus, and Mercury by Aelius Ianuarius, under the command of Claudius Sosius, prefect of the 1st Ala of Bosporani, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11300243.text_edition: \n Iovi Turmazgadi cohors II Flavia Commagenorum equitata sagittariorum cui praeest Marcus Arruntius Agrippinus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-11300243.translation: To Jupiter Turmazgades, the Second Cohort of Flavia Commagenorum, the mounted archers, commanded by Marcus Arruntius Agrippinus, gladly and deservedly fulfilled their vow.
EDCS-11300243.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by a military cohort to Jupiter Turmazgades.

EDCS-11300244.text_edition: \n Veneri sacrum Aelia Flaviae aram animo votum posuit\n
EDCS-11300244.translation: Sacred to Venus. Aelia Flavia placed this altar, a vow made with good intention.
EDCS-11300244.summary: This inscription records a woman named Aelia Flavia dedicating an altar to Venus.

EDCS-11300245.translation: Titus Va gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-11300245.summary: This inscription records an individual named Titus Va fulfilling a vow, likely on a small dedication.

EDCS-11300246.summary: This fragmentary inscription mentions Aelius [---]ntianus, a cavalry prefect.

EDCS-11300250.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelia Asinia annorum LI Marcus Antonius Viator coniugi bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-11300250.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aelia Asinia, aged 51, Marcus Antonius Viator placed this for his well-deserving wife.
EDCS-11300250.summary: This inscription is a tombstone set up by Marcus Antonius Viator for his wife Aelia Asinia.

EDCS-11300251.text_edition: \n A AL Aeli- XX PAT annos LXX vixit annos EN fili- \n
EDCS-11300251.translation: [---] Aelius [---]ntianus, aged 20, lived for [---] years. [---] son [---]
EDCS-11300251.summary: This fragmentary inscription seems to record the age of a person named Aelius and mentions a son.

EDCS-11300254.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Respecta Aureli- vixit annos LXXV Iustina X-I \n
EDCS-11300254.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Respecta, wife of Aurelius, lived 75 years. Iustina, [---] X-I [---]
EDCS-11300254.summary: This inscription is a tombstone for Respecta, wife of Aurelius, and possibly mentions another individual named Iustina.

EDCS-11300256.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cornelio Businnio vixit annos X \n
EDCS-11300256.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Cornelius Businnius, lived 10 years.
EDCS-11300256.summary: This inscription is a tombstone for a child named Cornelius Businnius.

EDCS-11300257.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Mattavia Laeda vixit annos XXV et Blaste filia Lucius Mattavius Blastus coniugi et filiae bene merentibus posuit\n
EDCS-11300257.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Mattavia Laeda, aged 25, and her daughter Blaste. Lucius Mattavius Blastus placed this for his well-deserving wife and daughter.
EDCS-11300257.summary: This inscription is a tombstone set up by Lucius Mattavius Blastus for his wife Mattavia Laeda and daughter Blaste.

EDCS-11300258.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Quintus, standard-bearer of the ala [---] epagonum.
EDCS-11300258.summary: This inscription records Quintus, a standard-bearer (imaginifer) of a cavalry unit.

EDCS-11300288.summary: A simple dedication to an unspecified deity or person by Optatus, likely erected in the Imperial Roman period.

EDCS-11300289.translation: To Silvanus Domesticus, Publius C. C. [dedicated this].
EDCS-11300289.summary: A dedication to the household god Silvanus by Publius C. C., likely dating to the Imperial Roman period.

EDCS-11300292.text_edition: \n Deo aeterno Aurelius Maximus decurio coloniae quaestor municipii Apulensis posuit\n
EDCS-11300292.translation: Aurelius Maximus, decurion of the colony and quaestor of the municipality of Apula, erected this to the eternal god.
EDCS-11300292.summary: A dedication by Aurelius Maximus, an official in the municipality of Apula, to the eternal god, likely from the later Imperial period.

EDCS-11300294.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Caius Valerius Iustus |(centurio) \n
EDCS-11300294.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Gaius Valerius Iustus, centurion, [dedicated this].
EDCS-11300294.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Gaius Valerius Iustus, a centurion, likely dating to the Imperial Roman period.

EDCS-11300295.text_edition: \n Marcus Aurelius Calpurnianus |(centurio) numeri singularium Britannicorum dibus Patronis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-11300295.translation: Marcus Aurelius Calpurnianus, centurion of the unit of singular troops of Britannia, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to the divine Patrons.
EDCS-11300295.summary: A soldier's fulfillment of a vow to the divine Patrons, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11300296.text_edition: \n Silvanis et Silvano Campestribus pro pro salute Boceni Fronto pater p(osuit)\n
EDCS-11300296.translation: For the safety of Bocenus Fronto, his father erected this to the Silvani and Silvanus Campestris.
EDCS-11300296.summary: A dedication for the well-being of Bocenus Fronto by his father to the woodland deities Silvani and Silvanus Campestris, likely from the Imperial Roman period.

EDCS-11300297.text_edition: \n Soli invicto sacrum Aelius Iulius solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-11300297.translation: Aelius Iulius willingly and deservedly dedicated this sacred offering to Sol Invictus.
EDCS-11300297.summary: A private dedication to the Unconquered Sun (Sol Invictus) by Aelius Iulius, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11300305.text_edition: \n Numini Aesculapio sic et Hygiae Aurelius C<hr=RH>estus votum libens solvit merito \n
EDCS-11300305.translation: Aurelius Chrestus willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to the divine power of Aesculapius and also Hygieia.
EDCS-11300305.summary: A vow fulfillment to Aesculapius and Hygieia by Aurelius Chrestus, likely from the Imperial Roman period.

EDCS-11300306.text_edition: \n Aesculapio et Hygiae sacrum Publius Furius Saturninus legatus Augusti pro praetore\n
EDCS-11300306.translation: Publius Furius Saturninus, legate of the emperor with praetorian power, dedicated this sacred offering to Aesculapius and Hygieia.
EDCS-11300306.summary: A dedication to Aesculapius and Hygieia by Publius Furius Saturninus, a high-ranking imperial legate, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11300307.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo pro salute Publi Furii Saturnini legati Augusti pro praetore Publius Aelius Maximianus\n
EDCS-11300307.translation: Publius Aelius Maximianus [dedicated this] to Jupiter Best and Greatest for the safety of Publius Furius Saturninus, legate of the emperor with praetorian power.
EDCS-11300307.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Publius Aelius Maximianus for the well-being of the imperial legate Publius Furius Saturninus, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11300308.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Ulpius Maximinus |(centurio) legionis V Macedonicae compos peditum Britannicorum votum solvit libens laetus merito\n
EDCS-11300308.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Ulpius Maximinus, a centurion of the Fifth Legion Macedonica, commander of infantry of the Britons, gladly, joyfully, and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-11300308.summary: This inscription records a votive offering to Jupiter by Ulpius Maximinus, a centurion and infantry commander from the Fifth Legion Macedonica.

EDCS-11300309.text_edition: \n quod voverat Caius Caerellius Sabinus legatus Augusti legionis XIII geminae legatus Augusti pro praetore provinciae Raetiae posuit\n
EDCS-11300309.translation: Caius Caerellius Sabinus, legate of the Emperor of the Thirteenth Legion Gemina and legate of the Emperor with praetorian power of the province of Raetia, erected this.
EDCS-11300309.summary: This inscription records the erection of a monument by Caius Caerellius Sabinus, a high-ranking imperial legate in the province of Raetia.

EDCS-11300310.text_edition: \n decurialis tribunicius tribunus legionis XII fulminatae praefectus cohortis III Cyrenaicae flamen coloniae praefectus quinquennalis pro Impperatoribus Antonino et Vero Auggustis aedilicius decurio votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-11300310.translation: A decurion of the *decurialis tribunate*, a tribune of the Twelfth Legion Fulminata, prefect of the Third Cohort Cyrenaica, priest of the colony, quinquennial prefect for the Emperors Antoninus and Verus Augusti, *aedilicius decurio*, fulfilled his vow gladly, joyfully, and deservedly.
EDCS-11300310.summary: This inscription records a votive offering by a high-ranking official of a colony, who held various military and religious positions, likely during the reign of Emperors Antoninus Pius and Marcus Aurelius.

EDCS-11300311.translation: A decurion of the colony gladly and deservedly.
EDCS-11300311.summary: This inscription records a votive offering by a decurion of a colony.

EDCS-11300314.text_edition: \n us IL- vixit nnos IL Moesicus coniugi et fili heres posuit bene merentibus\n
EDCS-11300314.translation: Moesicus set this up for his wife and son, deserving of a good inheritance, who lived [---] years [---] months.
EDCS-11300314.summary: This inscription records a funerary monument set up by Moesicus for his deceased wife and son, indicating their lifespan.

EDCS-11300321.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulio Secundo exploratori stipendiorum XXXII domo Agrippina vixit annos LV h(eredes) faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-11300321.translation: To the Divine Shades. For Iulius Secundus, an explorer with 32 years of service, from Agrippina, who lived 55 years. His heirs arranged for this to be made.
EDCS-11300321.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Iulius Secundus, an explorer with 32 years of service, who lived to 55, and was honored by his heirs.

EDCS-11300322.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription that likely records a person's age at death.

EDCS-11300326.translation: The Second Legion Adjutrix, pious and faithful.
EDCS-11300326.summary: This inscription is a designation of the Second Legion Adjutrix, highlighting its loyalty.

EDCS-11300327.summary: This inscription is a designation of the Fourth Legion Flavia Felix.

EDCS-11300328.summary: This inscription is a designation of the Fourth Legion Flavia Felix.

EDCS-11300329.translation: Detachment of the Sixth Ferrata Legion.
EDCS-11300329.summary: This inscription records a detachment of the Sixth Ferrata Legion, likely a military marker or dedication.

EDCS-11300331.summary: This inscription records the names Decebalus and Scorilo, possibly indicating a personal dedication or ownership.

EDCS-11300338.text_edition: \n Cereri Augustae sacrum Suriacus Augusti nostri dispensator aurariarum cum suis dedicante Aulo Senecione Contiano viro egregio procuratore III Nonas Ianuarias Laeto II et Ceriale consulibus\n
EDCS-11300338.translation: Sacred to Ceres Augusta. Suriacus, administrator of the mines of our Augustus, with his staff, dedicated this, with Aulus Senecio Constantius, a distinguished man and procurator, acting as dedicator, on the third day before the Nones of January, when Laetus II and Cerialis were consuls.
EDCS-11300338.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Ceres Augusta by Suriacus, an administrator of mines, and Aulus Senecio Constantius, a procurator, dated to 4 January, 166 CE.

EDCS-11300339.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno pro salute imperii perpetui Marci Antoni Gordiani Augusti Aurelius Gaius beneficiarius consularis votum posuit merito\n
EDCS-11300339.translation: For the perpetual safety of the empire of Marcus Antonius Gordianus Augustus, Aurelius Gaius, a consular beneficiary, placed this vow, as is fitting.
EDCS-11300339.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Aurelius Gaius, a consular beneficiary, for the welfare of Emperor Gordian III.

EDCS-11300340.text_edition: \n DO sacerdos templum Iovis Optimi Maximi Dolicheni pro se et suorumque sic omnium votum posuit\n
EDCS-11300340.translation: The priest D.O. placed this temple of Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus for himself and his family, and for all in this way, as is fitting.
EDCS-11300340.summary: This inscription records a dedication of a temple to Jupiter Dolichenus by a priest for himself, his family, and all others.

EDCS-11300341.text_edition: \n Fortunae Reduci et Genio numeri Aurelius Redux praefectus numeri M H donum libens posuit\n
EDCS-11300341.translation: Aurelius Redux, prefect of the M H cohort, gladly placed this gift for Fortuna Redux and the Genius of the cohort.
EDCS-11300341.summary: This inscription records a gift dedicated by Aurelius Redux, prefect of a cohort, to Fortuna Redux and the cohort's Genius.

EDCS-11300342.text_edition: \n D<ea=AE>e Fortunae Salutari Marcus Aurelius Verecundus decurio municipii Apulensis pro salute sua et suorunque sic omnium votum libens merito posuit \n
EDCS-11300342.translation: To Dea Fortunae Salutari, Marcus Aurelius Verecundus, decurion of the municipium of Apula, placed this vow for his own safety and that of his family, and for all in this way, gladly and as is fitting.
EDCS-11300342.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Marcus Aurelius Verecundus, a decurion of Apula, for his own safety and that of his family.

EDCS-11300343.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Opelleus Surus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-11300343.translation: Opelleus Surus paid his vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, gladly and as is fitting.
EDCS-11300343.summary: This inscription records a votive offering paid by Opelleus Surus to Jupiter Optimus Maximus.

EDCS-11300345.translation: Onesimus placed this for Silvanus Domestticus.
EDCS-11300345.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Silvanus Domestticus by Onesimus.

EDCS-11300346.text_edition: \n Silvano Silvestri votum fecit Titius votum libens merito\n
EDCS-11300346.translation: Titius made this vow to Silvanus Silvestris, gladly and as is fitting.
EDCS-11300346.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Titius to Silvanus Silvestris.

EDCS-11300348.text_edition: \n Augusti verna adiutor tabularii dono dedit\n
EDCS-11300348.translation: Augustus' household slave, an assistant in the archive office, gave this as a gift.
EDCS-11300348.summary: This inscription records a gift of an imperial household slave who worked in an archive, likely related to a public building or office, dating to the early imperial period.

EDCS-11300349.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publius Aelius Gaius vixit annos L Aelia Ingenua coniugi et Aelius Gaianus filius bene merenti fecerunt\n
EDCS-11300349.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Publius Aelius Gaius, who lived 50 years, and Aelia Ingenua, his wife, and Aelius Gaianus, his son, made this for him, a well-deserving person.
EDCS-11300349.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by a wife and son for the deceased husband and father, Publius Aelius Gaius, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11300350.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Alexandrianus civis Bithynus vixit nnos LXV Tatius Lucius vixit annos XXX Antoninus Iulius Lucilianus fratres patri et fratri et Lucia mater bene merentibus posuerunt\n
EDCS-11300350.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Alexandrianus, a citizen of Bithynia, who lived 65 years; Tatius Lucius, who lived 30 years; Antoninus Iulius Lucilianus, brothers, erected this for their father and brother and mother, well-deserving persons.
EDCS-11300350.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by two brothers for their parents and each other, identifying the deceased and their origins and ages, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11300351.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Asclepiades vixit nnos LX Aurelia Auina coniugi \n
EDCS-11300351.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Aurelius Asclepiades, who lived 60 years; Aurelia Auina, for her wife.
EDCS-11300351.summary: This is a funerary inscription where Aurelia Auina dedicates a monument to her deceased wife, Aurelius Asclepiades, indicating the wife's age, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11300352.text_edition: \n vixit annos Septimiae Speratae vixit nnos IIII Caius Septimius Constans fratri et uxori eius et nepti bene merentibus faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-11300352.translation: He lived 4 years for Septimia Sperata. Caius Septimius Constans arranged for this to be made for his brother and his wife and his granddaughter, well-deserving persons.
EDCS-11300352.summary: This funerary inscription records Caius Septimius Constans commissioning a monument for his brother, his brother's wife, and his granddaughter, including the ages of the deceased, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11300353.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Syrillioni aedituo qui vixit annos LXXVIII Liberalis Auggustorum libertus a commentariis et Aelia Victoria patri bene merenti\n
EDCS-11300353.translation: To the Divine Spirits. For Syrillio, the temple attendant, who lived 78 years. Liberalis, freedman of the emperors and from the commentarii, and Aelia Victoria, for their well-deserving father.
EDCS-11300353.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated by two individuals, Liberalis and Aelia Victoria, to their father Syrillio, a temple attendant who lived to 78, with Liberalis identified by his administrative role, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11300360.text_edition: \n Apollini Augusto sacrum Panes Nosetis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-11300360.translation: Sacred to Apollo Augustus. Panes Nosetis willingly paid his vow, deservedly.
EDCS-11300360.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Apollo Augustus by Panes Nosetis, who willingly fulfilled a vow, likely associated with a temple or shrine, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11300362.text_edition: \n Dianae Augustae Panes Epicadi qui et Suttius dono dedit\n
EDCS-11300362.translation: To Diana Augusta. Panes Epicadi, who is also Suttius, gave this.
EDCS-11300362.summary: This dedicatory inscription records Panes Epicadi, also known as Suttius, presenting a gift to Diana Augusta, likely at her sanctuary, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11300363.text_edition: \n Genio collegii kastelli Baridustarum Seneca Bisonis dono dedit\n
EDCS-11300363.translation: To the Genius of the college of the castellum Baridustarum. Seneca Bisonis gave this.
EDCS-11300363.summary: This inscription records Seneca Bisonis dedicating a gift to the Genius of the Baridustarum castellum (fortress) college, suggesting a collegium associated with a fortified settlement, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11300364.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Marci Aurelii Superianus et Supe<r=N>atus pro salute sua suorumque votum libens merito posuerunt \n
EDCS-11300364.translation: For Jupiter Best and Greatest. Marcius Aurelius Superianus and Supernatus placed this vow willingly and deservedly for their own safety and that of their family.
EDCS-11300364.summary: This is a votive inscription where Marcius Aurelius Superianus and Supernatus dedicate a monument to Jupiter Optimus Maximus for the health of themselves and their families, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11300365.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo GOS Antonius Maximus et filii posuit sic\n
EDCS-11300365.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Antonius Maximus and his sons erected this.
EDCS-11300365.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Jupiter by Antonius Maximus and his sons.

EDCS-11300371.text_edition: \n Silvano aram posuit Varro Sceni cot sic promiserat collegio Aelius Be\n
EDCS-11300371.translation: Varro Sceni, as he had promised, erected this altar to Silvanus for the association.
EDCS-11300371.summary: This inscription records the erection of an altar to Silvanus by Varro Sceni for an association.

EDCS-11300372.text_edition: \n Silvano Augusto sacrum Hermes Myrini votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-11300372.translation: Hermes of Myrina willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to the sacred Silvanus Augustus.
EDCS-11300372.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Hermes of Myrina to Silvanus Augustus.

EDCS-11300374.text_edition: \n Silvano Silvestri sacrum SODAN -POS Annaius votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-11300374.translation: Annaius willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Silvanus Silvestris.
EDCS-11300374.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Annaius to Silvanus Silvestris.

EDCS-11300378.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Alexander Gai vixit annos XXXX posuit Gaius filius\n
EDCS-11300378.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Alexander Gai lived for 40 years. His son Gaius erected this.
EDCS-11300378.summary: This is a tombstone recording the death of Alexander Gai after 40 years of life, erected by his son Gaius.

EDCS-11300379.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelia Sambu vixit annis XXX Publius Aelius Licinianus coniugi innocentissimae posuit bene merenti\n
EDCS-11300379.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aurelia Sambu lived for 30 years. Publius Aelius Licinianus erected this for his most innocent wife, who deserved it well.
EDCS-11300379.summary: This tombstone records the death of Aurelia Sambu at the age of 30, erected by her husband Publius Aelius Licinianus.

EDCS-11300389.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus CENS AVG Publius Anto Baradus Augusti libertus patri bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-11300389.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Publius Anto Baradus, freedman of the Emperor, erected this for his well-deserving father.
EDCS-11300389.summary: This tombstone records the erection of a monument by Publius Anto Baradus, a freedman, for his father.

EDCS-11300390.summary: This inscription likely records a dedication or a marking by Tadius associated with the Legio XIII Gemina.

EDCS-11300395.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iuliae Maximae vixit annos LXVI \n
EDCS-11300395.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Iulia Maxima lived for 66 years.
EDCS-11300395.summary: This tombstone records the death of Iulia Maxima at the age of 66.

EDCS-11300397.summary: This inscription simply records the name Publius Aelius Terentius, possibly as a dedication or ownership mark.

EDCS-11300398.translation: To Leticia, his daughter, newly born.
EDCS-11300398.summary: This is a dedication to a child, possibly on a tombstone or as a familial offering.

EDCS-11300417.text_edition: \n Dianae aram posivit sic Martinus votum\n
EDCS-11300417.translation: Martinus placed this altar to Diana, fulfilling his vow.
EDCS-11300417.summary: This inscription records Martinus dedicating an altar to Diana, likely a private votive offering.

EDCS-11300425.summary: This is a dedication, likely a votive offering, made by Aselius Alexander for his well-being.

EDCS-11300429.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Claudius Valentinus V lia posuit\n
EDCS-11300429.translation: Claudius Valentinus erected this for Jupiter Optimus Maximus and Vlia.
EDCS-11300429.summary: This inscription records Claudius Valentinus dedicating a monument to Jupiter Optimus Maximus and Vlia, possibly a spouse.

EDCS-11300431.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Lucius Bellius Saturninus IIIIvir municipii Apulensis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-11300431.translation: Lucius Bellius Saturninus, a magistrate of the municipality of Apulensis, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus.
EDCS-11300431.summary: This inscription records Lucius Bellius Saturninus, a local magistrate, fulfilling a vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century CE given the office.

EDCS-11300432.text_edition: \n Libens votum Libero Patri solvit Paulianus\n
EDCS-11300432.translation: Paulinus willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Liber Pater.
EDCS-11300432.summary: This inscription records Paulinus fulfilling a vow to Liber Pater, likely a private votive offering.

EDCS-11300434.text_edition: \n Marcus Aurelius Maximus flamen municipii Apulensis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-11300434.translation: Marcus Aurelius Maximus, priest of the municipality of Apulensis, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-11300434.summary: This inscription records Marcus Aurelius Maximus, a local priest, fulfilling a vow, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century CE given the office.

EDCS-11300437.summary: This is a dedication to the deities Diana and Terra Mater, likely a votive offering.

EDCS-11300438.summary: This inscription simply records the name Publius Aelius Terentius, possibly on a tombstone or marker.

EDCS-11300449.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus of the Commageni.
EDCS-11300449.summary: This is a dedication to the syncretic deity Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus, indicating a local cult.

EDCS-11300451.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulia Magiona vixit annos XXI pater posuit filiae bene merenti hic est sita\n
EDCS-11300451.translation: To the Divine Shades. Iulia Magiona lived 21 years. Her father set this up for his deserving daughter, who rests here.
EDCS-11300451.summary: A funerary epitaph for Iulia Magiona, erected by her father, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11300457.summary: A fragmentary inscription likely naming Titus Aelius, possibly part of a dedication or funerary monument.

EDCS-11300459.text_edition: \n vixit annos Emeritus NAVS filiae pientissumae sic\n
EDCS-11300459.translation: Emeritus NAVS lived for his most devoted daughter. Thus...
EDCS-11300459.summary: A fragment of an inscription possibly recording the lifespan of Emeritus NAVS and mentioning a devoted daughter.

EDCS-11300460.text_edition: \n onis filius vix(it) an(nos) --- et Mel vixit annos LX FIL A\n
EDCS-11300460.translation: ...onis, his son, lived... years, and Mel lived 60 years. FIL A...
EDCS-11300460.summary: A fragmented epitaph recording the lifespans of two individuals, possibly father and son, or relatives.

EDCS-11300461.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelia vixit annos i filiae heredes et IV IINA\n
EDCS-11300461.translation: To the Divine Shades. Aelia lived 1 year. Her heirs and IV IINA [set this up]...
EDCS-11300461.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aelia, who died at one year old, set up by her heirs.

EDCS-11300465.text_edition: \n vixit annos LXX vixit annos XXXX us vixit annos Ulpius Castus bene meritis \n
EDCS-11300465.translation: ...lived 70 years, ... lived 40 years. Ulpius Castus [set this up] for the deserving...
EDCS-11300465.summary: A funerary inscription potentially commemorating multiple individuals, with Ulpius Castus mentioned as a benefactor.

EDCS-11300466.translation: To the Divine Shades. Valerius V [and] Aelius...
EDCS-11300466.summary: A fragmentary funerary inscription mentioning Valerius V and Aelius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11300471.summary: A funerary epitaph fragment for PVBIA, who died at four years old.

EDCS-11300472.translation: Vivia, wife to her husband Aurelius BII...
EDCS-11300472.summary: A fragment of a marriage epitaph, with Vivia dedicating it to her husband Aurelius.

EDCS-11300480.summary: A highly fragmented inscription with illegible names and lifespans, likely funerary or dedicatory.

EDCS-11300481.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Quinta Quinti vixit nnos XL Quintus Quinti vixit nnos XXX posuerunt \n
EDCS-11300481.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Quinta Quinti lived 40 years; Quintus Quinti lived 30 years. They placed this monument.
EDCS-11300481.summary: This is a tombstone recording the lives and deaths of Quinta Quinti and Quintus Quinti, likely a married couple, placed by themselves or a relative.

EDCS-11300488.summary: This inscription is likely a name, possibly of the deceased or the dedicator, on a tombstone or dedication, with the letters KA perhaps indicating a military unit or status.

EDCS-11300489.text_edition: \n rvii filia vixit annos XX Postumius Clemens Tyranni faciendum curavit bene merenti\n
EDCS-11300489.translation: [---], daughter, lived 20 years. Postumius Clemens arranged for this monument to be made for Tyrannis, who deserved well.
EDCS-11300489.summary: This is a tombstone for Tyrannis, daughter of an unnamed father, erected by Postumius Clemens, recording her age and acknowledging her merit.

EDCS-11300492.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Finitus vixit annos filius heres posuit et Supinus filius annorum\n
EDCS-11300492.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Finitus lived [XX] years. His son and heir placed [this]. And Supinus, his son, aged [---].
EDCS-11300492.summary: This is a tombstone for Finitus, with his son and heir and possibly another son named Supinus mentioned, recording the former's age and the familial relationship.

EDCS-11300493.text_edition: \n PONI annos L s patri bene merenti pos(uit)\n
EDCS-11300493.translation: [He lived] 50 years to his deserving father, [he] placed [this].
EDCS-11300493.summary: This fragment of a tombstone records the age of the deceased, who is identified as a father, and indicates it was placed by his son.

EDCS-11300509.summary: This is a short inscription likely indicating ownership or a dedication by a person named Gaius Gemelli, possibly on a tomb or monument.

EDCS-11300524.summary: This inscription refers to a military unit, the Cohors Numidarum, likely found on a monument related to the unit itself or its members.

EDCS-11300525.summary: This inscription refers to a military unit, the Cohors Numidarum, likely found on a monument related to the unit itself or its members.

EDCS-11300526.summary: This inscription identifies a specific Roman auxiliary unit, the Cohors Numidarum Antoniniana, likely part of a larger monument or structure.

EDCS-11300528.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Carvilius Secundinus vixit annos Lucius Carvilius Rusticinus praefectus cohortis II Flaviae Bessorum fratri in exemplum piissimo ponendum curavit\n
EDCS-11300528.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Carvilius Secundinus lived [XX] years. Lucius Carvilius Rusticinus, prefect of the II Flavia Bessorum cohort, arranged for this to be placed as an example for his most devoted brother.
EDCS-11300528.summary: This tombstone honors Carvilius Secundinus, erected by his brother Lucius Carvilius Rusticinus, a military officer, who commends his brother's devotion.

EDCS-11300537.text_edition: \n Candia Maximi vixit annos LXXXX Aelius Talanus Dugionis vixit annos LXX titulum posuit filius patri et matri bene merentibus\n
EDCS-11300537.translation: Candia Maxima lived 90 years, Aelius Talanus Dugionis lived 70 years. A son placed this monument for his deserving father and mother.
EDCS-11300537.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Candia Maxima and Aelius Talanus Dugionis, placed by their son.

EDCS-11300538.text_edition: \n Dantica Cusiona vixit annos XX titulum posuit mater filiae bene merenti\n
EDCS-11300538.translation: Dantica Cusiona lived 20 years. A mother placed this monument for her deserving daughter.
EDCS-11300538.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Dantica Cusiona, placed by her mother.

EDCS-11300539.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Sabina Dasmeni vixit annos L posuit Senecio coniugi bene merenti et filii Prinada et Surilla et Surinius et Sanilis posuerunt titulum matri bene merenti\n
EDCS-11300539.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Sabina Dasmeni lived 50 years. Senecio placed this monument for his deserving wife; and her sons Prinada, Surilla, Surinius, and Sanilis placed this monument for their deserving mother.
EDCS-11300539.summary: This is a funerary inscription honoring Sabina Dasmeni, with separate dedications by her husband Senecio and her sons.

EDCS-11300567.text_edition: \n Imperatoribus Caesaribus Marco Aurelio Antonino et Lucio Aurelio Commodo Auggustis cohors III Gallorum\n
EDCS-11300567.translation: For the Emperors Caesars Marcus Aurelius Antoninus and Lucius Aurelius Commodus Augusti, the Third Cohort of Gauls.
EDCS-11300567.summary: This is a dedication by the Third Cohort of Gauls to Emperors Marcus Aurelius Antoninus and Commodus, likely from the late 2nd century AD.

EDCS-11300568.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Valerianus AV [lived...].
EDCS-11300568.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription for Valerianus.

EDCS-11300570.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely a dedication or ownership marker, for Titius Avitus Germanus.

EDCS-11300573.text_edition: \n Soli Invicto pro salutem sic Cai Iuli Valentini conductoris salinarum Iulius Omucio libertus actor posuit\n
EDCS-11300573.translation: To Sol Invictus, for the safety of Caius Julius Valentinus, manager of the salt works. Julius Omucius, freedman, his agent, placed this.
EDCS-11300573.summary: This is a dedication to the god Sol Invictus by an agent named Julius Omucius on behalf of the manager of the salt works, Caius Julius Valentinus.

EDCS-11300575.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iustina vixit annis LV Aurelia vixit annis s veteranus \n
EDCS-11300575.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Iustina lived 55 years. Aurelia lived [xx] years. Veteran [perhaps the husband or father].
EDCS-11300575.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription for Iustina and Aurelia, possibly mentioning a veteran.

EDCS-11300580.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Severus decurio vixit annos XXXX Aurelius Qui\n
EDCS-11300580.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aurelius Severus, a decurion, lived 40 years. Aurelius Qui [continued...].
EDCS-11300580.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription for Aurelius Severus, a decurion, and likely another individual named Aurelius Qui.

EDCS-11300582.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari divi Traiani Parthici filio divi Nervae nepoti Traiano Hadriano Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate XIII consuli III patri patriae cohors VIII Raetorum civium Romanorum equitata torquata\n
EDCS-11300582.translation: For the Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrian Augustus, son of the deified Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the deified Nerva, Pontifex Maximus, with Tribunician Power for the 13th time, Consul for the 3rd time, Father of the Fatherland, the Eighth Cohort of Raetians, Roman citizens, cavalry, awarded the torque.
EDCS-11300582.summary: This is a lengthy dedication to Emperor Hadrian by the Eighth Cohort of Raetians, highlighting his titles and military honors, likely from the period of his 13th tribunician power (128-129 AD).

EDCS-11300585.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto \n
EDCS-11300585.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus.
EDCS-11300585.summary: This is a dedication or honorific inscription addressed to the Roman Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, likely from the period of his reign (161-180 AD).

EDCS-11300590.text_edition: \n Dianae Augustae sacrum Titus Aelius Crescentianus praefectus cohortis IIII Hispanorum [equitatae Antoni][nia]nae votum solvit libens\n
EDCS-11300590.translation: To the August Goddess Diana, Titus Aelius Crescentianus, prefect of the 4th Cohort of Spanish Horsemen, granted by Antoninus, fulfilled his vow willingly.
EDCS-11300590.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Diana by Titus Aelius Crescentianus, a prefect of a cavalry cohort, likely dating to the Antonine period (mid-2nd century AD).

EDCS-11300591.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Publius Aelius Honoratus praefectus cohhortis IIII Hispanorum domo Romae v(otum) s(olvit) l(ibens) m(erito)\n
EDCS-11300591.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Publius Aelius Honoratus, prefect of the 4th Cohort of Spanish Horsemen, from Rome, fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-11300591.summary: This is a votive inscription to Jupiter by Publius Aelius Honoratus, a prefect of a Spanish cavalry cohort from Rome, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-11300592.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Aurelius Firminus and Aurelius Firimanus fulfilled their vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-11300592.summary: This is a partially preserved votive inscription to Jupiter by two individuals, Aurelius Firminus and Aurelius Firimanus, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-11300593.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Aurelius Serenus praefectus cohortis IIII Hispanorum equitatae Gordianae\n
EDCS-11300593.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Aurelius Serenus, prefect of the 4th Gordian Cohort of Spanish Horsemen.
EDCS-11300593.summary: This is an inscription dedicated to Jupiter by Aurelius Serenus, prefect of the 4th Gordian Cohort of Spanish Horsemen, likely dating to the period of Emperor Gordian III (238-244 AD).

EDCS-11300594.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Eros Zotici signifer votum libens solvit\n
EDCS-11300594.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Eros Zotici, standard-bearer, fulfilled his vow willingly.
EDCS-11300594.summary: This is a votive inscription to Jupiter by Eros Zotici, a standard-bearer (signifer), likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-11300595.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Lucius Iulius Iucundus praefectus cohortis IIII Hispanorum equitatae <v=N>(otum) solvit libens merito \n
EDCS-11300595.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Lucius Iulius Iucundus, prefect of the 4th Cohort of Spanish Horsemen, fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-11300595.summary: This is a votive inscription to Jupiter by Lucius Iulius Iucundus, a prefect of a Spanish cavalry cohort, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-11300596.text_edition: \n Libero Patri - Aelius Fuscus decurio cohortis IIII Hispanorum equitata voto libens rettulit\n
EDCS-11300596.translation: To Liber Pater, Aelius Fuscus, decurion of the 4th Cohort of Spanish Horsemen, willingly returned his vow.
EDCS-11300596.summary: This is an inscription dedicating a vow to Liber Pater by Aelius Fuscus, a decurion of a Spanish cavalry cohort, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-11300598.text_edition: \n ------] I MION AVIZ CIN I OALV S VS I M \n
EDCS-11300598.translation: [Restored text] I MION AVIZ CIN I OALV S VS I M
EDCS-11300598.summary: This is a heavily damaged inscription with illegible text, making its purpose and date impossible to determine.

EDCS-11300602.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ulpius Restitutus vixit annos LV \n
EDCS-11300602.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed, Ulpius Restitutus lived 55 years.
EDCS-11300602.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the name and age at death of Ulpius Restitutus, likely dating to the 1st to 3rd century AD.

EDCS-11300626.summary: A very brief inscription stating that Rusticus is the maker, likely on a personal object or dedication, from an unknown date.

EDCS-11800566.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno et Deae Suriae Magnae Caelesti pro salute perpetui imperii Romani et legionis XIII geminae <F=E>lavius Barhadadi sacerdos Iovis Dolicheni ad legionem supra scriptam votum libens merito posuit \n
EDCS-11800566.translation: For Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus and the great celestial goddess Syria, for the perpetual safety of the Roman Empire and the Thirteenth Legion Gemina, Flavius Barhadadi, priest of Jupiter Dolichenus attached to the aforementioned legion, gladly and deservedly made this vow.
EDCS-11800566.summary: This is a religious dedication by Flavius Barhadadi, a priest of Jupiter Dolichenus serving the Thirteenth Legion Gemina, made for the prosperity of the Roman Empire and the legion, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11800571.text_edition: \n Dedicatum X Kalendas Iulias Arriano et Papo consulibus\n
EDCS-11800571.translation: Dedicated on the tenth day before the Kalends of July, when Arrianus and Papus were consuls.
EDCS-11800571.summary: This inscription records the dedication date of an object or monument, specifically the 10th of the Kalends of July during the consulship of Arrianus and Papus, likely from the late Roman Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-12000862.text_edition: \n Genio publici portorii et Titi Iuli Saturnini conductoris publici portorii Illyrici Maximianus servus vilicus ex privatis pro salute sua suorumque\n
EDCS-12000862.translation: For the Genius of the publicani (tax collectors) and for Titius Iulius Saturninus, a contractor of the publicani of Illyricum, Maximianus, a slave and steward, from his own funds, for the safety of himself and his family.
EDCS-12000862.summary: This inscription is a dedication made by Maximianus, a slave and steward, for the well-being of himself, his family, and the publicani (tax collectors) and their contractor, Titius Iulius Saturninus, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-12000864.translation: The Thirteenth Legion Gemina, Aurelius Tertius.
EDCS-12000864.summary: This inscription simply names the Thirteenth Legion Gemina and an individual named Aurelius Tertius, possibly indicating his service or ownership related to the legion, from an unknown date.

EDCS-12000866.translation: The Thirteenth Legion Gemina, Aurelius Menander.
EDCS-12000866.summary: This inscription lists the Thirteenth Legion Gemina and Aurelius Menander, suggesting his affiliation or contribution to the legion, from an unknown date.

EDCS-12000869.text_edition: \n Deo Invicto Mithrae sacrum Statorius anus decurio et flamen municipii Septimi Apulensis Invicti templum pro salutem sic sua suorumque pecunia mea feci\n
EDCS-12000869.translation: Sacred to the Unconquered God Mithras. Statorius, an old man, a decurion and flamen of the municipium of Septimius Apulensis Invictus, built this temple with my own money for the safety of myself and my family.
EDCS-12000869.summary: This is a dedication of a temple to Mithras by Statorius, a decurion and flamen of Septimius Apulensis Invictus, who funded the construction for his and his family's well-being, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-12000870.text_edition: \n Silvano Valerius Martialis pro filio votum libens solvit\n
EDCS-12000870.translation: Valerius Martialis gladly and deservedly paid his vow to Silvanus for his son.
EDCS-12000870.summary: This inscription records Valerius Martialis paying a vow to Silvanus for the sake of his son, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-12000871.text_edition: \n Mercurio Hilari Marcus Herennius Faustus legatus Augusti\n
EDCS-12000871.translation: For Mercury, Marcus Herennius Faustus, legate of the emperor.
EDCS-12000871.summary: This is a dedication to Mercury by Marcus Herennius Faustus, an imperial legate, likely from the early to mid-Imperial period.

EDCS-12000872.text_edition: \n us Archelaus |(centurio) legionis XIII geminae votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-12000872.translation: Archelaus, a centurion of the Thirteenth Legion Gemina, gladly and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-12000872.summary: This inscription records Archelaus, a centurion of the Thirteenth Legion Gemina, fulfilling a vow, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-12100795.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-12100795.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Traiani Parthici filius divi Nervae nepos Traianus Hadrianus Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate IIII consul III Palmyrenis sagittariis ex Syria qui sunt in Dacia superiore sub Iulio Severo civitatem dedit iis quorum nomina subscripta sunt ante diem III Kalendas Iulias Caio Publicio Marcello Lucio Rutilio Propinquo consulibus Barici Male filio Palmyra descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aenea quae fixa est Romae in muro post templum divi Augusti ad Minervam Luci Attei Atteiani Iuni Eutychi Publi Atini Flori Quinti Fabi Titi Luci Pulli Anthi Titi Claudi Erotis Quinti Atini Crescentis\n
EDCS-12100795.translation: Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrian Augustus, son of the deified Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 4th time, consul for the 3rd time, granted citizenship to the Palmyrene archers from Syria who are in Upper Dacia under Julius Severus, to those whose names are inscribed below, on the third day before the Kalends of July, when Gaius Publicius Marcellus and Lucius Rutilius Propinquus were consuls, as recorded and confirmed from the bronze tablet which is fixed at Rome on the wall behind the temple of deified Augustus near Minerva, the names being: Lucius Atteius Atteianus, Iunius Eutychus, Publius Atinius Florus, Quintus Fabius Titus, Lucius Pullius Anthus, Titus Claudius Eros, Quintus Atinius Crescens.
EDCS-12100795.summary: This inscription, dated to 119 AD, records the granting of Roman citizenship by Emperor Hadrian to Palmyrene archers serving in Dacia.

EDCS-12100799.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-12100799.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Traiani Parthici filius divi Nervae nepos Traianus Hadrianus Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VII consul III proconsul equitibus et peditibus qui militaverunt in alis duabus et cohorte una quae appellantur II nniorum et I Britannica civium Romanorum et I nnica Mmiliaria quae sunt in Dacia Porolissensi sub Livio Grapo item ala I Brittonum civium Romanorum quae est in Pannonia inferiore quinis et vicenis pluribusve stipendiis emeritis dimissis honesta missione per Marcium Turbonem quorum nomina subscripta sunt ipsis liberis posterisque eorum civitatem dedit ------ ala I B]rittonum civium Romanorum quae est in nnia inferiore quinis et vicenis pluribusve stipendiis emeritis dimissis honesta missione per Marcium Turbonem quorum nomina subscripta sunt ipsis liberis posterisque eorum civitatem dedit et conubiam cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut si qui caelibes essent cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singuli singulas ante diem IIII Idus Augustas Tito Salvio Rufino Minicio Opimiano Cnaeo Sentio Aburiano consulibus alae Brittonum civium Romanorum cui praefuit Marcus Minicius Marcellinus ex gregale Glavo Navati filio Sirmio et Iubenae Bellagenti filiae uxori civis Eraviscae descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aenea quae fixa est Romae in muro post templum divi Augusti ad Minervam\n
EDCS-12100799.translation: Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrian Augustus, son of the deified Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 7th time, consul for the 3rd time, granted citizenship and the right to marry to the cavalrymen and infantry who served in the two alae and one cohort called the II Noricorum and the I Britannica civium Romanorum and the I Norica Miliaria, which are in Dacia Porolissensis under Livius Grappus, and also to the ala I Brittonum civium Romanorum, which is in Lower Pannonia, for twenty-five or more years of service with an honorable discharge through Marcius Turbo, to those whose names are inscribed below, to themselves, their children, and their descendants, and to marry with the wives whom they then had when citizenship was granted to them, or if any were unmarried, with those whom they would later marry, but each only one, on the fourth day before the Ides of August, when Titus Flavius Salvius Rufus and Minicius Opimianus and Gnaeus Sentius Aburianus were consuls, and to Glavus, son of Navatus, a soldier from the ala I Brittonum civium Romanorum, which was commanded by Marcus Minicius Marcellinus, and to Iubena, daughter of Bellagentes, a wife of the Eraviscan citizen, as recorded and confirmed from the bronze tablet which is fixed at Rome on the wall behind the temple of deified Augustus near Minerva.
EDCS-12100799.summary: This inscription, dated to 124 AD, records Emperor Hadrian granting citizenship and the right of legal marriage to auxiliary soldiers and their families who had completed long service in Dacia and Pannonia.

EDCS-12100805.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-12100805.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Traiani Parthici filius divi Nervae nepos Traianus Hadrianus Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate X consul III Palmyrenis sagittariis qui sunt in Dacia superiore sub Iulio Severo civitatem dedit iis quorum nomina subscripta sunt pridie Februarias Marco Annio Vero III consulibus Caio Eggio Ambibulo Perheu Athenatan filio descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aenea quae fixa est Romae in muro post templum divi Augusti ad Minervam Imperator Caesar divi Traiani Parthici filius divi Nervae nepos Traianus Hadrianus Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate X consul III Palmyrenis sagittariis qui sunt in Dacia superiore sub Iulio Severo civitatem dedit iis quorum nomina subscripta sunt Marco nno Vero III Caio Eggio Ambibulo consulibus Perheu Athenatan filio Tiberi Iuli Vrbani Cai Caesi Romani Luci Vibi Vibiani Luci Pulli Daphni Luci Equiti Gemelli Quinti Lolli Festi Cai Vettieni Hermetis\n
EDCS-12100805.translation: Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrian Augustus, son of the deified Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 10th time, consul for the 3rd time, granted citizenship to the Palmyrene archers who are in Upper Dacia under Julius Severus, to those whose names are inscribed below, on the day before the Kalends of February, when Marcus Annius Verus (for the 3rd time) and Gaius Eggius Ambibulus were consuls, to Perheus Athenatan, son of Marcus Annius Verus (for the 3rd time) and Gaius Eggius Ambibulus, as recorded and confirmed from the bronze tablet which is fixed at Rome on the wall behind the temple of deified Augustus near Minerva; to Perheus Athenatan, son of Tiberius Julius Urbanus, Gaius Caelius Romanus, Lucius Vibius Vibianus, Lucius Pullius Daphnis, Lucius Equitius Gemellus, Quintus Lollius Festus, Gaius Vettienus Hermetis.
EDCS-12100805.summary: This inscription, dated to 126 AD, records Emperor Hadrian granting Roman citizenship to Palmyrene archers serving in Dacia.

EDCS-12100813.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-12100813.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Traiani Parthici filius divi Nervae nepos Traianus Hadrianus Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XVII consul III pater patriae equitibus et peditibus qui militaverunt in alis II et cohortibus VI quae appellantur Siliana civium Romanorum et I Tungrorum Frontoniana et I Ulpia Brittonum |(miliaria) et I Britannica |(miliaria) et I Hispanorum et I Batavorum |(miliaria) et I Aelia Gaesatorum |(miliaria) et II Brittonum |(miliaria) et sunt in Dacia Porolissensi sub Flavio Italico quinis et vicenis pluribusve stipendiis emeritis dimissis honesta missione quorum nomina subscripta sunt ipsis liberis posterisque eorum civitatem dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut si qui caelibes essent cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singuli singulas ante diem VI Nonas Iulias Quinto Flavio Tertullo Quinto Iunio Rustico consulibus cohortis I Britannicae |(miliariae) cui praeest Tiberius Claudius Fortis Capua ex pedite Sepenesto Rivi filio Cornon descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aenea quae fixa est Romae in muro post templum divi Augusti ad Minervam Imperator Caesar divi Traiani Parthici filius divi Nervae nepos Traianus Hadrianus Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XVII consul III pater patriae equitibus et peditibus qui militaverunt in alis II et cohortibus VI quae appellantur Siliana civium Romanorum et I Tungrorum Frontoniana et I Ulpia Brittonum |(miliaria) et I Britannica |(miliaria) et I Hispanorum et II Batavorum |(miliaria) et I Aelia Gaesatorum |(miliaria) et II Brittonum |(miliaria) et sunt in Dacia Porolissensi sub Flavio Italico quinis et vicenis pluribusve stipendiis emeritis dimissis honesta missione quorum nomina subscripta sunt ipsis liberis posterisque eorum civitatem dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut siqui caelibes essent cum iis quas postea [duxissent dumtaxat singuli ------\n
