EDCS-12100813.translation: Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrian Augustus, son of the deified Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 17th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the fatherland, granted citizenship and the right to legal marriage to the cavalrymen and infantry who served in the II alae and VI cohorts which are called Siliana civium Romanorum, I Tungrorum Frontoniana, I Ulpia Brittonum miliaria, I Britannica miliaria, I Hispanorum, II Batavorum miliaria, I Aelia Gaesatorum miliaria, and II Brittonum miliaria, and are in Dacia Porolissensis under Flavius Italicus, having completed twenty-five or more years of service with an honorable discharge, to those whose names are inscribed below, to themselves, their children, and their descendants, and to marry with the wives whom they then had when citizenship was granted to them, or if any were unmarried, with those whom they would later marry, but each only one, on the sixth day before the Nones of July, when Quintus Flavius Tertullus and Quintus Junius Rusticus were consuls, and to Sepenestus, son of Rivi, a foot soldier from cohort I Britannicae miliariae, which is commanded by Tiberius Claudius Fortis Capua, as recorded and confirmed from the bronze tablet which is fixed at Rome on the wall behind the temple of deified Augustus near Minerva; Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrian Augustus, son of the deified Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 17th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the fatherland, granted citizenship and the right to legal marriage to the cavalrymen and infantry who served in the II alae and VI cohorts which are called Siliana civium Romanorum, I Tungrorum Frontoniana, I Ulpia Brittonum miliaria, I Britannica miliaria, I Hispanorum, II Batavorum miliaria, I Aelia Gaesatorum miliaria, and II Brittonum miliaria, and are in Dacia Porolissensis under Flavius Italicus, having completed twenty-five or more years of service with an honorable discharge, to those whose names are inscribed below, to themselves, their children, and their descendants, and to marry with the wives whom they then had when citizenship was granted to them, or if any were unmarried, with those whom they would later marry, but each only one.
EDCS-12100813.summary: This inscription, dated to 133 AD, records Emperor Hadrian granting citizenship and the right of legal marriage to auxiliary soldiers who had completed long service in Dacia Porolissensis.

EDCS-12100897.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-12100897.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Antonini filius divi Veri Parthici maximi frater divi Hadriani nepos divi Traiani Parthici pronepos divi Nervae abnepos Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Germanicus Sarmaticus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XXXIII imperator IX consul III pater patriae et Imperator Caesar Lucius Aelius Aurelius Commodus Augustus Antonini Augusti filius divi Pii nepos divi Hadriani pronepos divi Traiani Parthici abnepos divi Nervae adnepos Germanicus Sarmaticus tribunicia potestate III imperator II consul II pater patriae equitibus et peditibus qui militaverunt in alis duabus quae appellantur I Batavorum et I Bosporanorum et cohortium decem et duobus sic I Alpinorum et I Augusta Ituraeorum et III Dalmatarum et V Gallorum et I Thracium sagittariorum et I Ubiorum et II Gallorum et nnum et I Vindelicorum et VIII Raetorum et III Campestrum et IIII Hispanorum et II Flavia Commagenorum et vexillatio sic peditum singularium nnum et sunt in Dacia superiore sub Helvio Pertinace legato quinis et vicenis pluribusve stipendis emeritis dimissis honesta missione quorum nomina subscripta sunt civitatem Romanam qui eorum non haberent dederunt et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singulis a(nte) d(iem) Kalendis Aprilibus Marco Acilio Faustino Lucio Iulio Proculiano consulibus cohortis III Campestrum cui praefuit Avonius Saturninus ex peditibus Ulpio Ulpi filio Herculano Stobis descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aerea quae fixa est Romae in muro post templum divi Augusti ad Minervam Imperator Caesar divi Antonini filius divi Veri Parthici maximi frater divi Hadriani nepos divi Traiani Parthici pronepos divi Nervae abnepos Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Germanicus Sarmaticus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XXXIII imperator X consul III pater patriae et Imperator Caesar Lucius Aelius Aurelius Commodus Augustus Antonini Augusti filius divi Pii nepos divi Hadriani pronepos divi Traiani Parthici abnepos divi Nervae adnepos Germanicus Sarmaticus tribunicia potestate III imperator II consul II pater patriae equitibus et peditibus qui militaverunt in alis duabus quae appellantur I Batavorum et I Bosporanorum et cohortium decem et duobus sic I Alpinorum et Augusta Ituraeorum et III Dalmatarum et V Gallorum et I Thracium sagittariorum et I Ubiorum et VIII Raetorum et III Campestrum et IIII Hispanorum et II Flavia Commagenorum et vexillatio sic peditum singularium Brittannicianorum et sunt in Dacia superiore sub Helvio Pertinace legato quinis et vicenis pluribusve stipendis emeritis dimissis honesta missione quorum nomina subscripta sunt civitatem Romanam qui eorum non haberent dederunt et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singulis Kalendis Aprilibus Manio Acilio Faustino Lucio Iulio Proculiano consulibus cohortis III Campestrum cui praefuit Avonius Saturninus ex peditibus Ulpio Ulpi filio Herculano Stobis descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aerea quae fixa est Romae in muro post templum divi Augusti ad Minervam Decimi Aemili Felicis Cnaei Pompei Niconis Publi Tulli Callicrates sic Decimi Aemili Quadrati Publi Orvi Dii Publi Aeli Trofimi Decimi Aemili Agathocletis\n
EDCS-12100897.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Germanicus Sarmaticus, son of the deified Antoninus, brother of the deified Verus Parthicus Maximus, grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-great-grandson of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 33rd time, imperator for the 9th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the fatherland, and Emperor Caesar Lucius Aurelius Commodus Augustus, son of Antoninus Augustus, grandson of the deified Pius, great-grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-great-great-grandson of the deified Nerva, Germanicus Sarmaticus, with tribunician power for the 3rd time, imperator for the 2nd time, consul for the 2nd time, father of the fatherland, granted Roman citizenship and the right of legal marriage to the cavalrymen and infantry who served in the two alae, I Batavorum and I Bosporanorum, and the twelve cohorts, namely I Alpinorum, I Augusta Ituraeorum, III Dalmatarum, V Gallorum, I Thracium sagittariorum, I Ubiorum, II Gallorum, [ ]num, I Vindelicorum, VIII Raetorum, III Campestrum, IIII Hispanorum, and II Flavia Commagenorum, and a detachment of infantrymen [ ]num, and were in Upper Dacia under the legate Helvius Pertinax, having completed twenty-five or more years of service with an honorable discharge, to those whose names are inscribed below, and granted them Roman citizenship, if they did not already have it, and the right to marry with the wives whom they then had when citizenship was granted to them, or with those whom they would later marry, but each only one, on the Kalends of April, when Marcus Acilius Faustinus and Lucius Julius Proculianus were consuls, and to Ulpius, son of Ulpius Herculanus, a foot soldier from cohort III Campestrum, which was commanded by Avonius Saturninus, as recorded and confirmed from the bronze tablet which is fixed at Rome on the wall behind the temple of deified Augustus near Minerva; Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Germanicus Sarmaticus, son of the deified Antoninus, brother of the deified Verus Parthicus Maximus, grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-great-grandson of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 33rd time, imperator for the 10th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the fatherland, and Emperor Caesar Lucius Aurelius Commodus Augustus, son of Antoninus Augustus, grandson of the deified Pius, great-grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-great-great-grandson of the deified Nerva, Germanicus Sarmaticus, with tribunician power for the 3rd time, imperator for the 2nd time, consul for the 2nd time, father of the fatherland, granted Roman citizenship and the right of legal marriage to the cavalrymen and infantry who served in the two alae, I Batavorum and I Bosporanorum, and the twelve cohorts, namely I Alpinorum, Augusta Ituraeorum, III Dalmatarum, V Gallorum, I Thracium sagittariorum, I Ubiorum, VIII Raetorum, III Campestrum, IIII Hispanorum, and II Flavia Commagenorum, and a detachment of infantrymen Brittannicianorum, and were in Upper Dacia under the legate Helvius Pertinax, having completed twenty-five or more years of service with an honorable discharge, to those whose names are inscribed below, and granted them Roman citizenship, if they did not already have it, and the right to marry with the wives whom they then had when citizenship was granted to them, or with those whom they would later marry, but each only one, on the Kalends of April, when Manius Acilius Faustinus and Lucius Julius Proculianus were consuls, and to Ulpius, son of Ulpius Herculanus, a foot soldier from cohort III Campestrum, which was commanded by Avonius Saturninus, as recorded and confirmed from the bronze tablet which is fixed at Rome on the wall behind the temple of deified Augustus near Minerva; Decimus Aemilius Felix, Gnaeus Pompeius Nicon, Publius Tullius Callicrates; likewise Decimus Aemilius Quadratus, Publius Orbius Dius, Publius Aelius Trofimus, Decimus Aemilius Agathocles.
EDCS-12100897.summary: This inscription, dated to 179 AD, records Emperors Marcus Aurelius and Commodus granting Roman citizenship and the right of legal marriage to auxiliary soldiers who had completed long service in Upper Dacia.

EDCS-12200001.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-12200001.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Armeniacus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XVIII imperator II consul III et Imperator Caesar Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus Armeniacus tribunicia potestate IIII imperator II proconsul consul II divi Antonini filii divi Hadriani nepotes divi Traiani Parthici pronepotes divi Nervae abnepotes equitibus et peditibus qui militaverunt in alis tribus quae appellantur II Gallorum et nnoniorum et Siliana civium Romanorum et I Tungrorum Frontoniana et cohortibus decem et duabus I Brittonum |(miliaria) et I nnorum equitata et I Hispanorum |(miliaria) et I Batavorum |(miliaria) et I Aelia Gaesatorum et II Nervia Brittonum |(miliaria) et II nnorum |(miliaria) et I Hispanorum |(miliaria) et I nnefatium et II Hispanorum et V Lingonum et VI Thracum et sunt in Dacia Porolissensi sub Sempronio Ingenuo procuratore quinis et vicenis pluribusve stipendis emeritis dimissis honesta missione quorum nomina subscripta sunt civitatem Romanam qui eorum non haberent dederunt et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas is data aut cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singulis ante diem XII Kalendas Augustas Tiberio Haterio Saturnino Quinto Caecilio Avito consulibus alae Silianae civium Romanorum cui praeest Aurelius Atilianus ex gregale Acilio Sabini filio Dubitato castris descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aerea quae fixa est Romae in muro post templum divi Augusti ad Minervam Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Armeniacus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XVIII imperator II consul III et Imperator Caesar Lucius Aurelius Antoninus sic Augustus Armeniacus tribunicia potestate IIII imperator II proconsul consul II divi Antonini filii divi Hadriani nepotes divi Traiani Parthici pronepotes divi Nervae abnepotes equitibus et peditibus qui militaverunt in alis III quae appellantur II Gallorum et nniorum et Siliana civium Romanorum et I Tungrorum Frontoniana et cohortibus XII Brittonum |(miliaria) et I nnum equitata et I Hisp<a=N>norum |(miliaria) et I Batavorum |(miliaria) et Aelia Gaetsatorum sic II Nervia Brittonum |(miliaria) et I nnum |(miliaria) et I Hispanorum |(miliaria) et I Cannanefatium et II Hispanorum et V Lingonum et III Thracum et sunt in Dacia Porolissensi sub Sempronio Ingenuo procuratore quinis et vicenis pluribusve stipendis emeritis dimissis honesta missione quorum nomina subscripta sunt civitatem Romanam qui eorum non haberent dederunt et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singulis ante diem XII Kalendas Augustas Tiberio Haterio Saturnino et Avito consulibus alae Silianae civium Romanorum cui praeest Aurelius Atilianus ex gregale Acilio Sabini filio Dubitato castris descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aerea quae fixa est Romae in muro post templum divi Augusti ad Minervam Marci Servili Getae Tiberi Iuli Felicis Cai Belli Urbani Luci Pulli Primi Luci Senti Chrysogoni Cai Pomponi Statiani Luci Pulli Zosimi Marci Servili Getae Tiberi Iuli Felicis Cai Belli Urbani Luci Pulli Primi Luci Senti Chrysogoni Cai Pomponi Statiani Luci Pulli Zosimi \n
EDCS-12200001.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Armeniacus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 18th time, imperator for the 2nd time, consul for the 3rd time, and Emperor Caesar Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus Armeniacus, with tribunician power for the 4th time, imperator for the 2nd time, proconsul, consul for the 2nd time, grandsons of the deified Antoninus, grandsons of the deified Hadrian, great-grandsons of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-great-grandsons of the deified Nerva, granted Roman citizenship and the right of legal marriage to the cavalrymen and infantry who served in the three alae, II Gallorum et Noricorum, Siliana civium Romanorum, and I Tungrorum Frontoniana, and the twelve cohorts, I Brittonum miliaria, I Noricorum equitata, I Hispanorum miliaria, I Batavorum miliaria, I Aelia Gaesatorum, II Nervia Brittonum miliaria, II Noricorum miliaria, I Hispanorum miliaria, I Cannanefatium, II Hispanorum, V Lingonum, and VI Thracum, and were in Dacia Porolissensis under the procurator Sempronius Ingenuus, having completed twenty-five or more years of service with an honorable discharge, to those whose names are inscribed below, and granted them Roman citizenship, if they did not already have it, and the right to marry with the wives whom they then had when citizenship was granted to them, or with those whom they would later marry, but each only one, on the twelfth day before the Kalends of August, when Tiberius Haterius Saturninus and Quintus Caecilius Avitus were consuls, and to Dubitatus, son of Acilius Sabinus, a cavalryman from the ala Silianae civium Romanorum, which is commanded by Aurelius Atilianus, from the camp, as recorded and confirmed from the bronze tablet which is fixed at Rome on the wall behind the temple of deified Augustus near Minerva; Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Armeniacus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 18th time, imperator for the 2nd time, consul for the 3rd time, and Emperor Caesar Lucius Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Armeniacus, with tribunician power for the 4th time, imperator for the 2nd time, proconsul, consul for the 2nd time, grandsons of the deified Antoninus, grandsons of the deified Hadrian, great-grandsons of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-great-grandsons of the deified Nerva, granted Roman citizenship and the right of legal marriage to the cavalrymen and infantry who served in the three alae, II Gallorum et Noricorum, Siliana civium Romanorum, and I Tungrorum Frontoniana, and the twelve cohorts, I Brittonum miliaria, I Noricorum equitata, I Hispanorum miliaria, I Batavorum miliaria, I Aelia Gaesatorum, II Nervia Brittonum miliaria, I Noricorum miliaria, I Hispanorum miliaria, I Cannanefatium, II Hispanorum, V Lingonum, and III Thracum, and were in Dacia Porolissensis under the procurator Sempronius Ingenuus, having completed twenty-five or more years of service with an honorable discharge, to those whose names are inscribed below, and granted them Roman citizenship, if they did not already have it, and the right to marry with the wives whom they then had when citizenship was granted to them, or with those whom they would later marry, but each only one, on the twelfth day before the Kalends of August, when Tiberius Haterius Saturninus and Avitus were consuls, and to Dubitatus, son of Acilius Sabinus, a cavalryman from the ala Silianae civium Romanorum, which is commanded by Aurelius Atilianus, from the camp, as recorded and confirmed from the bronze tablet which is fixed at Rome on the wall behind the temple of deified Augustus near Minerva; Marcus Servilius Geta, Tiberius Julius Felix, Gaius Bellius Urbanus, Lucius Pullius Primus, Lucius Sentius Chrysogonus, Gaius Pomponius Statianus, Lucius Pullius Zosimus; Marcus Servilius Geta, Tiberius Julius Felix, Gaius Bellius Urbanus, Lucius Pullius Primus, Lucius Sentius Chrysogonus, Gaius Pomponius Statianus, Lucius Pullius Zosimus.
EDCS-12200001.summary: This inscription, dated to 158 AD, records Emperors Marcus Aurelius and Lucius Verus granting Roman citizenship and the right of legal marriage to auxiliary soldiers who had completed long service in Dacia Porolissensis.

EDCS-12200247.text_edition: \n Invicto deo Serapidi Marcus Lucceius Felix procurator Augusti nostri\n
EDCS-12200247.translation: To the unconquered god Serapis, Marcus Lucceius Felix, procurator of our Emperor.
EDCS-12200247.summary: This short dedication records a dedication to the god Serapis by Marcus Lucceius Felix, a procurator, likely in the 2nd century AD.

EDCS-12200248.translation: Lucceius Felix, procurator of our Emperor.
EDCS-12200248.summary: This is a brief inscription identifying Lucceius Felix as an imperial procurator, likely dating to the 2nd century AD.

EDCS-12200251.text_edition: \n Invicto Mithrae Marti Camulo Mercurio Rosmertae Quintus Axius Aelianus vir egregius procurator Auggustorum Ioni\n
EDCS-12200251.translation: To the unconquered Mithras, Mars Camulus, Mercury Rosmerta, Quintus Axius Aelianus, an excellent man and procurator of the Augusti, from Ionia.
EDCS-12200251.summary: This dedication records Quintus Axius Aelianus, an imperial procurator from Ionia, dedicating an offering to several deities, including Mithras, likely in the 2nd century AD.

EDCS-12200252.text_edition: \n Aesculapio Saluti Epionae Veneri ubique Neptuno Salaciae cupidinibus fontibus aquis Quintus Axius Aelianus vir egregius procurator Auggustorum Ioni\n
EDCS-12200252.translation: To Aesculapius, Salus, Epione, Venus, everywhere, Neptune, Salacia, the Cupids, the springs, the waters, Quintus Axius Aelianus, an excellent man and procurator of the Augusti, from Ionia.
EDCS-12200252.summary: This dedication records Quintus Axius Aelianus, an imperial procurator from Ionia, dedicating an offering to various deities associated with health and nature, likely in the 2nd century AD.

EDCS-12200257.summary: A fragmentary inscription that appears to record the name of a Roman official, likely dating to the imperial period.

EDCS-12200258.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publio Aelio Martiali vixit nnis XLV collegium fabrum\n
EDCS-12200258.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Publius Aelius Martialis lived 45 years. (Dedicated by the) college of artisans.
EDCS-12200258.summary: A funerary inscription for Publius Aelius Martialis, dedicated by his trade association, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-12200260.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Flavio Marci filio Papiria Epstelae veterano ex tribuni beneficiario vixit \n
EDCS-12200260.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Marcus Flavius, son of Marcus, of the Papiria tribe, a veteran, formerly a beneficiarius of a tribune, lived (for ...).
EDCS-12200260.summary: A funerary inscription for Marcus Flavius, a veteran who served as a beneficiarius, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-12300237.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-12300237.translation: Emperor Caesar Domitianus Augustus Germanicus, son of the deified Vespasian, pontifex maximus, with the tribunician power for the 5th time, imperator for the 12th time, perpetual censor, consul for the 12th time, father of the country, to the cavalrymen and infantrymen serving in the two alae named Veterana Gaetulorum and I Thracum Mauretana, and in the four cohorts I Augusta Lusitanorum, I and II Thracum, and II Cantabrorum, stationed in Judaea under Gnaeus Pompeius Longinus, who had completed twenty-five years of service, granted citizenship to them, their children, and their descendants, and the right of legal marriage with the wives they then had when citizenship was granted to them, or if they were unmarried, with those they would afterwards marry, only one wife each. On the day before the 11th of May, in the consulship of Sextus Octavius Fronto and Tiberius Julius Candidus Marius Celsus. (This record pertains to) a cavalryman of the II Thracum cohort, commanded by Claudius Montanus, Seuthes, son of Traibithus, of the Colotetic(i?). (This is) transcribed and verified from a bronze tablet fixed in Rome on the Capitol. Emperor Caesar Domitianus Augustus Germanicus, son of the deified Vespasian, pontifex maximus, with the tribunician power for the 5th time, imperator for the 12th time, perpetual censor, consul for the 12th time, father of the country, to the cavalrymen and infantrymen serving in the two alae named Veterana Gaetulorum and I Thracum Mauretana, and in the four cohorts I Augusta Lusitanorum, I and II Thracum, and II Cantabrorum, stationed in Judaea under Gnaeus Pompeius Longinus, who had completed twenty-five years of service, granted citizenship to them, their children, and their descendants, and the right of legal marriage with the wives they then had when citizenship was granted to them, or if they were unmarried, with those they would afterwards marry, only one wife each. On the day before the 11th of May, in the consulship of Sextus Octavius Fronto and Tiberius Julius Candidus Marius Celsus. (This record pertains to) a cavalryman of the II Thracum cohort, commanded by Claudius Montanus, Seuthes, son of Traibithus, of the Colotetic(i?). (This is) transcribed and verified from a bronze tablet fixed in Rome on the Capitol, behind the trophies of Germanicus, in the tribunal which is near the temple of Faith of the Roman People. (Names of witnesses/officials follow).
EDCS-12300237.summary: This extensive inscription records a grant of Roman citizenship and marriage rights by Emperor Domitian to auxiliary soldiers serving in Judaea who had completed 25 years of service, with a specific mention of a Thracian cavalryman, dating to 86 AD.

EDCS-12300312.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-12300312.translation: Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, son of the deified Hadrian, grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-grandson of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, with the tribunician power for the 21st time, imperator for the 2nd time, consul for the 4th time, father of the country, to the cavalrymen and infantrymen who served in the III alae and II cohorts and vexillations with the native Moors in Upper Dacia and are under the legate Statins Priscus, having completed 25 years of service and been honorably discharged, granted Roman citizenship to those among them who did not possess it, and the right of legal marriage with the wives they then had when citizenship was granted to them, or with those they would afterwards marry, only one wife each. On the day before the 8th of the Ides of July, in the consulship of Fabius Julianus and Bassus. (This record pertains to) the I ala of the Gauls and Bosporani, commanded by Licinius Nigrinus, from a trooper, Heptapor, son of Isus, a Besso. (This is) transcribed and verified. Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, son of the deified Hadrian, grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-grandson of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, with the tribunician power for the 21st time, imperator for the 2nd time, consul for the 4th time, father of the country, to the cavalrymen and infantrymen who served in the III alae (which are called I Batavorum milliaria, I Hispanorum Campagonum, and I Gallorum et Bosporanorum) and in the cohorts (I Thracum sagittaria, IV Hispanorum, I Augusta Ituraeorum) and in vexillations of Africa and Mauretania and Caesariensis, who are with the native Moors in Upper Dacia and are under the legate Statins Priscus, having completed twenty-five years of service and been honorably discharged, granted Roman citizenship to those among them who did not possess it, and the right of legal marriage with the wives they then had when citizenship was granted to them, or with those they would afterwards marry, only one wife each. On the day before the 8th of the Ides of July, in the consulship of Servilius Fabiano and Quintus Iallius Bassus. (This record pertains to) the I ala of the Gauls and Bosporani, commanded by Licinius Nigrinus, from a trooper, Heptapor, son of Isus, a Besso. (This is) transcribed and verified from a bronze tablet fixed in Rome on the wall behind the temple of the deified Augustus near the Temple of Minerva. (Names of witnesses/officials follow).
EDCS-12300312.summary: This inscription, dated to 147 AD, records Emperor Antoninus Pius granting Roman citizenship and marriage rights to auxiliary soldiers serving in Upper Dacia, specifically mentioning troopers from the I ala of the Gauls and Bosporani.

EDCS-12300364.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-12300364.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Nervae filius Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XIIII imperator VI consul V pater patriae peditibus et equitibus qui militant in cohorte I Brittonum milliaria Ulpia torquata pia fideli civium Romanorum quae est in Dacia sub Decimo Terentio Scauriano quorum nomina subscripta sunt pie et fideliter expeditione Dacica functis ante emerita stipendia civitatem Romanam dedit ante diem III Idus Augustas Darnithithi Lucio Minicio Natale Quinto Silvano Graniano consulibus pediti Marco Ulpio Adcobrovati filio Novanticoni Ratis descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aenea quae fixa est Romae in muro post templum divi Augusti ad Minervam Imperator Caesar divi Nervae filius Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus pontifex maximus tribuniciae potestatis XIIII imperator VI consul V pater patriae peditibus et equitibus qui militant in cohorte I Brittonum milliaria Ulpia torquata pia felici civium Romanorum quae est in Dacia sub Decimo Terentio Scauriano quorum nomina subscripta sunt pie et fideliter expeditione Dacica functis ante emerita stipendia civitatem Romanam dedit ante diem III Idus Augustas Darnithithi Lucio Minicio Natale Quinto Silvano Graniano consulibus pediti Marco Ulpio Adco<b=P>rovati filio Novantico Ratis descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aenea quae fixa est Romae Publi Corneli Alexandri Luci Pulli Verecundi Publi Atini Amerimni Cai Tuticani Saturnini Luci Pulli Trophimi Cai Iuli Parati Marci Iuni Eutychi \n
EDCS-12300364.translation: Emperor Caesar Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, son of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, with the tribunician power for the 14th time, imperator for the 6th time, consul for the 5th time, father of the country, to the infantrymen and cavalrymen serving in the I Cohort of Britons, milliaria, Ulpia torquata, pia fideli, of Roman citizens, which is in Dacia under Decimus Terentius Scaurianus, granted Roman citizenship to those among them who had faithfully and dutifully completed the Dacian expedition, before completing their full years of service. On the day before the 3rd of the Ides of August, in the consulship of Darnithithus, Lucius Minicius Natalis, and Quintus Silvanus Gratianus. To the infantryman Marcus Ulpius Adcobrovatus, son of Novanticon, of Ratis. (This is) transcribed and verified from a bronze tablet fixed in Rome on the wall behind the temple of the deified Augustus near the Temple of Minerva. Emperor Caesar Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, son of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, with the tribunician power for the 14th time, imperator for the 6th time, consul for the 5th time, father of the country, to the infantrymen and cavalrymen serving in the I Cohort of Britons, milliaria, Ulpia torquata, pia felici, of Roman citizens, which is in Dacia under Decimus Terentius Scaurianus, granted Roman citizenship to those among them who had faithfully and dutifully completed the Dacian expedition, before completing their full years of service. On the day before the 3rd of the Ides of August, in the consulship of Darnithithus, Lucius Minicius Natalis, and Quintus Silvanus Gratianus. To the infantryman Marcus Ulpius Adcobrovatus, son of Novantico, of Ratis. (This is) transcribed and verified from a bronze tablet fixed in Rome. (Names of witnesses/officials follow).
EDCS-12300364.summary: This inscription, dated to 108 AD, records Emperor Trajan granting Roman citizenship to an infantryman of the I Cohort of Britons, who had served in the Dacian Wars, as recorded on a bronze tablet.

EDCS-12300367.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-12300367.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Nervae filius Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XIIII imperator VI consul V pater patriae iis qui militaverunt equites et pedites in alis quattuor et cohortibus decem et octo quae appellantur I Augusta Ituraeorum et I Claudia et I Britannica civium Romanorum et II nnum veterana et I Brittonum |(miliaria) Ulpia torquata civium Romanorum et I Vindelicorum |(miliaria) civium Romanorum pia fidelis et I Britannica |(miliaria) civium Romanorum et I Flavia Hispanorum |(miliaria) et I Montanorum et I Thracum civium Romanorum et I Cretum sagittariorum et I Hispanorum pia fidelis et I Pannoniorum veterana pia fidelis et II Hispanorum et II Britannorum |(miliaria) civium Romanorum pia fidelis et II Gallorum Pannonica et II Gallorum Macedonica et II Flavia Commagenorum sagittariorum et V Lingonum et V Gallorum et VI Thracum et VIII Raetorum civium Romanorum et pedites Britannici et sunt in Dacia sub Decimo Terentio Scauriano quinis et vicenis pluribusve stipendiis emeritis dimissis honesta missione quorum nomina subscripta sunt ipsis liberis posterisque eorum civitatem dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut si qui caelibes essent cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singuli singulas ante diem VI Nonas Iulias Caio Eruciano Silone Lucio Catilio Severo consulibus cohortis I Brittonum |(miliariae) Ulpiae torquatae civium Romanorum cui praeest Marcus Aemilius Bassus ex pedite Marco Ulpio Sacci filio Longino Belgo et Vitali filio eius descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aenea quae fixa est Romae in muro post templum divi Augusti ad Minervam Imperator Caesar divi Nervae filius Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XIIII imperator VI consul V pater patriae is qui militaverunt equites et pedites in alis quattuor et cohortibus decem et octo quae appellantur I Augusta Ituraeorum et I Claudia et I Britannica civium Romanorum et II nnum veterana et I Brittonum |(miliaria) Ulpia torquata civium Romanorum et I Vindelicorum |(miliaria) civium Romanorum pia fidelis et I Britannica |(miliaria) civium Romanorum et I Flavia <H=II>ispanorum |(miliaria) et I Montanorum et I Thracum civium Romanorum et I Cretum sagittariorum et I Hispanorum pia fidelis et I Pannonio<r=Q>(um) veterana pia fidelis et II Hispanorum et II nnum |(miliaria) civium Romanorum pia fidelis et II Gallorum Pannonica et II Gallorum Macedonica et II Flavia Commagenorum sagittariorum et V Lingonum et V Gallorum et VI Thracum et VIII Raetorum civium Romanorum et pedites Britannici et sunt in Dacia sub Decimo Terentio Scauriano quinis et vicenis pluribusve stipendiis emeritis dimissis honesta missione quorum nomina subscripta sunt ipsis liberis posterisque eorum civitatem dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut si qui caelibes essent cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singuli singu singulas ante diem VI Nonas Iulias Caio Eruciano Silone Lucio Catilio Severo consulibus cohortis I Brittonum Ulpiae |(miliariae) torquatae civium Romanorum cui praeest Marcus Aemilius Bassus ex pedite Marco Ulpio Sacci filio Longino Belgo et Vitali filio eius descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aenea quae fixa est Romae Publi Corneli Alexandri Luci Pulli Verecundi Publi Atini Amerimni Luci Pulli Trophimi Cai Tuticani Saturnini Cai Iuli Parati Marci Iuni Eutychi \n
EDCS-12300367.translation: Emperor Caesar Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, son of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, with the tribunician power for the 14th time, imperator for the 6th time, consul for the 5th time, father of the country, granted citizenship to those infantrymen and cavalrymen who served in the four alae and eighteen cohorts (named: I Augusta Ituraeorum, I Claudia, I Britannica civium Romanorum, II veterana, I Brittonum milliaria Ulpia torquata civium Romanorum, I Vindelicorum milliaria civium Romanorum pia fidelis, I Britannica milliaria civium Romanorum, I Flavia Hispanorum milliaria, I Montanorum, I Thracum civium Romanorum, I Cretum sagittariorum, I Hispanorum pia fidelis, I Pannoniorum veterana pia fidelis, II Hispanorum, II Britannorum milliaria civium Romanorum pia fidelis, II Gallorum Pannonica, II Gallorum Macedonica, II Flavia Commagenorum sagittariorum, V Lingonum, V Gallorum, VI Thracum, VIII Raetorum civium Romanorum, and Britannic infantrymen) stationed in Dacia under Decimus Terentius Scaurianus, who had completed twenty-five or more years of service and been honorably discharged. To them, their children, and their descendants, he granted citizenship and the right of legal marriage with the wives they then had when citizenship was granted to them, or if they were unmarried, with those they would afterwards marry, only one wife each. On the day before the 6th of the Nones of July, in the consulship of Gaius Trebius Silo and Lucius Catilius Severus. (This record pertains to) an infantryman of the I Cohort of Britons, milliaria, Ulpia torquata, civium Romanorum, commanded by Marcus Aemilius Bassus, Marcus Ulpius Longinus, son of Saccius, a Belga, and his son Vitalis. (This is) transcribed and verified from a bronze tablet fixed in Rome on the wall behind the temple of the deified Augustus near the Temple of Minerva. Emperor Caesar Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, son of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, with the tribunician power for the 14th time, imperator for the 6th time, consul for the 5th time, father of the country, to those cavalrymen and infantrymen who served in the four alae and eighteen cohorts (named: I Augusta Ituraeorum, I Claudia, I Britannica civium Romanorum, II veterana, I Brittonum milliaria Ulpia torquata civium Romanorum, I Vindelicorum milliaria civium Romanorum pia fidelis, I Britannica milliaria civium Romanorum, I Flavia Hispanorum milliaria, I Montanorum, I Thracum civium Romanorum, I Cretum sagittariorum, I Hispanorum pia fidelis, I Pannoniorum veterana pia fidelis, II Hispanorum, II veterana milliaria civium Romanorum pia fidelis, II Gallorum Pannonica, II Gallorum Macedonica, II Flavia Commagenorum sagittariorum, V Lingonum, V Gallorum, VI Thracum, VIII Raetorum civium Romanorum, and Britannic infantrymen) stationed in Dacia under Decimus Terentius Scaurianus, who had completed twenty-five or more years of service and been honorably discharged. To them, their children, and their descendants, he granted citizenship and the right of legal marriage with the wives they then had when citizenship was granted to them, or if they were unmarried, with those they would afterwards marry, only one wife each. On the day before the 6th of the Nones of July, in the consulship of Gaius Trebius Silo and Lucius Catilius Severus. (This record pertains to) an infantryman of the I Cohort of Britons, Ulpia milliaria, torquata, civium Romanorum, commanded by Marcus Aemilius Bassus, Marcus Ulpius Longinus, son of Saccius, a Belga, and his son Vitalis. (This is) transcribed and verified from a bronze tablet fixed in Rome. (Names of witnesses/officials follow).
EDCS-12300367.summary: This extensive inscription, dated to 108 AD, documents Emperor Trajan granting Roman citizenship and marriage rights to a large number of auxiliary soldiers (over 30 units) serving in Dacia, with a specific mention of soldiers from the I Cohort of Britons.

EDCS-12700100.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Conservatori Titus Scruius Vitalis tribunus cohortis III Campestris votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-12700100.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, the Protector. Titus Scruius Vitalis, tribune of the III Cohort of Field Troops, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-12700100.summary: A votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Conservator by Titus Scruius Vitalis, a tribune of the III Campestris cohort, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-12700121.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulius Crescens veteranus ex vixit annos LV \n
EDCS-12700121.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Julius Crescens, veteran, lived 55 years.
EDCS-12700121.summary: A funerary inscription for Julius Crescens, a veteran, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-12700177.summary: This very brief inscription appears to be a territorial or place name designation, possibly related to the Roman province of Dacia Ripensis and a location named Dierna, likely dating to the late Roman period.

EDCS-12700185.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo veterani et cives Romani Micienses per Titum Aurelium Verum et Cornelium Fortunatum magistros votum solverunt libentes merito\n
EDCS-12700185.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, the veterans and Roman citizens of Micienses, through Titus Aurelius Verus and Cornelius Fortunatus, their magistrates, gladly and deservedly fulfilled their vow.
EDCS-12700185.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by veterans and citizens of Micienses to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-12700188.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Zenon Tarasi miles numeri Germanicianorum vixit annos XXXIII Iulia marito pientissimo\n
EDCS-12700188.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Zeno, soldier of the Germaniciani unit, lived 33 years. Julia for her most devoted husband.
EDCS-12700188.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Zeno, a soldier of the Germaniciani, set up by his wife Julia, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-12800327.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Cimisteno Primus et Primianus ex voto posuerunt\n
EDCS-12800327.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest Cimistenus, Primus and Primianus set this up in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-12800327.summary: This inscription records Primus and Primianus dedicating something to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Cimistenus in fulfillment of a vow, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-12800328.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Cimisteno pro salute imperi posuit Aurelius Iulianus\n
EDCS-12800328.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest Cimistenus, Aurelius Iulianus set this up for the safety of the empire.
EDCS-12800328.summary: This inscription records Aurelius Iulianus dedicating something to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Cimistenus for the safety of the empire, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-12800332.text_edition: \n Minervae Augustae exceptores consularis tinus ssianus ssianus Florus Severus Valens \n
EDCS-12800332.translation: To Minerva Augusta, the bodyguards of the consular governor, the freedmen of Titus Cassianus and of Severus Valens, [fulfilled their vow].
EDCS-12800332.summary: This inscription records freedmen of officials dedicating something to Minerva Augusta, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-13302477.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico Mucianus optio Palmyrenorum votum posuit libens merito\n
EDCS-13302477.translation: To Silvanus Domesticius, Mucianus, optio of the Palmyrenes, set up his vow gladly and deservedly.
EDCS-13302477.summary: This inscription records Mucianus, an optio of the Palmyrenes, dedicating something to Silvanus Domesticius, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-13302479.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo ala II Pannoniorum cui praeest Caius Caelius Salvianus praefectus\n
EDCS-13302479.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, the Second Wing of Pannonians, commanded by Caius Coelius Salvianus, prefect.
EDCS-13302479.summary: This inscription records the Second Wing of Pannonians, under the command of Prefect Caius Coelius Salvianus, setting up something for Jupiter Optimus Maximus, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-13302480.text_edition: \n Silvano Dom(estico) Marcus Ulpius Provincialis vota libens merito solvit\n
EDCS-13302480.translation: To Silvanus Domesticius, Marcus Ulpius Provincialis gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vows.
EDCS-13302480.summary: This inscription records Marcus Ulpius Provincialis dedicating something to Silvanus Domesticius, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-13302484.text_edition: \n Invicto V Va botum sic posuit\n
EDCS-13302484.translation: To Invictus V... [he] set up [his] vow thus.
EDCS-13302484.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription recording someone setting up a vow to the god Invictus, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-13302485.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Aelius Heupropes sic votum posuit libenter\n
EDCS-13302485.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Aelius Heupropes set up his vow thus gladly.
EDCS-13302485.summary: This inscription records Aelius Heupropes dedicating something to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-13302486.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Aurelius Aelianus miles legionis V Macedonicae votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-13302486.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Aurelius Aelianus, a soldier of the Fifth Legion Macedonica, willingly paid his vow as was deserved.
EDCS-13302486.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by a soldier of the Legio V Macedonica, likely a votive offering.

EDCS-13302487.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Ulpius Lucanus votum reddidit libens merito\n
EDCS-13302487.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Ulpius Lucanus willingly paid his vow as was deserved.
EDCS-13302487.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Ulpius Lucanus, likely a votive offering.

EDCS-13302488.text_edition: \n miles legionis V Macedonicae piae constantis voto Iovi Optimo Maximo voto rettulit\n
EDCS-13302488.translation: A soldier of the Fifth Legion Macedonica, pious and steadfast, fulfilled his vow to Jupiter Best and Greatest.
EDCS-13302488.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by a soldier of the Legio V Macedonica, noting their piety and steadfastness.

EDCS-13302490.text_edition: \n Iovi optimo maximo Caius Cassius Cai filius Collina Proculeianus Epiphania |(centurio) legionis XIII geminae votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-13302490.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Gaius Cassius Proculeianus, son of Caius, of the Collina tribe, Epiphania, a centurion of the Thirteenth Legion Gemina, willingly paid his vow as was deserved.
EDCS-13302490.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by a centurion of the Legio XIII Gemina, Gaius Cassius Proculeianus Epiphania.

EDCS-13302491.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Claudius Nic beneficiarius votum solvit\n
EDCS-13302491.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Claudius Nic, a beneficiary, paid his vow.
EDCS-13302491.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Claudius Nic, identified as a 'beneficiarius' (a specific military role).

EDCS-13302492.translation: To Jupiter the Repeller, Titus Valerius Plares.
EDCS-13302492.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Depulsor (the Repeller) by Titus Valerius Plares, likely a votive offering.

EDCS-13302493.text_edition: \n ] Saturninus Silvano votum libens animo solvit\n
EDCS-13302493.translation: ... Saturninus willingly paid his vow to Silvanus with a willing spirit.
EDCS-13302493.summary: This is a dedication to Silvanus by Saturninus, a votive offering.

EDCS-13302494.text_edition: \n Soli Mithrae P(ublius) Aelius Gordianus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-13302494.translation: To Sol Mithras, Publius Aelius Gordianus willingly paid his vow as was deserved.
EDCS-13302494.summary: This is a dedication to Sol Mithras by Publius Aelius Gordianus, likely a votive offering.

EDCS-13302495.text_edition: \n Ius Ianus domo Faventia veteranus legionis XIII geminae vixit annos LX Ulpia Priscilla coniugi et Veratia Statuta filio bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-13302495.translation: To Janus, from Faventia, a veteran of the Thirteenth Legion Gemina, who lived 60 years, Ulpia Priscilla for her husband and Veratia Statuta for her son, who deserved well, erected this.
EDCS-13302495.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Ulpia Priscilla and Veratia Statuta for their deceased husband and son, a veteran of the Legio XIII Gemina.

EDCS-13302527.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus Parthicus maximus Britannicus maximus pontifex maximus cohors II Nervia Brittonum |(miliaria) Antoniniana pia fidelis statuam posuit\n
EDCS-13302527.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, greatest conqueror of Parthia, greatest conqueror of Britain, chief priest, the Second Cohort of Nervian Britons, an Antonine unit, pious and loyal, erected this statue.
EDCS-13302527.summary: This is a dedication of a statue by the Cohors II Nervia Brittonum Antoniniana, a Roman auxiliary unit, to Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus.

EDCS-13302532.text_edition: \n Invicto Mythrae sic Potinus ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-13302532.translation: To the unconquered Mithras, Potinus erected this, in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-13302532.summary: This is a dedication to the god Mithras, set up by Potinus.

EDCS-13302533.translation: Marcus Cocceius Caupianus, prefect of the civitas Boiorum, to Cocceia Dagouassa.
EDCS-13302533.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Marcus Cocceius Caupianus, prefect of the civitas Boiorum, to Cocceia Dagouassa.

EDCS-13302758.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Conservatori pro salute Publi Aeli Antipatri sacerdotis arae Augusti sua suorumque omnium Onesimus actor votum solvit\n
EDCS-13302758.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Conservator, for the health of Publius Aelius Antipater, priest of the altar of Augustus, Onesimus, his steward, fulfilled his vow for himself and all his family.
EDCS-13302758.summary: This is a votive inscription for the health of Publius Aelius Antipater, priest of the altar of Augustus, set up by his steward Onesimus.

EDCS-13400172.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Sabasio pro salute Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Aureli Antonini Pii Felicis Augusti et Iuliae Augusti matris Augustae a deo iussus fecit Lucius Aurelius Marcianus aedilicius\n
EDCS-13400172.translation: Lucius Aurelius Marcianus, holding an aedilician office, made this to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Sabasius for the health of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus and Julia Augusta, mother of the Augusti, by order of a god.
EDCS-13400172.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Lucius Aurelius Marcianus to Jupiter Sabasius for the health of Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus and Julia Augusta.

EDCS-13400220.summary: This is a fragmentary dedication to the god Jupiter Sabazius by Aurelius.

EDCS-13500276.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Augustus Felix Parthicus maximus pontifex maximus Britannicus maximus tribunicia potestate XVI imperator II consul IIII pater patriae proconsul fecit\n
EDCS-13500276.translation: The Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix, conqueror of the Parthians, greatest priest, conqueror of the Britons, with tribunician power for the 16th time, Imperator for the 2nd time, Consul for the 4th time, father of the country, proconsul, made this.
EDCS-13500276.summary: This is an official dedication by Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, detailing his titles and honors, likely for a public work.

EDCS-13600270.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Terrae Matri pro salute Titi Aeli Mari flaminis coloniae conductoris pascui et salinarum Atticus actor eius votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-13600270.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus and the Earth Mother, for the health of Titus Aelius Marius, priest of the colony and contractor of pasture and salt works, his steward Atticus fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-13600270.summary: This is a votive inscription for the health of Titus Aelius Marius, a priest and contractor, set up by his steward Atticus.

EDCS-13600285.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Publius Aelius Marcellinus beneficiarius consularis legionis V Macedonicae Gordianae agens Samo cum regione Ansamensium sub segno sic pro salute sua et suorum votum libens posuit Arriano et Papo consulibus\n
EDCS-13600285.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Publius Aelius Marcellinus, a beneficiarius consularis of the Legio V Macedonica Gordiana, acting in the region of Samo with the Ansamensian people, under the protection of the divine sign, willingly set up this vow for himself and his family, in the consulship of Arrianus and Papus.
EDCS-13600285.summary: This is a votive inscription by Publius Aelius Marcellinus, a military official, to Jupiter Optimus Maximus for his health and that of his family, dated by consuls.

EDCS-13600286.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno Scantius Lucius beneficiarius consularis agens curam stationis ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-13600286.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus, Scantius Lucius, a beneficiarius consularis in charge of the station, erected this in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-13600286.summary: This is a votive dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus by Scantius Lucius, a military official in charge of a station.

EDCS-13600287.text_edition: \n Deae Nemesi Scantius Lucius beneficiarius consularis agens curam stationis multis insidiis numinibus liberatus votum posuit Claudio Iuliano II et Bruttio Crispino consulibus\n
EDCS-13600287.translation: To the Goddess Nemesis, Scantius Lucius, a beneficiarius consularis in charge of the station, freed from many ambushes by the divine powers, set up this vow in the consulship of Claudius Julianus II and Bruttius Crispinus.
EDCS-13600287.summary: This is a votive inscription to the Goddess Nemesis by Scantius Lucius, a military official, who attributed his survival from dangers to her divine intervention, dated by consuls.

EDCS-13600288.text_edition: \n Deae Nemesi Reginae Oclatius Florentinus beneficiarius consularis legionis V Macedonicae Severianae iterato agens statione Agricola et Clementino consulibus\n
EDCS-13600288.translation: To the Divine Queen Nemesis, Oclatius Florentinus, a beneficiary of consular rank of the Fifth Macedonian Severian Legion, again serving in duty during the consulship of Agricola and Clementinus.
EDCS-13600288.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the goddess Nemesis by a military official in AD 200.

EDCS-13600289.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Titus vixit annos LX Aurelia Gemellina vixit annos XX Aurelius Cotes eques alae Flaviae faciendum curavit maritae pientissimae et socro\n
EDCS-13600289.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aurelius Titus lived 60 years. Aurelia Gemellina lived 20 years. Aurelius Cotes, a cavalryman of the Flavian Ala, arranged for this to be made for his most dutiful wife and mother-in-law.
EDCS-13600289.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Aurelius Titus, Aurelia Gemellina, and Aurelius Cotes, set up by Cotes for his wife and mother-in-law.

EDCS-13600442.text_edition: \n Dis Penatibus Laribus Militaribus Lari Viali Neptuno Saluti Fortunae Reduci Aesculapio Dianae Apollini Herculi Spei Favori Publius Catius Sabinus tribunus militum legionis XIII geminae votum libens solvit\n
EDCS-13600442.translation: To the Household Gods, Military Lares, Lares Viales, Neptune, Salus, Fortuna Redux, Aesculapius, Diana, Apollo, Hercules, and Spes, Publius Catius Sabinus, military tribune of the Thirteenth Gemina Legion, willingly paid his vow.
EDCS-13600442.summary: This inscription records a military tribune, Publius Catius Sabinus, fulfilling vows to numerous deities.

EDCS-13600443.text_edition: \n Marco Ulpio Bono decurioni aedili IIIIviro municipii Apulensis STR Ulpius Lucius libertus\n
EDCS-13600443.translation: To Marcus Ulpius Bonus, decurion, aedile, and magistrate of the municipality of Apulis. Ulpius Lucius, his freedman.
EDCS-13600443.summary: This inscription records a freedman, Ulpius Lucius, honoring his patron Marcus Ulpius Bonus, who held multiple municipal offices.

EDCS-13600459.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Lucius Carvilius Statius votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-13600459.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Lucius Carvilius Statius willingly and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-13600459.summary: This inscription records Lucius Carvilius Statius fulfilling a vow to Jupiter.

EDCS-13600460.translation: To Silvanus Domestiscus, Iulianus paid his vow.
EDCS-13600460.summary: This inscription records Iulianus fulfilling a vow to the domestic deity Silvanus.

EDCS-14800191.text_edition: \n Claudius Maximus et Ingenuius Superstes stateram publicam cum servatore liberto publico posuerunt locus datus decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-14800191.translation: Claudius Maximus and Ingenuius Superstes, with their freedman public slave Servator, set up this public steelyard; the site was granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-14800191.summary: This inscription records the donation of a public steelyard by Claudius Maximus and Ingenuius Superstes, with the approval of the town council.

EDCS-14800196.text_edition: \n Eponae sanctae pro salute Cai Iulii Septimii Castini legati Augusti pro praetore III Daciarum Libella superiumentarius eius votum solvit\n
EDCS-14800196.translation: To the holy Epona, for the safety of Caius Julius Septimius Castinus, legate of the emperor with the power of a praetor in the province of Dacia, his superior official Libella paid his vow.
EDCS-14800196.summary: This inscription records a vow paid by Libella to the goddess Epona for the safety of his superior, the imperial legate Caius Julius Septimius Castinus.

EDCS-14800197.text_edition: \n Apollini Salutari pro salute filiorum sua coiiugis suorumque Caius Iulius Varianus\n
EDCS-14800197.translation: To Apollo Salutaris, for the safety of his sons, his wife, and his own family, Caius Julius Varianus [dedicated this].
EDCS-14800197.summary: This inscription records Caius Julius Varianus dedicating an offering to Apollo Salutaris for the well-being of his family.

EDCS-15000026.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Caius Iulius Priscus IIviralis muncipii Septimi Potaissensium ex visu libens posuit\n
EDCS-15000026.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Caius Julius Priscus, a magistrate of the municipality of Septem Potaissae, set this up willingly according to a vision.
EDCS-15000026.summary: This inscription records Caius Julius Priscus, a municipal official, setting up an offering to Jupiter Optimus Maximus after a divine vision.

EDCS-15000027.summary: This inscription is a dedication or memorial to Mani Servilius Donatus.

EDCS-15000028.text_edition: \n Herculi Invicto sacrum Trosius Crispus |(centurio) legionis V Macedonicae piae constantis votum libens solvit merito\n
EDCS-15000028.translation: Sacred to Invincible Hercules. Trosius Crispus, centurion of the Fifth Macedonian Legion Pia Constans, willingly paid his vow, as deserved.
EDCS-15000028.summary: This is a votive inscription from Trosius Crispus, a centurion, to the god Hercules, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15000065.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelius Dines visit sic annos LXXXV et posuit Aurelius Ditugentus filius et heres patri carissimo bene merenti\n
EDCS-15000065.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. Aelius Dines lived 85 years and was placed here by Aurelius Ditugentus, his son and heir, to his most beloved and deserving father.
EDCS-15000065.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Aelius Dines at the age of 85 and was set up by his son and heir.

EDCS-15000149.text_edition: \n Aeterno Caio Bet Galato ex voto\n
EDCS-15000149.translation: To the Eternal One, Caius Bet Galatus, by vow.
EDCS-15000149.summary: This is a votive inscription to the Eternal One, dedicated by Caius Bet Galatus.

EDCS-15000150.text_edition: \n Aeterno sanctissimo pientissimoque Caius Iulius Diocletianus equo publico decurio et augur coloniae Sarmizegetusae decurio coloniae Apulensium patronus causarum ex voto\n
EDCS-15000150.translation: To the Eternal, most holy and most pious, Caius Julius Diocletianus, by public equestrian rank a council member and augur of the colony Sarmizegetusa, council member of the colony Apulensis, patron of causes, by vow.
EDCS-15000150.summary: This votive inscription by Caius Julius Diocletianus details his civic and religious offices in several Roman colonies, likely dating to the later Roman Empire.

EDCS-15100006.text_edition: \n Aesculapio et Hygiae Lucius Iulius Bassinus decurio coloniae Apulensis IIvir coloniae Napocensis flamen coloniae Drobetensis flamen municipii Diernensis decurio municipiorum Apulensis et Porolissensis tribunus legionis IIII Flaviae pro salute Iuliae Beronices coniugis votum libens solvit\n
EDCS-15100006.translation: To Aesculapius and Hygieia, Lucius Julius Bassinus, council member of the colony Apulensis, twice a magistrate of the colony Napocensis, priest of the colony Drobeta, priest of the municipality Dierna, council member of the municipalities Apulensis and Porolissensis, tribune of the Fourth Flavian Legion, willingly paid his vow for the health of his wife, Julia Beronice.
EDCS-15100006.summary: This is a detailed votive inscription by Lucius Julius Bassinus, a prominent official in several Roman colonies and a military tribune, to Aesculapius and Hygieia for his wife's health.

EDCS-15100131.text_edition: \n Herculenti Gorgias Posidonius ex visu posuit\n
EDCS-15100131.translation: Gorgias Posidonius to Herculentus, placed it after a vision.
EDCS-15100131.summary: This inscription records Gorgias Posidonius setting up an object to Herculentus after a divine vision.

EDCS-15100132.text_edition: \n Herculi Iulius Victor veteranus et Iulius Herculanus votum solverunt libenter\n
EDCS-15100132.translation: To Hercules, Julius Victor, a veteran, and Julius Herculanus willingly paid their vows.
EDCS-15100132.summary: This is a votive inscription to Hercules by two individuals, Julius Victor (a veteran) and Julius Herculanus.

EDCS-15100133.summary: This is a partial inscription dedicating something to Hercules Augustus by Valerius Pontianus.

EDCS-15100134.summary: This inscription is a dedication or memorial to Julius Quinctus.

EDCS-15100135.text_edition: \n Isidi myrionimae sacrum Aelia Iusta pro Ver Fir<m=N>o filio votum solvit libens merito \n
EDCS-15100135.translation: Sacred to Isis of a myriad names, Aelia Iusta gladly and deservedly fulfilled her vow for her son Verfirminus.
EDCS-15100135.summary: A dedication to Isis made by Aelia Iusta on behalf of her son Verfirminus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15100136.translation: Sacred to Jupiter the Repeller, Avonius Saeclaris.
EDCS-15100136.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Depulsor by Avonius Saeclaris, likely an offering from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15100139.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Titus Aelius Onesimus Augustalis coloniae Apulense ex viso\n
EDCS-15100139.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Titus Aelius Onesimus, an Augustalis of the colony of Apula, made this in accordance with a vision.
EDCS-15100139.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Titus Aelius Onesimus, an official in the colony of Apula, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15200069.summary: A fragmentary inscription, likely a dedication to the Celestial Gods, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15300203.text_edition: \n Dianae Augustae Marcus Ulpius Respectus veteranus ex stratore consularis et Aelia Valentina votum libentes solverunt\n
EDCS-15300203.translation: To Augusta Diana, Marcus Ulpius Respectus, a veteran former orderly of a consular governor, and Aelia Valentina gladly and deservedly fulfilled their vow.
EDCS-15300203.summary: A dedication to Diana Augusta by Marcus Ulpius Respectus, a veteran, and Aelia Valentina, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15300209.text_edition: \n <Q=O>uadruvis aram Cornelia Daphnis votum solvit libens animo meritoque \n
EDCS-15300209.translation: To the Four Gods, Cornelia Daphnis gladly and deservedly fulfilled her vow with a willing heart.
EDCS-15300209.summary: A dedication to the Quadruvis by Cornelia Daphnis, likely an offering from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15300210.text_edition: \n Apollini ex viso cohors I Brittonum |(miliaria) equitata pia fidelis civium Romanorum cui praeest Cludius \n
EDCS-15300210.translation: To Apollo, from a vision, the First Cohort of Britons, cavalry, pious and loyal, of Roman citizens, commanded by Cludius...
EDCS-15300210.summary: A dedication to Apollo by the First Cohort of Britons (equitata), a Roman auxiliary unit, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15400014.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo LEIIP Atius Atianus posuerunt\n
EDCS-15400014.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, the family of Atius Atianus placed this.
EDCS-15400014.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by the family of Atius Atianus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15400017.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Aurelius Ianuarius b(ene)f(iciarius) consularis voto libens posuit\n
EDCS-15400017.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Aurelius Ianuarius, a beneficiary of a consular governor, gladly erected this in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-15400017.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Aurelius Ianuarius, a military subordinate, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15400018.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Florius Maximus |(centurio) votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-15400018.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Florius Maximus, a centurion, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-15400018.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Florius Maximus, a centurion, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15400019.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Gaius Iulius Epigenes votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-15400019.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Gaius Julius Epigenes willingly fulfilled his vow as deserved.
EDCS-15400019.summary: A votive inscription to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, recording the fulfillment of a vow by Gaius Julius Epigenes, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-15400020.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo pro salute Imperatoris Luci Septimi Severi Pertenacis Augusti et Aureli Antonini Caesaris Marcus Iulius Quirinus Augustalis coloniae Apulensis ob honorem patronatus collegii Pontobithynorum ianuas et valuas ad introitum templi fecit\n
EDCS-15400020.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, for the health of the Emperor Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax Augustus and Caesar Aurelius Antoninus, Marcus Julius Quirinus, Augustalis of the colony of Apulum, in honor of his patronate of the college of Pontobithyni, made the doors and gates at the entrance to the temple.
EDCS-15400020.summary: An inscription recording the dedication of temple doors and gates by Marcus Julius Quirinus, Augustalis of Apulum, to Jupiter Optimus Maximus for the emperor's health, likely dating to the Severan period.

EDCS-15400021.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Caius Pomponius Fuscus IIvir coloniae Apulensis votum libens solvit\n
EDCS-15400021.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Gaius Pomponius Fuscus, duovir of the colony of Apulum, willingly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-15400021.summary: A votive inscription to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Gaius Pomponius Fuscus, duovir of the colony of Apulum, recording the fulfillment of a vow, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-15400023.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Marcus Ulpius Respectus veteranus legionis XIII geminae Severianae ex stratore consularis votum libens solvit\n
EDCS-15400023.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Marcus Ulpius Respectus, veteran of the XIII Gemina Severiana legion, formerly a military supervisor (strator) of a consular governor, willingly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-15400023.summary: A votive inscription to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Marcus Ulpius Respectus, a veteran and former military supervisor, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-15400025.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Iunoni Reginae Caius Iulius Iustus veteranus legionis XIII geminae et Caius Iulius Octavianus filius eius ex voto\n
EDCS-15400025.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest and to Juno Regina, Gaius Julius Iustus, veteran of the XIII Gemina legion, and his son Gaius Julius Octavianus, in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-15400025.summary: A votive inscription to Jupiter Optimus Maximus and Juno Regina by a veteran of the XIII Gemina legion and his son, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-15400028.text_edition: \n Deo Libero Patri Aurelius Renatus miles legionis XIII geminae voto libens posuit\n
EDCS-15400028.translation: To Liber Pater, Marcus Aurelius Renatus, a soldier of the XIII Gemina legion, willingly set up this offering in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-15400028.summary: A votive inscription to Liber Pater by Aurelius Renatus, a soldier of the XIII Gemina legion, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-15400029.translation: To Liber Pater, Gaius Julius Chrestus.
EDCS-15400029.summary: A dedication to Liber Pater by Gaius Julius Chrestus, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-15400030.text_edition: \n Iulius beneficiarius consularis donum dedit dedicavitque\n
EDCS-15400030.translation: Iulius, a beneficiary of a consular governor, gave and dedicated this gift.
EDCS-15400030.summary: An inscription recording a gift and dedication by Iulius, a beneficiary of a consular governor, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-15400031.text_edition: \n Pro salute Augusti Magnae Deum Matri sanctum Titus Flavius Longinus veteranus ex decurione alae II nnoniorum decurio coloniae Dacicae decurio municipii Napocensis decurio kanabarum legionis XIII geminae et Claudia Candida coniunx et Flavii Longinus Clementina Marcellina filii ex imperio pecunia sua fecerunt locus datus decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-15400031.translation: For the health of the Emperor and the Holy Mother of the Gods, Titus Flavius Longinus, veteran, formerly a decurion of the II Nnoniorum ala, decurion of the Dacian colony, decurion of the municipium of Napoca, decurion of the kanabae of the XIII Gemina legion, and his wife Claudia Candida, and his sons Flavii Longinus Clementina Marcellina, made this at their own expense by decree. The site was granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-15400031.summary: An inscription recording the construction of something by Titus Flavius Longinus and his family, with multiple decurional roles, for the health of the emperor and the Magna Mater, likely dating to the late Roman imperial period.

EDCS-15400032.summary: A partial dedication to Sol Invictus Mithras, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-15400033.text_edition: \n Soli Invicto Mythrae sic Caius Iulius Marcianus signifer legionis XIII geminae libens posuit\n
EDCS-15400033.translation: To Sol Invictus Mithras, Caius Julius Marcianus, standard-bearer of the thirteenth legion Gemina, willingly set this up.
EDCS-15400033.summary: This is a dedication to Sol Invictus Mithras by a standard-bearer of Legio XIII Gemina.

EDCS-15400036.translation: Aelius Mestrius willingly set this up.
EDCS-15400036.summary: This is a dedication by Aelius Mestrius, likely an individual setting up a private votive offering.

EDCS-15400037.text_edition: \n ------] Marcus Ulpius Linus imaginifer legionis XIII geminae\n
EDCS-15400037.translation: Marcus Ulpius Linus, standard-bearer of the thirteenth legion Gemina...
EDCS-15400037.summary: This inscription records Marcus Ulpius Linus, a standard-bearer of Legio XIII Gemina, likely part of a larger monument, but the rest is lost.

EDCS-15400038.translation: The deputy of the governor fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-15400038.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow by a deputy to a governor, possibly an officer within a legion or provincial administration.

EDCS-15400039.text_edition: \n Quadruviis Marcus Gallius Caminas decurio coloniae Sarmizegetusae cum Sabina eius votum solvit\n
EDCS-15400039.translation: At the crossroads, Marcus Gallius Caminas, decurio of the colony of Sarmizegetusa, along with his wife Sabina, fulfilled their vow.
EDCS-15400039.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow by Marcus Gallius Caminas, a local official (decurio) in the colony of Sarmizegetusa, and his wife Sabina.

EDCS-15400040.text_edition: \n reginae Bersius Ingenuus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-15400040.translation: Bersius Ingenuus willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to the queen.
EDCS-15400040.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow by Bersius Ingenuus to a female deity, referred to as 'queen'.

EDCS-15400043.text_edition: \n Deanae sacrum Titus Flavius Italicus primus IIIIvir municipii cum Statilia Lucia coniuge et Flavio Statiliano filio ex voto\n
EDCS-15400043.translation: Sacred to Deana: Titus Flavius Italicus, chief magistrate of the municipality, with his wife Statilia Lucia and his son Flavius Statilianus, made this offering in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-15400043.summary: This is a religious dedication to the goddess Deana by Titus Flavius Italicus, a high-ranking municipal official, along with his family.

EDCS-15400044.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Titus Flavius Italicus primus IIIIvir municipii Aureli Apulensis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-15400044.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Titus Flavius Italicus, chief magistrate of the municipality Aurelia Apulensis, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-15400044.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Titus Flavius Italicus, a chief magistrate of the municipality of Aurelia Apulensis.

EDCS-15400045.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo dis deabusque paternis Publius Aelius Stefanus votum libens merito posuit\n
EDCS-15400045.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus and his ancestral gods and goddesses, Publius Aelius Stefanus willingly and deservedly set this up.
EDCS-15400045.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus and ancestral deities by Publius Aelius Stefanus.

EDCS-15400047.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Soli Bussurigio Publius December Aelius voto posuit\n
EDCS-15400047.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus and Sol Bussurigius, Publius December Aelius set this up by vow.
EDCS-15400047.summary: This inscription records a votive offering to Jupiter Optimus Maximus and the deity Sol Bussurigius by Publius December Aelius.

EDCS-15400048.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Custodi Iunoni Minervae ceterisque dis deabusque Publius Aelius Antipater IIviralis coloniae Apulensis et Antonia Iulia eius et Aelii Antipater Iulianus Genialis decccuriones coloniae equo publico et Iulia filii eorum pro salute sua suorumque\n
EDCS-15400048.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Guardian, to Juno, Minerva, and all the gods and goddesses, Publius Aelius Antipater, duovir of the colony of Apulis, and Antonia Iulia, his wife, and Aelius Antipater Julianus Genialis, decurions of the colony with public horse, and Iulia, their children, for their own well-being and that of their families.
EDCS-15400048.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Jupiter, Juno, Minerva, and other gods by a prominent family of Apulis for their health.

EDCS-15400049.text_edition: \n Iovi Victori Iovi Depulsori Marcus Domestius Restitutus |(centurio) legionis XIII geminae X hastatus posterior votum solvit libens merito Commodo et Laterano X Kalendas Septembres\n
EDCS-15400049.translation: To Jupiter the Victor, Jupiter the Repeller, Marcus Domestius Restitutus, centurion of the XIII Gemina legion, junior spearman, fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly, to Commodus and Lateranus, on the 7th day before the Kalends of September.
EDCS-15400049.summary: This is a votive inscription by a centurion of the XIII Gemina legion to Jupiter, possibly commemorating a military success, dated to the reign of Commodus.

EDCS-15400050.text_edition: \n L<i=L>bero Patri Claudius Heraclides decurio votum libens merito \n
EDCS-15400050.translation: To Liber Pater, Claudius Heraclides, decurion, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-15400050.summary: This is a votive inscription by Claudius Heraclides, a decurion, to Liber Pater.

EDCS-15400051.text_edition: \n Deo Invicto Mithrae Titus Aurelius Fabia Marcus veteranus legionis XIII geminae dono dedit dedicavit\n
EDCS-15400051.translation: To the Unconquered God Mithras, Titus Aurelius Fabia Marcus, a veteran of the XIII Gemina legion, gave and dedicated this offering.
EDCS-15400051.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a gift to the god Mithras by Titus Aurelius Fabia Marcus, a veteran of the XIII Gemina legion.

EDCS-15400052.text_edition: \n Deo Invicto Mithrae Titus Aurelius Fabia Marcus veteranus legionis XIII geminae\n
EDCS-15400052.translation: To the Unconquered God Mithras, Titus Aurelius Fabia Marcus, a veteran of the XIII Gemina legion.
EDCS-15400052.summary: This inscription identifies Titus Aurelius Fabia Marcus, a veteran of the XIII Gemina legion, in relation to the god Mithras.

EDCS-15400053.text_edition: \n Nemesi Reginae Caius Iulius Valens haruspex coloniae Apulensis et antistes huiusce loci somnio monitus libens posuit\n
EDCS-15400053.translation: To Nemesis Regina, Caius Iulius Valens, haruspex of the colony of Apulis and priest of this place, moved by a dream, willingly set this up.
EDCS-15400053.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Nemesis Regina by Caius Iulius Valens, a haruspex and priest in Apulis, who was inspired by a dream.

EDCS-15400054.text_edition: \n Saturno Securo Marcus Herennius Faustus legatus Augusti\n
EDCS-15400054.translation: To Saturnus Securus, Marcus Herennius Faustus, legate of the Emperor.
EDCS-15400054.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Saturnus Securus by Marcus Herennius Faustus, an imperial legate.

EDCS-15400055.translation: Iulius, for what he had vowed to Silvanus.
EDCS-15400055.summary: This inscription likely records a vow made by Iulius to the god Silvanus.

EDCS-15400056.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Silvanus Domestius by someone named Aurelius.

EDCS-15400057.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico Marcus Valerius Basssinus Iunior posuit\n
EDCS-15400057.translation: Marcus Valerius Basssinus Junior set this up to Silvanus Domestius.
EDCS-15400057.summary: This inscription records the erection of a monument to Silvanus Domestius by Marcus Valerius Basssinus Junior.

EDCS-15400058.text_edition: \n Deo Soli Lucius Valerius Felix votum solvit\n
EDCS-15400058.translation: Lucius Valerius Felix fulfilled his vow to the god Sol.
EDCS-15400058.summary: This is a votive inscription recording Lucius Valerius Felix fulfilling a vow to the god Sol, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15400059.text_edition: \n Marcus Aurelius Sila actarius peditum singularium pro se et suos sic votum libens solvit\n
EDCS-15400059.translation: Marcus Aurelius Sila, an *actarius* of the *pedites singulares*, willingly fulfilled his vow for himself and his family.
EDCS-15400059.summary: This is a votive inscription by Marcus Aurelius Sila, an *actarius* of the elite guard, fulfilling a vow for himself and his family.

EDCS-15500075.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publio Aelio Tertio domo Claudia Viruno veterano ex |(centurione) cohortis I Britannicae |(miliariae) civium Romanorum equitatae annorum LX Aelia Aestiva coniunx et heredes posuerunt\n
EDCS-15500075.translation: For Publius Aelius Tertius, from the region of Claudia Virunum, a veteran who was formerly a centurion of the First Cohort of Britons, a troop of Roman citizens, of equestrian rank, aged 60; his wife Aelia Aestiva and his heirs placed this.
EDCS-15500075.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Publius Aelius Tertius, a veteran centurion, and was erected by his wife and heirs, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15500076.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valeriae Rufinae vixit annos XX et Longiniano filio <e>ius annorum IIII Longinus beneficiarius consularis suis fecit\n
EDCS-15500076.translation: For Valeria Rufina, who lived 20 years, and her son Longinianus, aged 4. Longinus, a *beneficiarius consularis*, made this for them.
EDCS-15500076.summary: This funerary epitaph records the deaths of Valeria Rufina and her young son Longinianus, and was erected by Longinus, a *beneficiarius consularis*.

EDCS-15500084.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iustiniae Eutychiae vixit annos XXXXV Livius Rufus decurio coniugi posuit bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-15500084.translation: For Iustinia Eutychia, who lived 45 years. Livius Rufus, a decurion, placed this for his deserving wife.
EDCS-15500084.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Iustinia Eutychia and was placed by her husband Livius Rufus, a decurion.

EDCS-15500085.text_edition: \n Aelia Nice vixit annis XC Aelius Victor vixit annis IIII Aelia Mammutio vixit annis XXII Aelius Vitalianus augur NMOO matri et filio neptiae sic bene merentibus\n
EDCS-15500085.translation: Aelia Nice, who lived 90 years; Aelius Victor, who lived 4 years; Aelia Mammutio, who lived 22 years. Aelius Vitalianus, augur, for his mother and his daughter-in-law, who were deserving.
EDCS-15500085.summary: This funerary epitaph records the deaths of three individuals, including a mother and daughter-in-law, and was erected by Aelius Vitalianus, an augur.

EDCS-15500086.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Iulio Philippo Pio Felici Invicto Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate patri patriae consuli proconsuli res publica municipii Septimi Porolissensium\n
EDCS-15500086.translation: For the Emperor Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, chief priest, holding tribunician power, father of the fatherland, consul, proconsul, the state of the municipality of Septimius Porolissum [dedicated this].
