EDCS-15800170.translation: To Silvanus. Publius Aelius Fabianus, son of Publius, augur, pontifex, duovir, decurion of the colony of Sarmizegetusa, decurion of the colony of Napoca, augur of the same colony, decurion of the municipium of Apula, freely and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-15800170.summary: This extensive inscription records a vow fulfilled by Publius Aelius Fabianus, who held numerous prestigious civic and religious offices in several colonies and a municipium, probably dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-15800171.text_edition: \n Silvano Marcus Caecilius Rufinus Marianus legatus legionis XIII geminae\n
EDCS-15800171.translation: To Silvanus. Marcus Caecilius Rufinus Marianus, legate of the XIII Gemina legion.
EDCS-15800171.summary: This is a dedication to Silvanus by Marcus Caecilius Rufinus Marianus, a legate of the XIII Gemina legion, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-15800172.text_edition: \n Silvano Marcus Chrestus pro salute Marci Antoni Florentini fili mei votum solvi libens merito\n
EDCS-15800172.translation: To Silvanus. Marcus Chrestus freely and deservedly fulfilled his vow for the safety of his son, Marcus Antonius Florentinus.
EDCS-15800172.summary: This is a votive inscription to Silvanus by Marcus Chrestus, dedicating it for the health of his son, Marcus Antonius Florentinus, probably from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-15800173.text_edition: \n Silvano sacrum Philippus ex viso posuit\n
EDCS-15800173.translation: Sacred to Silvanus. Philippus placed this after a vision.
EDCS-15800173.summary: This inscription records Philippus placing a sacred object to Silvanus after experiencing a divine vision, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-15800174.text_edition: \n Deo Silvano Ulpius Salvinus quaestor voto libens posuit\n
EDCS-15800174.translation: To the god Silvanus. Ulpius Salvinus, quaestor, freely and deservedly placed this by vow.
EDCS-15800174.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Silvanus by Ulpius Salvinus, a quaestor, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-15800175.text_edition: \n Marcus Q Donatus Silvano Augusto sacrum votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-15800175.translation: Marcus Q. Donatus, sacred to Silvanus Augustus, freely and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-15800175.summary: This is a votive inscription to Silvanus Augustus by Marcus Q. Donatus, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-15800178.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico pro salute Titi Flavi Crescentis praefecti numeri Maurorum Hispanorum Titus Flavius Felicianus domus illius alumnus\n
EDCS-15800178.translation: To Silvanus Domesticus. Titus Flavius Felicianus, a freedman of that household, [dedicated this] for the safety of Titus Flavius Crescentis, prefect of the troop of Moorish Spaniards.
EDCS-15800178.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Silvanus Domesticus by Titus Flavius Felicianus for the safety of his patron, Titus Flavius Crescentis, a prefect, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-15800179.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico Caius Iulius Valens IIvir coloniae Apulensis pro salute sua suorumque posuit\n
EDCS-15800179.translation: To Silvanus Domesticus, Gaius Julius Valens, duovir of the colony of Apula, erected this for the health of himself and his family.
EDCS-15800179.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Silvanus Domesticus by Gaius Julius Valens, a duovir of Apula, likely in the 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-15800180.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico Marcus Opellius Secundus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-15800180.translation: Marcus Opellius Secundus gladly fulfilled his vow to Silvanus Domesticus, as was fitting.
EDCS-15800180.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow to Silvanus Domesticus by Marcus Opellius Secundus, likely in the 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-15800181.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico Terrae Matri Herculi sacrum pro salute sua suorumque Iulius Gaianus libens posuit\n
EDCS-15800181.translation: To Silvanus Domesticus, Mother Earth, and Hercules, Julius Gaianus gladly erected this as a sacred offering for the health of himself and his family.
EDCS-15800181.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Silvanus Domesticus, Terra Mater, and Hercules by Julius Gaianus, likely in the 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-15800182.text_edition: \n Silvano Sancto Lucius Aemilius Carus legatus Augusti pro praetore III Daciarum\n
EDCS-15800182.translation: Lucius Aemilius Carus, legate of the emperor and governor of the province of III Daciarum, (dedicated this) to Silvanus Sanctus.
EDCS-15800182.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Silvanus Sanctus by Lucius Aemilius Carus, a Roman legate and governor, likely in the 2nd century AD.

EDCS-15800183.text_edition: \n Silvano Silvestri et Dianae Marcus Aurelius Comatius Super decurio antistes pro salute sua et Comatiorum Superiani Superes Exsuperantiani Superstitis filiorum votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-15800183.translation: Marcus Aurelius Comatius Super, decurion and priest, gladly fulfilled his vow, as was fitting, to Silvanus Silvestris and Diana, for the health of himself and his sons Comatius Superianus, Superes, Exsuperantius, and Superstitis.
EDCS-15800183.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow by Marcus Aurelius Comatius Super to Silvanus Silvestris and Diana for his family's health, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-15800184.text_edition: \n Deo Silvano Silvestri Caius Longius Aquilinus veteranus legionis XIII geminae pro se et suis voto libens posuit\n
EDCS-15800184.translation: Gaius Longius Aquilinus, a veteran of the Thirteenth Legion Gemina, gladly set this up by vow for himself and his family, to the god Silvanus Silvestris.
EDCS-15800184.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Silvanus Silvestris by Gaius Longius Aquilinus, a veteran of the XIII Gemina legion, likely in the 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-15800185.text_edition: \n Suleviis Flavius Attalus votum libens solvit\n
EDCS-15800185.translation: Flavius Attalus gladly fulfilled his vow to the Suleviae.
EDCS-15800185.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow to the Suleviae by Flavius Attalus, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-15800186.text_edition: \n Veneri Augustae Fabius Pulcher Aug3 coloniae votum? solvit\n
EDCS-15800186.translation: Fabius Pulcher, Augur of the colony, fulfilled his vow to Venus Augusta.
EDCS-15800186.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow to Venus Augusta by Fabius Pulcher, an Augur of the colony, likely in the 2nd century AD.

EDCS-15800187.text_edition: \n Victoriae Augustae Lucius Iulius Luci Galeria Leuganus Clunia veteranus legionis XIIII geminae Martiae victricis aedis custos civium Romanorum legionis XIII geminae nomine suo et Cai Iuli Paterni fili sui donum dedit\n
EDCS-15800187.translation: Lucius Iulius Luci Galeria Leuganus Clunia, veteran of the Fourteenth Legion Gemina Martia Victrix and custos of the temple for Roman citizens of the Thirteenth Legion Gemina, dedicated this gift in his own name and in the name of his son Gaius Iulius Paternus, to Victory Augusta.
EDCS-15800187.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Victoria Augusta by Lucius Iulius Luci Galeria Leuganus Clunia, a veteran and temple custos, likely in the 2nd century AD.

EDCS-15800189.text_edition: \n ] ibus mu--- Publius Aelius Crescens duplicarius singularis consularis equitum singularium per quem templum factum est posuit\n
EDCS-15800189.translation: Publius Aelius Crescens, a duplicarius singularis consularis of the equites singulares, by whom the temple was built, set this up.
EDCS-15800189.summary: This inscription records the erection of a temple, by Publius Aelius Crescens, a distinguished cavalry officer, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-15800191.text_edition: \n C(aius)? Valerius Salvius Augustalis coloniae Apulensis pro salute sua et Flaviae Syrae coniugis et Cai Valerii Flaviani filii et omnium suorum ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-15800191.translation: Gaius Valerius Salvius Augustalis of the colony of Apulum, for his own safety and that of his wife Flavia Syra, his son Caius Valerius Flavianus, and all his household, erected this in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-15800191.summary: This is a votive dedication by Gaius Valerius Salvius Augustalis for his family's well-being, likely erected during the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15800194.translation: Sacred to Nutius, from Caecilia, daughter of Sextus.
EDCS-15800194.summary: This is a dedication to a deity named Nutius, made by Caecilia, daughter of Sextus, probably in the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15800195.text_edition: \n Marcus Marcellus librarius legati votum solvit merito\n
EDCS-15800195.translation: Marcus Marcellus, a copyist, fulfilled his vow deservedly.
EDCS-15800195.summary: This records Marcus Marcellus, a librarius, fulfilling a vow, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15800196.text_edition: \n Silvano sacrum Marcus Ulpius Terentius ex viso posuit\n
EDCS-15800196.translation: Sacred to Silvanus, Marcus Ulpius Terentius erected this in fulfillment of a vision.
EDCS-15800196.summary: This is a dedication to the god Silvanus by Marcus Ulpius Terentius, who erected it after a vision, likely during the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15800200.text_edition: \n Imperatore Caesare Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto III et Imperatore Caesare Lucio Aurelio Vero Augusto II consulibus per legionem XIII geminam sub cura Publi Furi Saturnini legati pro praetore consulis designati\n
EDCS-15800200.translation: During the consulships of Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus for the third time and Emperor Caesar Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus for the second time, by the Thirteenth Double Legion, under the supervision of Publius Furius Saturninus, legate pro praetore and designated consul.
EDCS-15800200.summary: This text records the consuls for the year 215 CE and mentions the Thirteenth Legion and a legate, indicating a high-status military inscription.

EDCS-15800201.text_edition: \n propro Imperatoris Commodi Augusti Pii Felicis veteranis legionis XIII geminae militibus factis Pudente et Pollione consulibus missis honesta missione per Manilium Fuscum legatum Augusti Aproniano et Bradua consulibus Acutius Quintinus contiro conveteranis suis columnam donum dedit\n
EDCS-15800201.translation: For the prosperity of Emperor Commodus Augustus Pius Felix, the veterans of the Thirteenth Double Legion, who were made soldiers by Pudens and Pollio as consuls and dismissed with an honorable discharge, through Manilius Fuscus, legate of the emperor, donated this column as a gift to their fellow veterans, during the consulships of Apronianus and Bradua, Acutius Quintinus, a centurion, and his fellow veterans.
EDCS-15800201.summary: This inscription records a donation of a column by Acutius Quintinus and fellow veterans to Emperor Commodus, likely from the late 2nd century CE.

EDCS-15800203.text_edition: \n Pro salute Augggustorum Luci Septimi Severi Pii Pertinacis et Marci Aurelii Antonini Impperatorum et Publi Septimi Getae Caesaris collegium centonariorum scholam cum aetoma pecunia sua fecit dedicante Lucio Pomponio Liberale consulari Daciarum III\n
EDCS-15800203.translation: For the well-being of our Emperors Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Emperors, and Caesar Publius Septimius Geta, the guild of fullers (centonarii) built this school with its annex with their own money, dedicated by Lucius Pomponius Liberalis, consular governor of Dacia for the third time.
EDCS-15800203.summary: This records the construction of a school building by the guild of fullers for the imperial well-being, dedicated by a high-ranking official, Lucius Pomponius Liberalis, likely in the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-15800204.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Caio Messio Quinto Traiano Decio Pio Felici Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate II consuli II patri patriae restitutori Daciarum colonia nova Apulensis\n
EDCS-15800204.translation: To Emperor Caesar Gaius Messius Quintus Trajanus Decius Pius Felix Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, holder of tribunician power for the second time, consul for the second time, father of the country, restorer of Dacia, the new colony of Apulensis.
EDCS-15800204.summary: This is an honorific inscription dedicated to Emperor Trajan Decius by the colony of Apulensis, likely from his reign (249-251 CE).

EDCS-15800205.text_edition: \n Lucio Mario Perpetuo consulari Daciarum III legato Augusti pro praetore provinciae Moesiae Superioris curatori rerum publicarum Urbis item Tusculanorum praesidi provinciae Arabiae laticlavio legionis IIII Scythicae praesidi iustissimo Marcus Ulpius Caius |(centurio) legionis III Italicae Antoninianae\n
EDCS-15800205.translation: Marcus Ulpius Caius, a centurion of the Third Italian Antoninian Legion, [dedicated this] to Lucius Marius Perpetuus, consular governor of Dacia for the third time, legate of the emperor pro praetore of the province of Upper Moesia, curator of public affairs of the City, likewise governor of the province of Arabia, and a broad-striped senator of the Fourth Scythian Legion, and governor, most just.
EDCS-15800205.summary: This inscription is a dedication by a centurion to Lucius Marius Perpetuus, a high-ranking official with extensive provincial commands, likely from the late 2nd or early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-15800207.text_edition: \n Publio Aelio Antipatro Marcello equiti Romano decurioni coloniae Apulensis filio Publi Aeli Antipatri a militiis et IIviri coloniae supra scriptae et adoptivo Publi Aeli Marcelli viri egregii ex praefecto legionum VII Claudiae et I adiutricis Dades et Filetus actores\n
EDCS-15800207.translation: To Publius Aelius Antipater Marcellus, a Roman knight, decurion of the colony of Apulensis, son of Publius Aelius Antipater, from military service, and duovir of the aforementioned colony, and adoptive son of Publius Aelius Marcellus, an outstanding man, former prefect of the Legions VII Claudia and I Adiutrix, Dades and Filetus, estate managers.
EDCS-15800207.summary: This inscription honors Publius Aelius Antipater Marcellus, a prominent local official and son of a military leader, with dedications from his estate managers, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15800208.text_edition: \n Publiae Aeliae Iulianae Marcellae splendidissimae puellae filiae Publi Aeli Iuliani equitis Romani flaminis et IIviralis coloniae Apulensis et adoptivae Publi Aeli Marcelli viri egregii ex praefecto leggionum VII Claudiae et I adiutricis Dades et Filetus actores\n
EDCS-15800208.translation: To Publia Aelia Iuliana Marcella, a most splendid girl, daughter of Publius Aelius Iulianus, a Roman eques, flamen, and former duovir of the colony of Apula, and adopted daughter of Publius Aelius Marcellus, an outstanding man, former prefect of legions VII Claudia and I Adiutrix. Dades and Filetus, the managers.
EDCS-15800208.summary: This inscription is a dedication to a young girl, Publia Aelia Iuliana Marcella, daughter and adopted daughter of prominent local officials, likely on her tomb.

EDCS-15800209.text_edition: \n Lucius Aelius Silvanus beneficiarius consularis libens posuit\n
EDCS-15800209.translation: Lucius Aelius Silvanus, a volunteer assistant to the consular governor, placed this willingly.
EDCS-15800209.summary: This is a dedication by Lucius Aelius Silvanus, identified as a beneficiarius consularis, likely to a deity or in a public space.

EDCS-15800210.text_edition: \n librarius benef(iciarius) consularis Aurelius Iustus Aurelius Lucilius agrimensor Valerius Romulus Valerius Sabinus Septimius Magnus Septimius Victor Aurelius Senecio duplarius Iulius Militaris Por Iulianus tesserarius cohortis IIII Ulpius Surus \n
EDCS-15800210.translation: Aurelius Iustus, a librarius beneficiarius consularis; Aurelius Lucilius, a surveyor; Valerius Romulus; Valerius Sabinus; Septimius Magnus; Septimius Victor; Aurelius Senecio, a duplarius; Iulius Militaris; Por Iulianus, a tesserarius of the fourth cohort; Ulpius Surus.
EDCS-15800210.summary: This inscription lists the names of several individuals holding various military and administrative roles, possibly as a record or dedication by a group.

EDCS-15800211.text_edition: \n ADIZ candidatus s candidatus inus duplicarius ianus duplicarius arius secutor tribuni mus beneficiarius tribuni candidatus DI Septimius Carpianus Septimius Statilianus Septimius Castus Septimius Celsus candidatus Septimius Alexander candidatus Septimius Nepos candidatus Claudius Alexander beneficiarius consularis \n
EDCS-15800211.translation: ADIZ, a candidate; [---] a candidate; [---] a duplicarius; Ianus, a duplicarius; [---]arius, a secutor; [---]mus, a beneficiarius of the tribune; [---] a candidate; DI Septimius Carpianus; Septimius Statilianus; Septimius Castus; Septimius Celsus, a candidate; Septimius Alexander, a candidate; Septimius Nepos, a candidate; Claudius Alexander, a beneficiarius consularis.
EDCS-15800211.summary: This inscription lists numerous individuals with military ranks such as candidate, duplicarius, secutor, and beneficiarius, likely related to a military unit or collegium.

EDCS-15800212.text_edition: \n Marco Ulpio Caio praefecto legionum III Flaviae et XIII geminae \n
EDCS-15800212.translation: To Marcus Ulpius Caius, prefect of legions III Flavia and XIII Gemina.
EDCS-15800212.summary: This is a dedication to Marcus Ulpius Caius, who held the high military command of two legions, likely in a public or military context.

EDCS-15800213.text_edition: \n Publio Aelio Publi filio Papiria Geniali decurioni et pontifici coloniae Apulensis patrono collegii centonariorum Publius Aelius Euthymus libertus\n
EDCS-15800213.translation: To Publius Aelius Genialis, son of Publius Aelius, of the Papiria tribe, decurion and pontiff of the colony of Apula, and patron of the college of weavers. Publius Aelius Euthymus, his freedman.
EDCS-15800213.summary: This inscription records a dedication by a freedman, Publius Aelius Euthymus, to his patron, Publius Aelius Genialis, a local official and patron of a guild in Apula.

EDCS-15800214.text_edition: \n Publio Aelio Publi filio Papiria Strenuo equo publico sacerdoti arae Augusti auguri et IIvirali coloniae Sarmizegetusae auguri coloniae Apulensis decurioni coloniae Drobetensis patrono collegiorum fabrum centonariorum et nautarum conductori pascui salinarum et commerciorum Rufinus eius\n
EDCS-15800214.translation: To Publius Aelius Strenuus, son of Publius Aelius, of the Papiria tribe, who held the public horse, priest of the altar of Augustus, augur, and former duovir of the colony of Sarmizegetusa, augur of the colony of Apula, decurion of the colony of Drobeta, patron of the colleges of smiths and weavers, and conductor of the pasture of the saltworks and commerce. Rufinus, his client.
EDCS-15800214.summary: This lengthy inscription honors Publius Aelius Strenuus, a prominent figure with multiple civic, religious, and economic roles in several colonies, with the dedication made by his client Rufinus.

EDCS-15800215.text_edition: \n Marcus Aurelius Chrestus ob honorem patronatus collegii fabrum coloniae Apulensis ad exstructionem aetomae dedit HS nummorum VIsex milia\n
EDCS-15800215.translation: Marcus Aurelius Chrestus gave six thousand sesterces for the honor of the patronate of the college of smiths of the colony of Apula towards the construction of the etoma.
EDCS-15800215.summary: This inscription records a donation of 6,000 sesterces by Marcus Aurelius Chrestus to fund the construction of an 'etoma' in honor of his patronate of the guild of smiths in Apula.

EDCS-15800216.text_edition: \n instantibus Ulpio Artemidoro et Aurelio Maximo quaestoribus\n
EDCS-15800216.translation: At the urging of Ulpius Artemidorus and Aurelius Maximus, quaestors.
EDCS-15800216.summary: This short inscription mentions the involvement of two quaestors, Ulpius Artemidorus and Aurelius Maximus, possibly relating to a financial or administrative matter.

EDCS-15800218.summary: This brief inscription records the name and profession of Aurelius Maximillianus, identified as a surveyor.

EDCS-15800221.text_edition: \n Deo Aeterno lius Gallicanus veteranus ex beneficiario consularis pro salute sua suorumque posuit\n
EDCS-15800221.translation: lius Gallicanus, a veteran, formerly a beneficiary of the governor, dedicated this for the welfare of himself and his family.
EDCS-15800221.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the Eternal God by a veteran, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-15800222.translation: To the Eternal One, Clodia Maxima and Flavia Valeria.
EDCS-15800222.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the Eternal One by two women, Clodia Maxima and Flavia Valeria, probably from the Imperial period.

EDCS-15800224.text_edition: \n Caius Nummius Verus eques Romanus IIviralis coloniae Apulensis et sacerdos numinis Aesculapi consecravit\n
EDCS-15800224.translation: Gaius Nummius Verus, a Roman knight, duumvir of the colony of Apula, and priest of the divine Aesculapius, dedicated this.
EDCS-15800224.summary: This inscription records a dedication by a Roman knight and local official to Aesculapius, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15800225.text_edition: \n Asclepio et Hygiae Caius Fabricius Dexter votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-15800225.translation: Gaius Fabricius Dexter willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Asclepius and Hygieia.
EDCS-15800225.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow to the gods Asclepius and Hygieia by Gaius Fabricius Dexter, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15800227.text_edition: \n Dianae Augustae Caius Iulius Valerius veteranus legionis XIII geminae decurio coloniae Sarmizegetusae missus ex beneficiario consularis\n
EDCS-15800227.translation: To Diana Augusta, Gaius Iulius Valerius, a veteran of the Legio XIII Gemina, a decurion of the colony of Sarmizegetusa, discharged as a beneficiary of the governor.
EDCS-15800227.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Diana Augusta by Gaius Iulius Valerius, a veteran and local official, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15800229.text_edition: \n Dianae ex voto Ulpius Vindex votum posuit libens\n
EDCS-15800229.translation: Ulpius Vindex willingly placed this offering to Diana according to his vow.
EDCS-15800229.summary: This inscription records a votive offering to Diana by Ulpius Vindex, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15800231.text_edition: \n Deis deabusque Publius Fabius Herclianus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-15800231.translation: Publius Fabius Herclianus willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to all the gods and goddesses.
EDCS-15800231.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow to all gods and goddesses by Publius Fabius Herclianus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15800233.translation: Scentius Alexander willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to the god Bussumarus.
EDCS-15800233.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow to the god Bussumarus by Scentius Alexander, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15800235.text_edition: \n Eponae Reginae sanctae Caius [] S[] legatus Augusti pro praetore consularis Daciarum III V IENTI\n
EDCS-15800235.translation: To Epona Regina, the holy one, Gaius [], S[.], governor with the rank of proconsul, imperial legate for the Dacian provinces.
EDCS-15800235.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Epona Regina by a high-ranking Roman official in Dacia, likely dating to the 2nd century AD.

EDCS-15800236.text_edition: \n Herculi Augusto Reginus sacerdos institutus ab Helvio Pertinace cons(ulari)\n
EDCS-15800236.translation: Reginus, a priest appointed by Helvius Pertinax, governor, dedicated this to Hercules Augustus.
EDCS-15800236.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Hercules Augustus by Reginus, a priest appointed by a governor, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15800238.text_edition: \n Iunoni Semliae Iulius Alexander actarius legati legionis XIII geminae et Aelia Vincentia libentes posuerunt\n
EDCS-15800238.translation: To Juno Semleia, Julius Alexander, chief clerk of a legionary legate of the XIII Gemina legion, and Aelia Vincentia, willingly set this up.
EDCS-15800238.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Juno Semleia by a military official and a woman, likely on a private monument, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-15800240.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Aurelius Marinus Bassus et Aurelius Castor Polydi circumstantes viderunt numen aquilae descidisse monte supra draconem res validavit supstrinxit sic aquilam hi supra scripti aquilam de periculo liberaverunt voto libentes merito posuerunt\n
EDCS-15800240.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Aurelius Marinus Bassus and Aurelius Castor Polydi, seeing the divine power of the eagle descend from the mountain above the dragon, reinforced the matter and bound it. Thus, the aforementioned freed the eagle from peril and willingly and deservedly set this up by vow.
EDCS-15800240.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Jupiter Best and Greatest by two individuals who claim to have witnessed and intervened in a miraculous event involving an eagle, likely a votive offering from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-15800241.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Iulius Memnon votum reddidit donum dedit\n
EDCS-15800241.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Julius Memnon paid his vow and gave this gift.
EDCS-15800241.summary: This is a simple votive inscription to Jupiter Best and Greatest by Julius Memnon, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-15800244.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno pro salute Imperatorum Aelius Valentinus veteranus sacerdos templum impendio suo restituit\n
EDCS-15800244.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest Dolichenus, for the safety of the Emperors, Aelius Valentinus, veteran and priest, restored the temple at his own expense.
EDCS-15800244.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a temple to Jupiter Dolichenus by a veteran priest, dedicated to the safety of the emperors, likely in the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-15800245.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno Aurelii Alexander et Flavus Suri negotiatores ex voto libentes posuerunt\n
EDCS-15800245.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest Dolichenus, Aurelii Alexander and Flavus Suri, merchants, willingly set this up in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-15800245.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Dolichenus by two merchants, made in fulfillment of a vow, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-15800250.text_edition: \n Nemesi Reginae Caius Spedius Valerianus decurio collegii fabrum pro salute sua suorumque omnium votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-15800250.translation: To Nemesis Regina, Gaius Spedius Valerianus, a decurion of the guild of builders, released his vow willingly and deservedly for his own safety and that of all his family.
EDCS-15800250.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by a decurion of a builders' guild to Nemesis Regina for his and his family's safety, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-15800251.text_edition: \n Sarapi Augusto sacrum Tiberius Iulius Flaccinus legatus Augusti pro praetore\n
EDCS-15800251.translation: Sacred to Sarapis Augustus, Tiberius Julius Flaccinus, imperial legate pro praetore.
EDCS-15800251.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Sarapis Augustus by an imperial legate, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-15800253.text_edition: \n Sarapi et Isidi Lucius Iunius Rufinus Proculianus tribunus lati clavius militum legionis XIII geminae\n
EDCS-15800253.translation: To Sarapis and Isis, Lucius Junius Rufinus Proculianus, tribune of the broad-striped soldiers of the XIII Gemina legion.
EDCS-15800253.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Sarapis and Isis by a military tribune of the XIII Gemina legion, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-15800254.text_edition: \n Sarapidi Iovi Soli Isidi Lunae Dianae dis deabusque conservatoribus Lucius Aemilius Carus legatus Augusti pro praetore III Daciarum\n
EDCS-15800254.translation: To Sarapis, the Sun God, Isis, the Moon Goddess, Diana, and all the gods and goddesses who preserve, Lucius Aemilius Carus, imperial legate pro praetore of III Daciarum.
EDCS-15800254.summary: This extensive dedication to various deities, including Sarapis, Isis, and Diana, was made by an imperial legate, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-15800255.text_edition: \n Silvano merita libens posuit Titus Annius Tertius\n
EDCS-15800255.translation: To Silvanus, Titus Annius Tertius willingly set this up for his merits.
EDCS-15800255.summary: This is a personal dedication to the god Silvanus by Titus Annius Tertius, likely a private votive offering from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-15800256.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico Marcus Lucilius Philoctemon IIvir coloniae Aureliae Apulensis voto libens posuit\n
EDCS-15800256.translation: Marcus Lucilius Philoctemon, aedile of the colony of Apulensis, willingly dedicated this to Silvanus Domesticus.
EDCS-15800256.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Silvanus Domesticus by Marcus Lucilius Philoctemon, an official in the colony of Apulensis.

EDCS-15800257.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico Lucius A Lucianus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-15800257.translation: Lucius A. Lucianus willingly and deservedly paid his vow to Silvanus Domesticus.
EDCS-15800257.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow to Silvanus Domesticus by Lucius A. Lucianus.

EDCS-15800258.text_edition: \n Soli Invicto Mithrae Caius Nummius Amandus qui et templum\n
EDCS-15800258.translation: Caius Nummius Amandus, who also [built?] the temple to Sol Invictus Mithras.
EDCS-15800258.summary: This inscription records a dedication or the construction of a temple to Sol Invictus Mithras by Caius Nummius Amandus.

EDCS-15800259.text_edition: \n Invicto Mythrae Sextius Syntrofus votum solvit libens\n
EDCS-15800259.translation: Sextius Syntrofus willingly paid his vow to Invictus Mithras.
EDCS-15800259.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow to Invictus Mithras by Sextius Syntrofus.

EDCS-15800260.translation: Lucanus paid his vows to Deus Mithras.
EDCS-15800260.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of vows to Deus Mithras by Lucanus.

EDCS-15800264.text_edition: \n LLO TIANI ILVM Uplius Vitalis |(centurio) legionis III Italicae voto libens posuit\n
EDCS-15800264.translation: Uplius Vitalis, a centurion of the III Italian Legion, willingly dedicated this.
EDCS-15800264.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Uplius Vitalis, a centurion of the III Italian Legion.

EDCS-15800265.text_edition: \n Aurelius Mucianus ex voto strator consularis posuit\n
EDCS-15800265.translation: Aurelius Mucianus dedicated this in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-15800265.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by Aurelius Mucianus, who served as a consular train-bearer.

EDCS-15800266.text_edition: \n D decurio equitum singularium ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-15800266.translation: A decurion of the select horsemen dedicated this in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-15800266.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by an unnamed decurion of the select horsemen.

EDCS-15800267.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Caius Aelius Primus decurio coloniae\n
EDCS-15800267.translation: Caius Aelius Primus, a decurion of the colony, [dedicated this?] to Jupiter Optimus Maximus.
EDCS-15800267.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Caius Aelius Primus, a decurion of the colony.

EDCS-15800268.translation: Aurelius Tzolotus dedicated this in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-15800268.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by Aurelius Tzolotus.

EDCS-15800269.summary: This is a votive inscription by Arrius Hermes, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15800270.text_edition: \n Deo Commag(eno) sacrum per Antiochum sacerdos sic loci\n
EDCS-15800270.translation: Sacred to the god Commagenus, by Antiochus, priest of this place.
EDCS-15800270.summary: This is a dedication to the god Commagenus by his priest Antiochus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15800271.text_edition: \n optio legionis XIII geminae Antoninianae pater et \n
EDCS-15800271.translation: The optimate of the thirteenth Gemina Antoninian Legion, father, and...
EDCS-15800271.summary: This inscription names an optimate of the XIII Gemina Antoninian Legion and seems to be part of a larger text, possibly recording familial relationships or a dedication.

EDCS-15800272.text_edition: \n TINUS I-- et Marcus Antonius Sabinus decurio coloniae votum solverunt\n
EDCS-15800272.translation: Tinus I-- and Marcus Antonius Sabinus, decurion of the colony, fulfilled their vow.
EDCS-15800272.summary: This is a votive inscription by Tinus I-- and Marcus Antonius Sabinus, a decurion of a colony, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15800275.text_edition: \n qquaestionarii Longius Orestus Iulius Victorinus qqaestionarii Ulpius Valentinus Iulius Seneca qqaestionarii Aelius Caesianus Aelius Valerius \n
EDCS-15800275.translation: The questionarii: Longius Orestus, Iulius Victorinus; the questionarii: Ulpius Valentinus, Iulius Seneca; the questionarii: Aelius Caesianus, Aelius Valerius.
EDCS-15800275.summary: This inscription lists several individuals identified as 'questionarii', likely officials or tax collectors, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15800276.text_edition: \n Familiaricum a solo Prosmoni ex suo fecerunt per Aurelium Statium et Ulpium Paulum quaestores\n
EDCS-15800276.translation: They made the Familiaricum from the ground for himself, through Aurelius Statius and Ulpius Paulus, quaestors.
EDCS-15800276.summary: This inscription records the construction of a building or facility (Familiaricum) by unnamed individuals, overseen by quaestors Aurelius Statius and Ulpius Paulus.

EDCS-15800277.text_edition: \n Marcus Licinnius sic Moesicus et Marcus Licinius Euangelus fratres\n
EDCS-15800277.translation: Marcus Licinius Moesicus and Marcus Licinius Euangelus, brothers.
EDCS-15800277.summary: This inscription mentions two brothers, Marcus Licinius Moesicus and Marcus Licinius Euangelus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15800466.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus P-VLSTI V SVA VLI ius Iilino IMP\n
EDCS-15800466.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. P. Ulstius V. Sva. Vlius Iulius Iilino, Emperor.
EDCS-15800466.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the 'Dis Manibus' (Spirits of the Departed), mentioning P. Ulstius, V. Sva. Vlius Iulius Iilino, and possibly an emperor, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15800469.text_edition: \n Publius Aelius Athenodorus vixit nnos III diebus XL\n
EDCS-15800469.translation: Publius Aelius Athenodorus lived 3 years and 40 days.
EDCS-15800469.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the brief life of Publius Aelius Athenodorus, who died at the age of 3 years and 40 days, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15800470.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelius Crescens Ulpio Lupo filio carissimo vixit annis III mensibus VI bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-15800470.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aelius Crescens dedicated this to his dearest son Ulpius Lupus, who lived 3 years and 6 months, a deserving man.
EDCS-15800470.summary: This is a funerary inscription by Aelius Crescens for his son Ulpius Lupus, who died at the age of 3 years and 6 months, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15800471.translation: To Publius Aelius Fortunatus, his wife Aelia.
EDCS-15800471.summary: This is a funerary dedication from Aelia to her husband, Publius Aelius Fortunatus.

EDCS-15800472.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelio Geniali ex singulari vixit annis XXXII vexillarius Valerius librarius primus heres et Atticus secundus heres bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-15800472.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Aelius Genialis, a standard-bearer, lived 32 years. Valerius, a clerk of the first rank, and Atticus, a clerk of the second rank, his heirs, erected this for him who deserved well.
EDCS-15800472.summary: This is a tombstone for Aelius Genialis, a standard-bearer who died at age 32, set up by his heirs Valerius and Atticus.

EDCS-15800473.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aelia, daughter of Publius.

EDCS-15800474.text_edition: \n Aelius Niger veteranus nnorum LXX cohortis I Alpinorum equitatae Saturninae sic Saturnini filia coniugi pientissimo posuit et sibi vivae\n
EDCS-15800474.translation: To her most devoted husband, Aelius Niger, a veteran of the I Alpinorum Equitata Cohort, aged 70. His daughter Saturnina, daughter of Saturninus, set this up for him and for herself while alive.
EDCS-15800474.summary: This is a funerary monument for Aelius Niger, a veteran, set up by his daughter Saturnina, who also dedicated it for herself.

EDCS-15800475.text_edition: \n Publius Aelius Proclus veteranus legionis XIII geminae vixit nnis LV et Aelia Claudia vixit annis XL Publius Aelius Proclinus et Aelia Procla parentibus pientissimis posuerunt\n
EDCS-15800475.translation: Publius Aelius Proclus, a veteran of the Legio XIII Gemina, lived 55 years, and Aelia Claudia lived 40 years. Publius Aelius Proclinus and Aelia Procla erected this for their most devoted parents.
EDCS-15800475.summary: This is a funerary monument for Publius Aelius Proclus and Aelia Claudia, set up by their children Publius Aelius Proclinus and Aelia Procla.

EDCS-15800476.text_edition: \n Publio Aelio Publi filio Papiria Silvano IIvirali et sacerdotali coloniae Apulensis equiti Romano egregiae memoriae viro Fabia Lucilla egregiae memoriae viri filia mater collegiorum fabrum et centonariorum coloniae supra scriptae socero sui amantissimo\n
EDCS-15800476.translation: To Publius Aelius Silvanus, son of Publius, who held the office of IIvir and priesthood in the colony of Apulensis, a Roman knight of excellent memory. Fabia Lucilla, daughter of a man of excellent memory, mother of the guilds of builders and weavers of the aforesaid colony, his most loving father-in-law.
EDCS-15800476.summary: This inscription honors Publius Aelius Silvanus, a Roman knight and local magistrate, set up by his mother-in-law Fabia Lucilla.

EDCS-15800477.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publius Aelius Valerianus patronus et decurio collegii fabrum vixit annos LX deccuriones et principales collegii supra scriptiae re conlato secundum decretum ipsorum faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-15800477.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Publius Aelius Valerianus, patron and decurion of the guild of builders, lived 60 years. The decurions and officials of the aforesaid college arranged for this to be made in common, according to their decree.
EDCS-15800477.summary: This funerary inscription for Publius Aelius Valerianus, a patron and decurion of the builders' guild, was erected by his colleagues.

EDCS-15800478.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publius Aelius Vetustinus miles legionis XIII geminae stipendiorum V vixit annis \n
EDCS-15800478.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Publius Aelius Vetustinus, soldier of the Legio XIII Gemina, served 5 years, lived [incomplete].
EDCS-15800478.summary: This is an incomplete funerary inscription for Publius Aelius Vetustinus, a soldier of the Legio XIII Gemina.

EDCS-15800480.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus quinque hic annorum aetatis conditur infans Aemilius Hermes hanc generavit matris de nomine dixit Plotiam patris praenomine Aemiliam vixit rapuit quam mors inlimine vitae\n
EDCS-15800480.translation: Here is laid to rest a child of five years. Aemilius Hermes begot him; he was named from his mother's name, Plotia, and his father's praenomen, Aemilia. Death snatched him away at the threshold of life.
EDCS-15800480.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a five-year-old child named Aemilius Hermes, who was the son of Plotia and Aemilia.

EDCS-15800481.text_edition: \n Aemilia Hermias liberta et heres patron- bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-15800481.translation: Aemilia Hermias, freedwoman and heir, made this for her deserving patron.
EDCS-15800481.summary: This is a funerary monument for a patron, set up by his freedwoman and heir, Aemilia Hermias.

EDCS-15800482.translation: To the Divine Shades of Caius Aesernius.
EDCS-15800482.summary: A funerary dedication to the divine shades of Caius Aesernius, likely from a tombstone.

EDCS-15800483.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus - Annius Firmus vixit annis L Domitia Iusta coniux et filii bene merenti posuerunt hic situs est\n
EDCS-15800483.translation: To the Divine Shades. Annius Firmus lived for 50 years. Domitia Iusta, his wife, and his sons placed this for him, who deserved well. He is buried here.
EDCS-15800483.summary: A funerary inscription for Annius Firmus, set up by his wife and sons, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15800484.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Antonius Marci filius Geminianus miles cohortis Ubiorum vixit annos XXXX militavit annis XVIII\n
EDCS-15800484.translation: To the Divine Shades. Antonius Geminianus, son of Marcus, a soldier of the Ubian cohort, lived for 40 years and served for 18 years.
EDCS-15800484.summary: A funerary inscription for Antonius Geminianus, a soldier of the Ubii, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15800485.text_edition: \n Caio Antonio Cai filio Papiria Valentino quondam decurioni coloniae Apulensis Caius Antonius Agrippinus a militiis decurio coloniae Napocensis et municipii Potaissensis filius et Gaii Antonii Marcellus Agrippinus decuriones coloniae Apulensis Marcella et Agrippina nepotes eius\n
EDCS-15800485.translation: To Gaius Antonius Valentinus, son of Gaius, of the Papirian tribe, formerly a decurion of the colony of Apula. Gaius Antonius Agrippinus, decurion of the colony of Napoca and the municipium of Potaissa, his son, and honored with military service, and Gaii Antonii Marcellus Agrippinus, decurions of the colony of Apula, his grandsons.
EDCS-15800485.summary: An inscription honoring Gaius Antonius Valentinus, detailing his family lineage and civic offices in several Roman colonies, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15800486.text_edition: \n vixit annis LX Atilius pupillus cum BEL T \n
EDCS-15800486.translation: Lived for 60 years. Atilius Pupillus with [---]
EDCS-15800486.summary: A fragment of a funerary inscription recording the lifespan of Atilius Pupillus, with partial information about the dedicator, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15800487.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius Atius At C<e=F>l<e=F>ia veteranus vixit annos LXXXI C \n
EDCS-15800487.translation: To the Divine Shades. Lucius Atius Celsus, a veteran, lived for 81 years. [---]
EDCS-15800487.summary: A funerary inscription for Lucius Atius Celsus, a veteran, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15800488.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Atrius Cai filius Crustumina Crescens Carsulis miles legionis XIII geminae vixit annis XXXV Valentius Demetrius merenti posuit\n
EDCS-15800488.translation: To the Divine Shades. Gaius Atrius Crescens, son of Gaius, of the Crustuminian tribe, a soldier of the 13th Gemina legion, lived for 35 years. Valentius Demetrius placed this for him, who deserved well.
EDCS-15800488.summary: A funerary inscription for Gaius Atrius Crescens, a soldier in the 13th Legion, set up by Valentius Demetrius, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15800492.text_edition: \n Aureliae Apolloniae Titus Aelius Lupus eques Romanus pontifex et IIviralis coloniae Apulensis matri carissimae\n
EDCS-15800492.translation: To Aurelia Apollonia, his most cherished mother, by Titus Aelius Lupus, a Roman equestrian, pontifex, and former duovir of the colony of Apula.
EDCS-15800492.summary: A dedication to Aurelia Apollonia by her son, Titus Aelius Lupus, who held high civic and religious offices in the colony of Apula, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15800493.text_edition: \n Aureliae Placentinae vixit annis LX Titus Aurelius Stephanus libertus patronae bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-15800493.translation: To Aurelia Placentina. Lived for 60 years. Titus Aurelius Stephanus, her freedman, placed this for his patroness, who deserved well.
EDCS-15800493.summary: A funerary inscription for Aurelia Placentina, set up by her freedman, Titus Aurelius Stephanus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15800494.text_edition: \n Aureliae Priscillae coniugi pietate et castitate incomparabili Aurelius Sabinus primipilaris\n
EDCS-15800494.translation: To Aurelia Priscilla, his wife, of incomparable piety and chastity, by Aurelius Sabinus, a primipilaris.
EDCS-15800494.summary: A funerary dedication to Aurelia Priscilla by her husband, Aurelius Sabinus, a high-ranking centurion, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15800498.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cassiae Saturninae vixit annis L Cassia Ponticilla secundum voluntatem testamenti patronae bene meritae fecit arbitratu Publicii Ianuarii coniugis et Publicii Severi\n
EDCS-15800498.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Cassia Saturnina lived 50 years. Cassia Ponticilla, in accordance with the will of her late patroness who deserved well, erected this, with the approval of her husband Publicius Ianuarius and Publicius Severus.
EDCS-15800498.summary: A tombstone for Cassia Saturnina, erected by Cassia Ponticilla, likely a freedwoman, and approved by others, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-15800499.text_edition: \n Lucius Caventius Clemens legionis XIII geminae Severianae vixit annis V Lucius Caventius Apollinaris heres posuit\n
EDCS-15800499.translation: Lucius Caventius Clemens of the XIII Geminae Severianae legion lived 5 years. Lucius Caventius Apollinaris, his heir, set this up.
EDCS-15800499.summary: A tombstone for Lucius Caventius Clemens, a child who died at age 5, erected by his heir Lucius Caventius Apollinaris, likely from the Flavian or Antonine period.

EDCS-15800501.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Censorio Gai filio Sereno Flavia Solva |(centurioni) legionis XIIII geminae Fuscius Successus et Censorius Fortunatus heredes faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-15800501.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Gaius Censorins Serenus, son of Gaius, centurion of the XIV Geminae legion. Fuscius Successus and Censorinus Fortunatus, his heirs, took care to have this made.
EDCS-15800501.summary: A tombstone for Gaius Censorins Serenus, a centurion of the XIV Geminae legion, erected by his heirs, likely from the 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-15800503.text_edition: \n Claudiae Marcianae Ulpii Domitianus et Philetus patronae optimae\n
EDCS-15800503.translation: For Claudia Marciana, from Ulpius Domitianus and Philetus, her excellent patrons.
EDCS-15800503.summary: A dedication to Claudia Marciana by her patrons Ulpius Domitianus and Philetus, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-15800505.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Claudianae Candidae vixit annos XXX Aelius Firminus coniugi bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-15800505.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Claudiana Candida lived 30 years. Aelius Firminus erected this for his deserving wife.
EDCS-15800505.summary: A tombstone for Claudiana Candida, erected by her husband Aelius Firminus, likely from the 2nd century AD.

EDCS-15800506.text_edition: \n Cocceiae Valeriae Publius Tenacius Gemellinus Augustalis coloniae Apulensis ex testamento Publi Tenaci Vindicis |(centurionis) legionis Primigeniae XXII loco dato decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-15800506.translation: For Cocceia Valeria. Publius Tenacius Gemellinus Augustalis of the colony of Apulis, by the will of Publius Tenacius Vindex, centurion of the XXII Primigeniae legion, with land granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-15800506.summary: A dedication to Cocceia Valeria, funded by the will of Publius Tenacius Vindex, a centurion, with a plot of land allocated by local council decree, likely from the late 1st or early 2nd century AD.

EDCS-15800507.text_edition: \n Crescenti Platoris filio vixit annis XXXV Vibius Rufinus signifer legionis XIII geminae heres faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-15800507.translation: For Crescens, son of Plator, lived 35 years. Vibius Rufinus, standard-bearer of the XIII Geminae legion, his heir, took care to have this made.
EDCS-15800507.summary: A tombstone for Crescens, a standard-bearer in the XIII Geminae legion, erected by his heir Vibius Rufinus, likely from the 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-15900477.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Valerius Cai filius domo Claudia Viruni Silvanus veteranus legionis XIII geminae vixit annos LXX Titus Flavius Valerius Valens miles legionis XIII geminae patri piissimo posuit\n
EDCS-15900477.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Gaius Valerius Silvanus, son of Gaius Valerius, from Claudia in Virunum, a veteran of the XIII Geminae legion, lived 70 years. Titus Flavius Valerius Valens, a soldier of the XIII Geminae legion, erected this for his most dutiful father.
EDCS-15900477.summary: A tombstone for Gaius Valerius Silvanus, a veteran of the XIII Geminae legion, erected by his son Titus Flavius Valerius Valens, a soldier of the same legion, likely from the late 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-15900479.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcus Mociuncius Valentinus librarius legati legionis XIII geminae vixit annis XXVIIII posuit Valerius Constans veteranus legionis XIII geminae et Floria Ingenua mater filio bene merenti\n
EDCS-15900479.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Marcus Mociuncius Valentinus, scribe of the legate of the XIII Geminae legion, lived 29 years. Valerius Constans, a veteran of the XIII Geminae legion, and his mother Floria Ingenua, erected this for their deserving son.
EDCS-15900479.summary: A tombstone for Marcus Mociuncius Valentinus, a scribe serving a legate of the XIII Geminae legion, erected by his father (a veteran) and mother, likely from the late 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-15900480.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Mucasenus Censorini aeques sic ex singulari consularis vixit annis XX Rescuturme Soiae coniux pientissima posuit bene merenti\n
EDCS-15900480.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Mucasenus, son of Censorinus, an equestrian soldier who served as a special assistant to a consular governor, lived 20 years. Rescuturme Soiae, his most devoted wife, erected this for her deserving husband.
EDCS-15900480.summary: A tombstone for Mucasenus, an equestrian who served as a special assistant to a consular governor, erected by his wife, likely from the late 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-15900481.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Sentio Cai filio Sulpicia Flacco Antiquaria veterano legionis XIIII geminae Martiae victricis decurioni coloniae Dacicae Sarmizegetusae vixit annos LXXV Caius Sentius Flaccinus filius et heres faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-15900481.translation: To the departed spirits. To Gaius Sentius Flaccus, son of Gaius Sentius, veteran of the 14th Gemina Martia Victrix legion, decurion of the Dacian colony of Sarmizegetusa, who lived 75 years. Gaius Sentius Flaccinus, his son and heir, had this made.
EDCS-15900481.summary: A funerary inscription for Gaius Sentius Flaccus, a veteran and decurion, commissioned by his son and heir, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-15900482.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tutor Silvani eques alae Bosporanorum ex numero Illyricorum stipendiorum XXIII vixit nnos XLIII hic situs est\n
EDCS-15900482.translation: To the departed spirits. To Tutor Silvanus, equestrian of the Bosporan ala, from the Illyrian cohort, with 23 years of service, who lived 43 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-15900482.summary: A funerary inscription for Tutor Silvanus, an equestrian soldier with 23 years of service, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-15900483.text_edition: \n Tito Varenio Titi filio Pappiria Sabiniano equiti Romano flamini Laurentino item flamini coloniae Sarmizegetusae decurioni coloniarum Sarmizegetusae et Apulensis egregiae memoriae viro omnibus equestribus militiis perfuncto Cornelia Lucilla coniux pila extructa sarcofagum in quo Varenia Probina quondam Sabiniani soror condita erat etiam eius corpore conlocato superposuit\n
EDCS-15900483.translation: Cornelia Lucilla, his wife, placed on top of the built tomb and sarcophagus, in which Varenia Probina, formerly sister of Sabinianus, had been placed, also her body, for Titus Varenus Sabinianus, son of Titus, of the Papiria tribe, Roman equestrian, flamen of Laurentum, also flamen of the colony of Sarmizegetusa, decurion of the colonies of Sarmizegetusa and Apulensis, a man of distinguished memory, who had performed all equestrian military duties.
EDCS-15900483.summary: A funerary inscription honoring Titus Varenus Sabinianus, a prominent equestrian and civic official, detailing his wife's extensive funerary arrangements and family connections, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-15900485.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Sextus Viaeus Sergia Victor Iader eques legionis XIII geminae Sex(tus) Ingenuvius IVR \n
EDCS-15900485.translation: To the departed spirits. To Sextus Viaeus Victor, of the Sergia tribe, from Iader, equestrian of the 13th Gemina legion. Sextus Ingenuvius [erected this].
EDCS-15900485.summary: A funerary inscription for Sextus Viaeus Victor, an equestrian of the 13th Gemina legion, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-15900486.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Ulpio Marci filio Flavino optioni signiferoum legionis XIII geminae stipendiorum XXV vixit annis Titia Maximilla coniugi piissimo faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-15900486.translation: To the departed spirits. To Marcus Ulpius Flavius, son of Marcus, optio of the signiferi (standard bearers) of the 13th Gemina legion, with 25 years of service, who lived [X] years. Titia Maximilla had this made for her most devoted husband.
EDCS-15900486.summary: A funerary inscription for Marcus Ulpius Flavius, an optio and signifer of the 13th Gemina legion with 25 years of service, commissioned by his wife, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-15900487.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ulpiae Constantinae quae vixit annis XVI mensibus VIIII Ulpius Congonius veteranus ex decurione et Aelia Valeria parentes infelicissimi filiae pientissimae\n
EDCS-15900487.translation: To the departed spirits. For Ulpia Constantina, who lived 16 years and 9 months. Ulpius Congonius, veteran and former decurion, and Aelia Valeria, her most unhappy parents, for their most dutiful daughter.
EDCS-15900487.summary: A funerary inscription for Ulpia Constantina, who died at age 16, erected by her veteran father and mother, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-15900488.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Urbicius Condunus miles legionis XIII geminae stipendiorum IX vixit annis XXX Urbicia Ingenua vixit annis XX Urbicius Senilis vixit annos X posuit Urbicius Spectatus filiis bene merentibus\n
EDCS-15900488.translation: To the departed spirits. For Gaius Urbicius Condunus, soldier of the 13th Gemina legion, with 9 years of service, who lived 30 years. Urbicia Ingenua lived 20 years. Urbicius Senilis lived 10 years. Urbicius Spectatus placed this for his deserving sons.
EDCS-15900488.summary: A funerary inscription commemorating three individuals, Gaius Urbicius Condunus (a soldier), Urbicia Ingenua, and Urbicius Senilis, erected by Urbicius Spectatus for his sons, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-15900489.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tito Fabio Ibliomaro domo Augusta Treverorum quondam decurioni kanabarum vixit annis LX Fabii Pulcher Romana Aquileiensis per tutores suos posuerunt\n
EDCS-15900489.translation: To the departed spirits. For Titus Fabius Ibliomarus, from Augusta Treverorum, formerly decurion of the canabae, who lived 60 years. Fabii Pulcher of Aquileia, through their guardians, placed this.
EDCS-15900489.summary: A funerary inscription for Titus Fabius Ibliomarus, a former decurion of the canabae, erected by his relatives from Aquileia, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-15900490.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucio Rustio Finito decurioni coloniae vixit annos L Rustia Censorina \n
EDCS-15900490.translation: To the departed spirits. For Lucius Rustius Finitus, decurion of the colony, who lived 50 years. Rustia Censorina [had this made].
EDCS-15900490.summary: A funerary inscription for Lucius Rustius Finitus, a decurion of a colony, erected by Rustia Censorina, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-15900491.text_edition: \n Verzoviae Saturninae equitis Romani filiae Caius Nummius Certus eques Romanus augur coloniae Apulensis patronus collegiorum fabrum et dendrophorum coloniae supra scriptae suadente adfectione matri posuit\n
EDCS-15900491.translation: Gaius Nummius Certus, Roman equestrian, augur of the colony of Apulensis, patron of the guilds of smiths and dendrophori (tree-bearers) of the aforementioned colony, moved by affection, placed this for Verzovia Saturnina, daughter of a Roman equestrian.
EDCS-15900491.summary: An honorary inscription dedicated by Gaius Nummius Certus, an equestrian and civic official, to Verzovia Saturnina, daughter of a Roman equestrian, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-15900492.text_edition: \n Sisiata Vaussi filia Claudia Savaria vixit annis XXXV Iestinius Super coniugi bene merenti hic sita est\n
EDCS-15900492.translation: Sisiata, daughter of Vaussius, known as Claudia Savaria, lived 35 years. Iestinius Severus placed here his deserving wife.
EDCS-15900492.summary: This is a tombstone for Claudia Savaria, erected by her husband Iestinius Severus, who records her age at death.

EDCS-15900493.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Isidorae domo Asiae vixit nnis XVIII mensibus III diebus XIII Primus Augusti despensatoris vikarius sic bene merenti\n
EDCS-15900493.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Isidora, from Asia, lived 18 years, 3 months, and 13 days. Primus, freedman and vicarius of the Augustal prefect, [erected this] for his deserving wife.
EDCS-15900493.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Isidora, from Asia, detailing her lifespan, and was set up by her husband Primus.

EDCS-15900494.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Sertoriae Feliclae domi Aequo vixsit sic annos LX posuit Marcus Vaternius Primigenius bene merenti matri\n
EDCS-15900494.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Sertoria Felicula lived 60 years. Marcus Vaternius Primigenius placed this for his deserving mother.
EDCS-15900494.summary: This tombstone records the death of Sertoria Felicula at age 60 and was erected by her son Marcus Vaternius Primigenius.

EDCS-15900495.summary: This inscription simply records the name of a man, Domitius Luxatianus, likely on a tomb or monument.

EDCS-15900496.text_edition: \n Luci Ianuari Romuli vixit nnis VIIII et Ianuariae Pupae vixit nnis XIII Ianuaria Titia mater filiis posuit\n
EDCS-15900496.translation: Lucius Ianuarius Romulus lived 9 years and Ianuaria Pupa lived 13 years. Their mother Ianuaria Titia placed this for her children.
EDCS-15900496.summary: This funerary monument was dedicated by Ianuaria Titia for her two deceased children, Lucius Ianuarius Romulus and Ianuaria Pupa, recording their ages.

EDCS-15900497.text_edition: \n Ingenuus Gai vixit annis LXXV Gaius et Apollodorus posuerunt patri carissimo\n
EDCS-15900497.translation: Ingenuus lived 75 years. Gaius and Apollodorus placed this for their dearest father.
EDCS-15900497.summary: This inscription is from Gaius and Apollodorus, who dedicated a monument to their father Ingenuus, noting his age at death.

EDCS-15900498.translation: Iulius Censorinus arranged for this to be made for his father.
EDCS-15900498.summary: This inscription states that Iulius Censorinus commissioned a monument for his father.

EDCS-15900499.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulia Valeria vixit annis XXX Iulia Iunilla libertae etnon coniugi bene merenti hic siti s(unt)\n
EDCS-15900499.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Iulia Valeria lived 30 years. Iulia Iunilla placed [this] for her deserving freedwoman and wife. [They] are placed here.
EDCS-15900499.summary: This funerary inscription is for Iulia Valeria, who lived 30 years, and was erected by Iulia Iunilla, her freedwoman and wife.

EDCS-15900501.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Memmio Hel vixit annos XLV Iunia Beronice coniugi bene merenti\n
EDCS-15900501.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Memmius Hel lived 45 years. Iunia Beronice for her deserving husband.
EDCS-15900501.summary: This funerary epitaph records that Memmius Hel lived 45 years and was set up by his wife Iunia Beronice.

EDCS-15900503.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Seccia Cupiti filia vixit annos LX filii et coniux bene merenti posuerunt hic sita est\n
EDCS-15900503.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Seccia Cupiti's daughter lived 60 years. Her sons and husband placed [this] for her deserving self. She is placed here.
EDCS-15900503.summary: This tombstone commemorates Seccia Cupiti, who lived 60 years, and was erected by her sons and husband.

EDCS-15900506.text_edition: \n Tuticiae Adrastillae vixit nnos XVIII menses II dies XX Tuticia Victoria filia et heres matri karissimae\n
EDCS-15900506.translation: To Tuticia Adrastilla, who lived 18 years, 2 months, and 20 days. Tuticia Victoria, daughter and heir, to her most beloved mother.
EDCS-15900506.summary: This is a tombstone for Tuticia Adrastilla, erected by her daughter and heir, Tuticia Victoria, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15900507.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Vellecii filia Ursina vixit annos XII dies \n
EDCS-15900507.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Ursina, daughter of Vellecius, lived 12 years and [some] days.
EDCS-15900507.summary: This is a tombstone for Ursina, daughter of Vellecius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15900508.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Victorinus lived [some] years.
EDCS-15900508.summary: This is a tombstone for Victorinus, likely from the Roman Imperial period, recording an incomplete lifespan.

EDCS-15900509.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Viria Publia Virii Publii filia vixit annis XXI Virius\n
EDCS-15900509.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Viria Publia, daughter of Virius Publius, lived 21 years. Virius [erected this].
EDCS-15900509.summary: This is a tombstone for Viria Publia, daughter of Virius Publius, likely erected by a relative named Virius, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15900510.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ulpia Flava vixit annis LX Herennia Lucia et Herennia Secundina matri pientissimae posuerunt\n
EDCS-15900510.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Ulpia Flava lived 60 years. Herennia Lucia and Herennia Secundina placed this for their most devoted mother.
EDCS-15900510.summary: This tombstone for Ulpia Flava was erected by her daughters, Herennia Lucia and Herennia Secundina, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15900511.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ulpiae Renatae vixit nnis XXXV Aurelius Mac\n
EDCS-15900511.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Ulpia Renata lived 35 years. Aurelius Mac [erected this].
EDCS-15900511.summary: This tombstone for Ulpia Renata was erected by Aurelius Mac, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15900512.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ulpia Rustica vixit annis XXV IPXIITC \n
EDCS-15900512.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Ulpia Rustica lived 25 years. [Text is illegible].
EDCS-15900512.summary: This tombstone for Ulpia Rustica records her age at death as 25 years, with additional illegible text, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15900513.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ursulus vixit annis XXIIII Lupula fratri bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-15900513.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Ursulus lived 24 years. Lupula placed this for her deserving brother.
EDCS-15900513.summary: This tombstone for Ursulus was erected by his sister Lupula, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15900516.text_edition: \n Publio Tenacio Publi filio Papiria Vindici |(centurioni) legionis XXII Primigeniae buleutae civitatis Nicopolitanorum Publius Tenacius Gemellinus Augustalis coloniae Apulensis libertus testamentum ponendum curavit locus datus decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-15900516.translation: To Publius Tenacius, son of Publius, of the Papirian tribe, Vindicius, centurion of the 22nd Legion Primigenia, council member of the city of Nicopolis. Publius Tenacius Gemellinus Augustalis, freedman of the colony of Apula, took care to have this placed by testament; the site was granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-15900516.summary: This honorary inscription commemorates Publius Tenacius Vindicius, a centurion and local official, with his burial arrangements overseen by his freedman, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-15900517.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Fabricius Iucundus vixit annis L Fabricius Iucundus heres et Fabricii Silvanus et Licinianus fratres cum collegio fabrorum titulum posuerunt fretri pientissimo\n
EDCS-15900517.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Fabricius Iucundus lived 50 years. His heir Fabricius Iucundus, and his brothers Fabricius Silvanus and Licinianus, along with the college of smiths, placed this monument for their most devoted brother.
EDCS-15900517.summary: This tombstone for Fabricius Iucundus was erected by his heir and brothers, with the support of the smiths' guild, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15900518.text_edition: \n Dasanti Scenobarbi equiti alae Batavorum ex numero singularium stipendiorum Vannorum XXXI Bersius Ingenuus decurio ex numero eodem herres sic posuit\n
EDCS-15900518.translation: To Dasantius Scaenobarbus, an equestrian officer of the Batavorum cavalry, from the select cohort, after 31 years of service. Bersius Ingenuus, a decurion from the same cohort, his comrade, erected this.
EDCS-15900518.summary: This is a funerary monument dedicated to Dasantius Scaenobarbus, a cavalry officer, by his comrade Bersius Ingenuus.

EDCS-15900519.text_edition: \n Caio Cervonio Papiria Sabino quinquennali coloniae Dacicae decurioni municipii Apulensis patrono collegii fabrum coloniae et municipii supra scriptorum patrono causarum piissimo amico rarissimo Sextus Sentinas Maximus anno primo facti municipii posuit ob cuius statuae dedicationem Lucia Iulia uxor Cervonii per omnes balneas populo publice oleum posuit loco dato decurionum decreto\n
EDCS-15900519.translation: To Gaius Cervonius Sabinus, of the Papiria tribe, quinquennial magistrate of the Dacian colony, decurion of the Apulian municipality, patron of the guilds of craftsmen of the aforementioned colony and municipality, patron in legal matters, most devoted friend, rarest of men. Sextus Sentinas Maximus, in the first year of the municipality's establishment, erected this. Because of the dedication of his statue, Lucia Iulia, Cervonius's wife, publicly provided oil for the people in all the baths, on a site granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-15900519.summary: This is a funerary monument and record of a statue dedication for Gaius Cervonius Sabinus, a prominent local official and patron, erected by Sextus Sentinas Maximus, with his wife Lucia Iulia providing a public oil distribution.

EDCS-15900520.text_edition: \n Marco Firmio Bassiano et Marco Firmio Severo vixerunt \n
EDCS-15900520.translation: Marcus Firmus Bassianus and Marcus Firmus Severus lived.
EDCS-15900520.summary: This is a brief funerary inscription recording the names of Marcus Firmus Bassianus and Marcus Firmus Severus, likely a memorial.

EDCS-15900521.text_edition: \n D(is) Manibus M(arcus) Horatius Marci filius \n
EDCS-15900521.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Marcus Horatius, son of Marcus.
EDCS-15900521.summary: This is a simple funerary dedication to Marcus Horatius, identified by his filiation.

EDCS-15900522.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Mammulo vixit annis XXV Ulpio Clono vixit mense I Ulpius Iuvenalis Mammulo annorum XII Mammulo piissimo\n
EDCS-15900522.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Mammulus lived 25 years. Ulpius Clono lived 1 month. Ulpius Iuvenalis [erected this] for his most devoted Mammulus, aged 12 years.
EDCS-15900522.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Mammulus, detailing his age at death and the ages of Ulpius Clono and Ulpius Iuvenalis, who is identified as the dedicator of Mammulus's tomb.

EDCS-15900523.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Diofanis.
EDCS-15900523.summary: This is a very brief funerary inscription dedicating the monument to Diofanis.

EDCS-15900526.summary: This is a funerary inscription stating that a freedman and Flavius Silvanus jointly erected the monument.

EDCS-15900529.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulia Serena vixit annos LXXXI Gaius Bellicius Quintianus amitae piissimae fecit\n
EDCS-15900529.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Iulia Serena lived 81 years. Gaius Bellicius Quintianus made this for his most devoted aunt.
EDCS-15900529.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Iulia Serena, erected by her nephew Gaius Bellicius Quintianus.

EDCS-15900531.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcianus Avitianus vixerunt annos V posuit l Restuta nepotibus\n
EDCS-15900531.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Marcianus Avitianus lived 5 years. Restuta erected [this] for her grandsons.
EDCS-15900531.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Marcianus Avitianus, erected by his grandmother Restuta.

EDCS-15900533.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Mucatra Brasi miles numeri Palmyrenorum Tibiscensium vixit annis XXXVIIII Mucapor Mucatralis heres contubernali carissimo posuit\n
EDCS-15900533.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Mucatra Brasi, a soldier of the Palmyrene cohort stationed at Tibiscum, lived 39 years. Mucapor Mucatralis, his heir, erected this for his dearest tent-mate.
EDCS-15900533.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Mucatra Brasi, a soldier in a Palmyrene cohort at Tibiscum, erected by his heir and tent-mate Mucapor Mucatralis.

EDCS-15900539.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Secundinius Eutyches vixit annos XX-- Vibia Annia coniugi bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-15900539.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Gaius Secundinius Eutyches lived 25 years. Vibia Annia placed this for her deserving husband.
EDCS-15900539.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by Vibia Annia for her husband, Gaius Secundinius Eutyches, who died at the age of 25.

EDCS-15900540.translation: Severus, the heir, saw to its erection.
EDCS-15900540.summary: A brief statement indicating that the heir, Severus, arranged for the monument to be set up, likely for a relative.

EDCS-15900544.text_edition: \n LII vixit annos LV hic situs est Valeria Verecunda coniux et filii pientissimi posuerunt\n
EDCS-15900544.translation: He lived 55 years. Valeria Verecunda, his wife, and her most devoted sons placed him here.
EDCS-15900544.summary: A funerary epitaph for a man who lived 55 years, erected by his wife and sons.

EDCS-15900545.text_edition: \n ius Vindex Fabricio Apollinari liberto G adstante e liberto\n
EDCS-15900545.translation: Vindex, a freedman, for Fabricius Apollinaris, his freedman, with another freedman standing by.
EDCS-15900545.summary: This inscription seems to record a legal or administrative act involving freedmen and their patron, Fabricius Apollinaris.

EDCS-15900546.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ulpiae Maxsimillae posuit Artorius Victor coniugi bene merenti vixit annis XXV \n
EDCS-15900546.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Artorius Victor placed this for his deserving wife, Ulpia Maxsimilla, who lived 25 years.
EDCS-15900546.summary: A funerary epitaph erected by Artorius Victor for his wife, Ulpia Maxsimilla, who died at the age of 25.

EDCS-15900547.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ulpiae Victorinae omni obsequio maritali Caius Iulius Iulianus a militiis maritus et Iulia Ursula filia\n
EDCS-15900547.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Gaius Iulius Iulianus, who held military offices, and his wife, and Iulia Ursula, his daughter.
EDCS-15900547.summary: A funerary dedication to Ulpia Victorina, likely by her husband Gaius Iulius Iulianus and daughter Iulia Ursula, emphasizing marital devotion.

EDCS-15900556.summary: An inscription likely identifying the rank and legion of an individual, possibly part of a larger monument or inscription.

EDCS-15900570.summary: A fragment of an inscription recording a daughter setting up a monument for someone, possibly her parent.

EDCS-15900572.text_edition: \n libertus Qua et coniux et pia filia \n
EDCS-15900572.translation: His freedman, who was also his wife and devoted daughter.
EDCS-15900572.summary: This inscription describes a person identified as both a freedman and a devoted wife and daughter, likely honoring their deceased partner.

EDCS-15900576.text_edition: \n vixit annis X Proc E IO \n
EDCS-15900576.summary: A very brief funerary inscription recording the age of death for an individual who lived 10 years.

EDCS-15900587.summary: This is a funerary inscription likely marking a grave or tomb for M. Claponicus, a Roman citizen from Ulp who was associated with Sarmizegetusa.

EDCS-16000076.text_edition: \n Caio Iulio Cai filio Theveste Corinthiano praefecto cohortis VII Gallorum tribuno cohortis I Brittannicae item vexillationis Dacorum Parthicae cui ob virtutem suam sacratissimi imperatores coronam muralem hastam puram et vexilum argento insignem dederunt praefecto alae Campagonum idem |(miliariae) vixit annis XXXVIIII Marcius Arrianus et Iulii Clinias et Pisonianus heredes faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-16000076.translation: To Gaius Julius Theveste Corinthianus, son of Gaius, prefect of the VII Cohort of Gauls, tribune of the I Cohort of Britons, and also of the vexillation of Dacians and Parthians, to whom, for his bravery, the most sacred emperors gave a mural crown, a spear without ribbon, and a silver-adorned standard; prefect of the ala of the Campagones. He lived for 39 years. Marcius Arrianus and the heirs of Julius Clinias and Pisonianus took care to have this made.
EDCS-16000076.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Julius Theveste Corinthianus, a high-ranking Roman military officer who served in various prestigious units and was honored by emperors, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-16000081.summary: This is a simple funerary inscription identifying Marcus Ulpius Secundus, a Roman citizen.

EDCS-16000109.summary: This inscription, possibly found in a Roman context, names the Greek wind gods Zephyrus (West Wind) and Eurus (East Wind).

EDCS-16000110.text_edition: \n Dis Paternis Tiberius Claudius Crispinus pro salute sua suorumque ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-16000110.translation: Tiberius Claudius Crispinus placed this votive offering to the Chthonic Gods for the health of himself and his family.
EDCS-16000110.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated by Tiberius Claudius Crispinus to the 'Dis Paterni' (Chthonic Gods) for his own well-being and that of his family.

EDCS-16000111.text_edition: \n Diis Patriis Lucius Valerius Aristio pro salute sua et suorum voto libens posuit\n
EDCS-16000111.translation: Lucius Valerius Aristio willingly placed this votive offering to the Chthonic Gods for the health of himself and his family, according to his vow.
EDCS-16000111.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated by Lucius Valerius Aristio to the 'Diis Patriis' (Chthonic Gods) for his own well-being and that of his family, expressing his willingness and joy in fulfilling his vow.

EDCS-16000121.text_edition: \n Invicto Deo pro salute Cai Iuli Rufini liberorumque eius Spatalus servus actor votum solvit libens laetus merito\n
EDCS-16000121.translation: Spatalus, the steward, fulfilled his vow willingly, joyfully, and deservedly to the Unconquered God for the health of Gaius Julius Rufinus and his children.
EDCS-16000121.summary: This is a votive inscription by Spatalus, a freedman or slave, to an 'Invictus Deus' (Unconquered God) for the health of his master Gaius Julius Rufinus and his children.

EDCS-16000122.summary: This inscription identifies Publius Aelius Valerianus, a 'speculator', likely a soldier or a messenger, possibly on a tombstone or military marker.

EDCS-16000123.summary: This inscription likely records the names of two individuals, Maximius and Aelius Gaius, possibly in a funerary context.

EDCS-16000497.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico Aurelius Titus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-16000497.translation: Aurelius Titus willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Silvanus Domesticus.
EDCS-16000497.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated by Aurelius Titus to Silvanus Domesticus, the god of boundaries and domestic spirits, for the fulfillment of a vow.

EDCS-16000499.text_edition: \n Libero Patri et Liberae Conservatoribus Aelius Superi sic augur municipii Septimi Potaissensium voto libens posuit\n
EDCS-16000499.translation: To Liber Pater and Libera, the Protectors, Aelius Super, augur of the municipium of Septimia Potaissa, willingly set this up according to his vow.
EDCS-16000499.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Liber Pater and Libera by Aelius Super, an augur, around the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-16100179.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription mentioning a procurator Augusti, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-16100180.summary: This is a short votive inscription dedicated to Aphrodisia, possibly from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-16100188.text_edition: \n Marius Aurelianus princeps numeri porticum deum stravit\n
EDCS-16100188.translation: Marius Aurelianus, princeps of the numerical unit, paved the portico of the gods.
EDCS-16100188.summary: This inscription records the paving of a portico by Marius Aurelianus, a military officer, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-16100253.text_edition: \n Silvano Silvestri posuit Fortunatus Theupropi votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-16100253.translation: Fortunatus Theupropi willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Silvanus Silvestris.
EDCS-16100253.summary: This is a votive inscription by Fortunatus Theupropi to Silvanus Silvestris, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-16100254.text_edition: \n Libero Patri Marcus Val<e=I>ririus Victor decurio coloniae votum solvit libens merito \n
EDCS-16100254.translation: Marcus Valerius Victor, decurion of the colony, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Liber Pater.
EDCS-16100254.summary: This is a votive inscription by Marcus Valerius Victor, a decurion of a colony, to Liber Pater, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-16100322.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelia Prisosta vixit nnos XXXIII Aurelia Bassina filia vixit nnos VII Aurelius Denzi vixit nnos LXXX Aurelius Bassus signifer numeri M O coniugi et filiis bene merentibus posuit\n
EDCS-16100322.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Aelia Prisosta lived 33 years. Aurelia Bassina, daughter, lived 7 years. Aurelius Bassus, standard-bearer of a numerical unit, set this up for his wife and children who deserved well.
EDCS-16100322.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by Aurelius Bassus for his wife and children, with inscriptions detailing their ages and his military rank, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-16100743.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Quinto Canio Quinti filio Claudiae Celeiae Restituto veterano legionis XIII decurioni coloniae Sarmizegetusae vixit annos LXXX Canius Respectus et Cania Atticilla filii item Ulpia Admata coniunx posuerunt bene merenti\n
