EDCS-16100743.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Quintus Canius, son of Quintus. To Restitutus, veteran of the XIII Legion, decurion of the colony of Sarmizegetusa, he lived 80 years. Canius Respectus and Cania Atticilla, his children, and also Ulpia Admata, his wife, set this up for him, who deserved well.
EDCS-16100743.summary: This is a funerary monument for Quintus Canius, set up by his children and wife, detailing his military service as a veteran and decurion, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-16100853.text_edition: \n Aesculapio et Hygiae Marcus Antonius Saturninus decurio coloniae votum solvit libens merito Aesculapio et Hygiae Marcus Antonius Saturninus decurio coloniae votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-16100853.translation: To Aesculapius and Hygieia, Marcus Antonius Saturninus, decurion of the colony, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow. To Aesculapius and Hygieia, Marcus Antonius Saturninus, decurion of the colony, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-16100853.summary: This is a duplicate votive inscription by Marcus Antonius Saturninus, a decurion, to Aesculapius and Hygieia, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-16100854.text_edition: \n Dianae Marcus Antonius Saturninus decurio coloniae votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-16100854.translation: To Diana, Marcus Antonius Saturninus, decurion of the colony, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-16100854.summary: This is a votive inscription by Marcus Antonius Saturninus, a decurion, to Diana, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-16100855.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Marcus Antonius Saturninus decurio coloniae votum solvit libens merito Iovi Optimo Maximo Marcus Antonius Saturninus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-16100855.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, Marcus Antonius Saturninus, decurion of the colony, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-16100855.summary: This is a votive dedication to Jupiter, Best and Greatest, made by Marcus Antonius Saturninus, a decurion of a colony, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16100857.text_edition: \n Terrae Matri Marcus Antonius Saturninus decurio coloniae votum solvit libens merito Terrae Matri Marcus Antonius Saturninus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-16100857.translation: To Mother Earth, Marcus Antonius Saturninus, decurion of the colony, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-16100857.summary: This is a votive dedication to Mother Earth, made by Marcus Antonius Saturninus, a decurion of a colony, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16100858.text_edition: \n Deo aeterno Zmaragdus Augusti libertus tabularius et Aurelia Urbica coniux et Matrona filia votum solverunt libentes merito\n
EDCS-16100858.translation: To the eternal god, Zmaragdus, freedman of the emperor, a clerk, and Aurelia Urbica, his wife, and Matrona, his daughter, gladly and deservedly fulfilled their vow.
EDCS-16100858.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Zmaragdus, an imperial freedman and clerk, along with his wife Aurelia Urbica and daughter Matrona, dedicated to an eternal god, probably from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16100860.text_edition: \n Dianae sacrum pro salute Antoniae Rufae Victorinus servus\n
EDCS-16100860.translation: Sacred to Diana, for the health of Antonia Rufa, by Victorinus, a slave.
EDCS-16100860.summary: This is a dedication to the goddess Diana, made by a slave named Victorinus for the health of Antonia Rufa, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16100861.text_edition: \n Domino et Dominae Satrius Felix beneficiarius procuratoris pro se et suos sic votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-16100861.translation: To the Lord and Lady, Satrius Felix, a beneficiary of the procurator, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow for himself and his family.
EDCS-16100861.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Satrius Felix, a beneficiary of a procurator (financial official), for himself and his family, likely dedicated to imperial deities, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16100863.text_edition: \n Fortunae Augustae Publi Aelii Victorinus Victor Fabianus Victorinus pater cum filiis\n
EDCS-16100863.translation: To Fortuna Augusta, Publius Aelius Victorinus, Victor Fabianus, and Victorinus the father with his sons.
EDCS-16100863.summary: This dedication to Fortuna Augusta was made by Publius Aelius Victorinus, Victor Fabianus, and Victorinus the father with his sons, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16100864.text_edition: \n Herculi Invicto sacrum Aelius Theseus aurarius libens laetus posuit\n
EDCS-16100864.translation: Sacred to Hercules the Invincible, Aelius Theseus, a goldsmith, gladly and joyfully dedicated this.
EDCS-16100864.summary: This is a dedication to Hercules the Invincible, made by Aelius Theseus, a goldsmith, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16100865.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo [[]] [[]] [[]] []R ductum ma domo IIviri et ordo Ampelensium Caio Aurelio Salviano procuratore Augusti nostri curante\n
EDCS-16100865.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus... by the IIviri and the council of the people of Ampelus, under the supervision of Gaius Aurelius Salvianus, our emperor's procurator.
EDCS-16100865.summary: This dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus was made by the local magistrates and council of Ampelus, overseen by the imperial procurator Gaius Aurelius Salvianus, suggesting a provincial context from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16100866.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Marcus Aurelius Alexander miles numeri Maurorum Hispanorum Antoniniani ex iussu dei fecit\n
EDCS-16100866.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Marcus Aurelius Alexander, a soldier of the cohort of Spanish Moors Antoniniana, made this by order of the god.
EDCS-16100866.summary: This dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus was made by a soldier named Marcus Aurelius Alexander of the Spanish Moors cohort, acting on divine command, likely from the Antonine period.

EDCS-16100867.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Titus Aurelius Diocles beneficiarius procuratoris votum solvit libens Imperatoribus Augustis consulibus\n
EDCS-16100867.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Titus Aurelius Diocles, a beneficiary of the procurator, gladly fulfilled his vow, when the Emperors Augustus were consuls.
EDCS-16100867.summary: This votive inscription to Jupiter Optimus Maximus was made by Titus Aurelius Diocles, a beneficiary of a procurator, and is dated by the consulship of the emperors, placing it in the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16100868.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo sacrum Iunius Nedymus\n
EDCS-16100868.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Best and Greatest. Iunius Nedymus.
EDCS-16100868.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Iunius Nedymus.

EDCS-16100869.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Neptunalis Augusti libertus tabularius aurariarum Dacicarum votum bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-16100869.translation: Neptunalis, freedman of Augustus, a clerk in charge of Dacian mines, set up this votive offering to Jupiter Best and Greatest, deserving of good.
EDCS-16100869.summary: This is a votive dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Neptunalis, a freedman and clerk of Dacian mines.

EDCS-16100870.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Caius Semppronius Urbanus procurator Augusti\n
EDCS-16100870.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest. Caius Semppronius Urbanus, procurator of Augustus.
EDCS-16100870.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Caius Semppronius Urbanus, procurator of Augustus.

EDCS-16100871.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo pro salute Imperatoris Caesaris Titi Aeli Hadriani Antonini Augusti Pii patris patriae Marcus Statius Priscus legatus eius pro praetore\n
EDCS-16100871.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, for the safety of the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of the country, Marcus Statius Priscus, his legate pro praetore.
EDCS-16100871.summary: This records a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Marcus Statius Priscus, a legate, for the health of Emperor Antoninus Pius.

EDCS-16100872.text_edition: \n Inferis Dis Manibus MMORTM AVG RPI MS Mercurius Caesaris n(ostri) servus RIRMIRO\n
EDCS-16100872.translation: To the Infernal Gods, Manes. For the deified Emperor. Mercurius, servant of our Caesar, placed this.
EDCS-16100872.summary: This is an offering to the Infernal Gods and Manes, placed by Mercurius, a servant of the emperor.

EDCS-16100873.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo aeterno conservatori Callistus Augusti nostri dispensator pro salute sua et suorum ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-16100873.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, the eternal preserver. Callistus, steward of our Augustus, set up this votive offering for his own safety and that of his family.
EDCS-16100873.summary: Callistus, a steward of the emperor, made this votive offering to Jupiter Optimus Maximus for his and his family's well-being.

EDCS-16100874.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Commagenorum Aeterno Marinus Mariani Bassus sacerdos Iovis Optimi Maximi Dolicheni pro salute sua suorumque omnium votum VH\n
EDCS-16100874.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, the Eternal One of the Commageni. Marinus Mariani Bassus, priest of Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus, made a vow for the safety of himself and all his family.
EDCS-16100874.summary: This records a vow made by Marinus Mariani Bassus, priest of Jupiter Dolichenus, for himself and his family.

EDCS-16100875.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno et deo Commagenorum Aurelius Marinus et Adde Barsemei et Oceanus Socratis sacerdotes votum libentes posuerunt\n
EDCS-16100875.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest Dolichenus and to the god of the Commageni. Aurelius Marinus, Adde Barsemei, and Oceanus Socratis, priests, gladly set up this votive offering.
EDCS-16100875.summary: Priests Aurelius Marinus, Adde Barsemei, and Oceanus Socratis set up this votive offering to Jupiter Dolichenus and the god of the Commageni.

EDCS-16100876.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest Dolichenus. Modestius.
EDCS-16100876.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus by Modestius.

EDCS-16100877.text_edition: \n Libero Patri et Liberae Herculianis et cervabus Romanus Augusti nostri et A<u=Y>relia Chreste votum libenter posuerunt \n
EDCS-16100877.translation: To Liber Pater and Libera Herculiana and the guardian goddesses. Romanus, servant of our Augustus, and Aurelia Chreste gladly set up this votive offering.
EDCS-16100877.summary: Romanus, a servant of the emperor, and Aurelia Chreste made a votive offering to Liber Pater, Libera, and local guardian goddesses.

EDCS-16100878.text_edition: \n Nemesi Augustae Vitalis Cai Aponi votum solvit\n
EDCS-16100878.translation: Vitalis Cai Aponi fulfilled his vow to the Augustan Nemesis.
EDCS-16100878.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow to the goddess Nemesis by Vitalis Cai Aponi.

EDCS-16100879.text_edition: \n Silvano sacrum Leonas Augusti libertus adiutor tabularii pro salute sua suorumque omnium ex voto libens posuit\n
EDCS-16100879.translation: Leonas, freedman of Augustus and assistant in the record office, willingly set up this sacred offering to Silvanus for his own health and that of all his family, in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-16100879.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the god Silvanus by Leonas, freedman of Augustus and an official, for his health and that of his family.

EDCS-16100880.text_edition: \n Deo Silv<an=MI>o Domestico Senti Aper et Philumenus votum libentes posuerunt \n
EDCS-16100880.translation: Senti Aper and Philumenus willingly set up this offering to the Household God Silvanus.
EDCS-16100880.summary: This inscription records the dedication of an offering to the god Silvanus by Senti Aper and Philumenus.

EDCS-16100882.text_edition: \n Anniae Lucillae Augustae Imperatoris Veri Augusti Armeniaci Parthici maximi liberti et familia et leguli aurariarum\n
EDCS-16100882.translation: The freedmen and household, and the small gold refiners, of Annia Lucilla, freedwoman of the Emperor, of the deified Verus Augustus Armenicus Parthicus Maximus.
EDCS-16100882.summary: This inscription lists the freedmen, household, and workers of a gold refinery belonging to Annia Lucilla, a freedwoman of Emperor Verus.

EDCS-16100884.text_edition: \n Pro salute Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Aureli Antonini Pii Felicis patris patriae eius legatus Augusti pro praetore ETLBD\n
EDCS-16100884.translation: For the health of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix, father of the country, his legate with the status of Augusti pro praetore.
EDCS-16100884.summary: This inscription records the good wishes for the health of Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus by his legate.

EDCS-16100885.text_edition: \n I(ovi)? O(ptimo) Maximo Lucius Macrius Macer procurator Auggustorum\n
EDCS-16100885.translation: Lucius Macrius Macer, procurator of the Augusti, [dedicated this] to Jupiter Optimus Maximus.
EDCS-16100885.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Lucius Macrius Macer, a procurator of the emperors.

EDCS-16100886.text_edition: \n Memoriae Papirii Rufi FA PINAPQ procuratoris aurariarum liberti et heredes patrono pientissimo fecerunt et sibi\n
EDCS-16100886.translation: For the memory of Papirius Rufus, freedman and freedmen of the gold refiners, his patron most devoted, his freedmen and heirs made this for him and for themselves.
EDCS-16100886.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a memorial by the freedmen and heirs of Papirius Rufus, a patron and freedman of gold refiners.

EDCS-16100887.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Ulpio Augusti liberto Hermiae procuratori aurariarum cuius reliquiae ex indulgentia Augusti nostri Romam latae sunt Salonia Palestrice coniunx et Diogenes libertus bene merenti fecerunt vixit nnos LV\n
EDCS-16100887.translation: To the spirits of the departed: Salonia Palestrice, his wife, and Diogenes, his freedman, made this for Marcus Ulpius Hermias, freedman of Augustus and procurator of the gold refineries, who deserved well, his remains having been brought to Rome by the indulgence of our Augustus; he lived 55 years.
EDCS-16100887.summary: This funerary inscription honors Marcus Ulpius Hermias, freedman of Augustus and a procurator of gold refineries, erected by his wife and freedman, noting his remains were brought to Rome.

EDCS-16100888.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ulpiae Marci libertae Trophimae coniugi sanctissimae Neptunalis Augusti nostri libertus\n
EDCS-16100888.translation: To the spirits of the departed: Neptunalis, freedman of our Augustus, erected this for his most chaste wife, Ulpia Trophima, freedwoman of Marcus.
EDCS-16100888.summary: This funerary inscription honors Ulpia Trophima, freedwoman of Marcus, erected by her husband Neptunalis, a freedman of the emperor.

EDCS-16100889.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iustinus Caesaris nostri verna subsequens librariorum vixit annis XV Tertius verna valdenio pientissimo bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-16100889.translation: To the spirits of the departed: Tertius, a most devoted attendant of the scribes, erected this for Iustinus, attendant of our Caesar, who deserved well; he lived 15 years.
EDCS-16100889.summary: This funerary inscription records the memorial erected by Tertius for Iustinus, a young attendant in the imperial administration, who died at age 15.

EDCS-16100890.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Sossia Sabina vixit nnos XXVI et fecit in matrimonio nnos XI menses X sine ulla querella Fuscinus verna ab instrumentis tabularii coniugi bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-16100890.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Sossia Sabina lived for 26 years and was married for 11 years and 10 months without any complaint. Fuscinus, a freedman from the records office, made this for his deserving wife.
EDCS-16100890.summary: This is a tombstone for Sossia Sabina, erected by her freedman husband, Fuscinus, in an unspecified location and time.

EDCS-16100892.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marci Aureli Antonini militis legionis XIII geminae vixit nnis XXII mensibus XI diebus II militavit nnis V librarius Aurelius Marcianus et Valeria Valentina filio pientissimo\n
EDCS-16100892.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, soldier of the Legio XIII Gemina, lived for 22 years, 11 months, and 2 days; he served for 5 years as a scribe. Aurelius Marcianus and Valeria Valentina made this for their most devoted son.
EDCS-16100892.summary: This is a tombstone for Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, a soldier and scribe of Legio XIII Gemina, erected by his parents, with an approximate date inferred from the legion's presence.

EDCS-16100893.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publio Helvio Primano militi legionis XIII geminae librario cons(ularis) Publius Helvius Primus pater filio IVNL\n
EDCS-16100893.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Publius Helvius Primanus, soldier of the Legio XIII Gemina and scribe of the consular office. Publius Helvius Primus, his father, made this for his son.
EDCS-16100893.summary: This tombstone records the death of Publius Helvius Primanus, a soldier and scribe of Legio XIII Gemina, erected by his father in an unspecified location and time.

EDCS-16100895.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcus Valerianus vixit annos LX et mensem I Menonia Gemellina vixit annos LXV Marcus Aurelius Maximinus veteranus legionis XIII geminae vixit annos L Menonnianus bene meritis\n
EDCS-16100895.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Marcus Valerianus lived for 60 years and 1 month. Menonia Gemellina lived for 65 years. Marcus Aurelius Maximinus, veteran of the Legio XIII Gemina, lived for 50 years. Menonnianus made this for his deserving family members.
EDCS-16100895.summary: This tombstone commemorates Marcus Valerianus, Menonia Gemellina, and Marcus Aurelius Maximinus, a veteran of Legio XIII Gemina, erected by Menonnianus, with an approximate date inferred from the legion's presence.

EDCS-16100896.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publius Aelius Iulianus vixit mensibus V diebus XXV Publius Aelius Provincialis decurio coloniae filio posuit\n
EDCS-16100896.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Publius Aelius Iulianus lived for 5 months and 25 days. Publius Aelius Provincialis, a decurion of the colony, placed this for his son.
EDCS-16100896.summary: This tombstone records the death of the infant Publius Aelius Iulianus, erected by his father, a decurion of the colony, in an unspecified location and time.

EDCS-16100897.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Titus Aurelius Afer Delmata princeps adsignato sic ex municipio Splono vixit nnos XXX Aurelius Sattara libertus patrono optimo posuit\n
EDCS-16100897.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Titus Aurelius Afer Delmata, a chief tax assessor from the municipality of Splon, lived for 30 years. Aurelius Sattara, a freedman, placed this for his most excellent patron.
EDCS-16100897.summary: This tombstone records the death of Titus Aurelius Afer Delmata, a tax assessor from Splon, erected by his freedman, with an approximate date inferable from his age and profession.

EDCS-16100898.text_edition: \n Publio Celsenio Constanti decurioni coloniae Delmatiae sic Claudia Aequo item decurioni coloniae Dacicae vixit annos XXX Marcus Opellius Adiutor IIvir coloniae Dacicae heres testamento vivus posuit\n
EDCS-16100898.translation: For Publius Celsenus Constans, a decurion of the colony of Delmata, and for Claudia Aequa, also a decurion of the Dacian colony, who lived for 30 years. Marcus Opellius Adiutor, duovir of the Dacian colony, erected this as heir by will while alive.
EDCS-16100898.summary: This is a double tombstone for Publius Celsenus Constans and Claudia Aequa, both decurions, erected by Marcus Opellius Adiutor, a duovir, with an approximate date inferable from their positions.

EDCS-16100899.text_edition: \n Inferis Dis Manibus Asclepius et Asclepiades cives Bithinum sic vixit annos XXX Affia coniunx bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-16100899.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Asclepius and Asclepiades, citizens of Bithynia, lived for 30 years. Affia, his wife, placed this for her deserving husband.
EDCS-16100899.summary: This tombstone records the death of Asclepius and Asclepiades, Bithynian citizens who lived for 30 years, erected by his wife, Affia, with an approximate date inferable from their age.

EDCS-16100902.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Calaudianus posuit filio Claudio Maximo annorum septem AV S bene merenti\n
EDCS-16100902.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Calaudianus placed this for his son, Claudius Maximus, aged seven years, for his deserving son.
EDCS-16100902.summary: This tombstone records the death of Claudius Maximus, aged seven, erected by his father, Calaudianus, in an unspecified location and time.

EDCS-16100903.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Claudia Tertullina vixit annos IIII Claudia Vera mater \n
EDCS-16100903.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Claudia Tertullina lived for 4 years. Claudia Vera, her mother, [made this].
EDCS-16100903.summary: This tombstone records the death of the young child Claudia Tertullina, erected by her mother, Claudia Vera, in an unspecified location and time.

EDCS-16100905.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Opellia Victorina vixit annos X menses III et Marcus Opilius Crispinus vixit annos IIII menses V dies XIII et Marcus Opilius Firminus vixit annos \n
EDCS-16100905.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Opellia Victorina lived 10 years, 3 months, and Marcus Opilius Crispinus lived 4 years, 5 months, 13 days, and Marcus Opilius Firminus lived...
EDCS-16100905.summary: This funerary epitaph records the ages of Opellia Victorina and Marcus Opilius Crispinus, likely children who died young.

EDCS-16100906.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valerius Rufinus vixit nnos L et Opelia Calityche vixit annos LXVIII Valerius Crescens libertus eorum bene merentibus posuit\n
EDCS-16100906.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Valerius Rufinus lived 50 years and Opelia Calityche lived 68 years. Valerius Crescens, their freedman, erected this for the well-deserving.
EDCS-16100906.summary: This funerary inscription records the ages of Valerius Rufinus and Opelia Calityche and was dedicated by their freedman, Valerius Crescens.

EDCS-16100907.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Victorina quae vixit annis plus minus -- posuit pater Herculan(us)\n
EDCS-16100907.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Victorina, who lived approximately -- years, her father Herculanus dedicated this.
EDCS-16100907.summary: This funerary epitaph records the approximate age of Victorina and mentions her father, Herculanus, as the dedicator.

EDCS-16100911.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus IV DVP vixit annos V MP FRNI DASNI VCISE TVSRV Aureli- C\n
EDCS-16100911.translation: To the Divine Spirits. [A name or dedication, illegible] lived 5 years. [Illegible text] Aureli- C.
EDCS-16100911.summary: This is a fragmented funerary epitaph that records the lifespan of a person, possibly a child, with illegible portions.

EDCS-16200025.text_edition: \n Iuliae Augustae matri sanctissimi piissimique Antonini Augusti et castrorum senatusque ac patriae cohors I Brittanica |(miliaria) Antoniniana\n
EDCS-16200025.translation: To Julia Augusta, mother of the most holy and pious Antoninus Augustus, and of the camp, the senate, and the fatherland, the First Cohort of Britons, known as the Antonine Cohort, (miliaria).
EDCS-16200025.summary: This inscription honors Julia Augusta, mother of Emperor Antoninus Pius, and is dedicated by the First Cohort of Britons.

EDCS-16300047.translation: The Thirteenth Double Legion of Annaeus Saturninus.
EDCS-16300047.summary: This inscription likely refers to a detachment or commander of the Legio XIII Gemina, associated with Annaeus Saturninus.

EDCS-16300421.text_edition: \n A N ex voto posuit Silvanno sic Libero Patri\n
EDCS-16300421.translation: In accordance with a vow, Silvanno, thus to Liber Pater, A N [name or title] placed this.
EDCS-16300421.summary: This inscription records a votive offering dedicated to Silvanus and Liber Pater by an individual identified by the initials A.N.

EDCS-16400035.translation: Lucretius Aquila of the Thirteenth Double Legion.
EDCS-16400035.summary: This inscription records the name Lucretius Aquila, likely a soldier or officer, associated with the Legio XIII Gemina.

EDCS-16400367.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Fulguratori Publius Aelius Maximianus augur coloniae votum solvit\n
EDCS-16400367.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, the Thunderer, Publius Aelius Maximianus, augur of the colony, fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-16400367.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made to Jupiter Fulgurator by Publius Aelius Maximianus, an augur of the colony.

EDCS-16400369.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Petronius Marcianus votum libens solvit\n
EDCS-16400369.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, Petronius Marcianus willingly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-16400369.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Petronius Marcianus.

EDCS-16700204.text_edition: \n Timocleti Caesaris nostri ab instrumentis vixit annos XXXV Bona Caesaris nostri serva nepoti posuit\n
EDCS-16700204.translation: To Timocletus, servant of our Caesar, who lived 35 years. Bona, servant of our Caesar, placed this for her grandson.
EDCS-16700204.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Timocletus, a servant of Caesar, set up by Bona, another servant of Caesar.

EDCS-16700298.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Marcus Aurelius Ex\n
EDCS-16700298.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus. Marcus Aurelius Ex.
EDCS-16700298.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Marcus Aurelius.

EDCS-18300738.text_edition: \n Legio XIII gemina Aeli Brogitarus IIC\n
EDCS-18300738.translation: The Thirteenth Legion Gemina. Aelius Brogitarus, the centurion.
EDCS-18300738.summary: This inscription records the name of the Thirteenth Legion Gemina and its centurion, Aelius Brogitarus.

EDCS-20400534.text_edition: \n Lucius Flavius Valens ob honorem flamonii Bono Puero dedit\n
EDCS-20400534.translation: Lucius Flavius Valens dedicated this in honor of the priesthood of the Good Boy.
EDCS-20400534.summary: This is a dedication by Lucius Flavius Valens for an honor related to a priesthood.

EDCS-20400536.text_edition: \n Publio Furio Saturnino legato Augusti pro praetore consuli colonia Dacica Sarmizegetusa praesidi dignissimo\n
EDCS-20400536.translation: To Publius Furius Saturninus, legate of the emperor with praetorian authority, consul, governor of the Dacian colony Sarmizegetusa, most worthy protector.
EDCS-20400536.summary: This is an honorific inscription for Publius Furius Saturninus, a high-ranking imperial official.

EDCS-20900025.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Lucius vixit annis X Aurelius Iulianus pater filio posuit\n
EDCS-20900025.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. Aurelius Lucius lived 10 years. Aurelius Iulianus, father, placed this for his son.
EDCS-20900025.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aurelius Lucius, set up by his father Aurelius Iulianus.

EDCS-20900029.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Genio collegii pomarensium Valerius Ianuarius pater et Flavia Crescentina mater pro salute filiorum suorum cultores Iovis Optimi Maximi ex promisso fecerunt Numini aeterno Iovis Optimi Maximi cultores pomarensium Valerius Valerianus I Octavius Cassianus Iulius Silvanus Iulius Dignus Valerius Ianuarius IARSIA \n
EDCS-20900029.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus and the Genius of the college of pomarensium. Valerius Ianuarius, father, and Flavia Crescentina, mother, for the well-being of their sons, worshippers of Jupiter Optimus Maximus, made this according to their vow. To the eternal divine power of Jupiter Optimus Maximus. Worshippers of pomarensium: Valerius Valerianus, I. Octavius Cassianus, Iulius Silvanus, Iulius Dignus, Valerius Ianuarius IARSIA.
EDCS-20900029.summary: This is a votive inscription to Jupiter and the college's genius, dedicated by parents for their sons' well-being, and listing several members of the college.

EDCS-20900032.summary: This is a dedication made by Comitialis for someone named Iuvenius.

EDCS-23500001.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulius Herculanus decurio scholae fabrum iimaginifer vixit nnos LXXX Iulia Vivenia coniux Iulius Marcianus filius immaginifer scholae fabrum vixit nnos XXVII Aurelius Iulius miles cohortis I sagittariorum immaginifer vixit nnos XXX Iulius Marcellinus filius vexillarius scholae fabrum vixit nnos XXV Iulia Marcia filia vixit nnos XIIII Iulia Heraclia filia vixit nnos VIIII Iulia Marcell<i>na nepos vixit nnos IIII Vivenia mater se viva fecit\n
EDCS-23500001.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. Iulius Herculanus, decurion of the school of fabricae, standard-bearer, lived 80 years. Iulia Vivenia, wife. Iulius Marcianus, son, standard-bearer of the school of fabricae, lived 27 years. Aurelius Iulius, soldier of the first cohort of sagittarii, standard-bearer, lived 30 years. Iulius Marcellinus, son, standard-bearer of the school of fabricae, lived 25 years. Iulia Marcia, daughter, lived 14 years. Iulia Heraclia, daughter, lived 9 years. Iulia Marcellina, granddaughter, lived 4 years. Vivenia, mother, made this in her lifetime.
EDCS-23500001.summary: This is a large funerary monument commemorating multiple family members, some of whom held military or guild positions and served as standard-bearers.

EDCS-24100202.text_edition: \n Divo Vero Parthico maximo fratri Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Aureli Antonini Augusti Armeniaci Medici Germanici Parthici maximi tribuniciae potestatis XXVI imperatoris V patris patriae consulis III proconsulis colonia Ulpia Traiana Augusta Dacica Sarmizegetusa\n
EDCS-24100202.translation: To the Divine Verus Parthicus Maximus, brother of Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Armeniacus Medicus Germanicus Parthicus Maximus, with tribunician power for the 26th time, emperor for the 5th time, father of the country, consul for the 3rd time, proconsul. The colony Ulpia Traiana Augusta Dacica Sarmizegetusa.
EDCS-24100202.summary: This is an inscription from the colony of Ulpia Traiana Augusta Dacica Sarmizegetusa honoring the divine Verus, brother of Marcus Aurelius.

EDCS-24400843.text_edition: \n Placidae Reginae equites voto libentes posuerunt per Proculo sic principe et Gaio optione\n
EDCS-24400843.translation: The equites gladly erected this at their vow to Placida Regina, with Proculus as princeps and Gaius as optio.
EDCS-24400843.summary: An inscription recording a dedication by equites to Placida Regina, likely a local deity or personification, dated by the mention of local officials.

EDCS-24400844.text_edition: \n Isidi et Serapi Caius Iulius Antigonus |(centurio) legionis V Macedonicae piae <c=O>(onstantis) et Flavia Apollinaria eius votum libentes solverunt \n
EDCS-24400844.translation: Caius Iulius Antigonus, centurion of the Fifth Macedonian Legion, the Pious and Constant, and Flavia Apollinaria, his wife, gladly fulfilled their vow.
EDCS-24400844.summary: A dedication to Isis and Serapis by a military officer and his wife, fulfilling a vow, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24400845.text_edition: \n Isidi myrionimae sic Caius Iulius Martialis pater et Lucius Livius Victorinus quaestor collegio Isidis dono dederunt\n
EDCS-24400845.translation: Caius Iulius Martialis, father, and Lucius Livius Victorinus, quaestor, presented this gift to Isis of many names on behalf of the college of Isis.
EDCS-24400845.summary: A donation to the multifaceted goddess Isis by a father and a quaestor on behalf of their collegium, probably from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24500527.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-24500527.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Hadriani filius divi Traiani Parthici nepos divi Nervae pronepos Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XIV imperator II consul IV pater patriae equitibus et peditibus qui militaverunt in alis III et cohortibus XII quae appellantur Gallorum et nnoniorum et Gal(lorum) et Pan(noniorum) Siliana et Tungrorum Frontoniana et I Ulpia Brittonum |(milliaria) et I Brittanica |(milliaria) et I Flavia Ulpia Hispanorum |(milliaria) et I Batavorum |(milliaria) civium Romanorum et I Aelia Gaesatorum |(milliaria) et II Augusta Nervia Pacensis Brittonum |(milliaria) et II Britannorum |(milliaria) et I Hispanorum et I Cannanefatium et II Hispanorum scutata Cyrenaica et V Lingonum et VI Thracum et sunt in Dacia Porolissensi sub Macrinio Vindice procuratore quinque et viginti stipendiis emeritis dimissis honesta missione quorum nomina subscripta sunt civitatem Romanam qui eorum non haberent dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singulis ante diem VIII Kalendas Octobres Marco Cominio Secundo Lucio Attidio Corneliano consulibus cohortis I Ulpiae Brittonum |(milliariae) cui praeest Titus Iulius Arrianus Roma ex pedite Prososto Ianuari filio nnio descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aerea quae fixa est Romae in muro post templum divi Augusti ad Minervam Imperator Caesar divi Hadriani filius divi Traiani Parthici nepos divi Nervae pronepos Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XIV imperator II consul IV pater patriae equitibus et peditibus qui militaverunt in alis III et cohortibus XII et sunt in Dacia Porolissensi sub Macrinio Vindice procuratore XXV stipendiis emeritis dimissis honesta missione quorum nomina subscripta sunt civitatem Romanam qui eorum non haberent dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singulis Cohortis I Ulpiae Brittonum |(milliariae) cui praeest Titus Iulius Arrianus Roma ex pedite Prososto Ianuari filio Pannonio Marci Servili Getae Luci Pulli Chresimi Marci Sentili Iasi Tiberi Iuli Felicis Cai Iuli Silvani Luci Pulli Velocis Publi Ocili Prisci\n
EDCS-24500527.translation: Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, son of the deified Hadrian, grandson of the deified Trajan the Parthian, great-grandson of the deified Nerva, Pontifex Maximus, with tribunician power for the 14th time, Imperator for the 2nd time, Consul for the 4th time, Father of the Fatherland, granted Roman citizenship and the right of marriage to the equites and pedites who served in three alae and twelve cohorts (namely the Gallorum et Nnoniorum, Gal(lorum) et Pan(noniorum) Siliana, et Tungrorum Frontoniana, I Ulpia Brittonum milliaria, I Brittanica milliaria, I Flavia Ulpia Hispanorum milliaria, I Batavorum milliaria, the citizens of Rome, and I Aelia Gaesatorum milliaria, II Augusta Nervia Pacensis Brittonum milliaria, II Britannorum milliaria, I Hispanorum, I Cannanefatium, II Hispanorum scutata Cyrenaica, V Lingonum, VI Thracum) serving in Dacia Porolissensis under the procurator Macrinius Vindex, having completed twenty-five years of service and being honourably discharged, whose names are listed below. He granted these privileges to those among them who did not yet have Roman citizenship, along with the right of marriage with the wives they then had when citizenship was granted to them, or with those they would subsequently marry, but to each individual only. This was done before the eighth day before the Kalends of October, when Marcus Cominius Secundus and Lucius Attidius Cornelius were consuls. For the First Cohort of Ulpii Brittones (milliaria), commanded by Titus Iulius Arrianus, from Rome, for the foot-soldier Prosostus, son of Ianuarius, a Pannonian. Recorded and verified from a bronze tablet fixed in Rome on the wall behind the Temple of the deified Augustus, near the Temple of Minerva. Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, son of the deified Hadrian, grandson of the deified Trajan the Parthian, great-grandson of the deified Nerva, Pontifex Maximus, with tribunician power for the 14th time, Imperator for the 2nd time, Consul for the 4th time, Father of the Fatherland, granted Roman citizenship and the right of marriage to the equites and pedites who served in three alae and twelve cohorts and are in Dacia Porolissensis under the procurator Macrinius Vindex, having completed twenty-five years of service and being honourably discharged, whose names are listed below. He granted these privileges to those among them who did not yet have Roman citizenship, along with the right of marriage with the wives they then had when citizenship was granted to them, or with those they would subsequently marry, but to each individual only. For the First Cohort of Ulpii Brittones (milliaria), commanded by Titus Iulius Arrianus, from Rome, for the foot-soldier Prosostus, son of Ianuarius, a Pannonian. Marcus Servilius Geta, Lucius Pullus Chresimus, Marcus Sentilius Iasus, Tiberius Iulius Felix, Caius Iulius Silvanus, Lucius Pullus Velox, Publius Ocilius Priscus.
EDCS-24500527.summary: This extensive rescript by Emperor Antoninus Pius, issued during his 14th tribunician power and 2nd consulship, grants Roman citizenship and marriage rights to veteran auxiliary soldiers from various units serving in Dacia Porolissensis, with specific names of soldiers and officers listed, and records the decree on bronze tablets.

EDCS-24500528.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno pro salute et incolumitate Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Antoni Gordiani Pii Felicis Augusti et cohortis III Campestris Marcus Aurelius Flavus IIIIvir municipii Septimi Porolissensis Marcus Antonius Maximus veteranus et decurio ornatus ornamentis IIIIviralibus municipii supra scripti et Aurelius Flavus decurio municipii vegesimarius sic sacerdotes dei et cohortis supra scriptae templum cum tabernis aere suo fecerunt\n
EDCS-24500528.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus, for the health and safety of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Pius Felix Augustus, and of the Third Campestres Cohort, Marcus Aurelius Flavus, aedile of the municipality of Septimus Porolissensis, Marcus Antonius Maximus, a veteran and decurion decorated with aedileship ornaments of the aforementioned municipality, and Aurelius Flavus, a decurion of the Vege(simarian?) municipality, as priests of the god and of the aforementioned cohort, built this temple with shops at their own expense.
EDCS-24500528.summary: A dedication and construction of a temple with shops to Jupiter Dolichenus by municipal officials and priests, for the welfare of Emperor Gordian III and a military cohort, likely from the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-24500530.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Terentio Liccai filio vixit annos X posuit Licinius fratri bene merenti\n
EDCS-24500530.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Terentius, son of Liccai, lived 10 years. Licinius set this up for his well-deserving brother.
EDCS-24500530.summary: A tombstone inscription for a young boy named Terentius, set up by his brother, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24500532.summary: A simple funerary inscription, likely the name of the deceased and possibly the person dedicating the monument.

EDCS-26600721.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, by designation of Ioncutus and Lonquina.
EDCS-26600721.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, likely indicating the dedicators or those by whose arrangement the offering was made.

EDCS-26600722.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Fulguratori pro salute sua et suorum Marcus Aurelius Decoratus decurio coloniae Aequi flamen aedilis et Marcus Aurelius Philippianus decurio coloniae Aequi quaestor votum solverunt libentes merito Perpetuo et Corneliano consulibus VIII Idus Iunias\n
EDCS-26600722.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Fulgurator, for his own safety and that of his family, Marcus Aurelius Decoratus, decurion of the colony of Aequi, flamen, and aedile, and Marcus Aurelius Philippianus, decurion of the colony of Aequi, quaestor, gladly fulfilled their vow with good reason, on the eighth day before the Ides of June, when Perpetuus and Cornelianus were consuls.
EDCS-26600722.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Fulgurator by two high-ranking municipal officials of the colony of Aequi, offering a votive prayer for their well-being, dated to 198 CE.

EDCS-26600723.text_edition: \n Iunoni Marcus Aurelius Rogatianus pro salute sua et suorum votum libens merito solvit\n
EDCS-26600723.translation: To Juno, Marcus Aurelius Rogatianus gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow for his own safety and that of his family.
EDCS-26600723.summary: A votive inscription to Juno by Marcus Aurelius Rogatianus, a decurion, for his personal well-being.

EDCS-26600724.text_edition: \n Mercurio sacrum Marcus Aurelius Rogatianus pro salute sua et suorum ex viso\n
EDCS-26600724.translation: Sacred to Mercury. Marcus Aurelius Rogatianus, for the health of himself and his family, by vision.
EDCS-26600724.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the god Mercury by Marcus Aurelius Rogatianus, likely an altar or small shrine, dated by the style of the name to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-26600725.text_edition: \n Terrae Matri Marcus Aurelius Rogatianus pro salute sua et suorum votum libens merito solvit\n
EDCS-26600725.translation: To Mother Earth. Marcus Aurelius Rogatianus gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow for the health of himself and his family.
EDCS-26600725.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Marcus Aurelius Rogatianus to Terra Mater (Mother Earth), likely a votive altar or dedication, dated to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-26600726.text_edition: \n Marti Patri conservatori et bonae Victoriae Lucius Aelius Rufinus pro se et suos voto libens posuit\n
EDCS-26600726.translation: To Mars the Father, protector, and to good Victory. Lucius Aelius Rufinus gladly erected this by vow for himself and his family.
EDCS-26600726.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the gods Mars and Victoria by Lucius Aelius Rufinus, likely a shrine or altar, dated to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-26600727.text_edition: \n pro salute dominorum nn[nostrorum] Luci Septimi Severi et Marci Aureli Antonini et [Publi Septimi Getae] Caesaris Augg[gustorum] cultores Iovis dedicatum VIII Kalendas Augustas Muciano et Fabiano consulibus\n
EDCS-26600727.translation: For the health of our lords [emperors] Lucius Septimius Severus, Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, and [Publius Septimius Geta] Augusti, dedicated by the worshippers of Jupiter on the 8th day before the Kalends of August, when Mucianus and Fabius were consuls.
EDCS-26600727.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Jupiter by his worshippers for the health of the emperors Septimius Severus, Caracalla, and Geta, and is precisely dated to July 25, 205 AD.

EDCS-26600728.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Scauriani Caesaris nostri servus villicus sic vixit annos XXIII Fuscianus fratri bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-26600728.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. Scaurianus, our emperor's slave, the farm manager, lived 23 years. Fuscianus erected this for his well-deserving brother.
EDCS-26600728.summary: This tombstone inscription records the death of Fuscianus's brother, who was a slave and farm manager for someone named Scaurianus, likely dated to the imperial period.

EDCS-26600729.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Marcus Romanius Encolpus ex voto\n
EDCS-26600729.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest. Marcus Romanius Encolpus, by vow.
EDCS-26600729.summary: This inscription is a votive dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Marcus Romanius Encolpus, likely a small altar or dedication, dated to the imperial period.

EDCS-26600730.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno ex praecepto numinis Aesculapi somno monitus Veturius Marcianus veteranus legionis XIII geminae pro salute sua suorumque\n
EDCS-26600730.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus, by instruction of the divinity, having been warned in a dream, Veturius Marcianus, a veteran of the XIII Gemina legion, for the health of himself and his family.
EDCS-26600730.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Jupiter Dolichenus by a veteran named Veturius Marcianus, prompted by a divine oracle and dream, for his own and his family's health, likely dated to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-26600733.text_edition: \n Marti Gradivo cohors II Flavia Commagenorum cui praeest Caius Vettius Sabinianus praefectus\n
EDCS-26600733.translation: To Mars Gradivus. The II Flavia Commagenorum cohort, commanded by Gaius Vettius Sabinianus, prefect.
EDCS-26600733.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Mars Gradivus by the II Flavia Commagenorum cohort, commanded by Prefect Gaius Vettius Sabinianus, likely from the Roman army stationed in the provinces, dated to the imperial period.

EDCS-26600734.text_edition: \n Aureliae Ianuariae filiae piissimae quae vixit annis IIII mensibus VII diebus XVI Romanus Augusti libertus procurator ACAD pater \n
EDCS-26600734.translation: To Aurelia Ianuaria, his most dutiful daughter, who lived 4 years, 7 months, and 16 days. Romanus, freedman of Augustus, procurator ACAD, [her] father.
EDCS-26600734.summary: This tombstone inscription records the death of Aurelia Ianuaria at a young age and is dedicated by her father, Romanus, who held a significant procuratorial position, likely dated to the imperial period.

EDCS-26600737.text_edition: \n pro Herennio Gemellino viro egregio procuratore Auggustorum nnostrorum agente vice praesidis marito suo libens solvit\n
EDCS-26600737.translation: For Herennius Gemellinus, an outstanding man, procurator of our emperors Augustus, acting in place of the governor, [she] gladly fulfilled [this] for her husband.
EDCS-26600737.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by a woman for her husband, Herennius Gemellinus, who held the high-ranking position of imperial procurator acting as governor, likely dated to the imperial period.

EDCS-26600742.text_edition: \n Deae Nemesi Aelius Diogenes et Silia Valeria pro salute sua et filiorum suorum mater et pater ex voto a solo templum ex suo fecerunt collegio utriculariorum\n
EDCS-26600742.translation: To the goddess Nemesis, Aelius Diogenes and Silia Valeria, his mother and father, built this temple from the ground up at their own expense, fulfilling a vow, for their own well-being and that of their sons, for the guild of the pouch-makers.
EDCS-26600742.summary: This is a votive dedication to the goddess Nemesis by Aelius Diogenes and Silia Valeria, for themselves and their sons, and records the construction of a temple for the pouch-makers' guild.

EDCS-26600743.text_edition: \n Libero Patri pro salute Marci Turrani Dii et Flaviae Aeliae Nices Marcus Turranius Patroclus ex voto\n
EDCS-26600743.translation: To Liber Pater, Marcus Turranius Patroclus fulfilled his vow for the well-being of Marcus Turranius Dii and Flavia Aelia Nices.
EDCS-26600743.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Marcus Turranius Patroclus to Liber Pater for the health of Marcus Turranius Dii and Flavia Aelia Nices.

EDCS-26600744.text_edition: \n Soli Invicto Numini Mithrae pro salute Publi Aeli Mari Hermadio actor Turrani Dii votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-26600744.translation: For Sol Invictus Mithras, Hermadio, manager for Publius Aelius Marius, willingly and deservedly paid his vow for the well-being of Publius Aelius Marius.
EDCS-26600744.summary: This is a votive inscription to Sol Invictus Mithras, with Hermadio fulfilling a vow for the well-being of his employer, Publius Aelius Marius.

EDCS-26600745.text_edition: \n Corneliae Saloninae Augustae coniugi Gallieni Augusti nostri ordo municipii Tibiscensium devotus numini maiestatique eius\n
EDCS-26600745.translation: To Cornelia Salonia Augusta, wife of our Emperor Gallienus Augustus, the town council of Tibiscensium, devoted to her divinity and majesty.
EDCS-26600745.summary: This inscription dedicates the monument to Cornelia Salonina Augusta, wife of Emperor Gallienus, by the devoted town council of Tibiscensium.

EDCS-26600746.text_edition: \n Quinto Marcio Turboni Frontoni Publicio Severo praefecto praetorio Imperatoris Caesaris Aelii Traiani Hadriani Augusti patris patriae colonia U<lp=II>(ia) Trainana Augusta Dacica Sarmizegetusa \n
EDCS-26600746.translation: To Quintus Marcius Turbon Fronton Publicius Severus, praetorian prefect of the Emperor Caesar Aelius Hadrianus Augustus, father of the country, from the colony Ulpia Traiana Augusta Dacica Sarmizegetusa.
EDCS-26600746.summary: This inscription records the name and titles of Quintus Marcius Turbon Fronton Publicius Severus, praetorian prefect, and the city from which it originates, Ulpia Traiana Augusta Dacica Sarmizegetusa.

EDCS-26600747.text_edition: \n D(is) M(anibus) Publi Aeli Ulpi veterani ex decurione hanc sedem longo placuit sacrare labori hanc requiem fessos tandem qua conderet artus Ulpius emeritis longaevi muneris annis ipse suo curam titulo dedit ipse sepulcri arbiter hospitium membris fatoque paravit\n
EDCS-26600747.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Ulpius himself dedicated this seat for his long and restful labor, this rest where his weary limbs might finally be laid, after years of honorable service; he himself provided the care and oversaw the tomb, and prepared this lodging for his body by fate.
EDCS-26600747.summary: This is a self-funded tomb inscription by Ulpius, a veteran, who meticulously prepared his final resting place and provides a poetic description of his life and death.

EDCS-26600749.text_edition: \n Iarse Caes(aris vixit nnos L posuit Iulia Succcessa coniugi bene merenti\n
EDCS-26600749.translation: Caesar [Augustus?]. Lived 50 years. Julia Successa placed this for her deserving husband.
EDCS-26600749.summary: This is a tombstone recording the death of a man named Iarse, believed to be Caesar, who lived 50 years, and was commemorated by his wife, Julia Successa.

EDCS-26600750.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Iunoni Reginae Minervae Terrae Matri Caius Kaninius Sabinianus D C M IIvir \n
EDCS-26600750.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Juno Regina, Minerva, and Mother Earth, Gaius Kaninius Sabinianus, IIvir, [made this dedication?].
EDCS-26600750.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Jupiter, Juno, Minerva, and Terra Mater by Gaius Kaninius Sabinianus, who held the office of IIvir.

EDCS-26600751.text_edition: \n Aurelius Faustus decurio? municipii Tibiscensium veteranus vixit annos XLVI Septimia Marcia coniux fecit Dis Manibus Iulius Martialis veteranus vixit \n
EDCS-26600751.translation: Aurelius Faustus, a decurion of the municipium of Tibiscensium, a veteran, lived 46 years. Septimia Marcia, his wife, made this for the Spirits of the Departed. Julius Martialis, a veteran, lived [?].
EDCS-26600751.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Aurelius Faustus, a veteran decurion, and likely for Julius Martialis as well, set up by Aurelius Faustus' wife, Septimia Marcia.

EDCS-26600754.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publio Aelio Ariorto IIIIviro annuali municipii Drobetae interfecto a latronibus vixit annos L Ulpia Digna coniugi pientissimo et Publius Aelius filius et Publii Aelii Valens et Audarus nepotes bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-26600754.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Publius Aelius Ariortus, annual IIvir of the municipium of Drobeta, killed by brigands, who lived 50 years. Ulpia Digna, his most devoted wife, and his son Publius Aelius, and his grandsons Publius Aelius Valens and Audarus, placed this for him who deserved well.
EDCS-26600754.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Publius Aelius Ariortus, an annual IIvir of Drobeta killed by brigands, and was erected by his wife, son, and grandsons.

EDCS-26600755.text_edition: \n Diis magnis et bonis Aesculapio et Hygiae Marcus Aurelius Veteranus praefectus legionis XIII geminae Gallienianae votum libens merito posuit\n
EDCS-26600755.translation: To the great and good gods Aesculapius and Hygieia, Marcus Aurelius Veteranus, prefect of the Thirteenth Gemina Legion, Gallieniana, willingly and deservedly set up this vow.
EDCS-26600755.summary: A military officer dedicates an offering to Aesculapius and Hygieia, likely during the reign of Gallienus.

EDCS-26600756.text_edition: \n Aesculapio et Hygiae pro salute Iuniae Cyrillae quod a longa infirmitate virtute aquarum numinis sui revocaverunt Titus B A eius votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-26600756.translation: To Aesculapius and Hygieia, for the health of Iunia Cyrilla, who they recalled from a long illness by the power of their divine waters, Titus B A, her freedman, willingly and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-26600756.summary: A freedman dedicates an offering to Aesculapius and Hygieia for the recovery of his patroness Iunia Cyrilla.

EDCS-26600757.text_edition: \n Dis et Numinibus Aquarum Ulpius Secundinus Marius Valens Pomponius Haemus Iulius Carus Valerius Valens legati Romam ad consulatum Severiani clarissimi viri missi incolumes reversi ex voto e a\n
EDCS-26600757.translation: To the gods and divine spirits of the Waters, Ulpius Secundinus, Marius Valens, Pomponius Haemus, Iulius Carus, and Valerius Valens, envoys sent to Rome for the consulship of Severianus, a most distinguished man, returned safely and fulfilled their vow.
EDCS-26600757.summary: A group of officials dedicated an offering to the spirits of the waters upon their safe return from a diplomatic mission to Rome.

EDCS-26600759.text_edition: \n Herculi pro salute Imperatorum Severi et Antonini fili conservatori Augustorum dominorum nostrorum Claudius Gallus clarissimus vir legatus eorum pro praetore cum suis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-26600759.translation: To Hercules, for the safety of the Emperors Severus and Antoninus, sons of the conservator of the Augusti, our lords, Claudius Gallus, a most distinguished man, their legate pro praetore, along with his retinue, willingly and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-26600759.summary: A governor dedicates an offering to Hercules for the safety of the emperors, likely during the reign of Caracalla and Geta or Severus Alexander.

EDCS-26600760.text_edition: \n Herculi pro salute Imperatoris Marci Aureli Antonini Pii Augusti et Iuliae Domnae Augustae matri Augusti et castrorum sub cura Iuli Paterni procuratis Syntrophus vilicus\n
EDCS-26600760.translation: To Hercules, for the health of Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Augustus, and of Iulia Domna Augusta, mother of the Augustus and of the camp, under the supervision of Syntrophus, procurator, vilicus [set this up].
EDCS-26600760.summary: A procurator dedicates an offering to Hercules for the health of Emperor Caracalla and his mother Iulia Domna.

EDCS-26600761.text_edition: \n Herculi Genio loci fontibus calidis Calpurnius Iulianus vir clarissimus legatus legionis V Macedonicae legatus Augusti pro praetore provinciae Daciae superioris votum libens solvit\n
EDCS-26600761.translation: To Hercules, to the Genius of the place, and to the hot springs, Calpurnius Iulianus, a most distinguished man, legate of the Fifth Macedonian Legion, legate of the Augusti pro praetore of the province of Upper Dacia, willingly paid his vow.
EDCS-26600761.summary: A high-ranking military governor dedicates an offering to Hercules, the local genius, and the hot springs in Upper Dacia.

EDCS-26600762.text_edition: \n Herculi et Veneri Mercurius pr EL N cum suis\n
EDCS-26600762.translation: To Hercules and Venus, Mercurius [set this up] with his retinue.
EDCS-26600762.summary: A dedication to Hercules and Venus by Mercurius and his companions.

EDCS-26600763.text_edition: \n Herculi Augusto sacrum Felix Iuli Saturnini conductoris publici portorii tertiae partis ex privatis stationis Tsiernensis IIII Idibus Decembribus anno XI Barbaro et Regulo consulibus ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-26600763.translation: Sacred to Hercules Augustus. Felix, son of Iulius Saturninus, a public contractor of the customs revenue of the third part from his private stations at Tsiernensis, set this up by vow on the 12th day before the Ides of December in the eleventh year, when Barbarus and Regulus were consuls.
EDCS-26600763.summary: A contractor for public customs revenue dedicates an offering to Hercules Augustus in the year 11 of an unnamed reign, possibly Commodus or Diocletian.

EDCS-26600764.text_edition: \n Herculi invicto Titus Aurelius Geminianus veteranus legionis XIII geminae Antoninianae ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-26600764.translation: To Hercules the Invincible, Titus Aurelius Geminianus, a veteran of the Thirteenth Gemina Antoninianae Legion, set this up by vow.
EDCS-26600764.summary: A veteran of the Thirteenth Gemina Legion dedicates an offering to Hercules the Invincible.

EDCS-26600765.text_edition: \n Herculi invicto Publius Claudius Iulius decurio coloniae Drubetae votum solvit\n
EDCS-26600765.translation: To Hercules the Invincible, Publius Claudius Iulius, decurion of the colony of Drubetae, paid his vow.
EDCS-26600765.summary: A decurion from the colony of Drubetae dedicates an offering to Hercules the Invincible.

EDCS-26600766.text_edition: \n Herculi invicto Lucius Pompeius Celer praefectus cohortis I Ubiorum votum solvit\n
EDCS-26600766.translation: Lucius Pompeius Celer, prefect of the First Cohort of Ubii, fulfilled his vow to the unconquered Hercules.
EDCS-26600766.summary: A military dedication to Hercules by a cohort prefect, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-26600767.text_edition: \n Herculi salutifero Quintus Vibius Amillus Augustalis coloniae Dacicae pro salute Iuliani fili sui\n
EDCS-26600767.translation: Quintus Vibius Amillus, an Augustalis of the colony of Dacia, [dedicated this] to health-giving Hercules for the well-being of his son, Iulianus.
EDCS-26600767.summary: A vow to Hercules by a local official for his son's health, from a Dacian colony.

EDCS-26600768.text_edition: \n Herculi sancto Simonius Iulianus vir clarissimus praeses Daciarum\n
EDCS-26600768.translation: To the holy Hercules, Simonius Iulianus, most distinguished man and governor of Dacia, [dedicated this].
EDCS-26600768.summary: A dedication to Hercules by a Roman governor of Dacia, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-26600769.text_edition: \n Herculi sancto Eutyches actor Publi Aeli Antipatri ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-26600769.translation: Eutyches, steward of Publius Aelius Antipater, set this up to the holy Hercules in accordance with his vow.
EDCS-26600769.summary: A private dedication to Hercules by a freedman or agent.

EDCS-26600770.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Antestius Gaius pro salute sua et suorum votum libens posuit\n
EDCS-26600770.translation: Antestius Gaius willingly set this up as a vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus for his own well-being and that of his family.
EDCS-26600770.summary: A vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by an individual for his family's welfare.

EDCS-26600771.text_edition: \n Deo Sucello pro salute Marci Sedati Severiani legati Augusti\n
EDCS-26600771.translation: To the god Sucellus, for the well-being of Marcus Sedatius Severianus, imperial legate.
EDCS-26600771.summary: A dedication to the god Sucellus by an imperial legate, likely from the Roman provinces.

EDCS-26600772.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari divi Hadriani filio divi Traiani nepoti divi Nervae pronepoti Tito Aelio Hadriano Antonino Augusto Pio pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate XXIII consuli IIII \n
EDCS-26600772.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, son of the deified Hadrian, grandson of the deified Trajan, great-grandson of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 23rd time, consul for the 4th time.
EDCS-26600772.summary: The titulature of Emperor Antoninus Pius, detailing his imperial dignities and ancestry, likely inscribed on a public monument.

EDCS-26600773.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Publio Licinio Gallieno Pio Felici Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate consuli III proconsuli cohors III Delmatarum Valeriana Galliena |(miliaria) eqquitata civium Romanorum pia fidelis devota numini maiestatique eorum sic\n
EDCS-26600773.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Publius Licinius Gallienus Pius Felix Augustus, supreme pontiff, with tribunician power, consul for the 3rd time, proconsul, the Third Cohort of Dalmatians, named Valeriana Galliena, equipped with auxiliaries and citizens of Rome, pious, faithful, devoted to his numen and majesty, [dedicated this inscription thus].
EDCS-26600773.summary: An inscription honoring Emperor Gallienus by a Roman auxiliary cavalry unit, reflecting the mid-3rd century CE period.

EDCS-26600774.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Peditianus nondum plenos annos tres vixit annis duobus mensibus VI diebus XIIII qui et a innocentia parentum suorum sua manu cecidit Aurelius Peditus et Aurelia Donata filio innocenti \n
EDCS-26600774.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aurelius Peditianus lived less than three full years, two years, six months, and fourteen days, and also died by his own hand out of his parents' innocence. Aurelius Peditus and Aurelia Donata [dedicated this] to their innocent son.
EDCS-26600774.summary: A tombstone for a young child who died tragically, set up by his parents.

EDCS-26600775.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucio Iulio Luci filio Sergia Basso decurioni municipii Drobetae quaestori interfecto a latronibus vixit annos XXXX Iulii Iulianus et Bassus patri piissimo et Iulius Valerianus frater mortem eius exsecutus faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-26600775.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Lucius Julius, son of Lucius, of the tribe Sergia, Basso, decurion of the municipality of Drobeta, quaestor, killed by brigands, lived 40 years. Iulius Iulianus and Bassus [set this up] for their most loving father, and Julius Valerianus, his brother, oversaw its construction to avenge his death.
EDCS-26600775.summary: A tombstone for a local official killed by brigands, erected by his sons and brother.

EDCS-26600776.text_edition: \n Paulus decurio coloniae coniugi carissimae posuit\n
EDCS-26600776.translation: Paulus, a decurion of the colony, erected this for his dearest wife.
EDCS-26600776.summary: This epitaph records a tombstone erected by Paulus for his wife, likely in a Roman colony.

EDCS-26600777.text_edition: \n Genio Turmazgadae Aurelius Dionisius sic curator posuit\n
EDCS-26600777.translation: Aurelius Dionisius, as curator, erected this for the Genius of Turmazgada.
EDCS-26600777.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the Genius of Turmazgada by Aurelius Dionisius in his capacity as curator.

EDCS-26600778.text_edition: \n Herculi Augusto sacrum Lucilius Felix et Domitius Herculanus magistri cultorum Herculis donum dederunt\n
EDCS-26600778.translation: Lucilius Felix and Domitius Herculanus, as masters of the cult of Hercules, gave this gift to Augustus Hercules.
EDCS-26600778.summary: This dedication records a gift made by the masters of the cult of Hercules to the deity, likely during the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600780.text_edition: \n Isidi ex voto templum fecit Domitia Varenii Pudentis Vettia et Au\n
EDCS-26600780.translation: Domitia, wife of Varenii Pudentis, and Vettia erected a temple to Isis in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-26600780.summary: This inscription records the construction of a temple to Isis by two women, Domitia and Vettia, fulfilling a vow.

EDCS-26600783.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo ala I Bosporanorum cui praeest Caius Valerius Gracilis praefectus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-26600783.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, the 1st Cohort of Bosporani, commanded by Caius Valerius Gracilis, prefect, fulfilled a vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-26600783.summary: This military dedication records the fulfillment of a vow by a cohort of Bosporani and their prefect to Jupiter Optimus Maximus.

EDCS-26600785.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Marcus Cocce<i=L>us Themo votum solvit libens merito \n
EDCS-26600785.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Marcus Cocceius Themo fulfilled a vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-26600785.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Marcus Cocceius Themo.

EDCS-26600786.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Crispus Luci |(centurio) cohortis II Commagenorum votum solvit\n
EDCS-26600786.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Crispus, a centurion of the 2nd Cohort of Commageni, fulfilled a vow.
EDCS-26600786.summary: This dedication records a veteran, Crispus, fulfilling a vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, likely during the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600789.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Iunoni Reginae pro salute Miciensium Lucius Atilius Faustinus et Marcus Ulpius Romanus magistri votum solverunt libentes merito\n
EDCS-26600789.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus and Juno Regina, for the well-being of the people of Miciensium, Lucius Atilius Faustinus and Marcus Ulpius Romanus, as masters, fulfilled their vows willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-26600789.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of vows by two officials for the well-being of the inhabitants of Miciensium, dedicated to Jupiter and Juno.

EDCS-26600790.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Terrae Daciae et Genio populi Romani et commerci Felix Caesaris nostri servus vilicus stationis Pontis Augusti promotus ex statione Micia ex vicario\n
EDCS-26600790.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, to the Land of Dacia and the Genius of the Roman People and commerce, Felix, slave of our Caesar, vilicus of the station at Pons Augustus, promoted from the station of Micia as vicarius.
EDCS-26600790.summary: This complex dedication acknowledges multiple deities and entities, made by Felix, an imperial freedman and administrator, likely during the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600791.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo veterani et cives Romani per Marcum Ulpium Quintum magistrum\n
EDCS-26600791.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, the veterans and Roman citizens, through Marcus Ulpius Quintus, master, [fulfilled a vow].
EDCS-26600791.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus made by veterans and Roman citizens, organized by Marcus Ulpius Quintus.

EDCS-26600792.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Heliopolitano Quintus Licinius Macrinus |(centurio) legionis IIII Flaviae Felicis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-26600792.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Heliopolitanus, Quintus Licinius Macrinus, centurion of the Legio IIII Flavia Felix, willingly fulfilled his vow, as was deserved.
EDCS-26600792.summary: This is a votive inscription to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Heliopolitanus by Quintus Licinius Macrinus, a centurion, probably dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-26600793.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Heliopolitano <L=I>(ucius) L<i=L>cinius Messalinus |(centurio) legionis XIII geminae \n
EDCS-26600793.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Heliopolitanus, Lucius Licinius Messalinus, centurion of the Legio XIII Gemina.
EDCS-26600793.summary: This is an inscription dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Heliopolitanus by Lucius Licinius Messalinus, a centurion, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-26600794.text_edition: \n Libero Patri sacrum Aelius Sossius decurio cohortis II Flaviae Commagenorum ex voto libens posuit\n
EDCS-26600794.translation: Sacred to Liber Pater. Aelius Sossius, decurion of the Cohors II Flavia Commagenorum, willingly set this up according to his vow.
EDCS-26600794.summary: This is a dedication to Liber Pater by Aelius Sossius, a decurion, likely set up in the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-26600796.summary: This is a dedication to Sol Invictus Mithras by Aurelius, probably dating to the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-26600800.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico Catullus immunis votum solvit libens\n
EDCS-26600800.translation: To Silvanus Domesticus, Catullus, who is exempt from services, willingly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-26600800.summary: This is a votive inscription to Silvanus Domesticus by Catullus, an exempt soldier, likely from the late 1st to 3rd century CE.

EDCS-26600801.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico Publius Aelius Euphorus pro salute Publi Aeli Mari conductoris pascui et salinarum <l=I>(ibens) votum vovit \n
EDCS-26600801.translation: To Silvanus Domesticus, Publius Aelius Euphorus, for the well-being of Publius Aelius Marius, contractor for the pasture and salt works, willingly made a vow.
EDCS-26600801.summary: This is a votive inscription to Silvanus Domesticus by Publius Aelius Euphorus for the health of Publius Aelius Marius, a contractor, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-26600803.text_edition: \n Victoriae Augustae et Genio collegii eiius sic Marcus Cocceius Lucius lapidarius donum dedit\n
EDCS-26600803.translation: To Victory Augusta and the Genius of her college, Marcus Cocceius Lucius, a stonecutter, thus gave this gift.
EDCS-26600803.summary: This inscription records a gift by Marcus Cocceius Lucius, a stonecutter, to Victory Augusta and the college's Genius, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-26600804.text_edition: \n Dianae aeternae sanctissimeis sic Statilia Bassa filia Pergamiani praefecti equitum votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-26600804.translation: To the eternal and most holy Diana, Statilia Bassa, daughter of Pergamianus, prefect of cavalry, willingly fulfilled her vow, as was deserved.
EDCS-26600804.summary: This is a votive inscription to Diana by Statilia Bassa, daughter of a prefect of cavalry, likely dating to the late 1st to 3rd century CE.

EDCS-26600805.summary: This partially preserved inscription records a vow willingly fulfilled by Terentius Augustalis, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-26600806.text_edition: \n N EB Rufinus IIviralis coloniae votum solvit\n
EDCS-26600806.translation: N. EB Rufinus, aedile, of the colony, fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-26600806.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by N. EB Rufinus, an aedile of the colony, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-26600807.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari divi Traiani Parthici filio divi Nervae nepoti Traiano Hadriano Augusto patri patriae consuli III cohors II Flavia Commagenorum\n
EDCS-26600807.translation: To Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrian Augustus, son of the deified Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the deified Nerva, father of the fatherland, consul for the third time, by the Second Flavian Cohort of Commageni.
EDCS-26600807.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Hadrian, likely by the military unit it names, dating to his consulship.

EDCS-26600808.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari divi Antonini filio divi Hadriani nepoti divi Traiani Parthici pronepoti divi Nervae abnepoti Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto Armeniaco tribunicia potestate XVIII consuli III cohors II Flavia Commagenorum\n
EDCS-26600808.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Armenicus, son of the deified Antoninus, grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-great-grandson of the deified Nerva, with tribunician power for the eighteenth time, consul for the third time, by the Second Flavian Cohort of Commageni.
EDCS-26600808.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Marcus Aurelius, likely by the military unit it names, dating to his eighteenth tribunician power and third consulship.

EDCS-26600809.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari divi Antonini filio divi Hadriani nepoti divi Traiani Parthici pronepoti divi Nervae abnepoti Lucio Aurelio <V=N>ero Augusto Armeniaco tribunicia potestate IIII consuli II proconsuli cohors II Flavia Commagenorum \n
EDCS-26600809.translation: To Emperor Caesar Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus Armenicus, son of the deified Antoninus, grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-great-grandson of the deified Nerva, with tribunician power for the fourth time, consul for the second time, proconsul, by the Second Flavian Cohort of Commageni.
EDCS-26600809.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Lucius Aurelius Verus, likely by the military unit it names, dating to his fourth tribunician power and second consulship.

EDCS-26600810.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax Augustus consul balneas cohortis II Flaviae Commagenorum vetustate dilabsas sic restituit sub Polo Terentiano consulari III Daciarum curante Sexto Boebio Scribonio Casto praefecto cohortis\n
EDCS-26600810.translation: Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax Augustus restored the baths of the Second Flavian Cohort of Commageni, which had fallen into disrepair through age, during the third consulship of Polus Terentianus, governor of Dacia, under the supervision of Sextus Boebius Scribonius Castus, prefect of the cohort.
EDCS-26600810.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a bathhouse by Emperor Septimius Severus, detailing the officials involved and the date of the restoration.

EDCS-26600811.text_edition: \n Impperatoribus Lucio Septimio Severo Pio Pertinaci et Marco Aurelio Antonino Auggustis et [Lucio Septimio Getae Caesari] Caius Antonius Crispinus veteranus ex decurione alae Pannoniorum donum dedit\n
EDCS-26600811.translation: To the Emperors Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augusti, and [Lucius Septimius Geta Caesar], Caius Antonius Crispinus, a veteran, formerly a decurion of the Pannonian Wing, gave this offering.
EDCS-26600811.summary: This is an offering made by a veteran formerly serving in the Pannonian cavalry to Emperors Septimius Severus, Caracalla, and Geta.

EDCS-26600813.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Luci Septimi Severi Pii Pertinacis Augusti Arabici Adiabenici Parthici maximi filio divi Marci Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici nepoti divi Antonini Pii pronepoti divi Hadriani abnepoti divi Traiani Parthici et divi Nervae adnepoti Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto equites alae Campagonum dedicante Mevio Suro consulari\n
EDCS-26600813.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, son of the deified Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, grandson of the deified Marcus Aurelius Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, great-grandson of the deified Antoninus Pius, great-great-grandson of the deified Hadrian, and great-great-great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus and the deified Nerva, by the equites of the Campagonese Wing, with Mevius Sura, consulary, dedicating.
EDCS-26600813.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Caracalla by the Campagonese Wing, mentioning his extensive imperial ancestry and an officiating consular official.

EDCS-26600815.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Iulio Philippo Pio Felici invicto Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate II pater sic patriae cons(uli) proconsuli cohors II Flavia Commagenorum Philippiana devota numini maiestatique eius ex quaestura\n
EDCS-26600815.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the second time, father of the fatherland, consul, proconsul, the Philippian Second Flavian Cohort of Commageni, devoted to his divine majesty, from his quaestorship.
EDCS-26600815.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Philip the Arab by the Philippian Second Flavian Cohort of Commageni, indicating their devotion and the context of their quaestorship.

EDCS-26600816.text_edition: \n Marco Iulio Philippo nobilissimo Caesari principi iuventutis filio Imperatoris Marci Iuli Philippi invicti Augusti ala I Hispanorum Campagonum Philippiana devoti sic numini maiestatique\n
EDCS-26600816.translation: To Marcus Julius Philippus, most noble Caesar, prince of the youth, son of Emperor Marcus Julius Philippus Invictus Augustus, the Philippian First Campagonese Wing of Spaniards, devoted to his divine majesty.
EDCS-26600816.summary: This is an inscription honoring Marcus Julius Philippus Caesar, the son of Emperor Philip the Arab, by the Philippian First Campagonese Wing of Spaniards.

EDCS-26600817.text_edition: \n stipendiorum XII vixit annos hic situs est Aurelius Primus et Valerius Maximus et Valerius Valerianus heredes et Aelia Philippis mater bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-26600817.translation: Having completed twelve years of service, he lived for [X] years; he is buried here. Aurelius Primus and Valerius Maximus and Valerius Valerianus, his heirs, and Aelia Philippis, his mother, set this up for the well-deserving.
EDCS-26600817.summary: This is a tombstone recording the service and life span of a soldier, with the monument erected by his heirs and mother.

EDCS-26600818.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus memoriae Cai Licini Collina Caesarea Pontici veterano alae I Augustae Ituraeorum vixit annos L Licinius Euprepes libertus patrono fecit\n
EDCS-26600818.translation: To the Divine Spirits, for the memory of Caius Licinius Collina, veteran of the First Augustan Wing of Ituraeans, he lived for 50 years. Licinius Euprepes, his freedman, made this for his patron.
EDCS-26600818.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for a veteran of the First Augustan Wing of Ituraeans, set up by his freedman.

EDCS-26600819.text_edition: \n Caius Valerius Ursus vixit annos XX Caius Valerius Antestius veteranus ex decurione filio fecit\n
EDCS-26600819.translation: Gaius Valerius Ursus lived 20 years. Gaius Valerius Antestius, a veteran ex-decurion, made this for his son.
EDCS-26600819.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Gaius Valerius Ursus at age 20 and was erected by his father, a veteran ex-decurion.

EDCS-26600821.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cai Bovi PAL Symphori Augustalis coloniae Dacicae Sarmizegetusae qui vixit nnos XXV \n
EDCS-26600821.translation: To the Divine Shades. Gaius Bovius Symphorus Augustalis of the colony of Dacica Sarmizegetusa, who lived 25 years.
EDCS-26600821.summary: This funerary inscription honors Gaius Bovius Symphorus Augustalis, who died at age 25 in the colony of Dacica Sarmizegetusa.

EDCS-26600822.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelius Ianuarius vixit annos XVIII Publius Aelius Aelianus filio pientissimo posuit\n
EDCS-26600822.translation: To the Divine Shades. Aelius Ianuarius lived 18 years. Publius Aelius Aelianus set this up for his most devoted son.
EDCS-26600822.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Aelius Ianuarius at age 18, erected by his father.

EDCS-26600823.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Augus vixit annos IIIcianus Valer \n
EDCS-26600823.translation: To the Divine Shades. Aurelius Augus lived 3 years [cianus Valer...]
EDCS-26600823.summary: This fragmentary funerary inscription records the death of Aurelius Augus at age 3, with the rest of the text missing.

EDCS-26600824.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Iulius Sosipater vixit annos LXX et Caius Iulius Antipater vixit annos XVI et Caius Iulius Valentinus vixit annos XXIIII Iulius II Euhodus coniugi et filiis bene merentibus posuit\n
EDCS-26600824.translation: To the Divine Shades. Gaius Iulius Sosipater lived 70 years, and Gaius Iulius Antipater lived 16 years, and Gaius Iulius Valentinus lived 24 years. Iulius Euhodus set this up for his deserving wife and sons.
EDCS-26600824.summary: This funerary inscription honors three individuals, likely family members, erected by Iulius Euhodus.

EDCS-26600825.text_edition: \n ------ A]vita vixit annos L Ulpius Provincialis coniugi bene merenti posuit\n
