EDCS-26600825.translation: [...]. Avita lived 50 years. Ulpius Provincialis set this up for his deserving wife.
EDCS-26600825.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Avita at age 50, erected by her husband Ulpius Provincialis.

EDCS-26600826.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ulpius Sabinus vixit annos XIIII Ulpia Maxima mater filio bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-26600826.translation: To the Divine Shades. Ulpius Sabinus lived 14 years. His mother Ulpia Maxima set this up for her deserving son.
EDCS-26600826.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Ulpius Sabinus at age 14, erected by his mother.

EDCS-26600827.text_edition: \n quondam Pomponi? et in bellis corpo edem iuveni eris condidit \n
EDCS-26600827.translation: [...for] Pomponius? and in wars, he founded a body for the youth who died in battle.
EDCS-26600827.summary: This fragmentary inscription likely records the establishment of a group or memorial for young men who died in war, possibly dedicated to Pomponius.

EDCS-26600828.text_edition: \n Fortunae pro salute Auggustorum nnnostrorum Lucius Octavius Iulianus consularis III Daciarum fieri iussit instante Aelio Germano praefecto alae AsturumB\n
EDCS-26600828.translation: For the welfare of our Emperors. Lucius Octavius Iulianus, consular governor of Dacia, ordered this to be made, with Aelius Germanus, prefect of the Ala Asturum, overseeing it.
EDCS-26600828.summary: This inscription records the commissioning of a work by Lucius Octavius Iulianus, a consular governor of Dacia, for the emperors' welfare, overseen by Aelius Germanus.

EDCS-26600829.text_edition: \n Herculi invicto pro <sal>(ute) Inperatoris sic collegium <G=C>alatarum Lucius Livius Marcellinus donum dedit dedicavit \n
EDCS-26600829.translation: To Hercules the Invincible for the welfare of the Emperor. Thus the college of the Galatae. Lucius Livius Marcellinus gave and dedicated this gift.
EDCS-26600829.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Hercules by the college of the Galatae, made by Lucius Livius Marcellinus for the Emperor's welfare.

EDCS-26600830.text_edition: \n Hanc tibi marmoreo caesam de monte dicavi regina undarum Nympha decus nemorum voto damnasti perfecto quem prece Bassus moenitae propter moenia Germisarae\n
EDCS-26600830.translation: I dedicated this carving from the marble mountain to you, Nymph, queen of the waves and glory of the groves; Bassus, having completed the vow you imposed, has enclosed it, and, near the walls of Germisarae.
EDCS-26600830.summary: This is a votive dedication to a Nymph by Bassus, likely on a fountain or shrine, near the walls of Germisarae.

EDCS-26600831.text_edition: \n Nymphis sanctissimis Publius Aelius Marcellinus signifer et quaestor numeri Brittonum mortis periculo liberatus votum solvit libens merito Imperatore Commodo Augusto Felice V et Glabrione II consulibus\n
EDCS-26600831.translation: To the most sacred Nymphs, Publius Aelius Marcellinus, standard-bearer and quaestor of the cohort of Britons, freed from the peril of death, paid his vow willingly and deservedly, when Emperor Commodus Augustus Felix was consul for the fifth time and Glabrio for the second.
EDCS-26600831.summary: This is a votive inscription by Publius Aelius Marcellinus, a soldier, to the Nymphs, dedicating a vow after surviving a life-threatening situation, dated to the consulship of Commodus and Glabrio (likely 174 AD).

EDCS-26600832.text_edition: \n Nymphis salutiferis sacrum Marcus Lucilius Lucilianus Augustalis coloniae pro salute sua et Luci Antisti Onesimi Augustalis coloniae votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-26600832.translation: To the health-giving Nymphs, Marcus Lucilius Lucilianus, an Augustalis of the colony, paid his vow willingly and deservedly for his own health and that of Lucius Antistius Onesimus, also an Augustalis of the colony.
EDCS-26600832.summary: This is a votive inscription by Marcus Lucilius Lucilianus and Lucius Antistius Onesimus, local officials (Augustales), to the health-giving Nymphs for their well-being.

EDCS-26600834.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Aurelio Crescenti Auggustorum liberto vixit annos LXI et Aureliae Florae filiae eius defunctae Poetovio vixit annis XXIII mensibus XI diebus XXX Aelius Iulianus socero et coniugi pientissimis bene merentibus posuit\n
EDCS-26600834.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed: Marcus Aurelius Crescenti, freedman of the Emperors, lived 61 years, and Aurelia Flora, his daughter, who died in Poetovio, lived 23 years, 11 months, and 30 days. Aelius Iulianus erected this for his most dutiful father-in-law and wife.
EDCS-26600834.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Marcus Aurelius Crescenti and his daughter Aurelia Flora, set up by Aelius Iulianus, likely dating to the period when Emperors were honored by freedmen bearing their names.

EDCS-26600835.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Quintus Ingenuvius Attio vixit annis XV Ingenuvia Marcella filio pientissimo\n
EDCS-26600835.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed: Quintus Ingenuvius Attius lived 15 years. Ingenuvia Marcella erected this for her most dutiful son.
EDCS-26600835.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Quintus Ingenuvius Attius, erected by his mother Ingenuvia Marcella.

EDCS-26600836.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Mansotiniae sic Spectatae vixit nnos XX filiae pientissimae posuit mater \n
EDCS-26600836.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed: Mansotinia Spectata lived 20 years. Her most dutiful daughter erected this.
EDCS-26600836.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Mansotinia Spectata, erected by her dutiful daughter.

EDCS-26600837.text_edition: \n INV posuerunt coniux et filii patri suo bene merenti hic situs est et Severia Fida vixit annos LXVII coniux eius\n
EDCS-26600837.translation: His wife and sons erected this for their deserving father. And Severia Fida, his wife, lived 67 years.
EDCS-26600837.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for a man whose identity is lost, and his wife Severia Fida, erected by his family.

EDCS-26600839.text_edition: \n <Sil=AC>v<a=I>no Domestico V Saturninus votum libens solvit \n
EDCS-26600839.translation: Saturninus willingly paid his vow to Silvanus Domesticus.
EDCS-26600839.summary: This is a votive inscription by Saturninus to the god Silvanus Domesticus.

EDCS-26600840.text_edition: \n Fortunae Augustae Quintus Decius Vindex procurator\n
EDCS-26600840.translation: To Fortuna Augusta, Quintus Decius Vindex, procurator.
EDCS-26600840.summary: This is a dedication to the goddess Fortuna Augusta by Quintus Decius Vindex, a procurator (an imperial financial official).

EDCS-26600841.text_edition: \n Genio pagi Miciae Titus Aurelius Primanus magister pagi eiusdem ex suo fecit libens merito\n
EDCS-26600841.translation: To the Genius of the district of Micia, Titus Aurelius Primanus, magistrate of the same district, made this at his own expense willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-26600841.summary: This is a dedication to the Genius (guardian spirit) of the district of Micia by its magistrate, Titus Aurelius Primanus.

EDCS-26600842.text_edition: \n Herculi Marcus Iulius Proclianus veteranus votum libens solvit\n
EDCS-26600842.translation: Marcus Iulius Proclianus, a veteran, willingly fulfilled his vow to Hercules.
EDCS-26600842.summary: A votive inscription dedicated to Hercules by Marcus Iulius Proclianus, a veteran.

EDCS-26600843.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo pro salute dominorum nostrorum Caius Iulius Marcianus decurio coloniae praefectus pagi Aquensis Genio eorum posuit\n
EDCS-26600843.translation: Caius Iulius Marcianus, decurion of the colony, prefect of the Aquensian pagus, set this up for the Genii of our Lords, Jupiter Optimus Maximus, for their safety.
EDCS-26600843.summary: An inscription dedicating a monument to Jupiter Optimus Maximus and the Genii of the emperors by Caius Iulius Marcianus, a local official.

EDCS-26600844.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Titus Flavius Gratus votum solvit libens\n
EDCS-26600844.translation: Titus Flavius Gratus willingly fulfilled his vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus.
EDCS-26600844.summary: A votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Titus Flavius Gratus.

EDCS-26600847.text_edition: \n Libero Patri Ulpius Nepos votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-26600847.translation: Ulpius Nepos willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Liber Pater.
EDCS-26600847.summary: A votive inscription dedicated to Liber Pater by Ulpius Nepos.

EDCS-26600848.text_edition: \n Dis Faventibus et Concordia Imperatorum evenit quod a primo adventu suo Publius Furius Saturninus legatus Augustorum donec provincia decederet ita singulos universosque benignitate sua tractarit oneribus etiam relevaverit ut felicissima et praecipuis virtutibus eiius sic obstricta simul et devota provincia ei gratias agat GERMP\n
EDCS-26600848.translation: With the gods favoring and the harmony of the Emperors, what happened was that from his first arrival, Publius Furius Saturninus, legate of the Augusti, until he left the province, treated individuals and the collective with such kindness and even relieved them of burdens, that the province, bound and devoted by his most fortunate and outstanding virtues, offers him thanks. [Inscription ends with GERMP].
EDCS-26600848.summary: An inscription recording the grateful thanks of a province to Publius Furius Saturninus, a legate, for his benevolent governance and relief of burdens.

EDCS-26600849.summary: An inscription stating that Claudius Saturninus was the sculptor of the monument.

EDCS-26600851.text_edition: \n Apollini Augusto Lucius Aemilius Carus legatus Augusti pro praetore III Daciarum\n
EDCS-26600851.translation: Lucius Aemilius Carus, legate of the Augustus for the province of the III Daciarum, [dedicated this] to Apollo Augustus.
EDCS-26600851.summary: An inscription dedicating a monument to Apollo Augustus by Lucius Aemilius Carus, a Roman legate.

EDCS-26600852.text_edition: \n Victoriae Augustae pro salute Imperatoris Antonini Augusti Marcus Statius Priscus legatus eius pro praetore\n
EDCS-26600852.translation: Marcus Statius Priscus, his legate for the province, [dedicated this] to Victory Augusta for the safety of the Emperor Antoninus Augustus.
EDCS-26600852.summary: An inscription dedicating a monument to Victory Augusta for the emperor's safety by Marcus Statius Priscus, a Roman legate.

EDCS-26600853.text_edition: \n Numini Aesculapi et Hygiae Aelia Venusta votum solvit\n
EDCS-26600853.translation: Aelia Venusta willingly fulfilled her vow to the divine powers of Asclepius and Hygieia.
EDCS-26600853.summary: A votive inscription dedicated to Asclepius and Hygieia by Aelia Venusta.

EDCS-26600854.text_edition: \n Aesculapio Pergameno et Hygiae sacrum Caius Spedius Hermias flamen coloniae Sarmizegetusae posuit\n
EDCS-26600854.translation: Caius Spedius Hermias, flamen of the colony of Sarmizegetusa, dedicated this sacred offering to the Pergamene Asclepius and Hygieia.
EDCS-26600854.summary: A sacred dedication to the Pergamene Asclepius and Hygieia by Caius Spedius Hermias, a local priest.

EDCS-26600855.text_edition: \n Dianae sanctae Potentinus nummularius votum solvit\n
EDCS-26600855.translation: Potentinus the money changer fulfilled his vow to holy Diana.
EDCS-26600855.summary: This is a votive inscription by Potentinus to the goddess Diana, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600859.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Iunoni Reginae Minervae omnibus dis immortalibus Quintus Axius Aelianus vir egregius procurator Augg(ustorum) et Aelia Romana eius Ioni\n
EDCS-26600859.translation: Quintus Axius Aelianus, an excellent man, procurator of the Emperors, and his wife Aelia Romana, dedicated this to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Juno Regina, Minerva, and all the immortal gods.
EDCS-26600859.summary: This dedication to the Capitoline Triad and other gods was made by a high-ranking imperial official and his wife, likely in the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600860.text_edition: \n Genio collegii fabrum Aurelius Ingenuus decurio coloniae Sarmizegetusae \n
EDCS-26600860.translation: Aurelius Ingenuus, decurion of the colony of Sarmizegetusa, to the genius of the guild of craftsmen.
EDCS-26600860.summary: This inscription records a dedication by a local official to the protective spirit of a guild of craftsmen in Sarmizegetusa, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600861.text_edition: \n Genio ordinis quem Ulpius Domitius Hermes Augustalis coloniae ob honorem ornamentorum decurionalium promiserat Valerius Threptus et Domitii Regulus Hipponicus Hermes Onesimus Augustales coloniae heredes ponendum curaverunt locus datus decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-26600861.translation: Valerius Threptus and the heirs of Domitii Regulus, Hipponicus, Hermes, and Onesimus, Augustales of the colony, erected this monument to the genius of the council, which Ulpius Domitius Hermes, Augustalis of the colony, had promised in return for the honor of decurional ornaments; the site was granted by a decree of the decurions.
EDCS-26600861.summary: This inscription details the erection of a monument to the genius of the local council by the heirs of Augustales, with the site provided by decree, likely in a Roman colony.

EDCS-26600862.text_edition: \n Minervae Augustae Ulpius Domitius Hermes Augustalis coloniae ornatus ornamentis decurionalibus testamento poni iussit Valerius Threptus et Domitii Regulus Hipponicus Hermes Onesimus Augustales coloniae heredes ponendum curaverunt loco dato decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-26600862.translation: Ulpius Domitius Hermes, Augustalis of the colony, honored with decurional ornaments, ordered this monument to be erected by his will; Valerius Threptus and the heirs of Domitii Regulus, Hipponicus, Hermes, and Onesimus, Augustales of the colony, saw to its erection, with the site granted by a decree of the decurions.
EDCS-26600862.summary: This inscription records a monument erected in honor of Minerva Augusta, commissioned in a will by a local official who had received decurional honors, with the site granted by the council, likely in a Roman colony.

EDCS-26600863.text_edition: \n Aesculapio et Hygiae Augustis miles legionis V votum solvit\n
EDCS-26600863.translation: A soldier of the Legio V fulfilled his vow to Minerva Augusta.
EDCS-26600863.summary: This is a votive inscription by a soldier of the Roman Fifth Legion to Minerva Augusta, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600864.text_edition: \n Deae Isidi Priscianus Augustalis coloniae Sarmizegetusae metropolis et Aurelia Fortunata liberta eius\n
EDCS-26600864.translation: Priscianus, an Augustalis of the colony of Sarmizegetusa metropolis, and his freedwoman Aurelia Fortunata, to the goddess Isis.
EDCS-26600864.summary: This dedication to the goddess Isis was made by a prominent local official and his freedwoman in Sarmizegetusa, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600866.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Antonius Priscus ex voto\n
EDCS-26600866.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, from a vow.
EDCS-26600866.summary: This is a simple votive inscription to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600867.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno pro salute Marci Bassi Aquilae et Gai Gaiani ex collegio fabrum decuriarum IIII votum solutum libenter merito\n
EDCS-26600867.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus, for the safety of Marcus Bassus Aquila and Gaius Gaius, from the guild of craftsmen of the four decuriae; the vow was willingly and deservedly fulfilled.
EDCS-26600867.summary: This inscription records a vow made by a guild of craftsmen for the safety of two individuals, likely dedicated to Jupiter Dolichenus in the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600869.text_edition: \n pro salute Bassi Aquilini et Bassi Flaviani filiorum suorum votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-26600869.translation: For the safety of his sons Bassus Aquilinus and Bassus Flavianus, he willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-26600869.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated for the welfare of two sons, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600870.text_edition: \n Marti Augusto pro salute Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Antoni Gordiani Pii Felicis Augusti Marcus Antonius Valentinus eques Romanus decurio municipii Apulensis sacerdos arae Augusti nostri coronatus Daciarum III donum dedit\n
EDCS-26600870.translation: To Mars Augustus, for the safety of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Pius Felix Augustus, Marcus Antonius Valentinus, a Roman knight and decurion of the municipality of Apula, crowned priest of the altar of our Augustus, offered this gift from the three Dacian provinces.
EDCS-26600870.summary: This inscription records a votive offering to Mars Augustus made by Marcus Antonius Valentinus for the emperor's safety, likely from the mid-3rd century AD.

EDCS-26600871.text_edition: \n Caius Iavolenus Modestus |(centurio) legionis XIII geminae Mercurio Augusto sacrum votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-26600871.translation: Gaius Iavolenus Modestus, centurion of the Thirteenth Gemina Legion, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Mercury Augustus.
EDCS-26600871.summary: This inscription records a soldier's vow fulfilled to Mercury Augustus, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-26600872.text_edition: \n Mercurio Augusto sacrum Caius Sextius Atta pro salute filiorum\n
EDCS-26600872.translation: Gaius Sextius Atta, to Mercury Augustus, for the safety of his sons.
EDCS-26600872.summary: This inscription records a vow made to Mercury Augustus by Gaius Sextius Atta for his sons' safety, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-26600873.text_edition: \n Soli Invicto Mithrae Aniceto Hermadio votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-26600873.translation: Anicetus Hermadius willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Sol Invictus Mithras.
EDCS-26600873.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled to Sol Invictus Mithras by Anicetus Hermadius, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-26600875.text_edition: \n Deae Nemesi Reginae Cornelius Cornelianus defensor lecticariorum et Iulia Bessa coniux eius ex voto posuerunt\n
EDCS-26600875.translation: Cornelius Cornelianus, defender of the litter-bearers, and his wife Iulia Bessa, erected this in fulfillment of a vow to the Queen Goddess Nemesis.
EDCS-26600875.summary: This inscription records a votive dedication to the goddess Nemesis made by Cornelius Cornelianus and his wife Iulia Bessa, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-26600877.text_edition: \n Quadribis! Claudius Anicetus Augustalis coloniae Sarmizegetusae metropolis ex voto\n
EDCS-26600877.translation: To the goddess of the crossroads! Claudius Anicetus, Augustalis of the colony of Sarmizegetusa metropolis, [erected this] in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-26600877.summary: This inscription records a votive dedication to the goddess of the crossroads by Claudius Anicetus, an official of Sarmizegetusa, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-26600878.text_edition: \n Deo Silvano Lucius Coelius Aelianus decurio coloniae Sarmizegetusae metropolis aedilicius ex voto\n
EDCS-26600878.translation: To the god Silvanus, Lucius Coelius Aelianus, decurion of the colony of Sarmizegetusa metropolis, formerly an aedile, [erected this] in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-26600878.summary: This inscription records a votive dedication to the god Silvanus by Lucius Coelius Aelianus, a local official in Sarmizegetusa, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-26600881.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription honoring Emperor Trajan, likely from the early 2nd century AD.

EDCS-26600882.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari divi Traiani Parthici filio divi Nervae nepoti Traiano Hadriano Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate II consuli patri patriae colonia Ulpia Traiana Augusta Dacica Sarmizegetusa \n
EDCS-26600882.translation: To the Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the deified Nerva, Trajan Hadrian Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the second time, consul, father of the country, the colony Ulpia Traiana Augusta Dacica Sarmizegetusa [erected this].
EDCS-26600882.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument by the colony of Sarmizegetusa to Emperor Hadrian, likely during his second consulship and tribunician power, placing it around 119 AD.

EDCS-26600883.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari divi Traiani Parthici filio divi Nervae nepoti Traiano Hadriano Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate XVI consuli III patri patriae aqua inducta coloniae Dacicae Sarmizegetusae per Cnaeum Papirium Aelianum legatum eius pro praetore\n
EDCS-26600883.translation: To the Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the deified Nerva, Trajan Hadrian Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the sixteenth time, consul for the third time, father of the country, for the bringing of water into the Dacian colony of Sarmizegetusa, by his legate pro praetore, Gnaeus Papirius Aelianus.
EDCS-26600883.summary: This inscription records the completion of an aqueduct for the colony of Sarmizegetusa under the administration of Gnaeus Papirius Aelianus during Hadrian's 16th tribunician power and third consulship, dating it to around 132 AD.

EDCS-26600885.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari divi Hadriani filio divi Traiani Parthici nepoti divi Nervae pronepoti Tito Aelio Hadriano Antonino Augusto Pio pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate V consuli III patri patriae Quintus Aurelius Quinti filius Tertius Papiria Sarmizegetusa decurio coloniae ob honorem flamoni et HS LXXXmilia nummum ad annonam dedit locus datus decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-26600885.translation: To Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, son of the deified Hadrian, grandson of the deified Trajan the Parthian, great-grandson of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power for the 5th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the fatherland. Quintus Aurelius Tertius, son of Quintus, of the Papiria tribe, a decurion of the colony, for the honor of flamen perpetuus and on account of 80,000 sesterces, dedicated this for the grain supply; a site was granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-26600885.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Quintus Aurelius Tertius, a local official, for the honor of a priesthood and for the grain supply, with land granted by the town council, likely during the reign of Antoninus Pius.

EDCS-26600886.text_edition: \n Faustinae Augustae divi Pii filiae colonia Sarmizegetusa\n
EDCS-26600886.translation: To Faustina Augusta, daughter of the deified Pius. Colony Sarmizegetusa.
EDCS-26600886.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Empress Faustina, wife of Antoninus Pius, by the colony of Sarmizegetusa.

EDCS-26600887.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus maximus et Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Augustus et Publius Septimius Geta nobilissimus Caesar fecerunt\n
EDCS-26600887.translation: Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus and Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Augustus and Publius Septimius Geta Nobilissimus Caesar made this.
EDCS-26600887.summary: This inscription records the building activities of Emperors Septimius Severus, Caracalla, and Geta, likely in Sarmizegetusa.

EDCS-26600888.text_edition: \n Divo Severo Pio colonia Ulpia Traiana Augusta Dacica Sarmizegetusa\n
EDCS-26600888.translation: To the deified Severus Pius. Colony Ulpia Traiana Augusta Dacica Sarmizegetusa.
EDCS-26600888.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the deified Emperor Septimius Severus by the colony of Sarmizegetusa, indicating the emperor's recent death and deification.

EDCS-26600890.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Antonio Gordiano Pio Felici Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate IIII consuli II patri patriae proconsuli concilium provinciarum Daciarum III devoti numini maiestatique eius\n
EDCS-26600890.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power for the 4th time, consul for the 2nd time, father of the fatherland, proconsul. The council of the Dacian provinces, devoted to his divine will and majesty, [erected this].
EDCS-26600890.summary: This inscription records a dedication by the council of the Dacian provinces to Emperor Gordian III, likely erected during his fourth tribunician power and second consulship.

EDCS-26600892.text_edition: \n Quinto Axio Quinti filio Palatina Aeliano equiti Romano Laurenti Lavinati curatori ad populum viarum Traianae et Aureliae et Aeclanensis procuratori ad alimenta per Apuliam Calabriam Lucaniam et Bruttios procuratori rationis privatae provinciae Mauretaniae Caesariensis item per Belgicum et duas Germanias procuratori provinciae Daciae Apulensis bis vice praesidis ordo coloniae Sarmizegetusae metropolis patrono\n
EDCS-26600892.translation: To Quintus Axix, son of Quintus, of the Palatina tribe, a Roman knight, from Laurentum Lavinium, curator for the people of the Trajan, Aurelian, and Aeclanensis roads, procurator for the food supply throughout Apulia, Calabria, Lucania, and the Bruttii, procurator of the private revenue for the province of Mauretania Caesariensis, and also through Belgica and the two Germanies, procurator of the province of Dacia Apulensis twice, serving as vice-governor. The ordo of the colony Sarmizegetusa, metropolis, [honors him as] patron.
EDCS-26600892.summary: This inscription honors Quintus Axix, a prominent imperial official with extensive administrative and financial responsibilities across various provinces, as patron by the colony of Sarmizegetusa.

EDCS-26600893.text_edition: \n Marco Claudio Tiberi filio Quirina Frontoni consuli legato Augusti pro praetore trium Daciarum et Moesiae superioris comiti divi Veri Augusti donato donis militaribus bello Armeniaco et Parthico ab Imperatore Antonino Augusto et a divo Vero Augusto corona murali item vallari item classica item aurea item hastis puris IIII item vexillis curatori operum locorumque publicorum legato legionis I Minerviae legato legionis XI Claudiae praetori aedili curuli ab actis senatus quaestori urbano decemviro stlitibus iudicandis colonia Ulpia Traiana Augusta Dacica Sarmizegetusa patrono fortissimo duci amplissimo praesidi\n
EDCS-26600893.translation: To Marcus Claudius Fronton, son of Tiberius, of the Quirina tribe, consul, imperial legate with proconsular authority over the three Dacias and Upper Moesia, companion of the deified Verus Augustus, decorated with military gifts in the Armenian and Parthian war by Emperor Antoninus Augustus and the deified Verus Augustus with a mural crown, a vallaris, a classical crown, a golden crown, four spears, and four standards. Curator of public works and places, legate of Legio I Minervia, legate of Legio XI Claudia, praetor, curule aedile, clerk of the senate, urban quaestor, decemvir for settling lawsuits. The colony Ulpia Traiana Augusta Dacica Sarmizegetusa [honors him as] most brave leader, most excellent governor, [their] patron.
EDCS-26600893.summary: This inscription commemorates Marcus Claudius Fronton, a distinguished military and administrative official who served as legate in the Dacian provinces and Moesia, and received numerous military honors, honored as patron by the colony of Sarmizegetusa.

EDCS-26600894.text_edition: \n Caio Curtio Cai filio Pollia Iusto cons(uli) IIIIviro viarum curandarum quaestori urbano adlecto inter tribunicios a divo Hadriano praetori peregrino IIIIIIviro equitum Romanorum turmis ducendis praefecto frumenti dandi curatori viarum Clodiae Anniae Cassiae Ciminiae legato Imperatoris Antonini Augusti Pii legionis XX Valeriae Victricis proconsuli provinciae COLLIAE legato pro praetore Imperatoris Antonini Augusti Pii provinciae Daciae colonia Ulpia Traiana Dacica Sarmizegetusa patrono\n
EDCS-26600894.translation: To Gaius Curtius Justus, son of Gaius, of the Pollia tribe, four times consul, curator of roads, urban quaestor, admitted among the tribunes by the deified Hadrian, praetor for suits between foreigners, four times decurion of Roman knights leading troops, prefect responsible for distributing grain, curator of the roads Clodia, Annia, Cassia, and Ciminia, imperial legate of Legio XX Valeria Victrix, proconsul of the province Baetica, imperial legate with proconsular authority of the province of Dacia. The colony Ulpia Traiana Dacica Sarmizegetusa [honors him as] patron.
EDCS-26600894.summary: This inscription honors Gaius Curtius Justus, a high-ranking official with extensive experience in military command, provincial administration, and public works, as patron by the colony of Sarmizegetusa.

EDCS-26600895.text_edition: \n Caio Curtio Cai filio Pollia Rufino tribuno laticlavio legionis XIII geminae IIIviro aere argento auro flando feriundo colonia Ulpia Traiana Dacica Sarmizetegusa\n
EDCS-26600895.translation: To Gaius Curtius Rufinus, son of Gaius, of the Pollia tribe, military tribune with the broad stripe of Legio XIII Gemina, moneyer for bronze, silver, and gold. Colony Ulpia Traiana Dacica Sarmizegetusa.
EDCS-26600895.summary: This inscription records the career of Gaius Curtius Rufinus, a young man from an elite family who served as a military tribune and moneyer, by the colony of Sarmizegetusa.

EDCS-26600896.text_edition: \n Publio Furio Saturnino legato Augusti pro praetore consuli colonia Dacica Sarmizegetusa\n
EDCS-26600896.translation: To Publius Furius Saturninus, imperial legate with proconsular authority, consul. Colony Dacica Sarmizegetusa.
EDCS-26600896.summary: This inscription records the honors bestowed upon Publius Furius Saturninus, a governor and consul, by the colony of Sarmizegetusa.

EDCS-26600898.text_edition: \n Quinto Marcio Turboni Frontoni Publicio Severo praefecto praetorio Imperatoris Caesaris Traiani Hadriani Augusti patris patriae colonia Ulpia Traiana Augusta Dacica Sarmizegetusa\n
EDCS-26600898.translation: To Quintus Marcius Turbonius Frontonius Publicius Severus, praetorian prefect of the Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrian Augustus, father of the fatherland, from the colony Ulpia Traiana Augusta Dacica Sarmizegetusa.
EDCS-26600898.summary: This inscription honors Quintus Marcius Turbonius Frontonius Publicius Severus, praetorian prefect, as a patron of the colony Ulpia Traiana Augusta Dacica Sarmizegetusa.

EDCS-26600899.text_edition: \n Decimo Terentio Gentiano tribuno militum quaestori tribuno plebis praetori legato Augusti consuli pontifici censori provinciae Macedoniae colonia Ulpia Traiana Augusta Dacica Sarmizegetusa patrono\n
EDCS-26600899.translation: To Decimus Terentius Gentianus, military tribune, quaestor, tribune of the plebs, praetor, legate of Augustus, consul, pontiff, censor of the province of Macedonia, patron of the colony Ulpia Traiana Augusta Dacica Sarmizegetusa.
EDCS-26600899.summary: This inscription honors Decimus Terentius Gentianus, a prominent official, as a patron of the colony Ulpia Traiana Augusta Dacica Sarmizegetusa.

EDCS-26600900.text_edition: \n Ulpio --- v(iro) e(gregio) procuratori Auggustorum provinc(iae) Daciae Apulensis agenti vice praesidis item procuratori provinciae Porolissensis subpraefecto nnae sacrae urbis praeposito legionis VII geminae Philippianae item procuratori stationis privatae per Tusciam et Picenum item procuratori ad bona Plautiani tribuno militum legionis II Parthicae Philippianae praeposito vexillationis auxiliariorum nnoniae inferioris praefecto cohortis VII Breucorum Siscius Valerius |(centurio) legionis XIII geminae Philippianae patrono optimo\n
EDCS-26600900.translation: To Ulpius [---], a most distinguished man, procurator of the emperors for the province of Dacia Apulensis, acting in place of the governor, also procurator of the province of Porolissensis, subprefect of the sacred ceremonies of the city, commander of the Legio VII Gemina Philippiana, also procurator of the private estates through Tuscia and Picenum, also procurator for the property of Plautianus, military tribune of Legio II Parthica Philippiana, commander of a detachment of auxiliary troops of Pannonia Inferior, prefect of the Cohors VII Breucorum, Siscius Valerius, centurion of Legio XIII Gemina Philippiana, his most excellent patron.
EDCS-26600900.summary: This inscription honors Ulpius [---] for his many distinguished military and administrative roles, recognizing him as a patron.

EDCS-26600902.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Alliano Augusti nostri servo vernae vixit annis VI mensibus IX diebus XXII Constantianus Augusti libertus adiutor tabularii pater\n
EDCS-26600902.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Allianus, our Emperor's slave, born in the household. He lived 6 years, 9 months, and 22 days. Constantianus, freedman of the Emperor, assistant to the record keeper, his father, set this up.
EDCS-26600902.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Allianus, a young household slave, and is dedicated by his father, Constantianus.

EDCS-26600903.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Aurelio Onesimo Carpion Augusti libertus tabularius filio dulcissimo p(osuit)\n
EDCS-26600903.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. To Marcus Aurelius Onesimus Carpion, freedman of the Emperor, record keeper, his most beloved son, erected this.
EDCS-26600903.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Marcus Aurelius Onesimus Carpion, a record keeper, and is dedicated by his father.

EDCS-26600904.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aureliae Respectae rarissimae feminae Herculanus Auggustorum nnostrorum verna adiutor tabularii coniugi\n
EDCS-26600904.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. To Aurelia Respecta, a most rare woman. Herculanus, born in the household of our Emperors, assistant to the record keeper, erected this for his wife.
EDCS-26600904.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Aurelia Respecta and is dedicated by her husband, Herculanus, who also served as an assistant record keeper.

EDCS-26600905.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aureliae Victoriae incomparabili feminae Fortunatus Augusti libertus adiutor tabularii coniugi karissimae hoc monumentum heredem non sequetur\n
EDCS-26600905.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. To Aurelia Victoria, an incomparable woman. Fortunatus, freedman of the Emperor, assistant to the record keeper, erected this for his dearest wife; this monument shall not pass to an heir.
EDCS-26600905.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Aurelia Victoria and is dedicated by her husband, Fortunatus, stipulating that the tomb is not to be inherited.

EDCS-26600907.text_edition: \n Publius Aelius Septimius Audeo qui et Maximus veteranus ex |(centurione) numeri Palmyrenorum Optatianensium vixit nnis LX Aelius Septimius Romanus miles legionis XIII geminae adiutor officii corniculariorum et Septimia Septimina quae et Revocata filii et Cornelia Antonia uxor heredes ponendum curaverunt curam agente Septimio Asclepiade Augustali coloniae liberto eius\n
EDCS-26600907.translation: Publius Aelius Septimius Audeo, also called Maximus, a veteran who served as a centurion in the cohort of Palmyrenes Optatianenses, lived 60 years. Aelius Septimius Romanus, a soldier in Legio XIII Gemina, assistant in the office of the cornicularii, and Septimia Septimina, also called Revocata, and Cornelia Antonia, his children and wife, had this monument erected. The care was carried out by Septimius Asclepiades, freedman of the emperor and Augustalis of the colony, his client.
EDCS-26600907.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Publius Aelius Septimius Audeo, a veteran, and was erected by his family and overseen by his freedman and client.

EDCS-26600908.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publio Aelio Publi filio Papiria Theimeti veterano et IIvirali coloniae Sarmizegetusae vixit annos LXXXVIIII et Aeliae vixit annos LXXI et Publio legionis XX Victricis Publius Aelius Senecio gener \n
EDCS-26600908.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Publius Aelius, son of Publius, of the Papiria tribe, Theimetus, a veteran and former duovir of the colony of Sarmizegetusa, who lived 89 years, and for Aelia, who lived 71 years, and for Publius of Legio XX Victrix. Publius Aelius Senecio, their son-in-law, erected this.
EDCS-26600908.summary: This funerary inscription records the deaths of Publius Aelius Theimetus, a veteran and former duovir, his wife Aelia, and their son Publius, and was dedicated by their son-in-law.

EDCS-26600909.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus quod Aelia Adiuta mater sibi et Cominio Celerino pontifici coloniae equiti Romano tribuno legionis facere constituerat Cominius Quintus pontifex et quinquennalis et Cominia Sperata et Cominia Caecilia filii consummaverunt\n
EDCS-26600909.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. What Aelia Adiuta, mother, had decided to erect for herself and for Cominius Celerinus, pontiff of the colony and Roman knight, tribune of a legion, Cominius Quintus, pontiff and quinquennial magistrate, and Cominia Sperata and Cominia Caecilia, their children, completed.
EDCS-26600909.summary: This funerary inscription records the completion of a monument that Aelia Adiuta had intended for herself and her family, by her children after her passing.

EDCS-26600910.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Antoniae Bonosae vixit annis XXXVII Aurelio Constantio equiti Romano decurioni coloniae Viminacii vixit annis VL Publio Antonio Viatori militi legionis VI victricis frumentario vixit nnis XVIIII Publius Antonius Super infelix pater Publius Antonius Super tribunus cohortis III Delmatarum vixit annis LXXXIIII infelix pater\n
EDCS-26600910.translation: To the spirits of the underworld for Antonia Bonosa, who lived 37 years; for Aurelius Constantinus, Roman knight and decurion of the colony of Viminacium, who lived 45 years; for Publius Antonius Viator, soldier of the VI Victrix legion, who lived 19 years. Publius Antonius Super, unhappy father, Publius Antonius Super, tribune of the 3rd Cohort of Dalmatians, who lived 84 years, an unhappy father.
EDCS-26600910.summary: This funerary inscription records the ages at death for several individuals, including soldiers and an official, and mentions family relationships.

EDCS-26600911.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Claudia Serena vixit nnos LXXI Iulius Priscus veteranus coniugi bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-26600911.translation: To the spirits of the underworld for Claudia Serena, who lived 71 years. Julius Priscus, a veteran, erected this for his deserving wife.
EDCS-26600911.summary: This funerary inscription records the age at death for Claudia Serena and states that it was erected by her veteran husband.

EDCS-26600912.text_edition: \n item Lucio Dasumio Prisco veterano legionis XIII geminae vixit annos LX\n
EDCS-26600912.translation: Also for Lucius Dasumius Priscus, a veteran of the 13th Gemina legion, who lived 60 years.
EDCS-26600912.summary: This brief funerary inscription records the age at death for Lucius Dasumius Priscus, a veteran of the 13th Gemina legion.

EDCS-26600913.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Quinto Manlio Papiria Vero veterano legionis XV signiferi decurioni coloniae vixit annos LXXV Caius Iulius Macer |(centurio) heres fecit\n
EDCS-26600913.translation: To the spirits of the underworld for Quintus Manlius Verus, of the Papiria tribe, veteran of the 15th legion, standard-bearer, decurion, who lived 75 years. Caius Julius Macer, centurion and heir, made this.
EDCS-26600913.summary: This funerary inscription records the age at death for Quintus Manlius Verus, a veteran and decurion, and notes it was erected by his heir, Caius Julius Macer.

EDCS-26600914.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Maria Secundina vixit annos LIII et Aurelia Saturnina vixit nnos XVIII Aurelius Ingenuus miles legionis XIII geminae Antoninianae matri et sorori posuit\n
EDCS-26600914.translation: To the spirits of the underworld for Maria Secundina, who lived 53 years, and Aurelia Saturnina, who lived 18 years. Aurelius Ingenuus, soldier of the 13th Gemina Antoniniana legion, erected this for his mother and sister.
EDCS-26600914.summary: This funerary inscription records the ages at death for Maria Secundina and Aurelia Saturnina, and states it was erected by Aurelius Ingenuus, a soldier, for his mother and sister.

EDCS-26600915.text_edition: \n Sextus Pilonius Sexti filius Stellatina Modestus Benevento |(centurio) legionis IIII Flaviae Felicis III hastatus posterior nnorum XXXVII ordinem accepit ex equite Romano militavit in legione VII Claudia pia fideli et VIII Augusta XI Claudia pia fideli I Minervia pia fideli stipendiis centurionicis XVIIII hic situs est sit tibi terra levis\n
EDCS-26600915.translation: Sextus Pilonius Modestus, son of Sextus, of the Stellatina tribe, from Beneventum, centurion, of the 4th Flavia Felix legion, junior officer, who received his rank at age 37. He served as a Roman knight, served in the 7th Claudia Pia Fidelis legion, the 8th Augusta Claudia Pia Fidelis legion, and the 1st Minervia Pia Fidelis legion, serving 19 years in centurionate posts. He is buried here. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-26600915.summary: This funerary inscription details the military career of Sextus Pilonius Modestus, including his ranks, legions, and length of service, before stating he is buried here.

EDCS-26600916.text_edition: \n Tito Varenio Probo IIvirali item quinquennali coloniae Varenius Pudens flamen coloniarum equo publico praefectus cohortis patri ob stratam ab eo aream dimidiam in honorem suum ex SS Lmilibus nummum loco dato decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-26600916.translation: For Titus Varenus Probus, formerly duovir and quinquennalis of the colony. Varenius Pudens, flamen of the colonies, with public horse, prefect of a cohort, for his father, for the area he paved, half the size, in his own honor, from 50,000 sesterces, in a place granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-26600916.summary: This dedication records a public honor bestowed by Varenius Pudens upon his father, Titus Varenus Probus, a former magistrate of the colony, funded by a grant and decurial decree.

EDCS-26600917.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Verpidio Silvano equiti alae II Pannoniorum Valerius Proculus secundus heres ponendum curavit\n
EDCS-26600917.translation: To the spirits of the underworld for Marcus Verpidius Silvanus, Roman knight of the 2nd Ala of Pannonians. Valerius Proculus, second heir, arranged for this to be erected.
EDCS-26600917.summary: This funerary inscription records the dedication for Marcus Verpidius Silvanus, a Roman knight, and states it was arranged by his heir, Valerius Proculus.

EDCS-26600919.text_edition: \n Ulpiae Procillae vixit annos XXVI Lucius Valerius Rufus veteranus ex beneficiario consularis decurio coloniae coniugi bene merenti posuit Lucio Valerio Rufo veterano legionis XIII geminae ex beneficiario consularis decurioni coloniae quaestori IIviro vixit annos LXV Valerii Rufinus et Proculeianus filii statuendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-26600919.translation: For Ulpia Procilla, who lived 26 years. Lucius Valerius Rufus, veteran, formerly chief clerk of a governor, decurion of the colony, erected this for his deserving wife. For Lucius Valerius Rufus, veteran of the 13th Gemina legion, formerly chief clerk of a governor, decurion of the colony, quaestor, and duovir, who lived 65 years. His sons Valerius Rufinus and Proculeianus arranged for this monument.
EDCS-26600919.summary: This funerary inscription records the ages at death for Ulpia Procilla and Lucius Valerius Rufus, noting his various civic and military roles, and was erected by their sons.

EDCS-26600920.text_edition: \n Tito Varenio Titi filio Papiria Pudenti equo publico a militiis decurioni et flamini coloniae Sarmizegetusae quinquennali coloniae supra scriptae decurioni municipii Apulensis patrono municipii Porolissensis et collegiorum itemque causarum\n
EDCS-26600920.translation: For Titus Varenus Pudens, son of Titus, of the Papiria tribe, with public horse, from military service, decurion and flamen of the colony of Sarmizegetusa, quinquennalis of the aforementioned colony, decurion of the municipium of Apulum, patron of the municipium of Porolissensis and of the collegia and also of legal cases.
EDCS-26600920.summary: This dedication lists the numerous civic and religious offices held by Titus Varenus Pudens, a prominent figure in several Roman settlements.

EDCS-26600921.translation: As prefect of the equestrian order.
EDCS-26600921.summary: This inscription likely records the equestrian rank of an unnamed individual, possibly associated with a local magistracy or military service.

EDCS-26600922.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aeliae Andenae Aelius Macrinus Epidianus qui et Epidius Augustalis coloniae et Macrinia Marcia filiae posuerunt\n
EDCS-26600922.translation: To the spirits of the departed: Aelius Macrinus Epidianus, who was also known as Epidius Augustalis of the colony, and Macrinia Marcia, for their daughter Aelia Andena.
EDCS-26600922.summary: This is a funerary dedication by parents for their deceased daughter, Aelia Andena, and likely dates to the Roman Imperial period given the names and context.

EDCS-26600923.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucio Antonio Papiria Prisco vixit nnos LXII Antonius Rufus decurio coloniae et Antonia Priscilla patri\n
EDCS-26600923.translation: To the spirits of the departed: For Lucius Antonius Priscus, of the Papiria tribe, who lived 62 years. Antonius Rufus, decurion of the colony, and Antonia Priscilla, placed this for their father.
EDCS-26600923.summary: This funerary inscription honors Lucius Antonius Priscus, who lived to 62, and was set up by his son and daughter-in-law, who were local officials in the colony.

EDCS-26600924.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Quintiniae Magnillae vixit annos XXX Antonius Rufus IIviralis coloniae maritus et Antonii Priscus Rufus Rufinus decuriones coloniae filii\n
EDCS-26600924.translation: To the spirits of the departed: For Quintinia Magnilla, who lived 30 years. Antonius Rufus, husband and duumvir of the colony, and his sons Antonii Priscus, Rufus, and Rufinus, decurions of the colony, placed this for their mother.
EDCS-26600924.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Quintinia Magnilla, who died at 30, and was erected by her husband, a duumvir, and her sons, who were decurions in the colony.

EDCS-26600925.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Antonio Cai filio Papiria Ursino decurioni coloniae Dacicae Sarmizegetusae vixit nnos XXVIII Terentia Hermione mater infelicissima bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-26600925.translation: To the spirits of the departed: For Gaius Antonius Ursus, son of Gaius, of the Papiria tribe, decurion of the colony of Dacian Sarmizegetusa, who lived 28 years. Terentia Hermione, his most unhappy mother, placed this for him, who deserved well.
EDCS-26600925.summary: This funerary dedication honors Gaius Antonius Ursus, a decurion in the Dacian colony of Sarmizegetusa who died at 28, and was set up by his mother.

EDCS-26600927.text_edition: \n Collegio fabrum decuriae III Marcus Aurelius Fortunatus ob duplam sportulam collatam sibi\n
EDCS-26600927.translation: For the college of craftsmen, third decuria: Marcus Aurelius Fortunatus, because of the double donation given to him.
EDCS-26600927.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Marcus Aurelius Fortunatus to his craft guild, thanking them for a double distribution ('sportula'), likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600928.translation: Aurelius Theophilus, thus, in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-26600928.summary: This is a brief votive inscription by Aurelius Theophilus, indicating a dedication made in fulfillment of a promise or vow.

EDCS-26600929.text_edition: \n ? Valerio Cai filio Papiria Valentino decurioni coloniae Samizegetusae metropolis aedilicio IIvirali et quinquennali praefecto collegii fabrum et patrono eorundem Aurelii Theophilus et Castor deccuriones municipii Porolissensis amico fidissimo locus datus decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-26600929.translation: For Valerius Valentino, son of Caius, of the Papiria tribe, decurion of the colony of Samizegetusa metropolis, former aedile, former duumvir, quinquennial official, and prefect of the college of craftsmen, and patron of the same. Aurelii Theophilus and Castor, decurions of the municipality of Porolissensis, placed this for their most loyal friend, a site granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-26600929.summary: This is a funerary dedication honoring Valerius Valentino, a prominent local official and patron of a craft guild in Samizegetusa and Porolissensis, set up by fellow decurions, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600930.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelia Lucilla vixit nnos XXXVIII Caius Clodius Secundinus IIviralis coloniae coniugi pientissimae posuit\n
EDCS-26600930.translation: To the spirits of the departed: For Aurelia Lucilla, who lived 38 years. Gaius Clodius Secundinus, duumvir of the colony, placed this for his most devoted wife.
EDCS-26600930.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Gaius Clodius Secundinus, a duumvir of the colony, for his wife Aurelia Lucilla, who died at 38.

EDCS-26600931.text_edition: \n Marco Cominio Marci filio Papiria Quinto pontifici coloniae Sarmizegetusae et praefecto quinquennali pro Antonino Imperatore patrono collegii fabrum equo publico ordo coloniae ob merita ipsius\n
EDCS-26600931.translation: For Marcus Cominius Quintus, son of Marcus, of the Papiria tribe, pontiff of the colony of Sarmizegetusa and quinquennial official, for Antoninus Augustus, our patron, by the equestrian order of the colony, because of his merits.
EDCS-26600931.summary: This inscription honors Marcus Cominius Quintus, a local pontiff and quinquennial official in Sarmizegetusa, and dedicates something on behalf of the colony to Emperor Antoninus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600932.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Corneliae Faustinae undeviginti annos natae Caius Spedius Clemens IIvir coloniae coniugis memoriae erga se dignissimae fecit\n
EDCS-26600932.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Caius Spedius Clemens, duovir of the colony, erected this for his wife Cornelia Faustina, nineteen years old, a most worthy spouse.
EDCS-26600932.summary: A funerary monument erected by a husband for his young wife, dating to the Roman period when the office of duovir existed.

EDCS-26600933.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Corneliae Victorinae filiae Marcus Cornelius Primus Augustalis coloniae\n
EDCS-26600933.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Marcus Cornelius Primus, Augustalis of the colony, [erected this for] Cornelia Victorina, his daughter.
EDCS-26600933.summary: A funerary monument dedicated by a father to his daughter, likely dating to the Imperial period given the title Augustalis.

EDCS-26600934.text_edition: \n Crasso Macrobio negotiatores provinciae Apulensis defensori optimo locus datus decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-26600934.translation: A place was granted by decree of the decurions to Crassus Macrobius, a businessman of the province of Apulia, an excellent defender.
EDCS-26600934.summary: A decree by local councilors granting a plot of land to Crassus Macrobius, a businessman and advocate, during the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600935.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucio Flavio Valenti vixit annos LXXX collegium fabrum patrono posuit\n
EDCS-26600935.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Lucius Flavius Valens lived 80 years. The college of smiths placed this for their patron.
EDCS-26600935.summary: A funerary monument dedicated by a trade guild to a deceased member who lived to be 80 years old.

EDCS-26600936.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aulo Frisenio Papiria Vero decurioni coloniae Sarmizegetusae vixit annos XXXX et Lucio Clodio Papiria Etr<u=I>sco decurioni coloniae \n
EDCS-26600936.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Aulus Frisenius Papirius Verus, decurion of the colony of Sarmizegetusa, lived 40 years, and Lucius Clodius Papirius Etruscus, decurion of the colony [of Sarmizegetusa].
EDCS-26600936.summary: A funerary monument commemorating two decurions of the colony of Sarmizegetusa, one of whom lived to 40 years of age.

EDCS-26600937.text_edition: \n Quinto Ianuario Quinti filio Collina Rufo Tavio flamini quinquennali primo pro Imperatore ordo coloniae Ulpiae Traianae Dacicae Sarmizegetusae\n
EDCS-26600937.translation: To Quintus Ianuarius Rufus, son of Quintus, of the Collina tribe, the first quinquennial flamen for the Emperor, the town council of the colony of Ulpia Traiana Dacica Sarmizegetusa [erected this].
EDCS-26600937.summary: A monument erected by the town council to honor Quintus Ianuarius Rufus, the first quinquennial flamen (a priestly office) in the colony of Ulpia Traiana Dacica Sarmizegetusa.

EDCS-26600938.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Quinto Ianuario Zosimo vixit nnos XXXV et Ianuariae Candidae vixit nnos XXX Quintus Ianuarius Agathangelus filiis piissimis posuit ad funus sic autem Zosimi et titulo contulerunt collegium fabrum |(denarios) CCCC Quintus Ianuarius\n
EDCS-26600938.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Quintus Ianuarius Agathangelus placed this for his most dutiful sons, Quintus Ianuarius Zosimus, who lived 35 years, and Ianuaria Candida, who lived 30 years. The college of smiths also contributed 400 denarii towards Zosimus' funeral.
EDCS-26600938.summary: A funerary monument by a father for his two sons, with a contribution from the college of smiths towards one son's funeral expenses.

EDCS-26600939.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Iulio Marco ex collegio fabrum vixit annos LX collegium supra scriptum\n
EDCS-26600939.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Gaius Julius Marcus of the college of smiths lived 60 years. The aforementioned college placed this.
EDCS-26600939.summary: A funerary monument erected by the college of smiths for one of its members who lived to 60 years of age.

EDCS-26600940.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Iulio Victori Augustali coloniae Sarmizetegusae metropolis vixit annos LXXVI Iulia Maxima coniugi bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-26600940.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Marcus Iulius Victor, Augustalis of the metropolis colony of Sarmizegetusa, lived 76 years. Iulia Maxima placed this for her well-deserving husband.
EDCS-26600940.summary: A funerary monument dedicated by a wife to her husband, who was an Augustalis of the provincial capital of Sarmizegetusa and lived to 76.

EDCS-26600941.text_edition: \n In memoriam Laurentianus collegio fabrum vixit annos XXXVI Iulia \n
EDCS-26600941.translation: In memory of Laurentianus. Iulia [erected this for] the college of smiths. He lived 36 years.
EDCS-26600941.summary: A funerary monument likely erected by Iulia for Laurentianus, a member of the college of smiths, who died at the age of 36.

EDCS-26600942.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcio Augustalis coloniae Sarmizegetusae heres coniugi piissimae fecit\n
EDCS-26600942.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Marcius Augustalis of the colony of Sarmizegetusa made this for his most devoted wife.
EDCS-26600942.summary: This is a funerary inscription by Marcius Augustalis for his wife, likely dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600944.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Togernius Ingenuus Augustalis coloniae Sarmizegetusae metropolis vixit nnos XXX Petilia Victorina mater posuit\n
EDCS-26600944.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Caius Togernius Ingenuus Augustalis of the metropolis of Sarmizegetusa lived for 30 years. His mother, Petilia Victorina, erected this.
EDCS-26600944.summary: This is a funerary inscription by Petilia Victorina for her son Caius Togernius Ingenuus, who was an Augustalis in Sarmizegetusa, likely dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600945.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Turranio Frontoni decurioni coloniae vixit nnos XXII et Fanniae Maximae vixit annos XX et Turraniis Antonino vixit annos XX et Maximo decurioni coloniae vixit annos XXX Turranius Iustus decurio coloniae Metropolis et Maxima eius filiis posuerunt\n
EDCS-26600945.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Turranius Iustus, a decurion of the metropolis of the colony, and his wife Maxima erected this for their children: Marcus Turrannius Frontonius, a decurion of the colony, who lived for 22 years; Fannia Maxima, who lived for 20 years; Turrannius Antoninus, who lived for 20 years; and Maximus, a decurion of the colony, who lived for 30 years.
EDCS-26600945.summary: This is a funerary inscription by Turranius Iustus and his wife Maxima for their deceased children, some of whom held the position of decurion in the colony of Sarmizegetusa, likely dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600946.text_edition: \n Valeria Cara vixit annos XXIX Titus Flavius Aper scriba coloniae Sarmizegetusae coniugi rarissimae\n
EDCS-26600946.translation: Valeria Cara lived for 29 years. Titus Flavius Aper, scribe of the colony of Sarmizegetusa, erected this for his most rare wife.
EDCS-26600946.summary: This is a funerary inscription by Titus Flavius Aper for his wife Valeria Cara, who was the scribe of the colony of Sarmizegetusa, likely dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600947.text_edition: \n Cominiae coniugi Titi Vareni Probi quinquennali coloniae Sarmizegetusae Marcus Cominius Quintus pontifex et bis quinquennalis coloniae equo publico sacerdos arae Augusti sorori pientissimae loco dato decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-26600947.translation: Marcus Cominius Quintus, pontifex and twice quinquennalis of the colony, holding public office, priest of the altar of Augustus, erected this for his most devoted sister Cominia, wife of Titus Varenus Probus, quinquennalis of the colony of Sarmizegetusa, on a site granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-26600947.summary: This is an honorary inscription by Marcus Cominius Quintus for his sister Cominia, wife of Titus Varenus Probus, detailing their civic and religious offices in Sarmizegetusa, likely dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600949.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Venetius Privatus Augustalis coloniae Sarmizegetusae metropolis vixit annis L Ulpia Patricia coniux\n
EDCS-26600949.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Caius Venetius Privatus Augustalis of the metropolis of Sarmizegetusa lived for 50 years. His wife, Ulpia Patricia, erected this.
EDCS-26600949.summary: This is a funerary inscription by Ulpia Patricia for her husband Caius Venetius Privatus, an Augustalis in Sarmizegetusa, likely dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600950.text_edition: \n Marcus Veponius Maximinus Augustalis coloniae Sarmizegetusae porticum tectum ex solo de suo fecit\n
EDCS-26600950.translation: Marcus Veponius Maximinus Augustalis of the colony of Sarmizegetusa built the portico and roof from his own funds.
EDCS-26600950.summary: This is a public works inscription by Marcus Veponius Maximinus, an Augustalis of Sarmizegetusa, recording the construction of a portico and roof, likely dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600951.text_edition: \n Publius Vibidius - f(ilius) Valentinus aedilis et quinquennalis S honorem M pecunia sua loco publico\n
EDCS-26600951.translation: Publius Vibidius Valentinus, son of Vibidius, aedile and quinquennalis, [dedicated] this honor from his own funds in a public place.
EDCS-26600951.summary: This is an honorary inscription by Publius Vibidius Valentinus, an aedile and quinquennalis, recording his dedication of an honor from his own funds, likely dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600952.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aureliae feminae vixit annos XXV Marcus Ulpius Iustinus decurio coloniae coniugi bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-26600952.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Aurelia lived for 25 years. Marcus Ulpius Iustinus, a decurion of the colony, erected this for his deserving wife.
EDCS-26600952.summary: This is a funerary inscription by Marcus Ulpius Iustinus for his wife Aurelia, who was a decurion in the colony, likely dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600953.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Ulpio Maio decurioni coloniae Sarmizegetusae metropolis vixit annos XXX \n
EDCS-26600953.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Marcus Ulpius Maius, a decurion of the metropolis of the colony of Sarmizegetusa, lived for 30 years.
EDCS-26600953.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Marcus Ulpius Maius, a decurion in Sarmizegetusa, likely dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600954.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Ulpio Restuto IIviro coloniae Sarmizegetusae Ulpiae Marcellina et Restuta filiae et heredes titulum ponendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-26600954.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Ulpiana Marcellina and Restuta, daughters and heirs, saw to the erection of this monument for Marcus Ulpius Restutus, aedile of the colony of Sarmizegetusa.
EDCS-26600954.summary: This funerary inscription records the erection of a monument by the daughters and heirs for Marcus Ulpius Restutus, an aedile of the colony of Sarmizegetusa.

EDCS-26600958.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cai Iuli Cai fili Rufi domo Viminacio nnorum XX Iulia Gemellina fratri pientissimo et Marco Aurelio Marci filio Maximo mensium VIII Aurelius Helico et Iulia Gemellina parentes hoc monumentum heredem non sequetur\n
EDCS-26600958.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Caius Iulius Rufus, son of Caius, from Viminacio, aged 20 years. Iulia Gemellina to her most devoted brother. And to Marcus Aurelius Maximus, son of Marcus, aged 8 months. Aurelius Helico and Iulia Gemellina, parents. This monument will not pass to an heir.
EDCS-26600958.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates two individuals, Caius Iulius Rufus and Marcus Aurelius Maximus, with their parents providing the monument, and specifies it is not inheritable.

EDCS-26600959.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Antonia Sisiata vixit annos LX Caius Iulius Rusticinus vixit annos XL Antonia Protogenia vixit annos XXVI Caius Iulius Rusticus matri fratri et coniugi karissimis\n
EDCS-26600959.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Antonia Sisiata lived 60 years. Caius Iulius Rusticinus lived 40 years. Antonia Protogenia lived 26 years. Caius Iulius Rusticus to his dearest mother, brother, and wife.
EDCS-26600959.summary: This funerary inscription records the lifespans of Antonia Sisiata, Caius Iulius Rusticinus, and Antonia Protogenia, and is dedicated by Caius Iulius Rusticus to his family members.

EDCS-26600960.summary: This is a very brief inscription, likely a name alone, possibly marking ownership or a tomb.

EDCS-26600961.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aureliae Proculae vixit annis III mensibus II Bubalus Augusti libertus filiae innocentissimae posuit\n
EDCS-26600961.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aurelia Procula lived 3 years and 2 months. Bubalus, freedman of Augustus, placed this for his most innocent daughter.
EDCS-26600961.summary: This funerary inscription records the short life of Aurelia Procula, and states it was erected by her father, Bubalus, a freedman of Augustus.

EDCS-26600962.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ulpiae Candidae vixit annos XXV Ulpius Eucharistus coniugi bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-26600962.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Ulpia Candida lived 25 years. Ulpius Eucharistus placed this for his well-deserving wife.
EDCS-26600962.summary: This funerary inscription records the lifespan of Ulpia Candida and states it was erected by her husband, Ulpius Eucharistus.

EDCS-26600963.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Castena Nice vixit annis XXVII testamento poni sibi iussit arbitratu Herenniae Maximae \n
EDCS-26600963.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Castena Nice lived 27 years. She ordered this to be placed for herself in her will, under the supervision of Herennia Maxima.
EDCS-26600963.summary: This funerary inscription records the lifespan of Castena Nice and states that she arranged for its erection in her will, overseen by Herennia Maxima.

EDCS-26600964.text_edition: \n Maximo Fan annos XXX Domitia Secundina patrono pientissimo merito fecit\n
EDCS-26600964.translation: For Maximo Fan, 30 years old. Domitia Secundina made this for her most devoted patron, with merit.
EDCS-26600964.summary: This inscription records the age of Maximo Fan and states it was erected by Domitia Secundina for her deserving patron.

EDCS-26600965.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Hygiae Imperatorum nostrorum vixit annos XLVII Augustalis eorundem coniugi bene merenti posuit et ipse hic situs est vixit annos L\n
EDCS-26600965.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Hygia, wife of our Emperors, who lived 47 years. Augustalis, husband of the same, placed this for his well-deserving wife, and he himself is buried here; he lived 50 years.
EDCS-26600965.summary: This funerary inscription records the lives of Hygia and her husband Augustalis, who erected the monument for her and was also buried there.

EDCS-26600966.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cnaeo Ianuario vixit annos XXI A\n
EDCS-26600966.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Gnaeus Ianuarius, who lived 21 years. [Restoration needed].
EDCS-26600966.summary: This is a fragmented funerary inscription commemorating Gnaeus Ianuarius, who died at the age of 21.

EDCS-26600967.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Liciniae Valentinae vixit annos LX et Aureliae Maximinae vixit annos XX Aurelius Maximus et Aurelius Pusinnus coniugibus dignissimis\n
EDCS-26600967.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Licinia Valentina lived 60 years and Aurelia Maximina lived 20 years. Aurelius Maximus and Aurelius Pusinnus [dedicated this] to their most worthy wives.
EDCS-26600967.summary: This funerary inscription records the ages at death of Licinia Valentina and Aurelia Maximina, likely dedicated by their husbands or relatives.

EDCS-26600968.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tito Lisinio Amabili vixit annos XL Lisinia Callityche coniunx bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-26600968.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Titus Lisinius Amabilis lived 40 years. Lisinia Callityche, his wife, placed [this] for him, well-deserving.
EDCS-26600968.summary: This funerary inscription records the age at death of Titus Lisinius Amabilis, dedicated by his wife.

EDCS-26600969.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Gaio Longinio Maximo vixit annos LVIII Iulia Afrodisia coniugi bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-26600969.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Gaius Longinius Maximus lived 58 years. Iulia Afrodisia placed [this] for her well-deserving husband.
EDCS-26600969.summary: This funerary inscription records the age at death of Gaius Longinius Maximus, dedicated by his wife.

EDCS-26600970.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus hic pietatis honos haec sunt pia dona mariti cui multum dilecta fui ego Marcellina pro merita cernis quae mihi solus coniux Aelius <q=C>uoque post obitum memor amoris dicat \n
EDCS-26600970.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Here are the honorable and pious gifts of a husband, [for me] Marcellina, who was greatly beloved by him. You see the rewards for my merits; may my husband Aelius, after my death, speak out of remembrance of his love.
EDCS-26600970.summary: This funerary inscription is a lament from a wife, Marcellina, to her deceased husband, Aelius, expressing their mutual love and her merits.

EDCS-26600971.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aeliae Modestae vixit annis LV decurio coniugi pientissimae Dis Manibus Valeriae Felete sic vixit annis LII \n
EDCS-26600971.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aelia Modesta lived 55 years, [dedicated by her] decurio husband, to his most pious wife. To the Spirits of the Departed. Valeria Felete lived 52 years.
EDCS-26600971.summary: This inscription records the ages at death of Aelia Modesta and Valeria Felete, with Aelia's being dedicated by her husband, a decurio.

EDCS-26600972.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Octavio Nepoti vixit annos LXX Iulia Valentina heres coniugi pientissimo faciendum procuravit hic situs est\n
EDCS-26600972.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Gaius Octavius Nepos lived 70 years. Iulia Valentina, his heir, took care to have [this monument] made for her most pious husband. He is buried here.
EDCS-26600972.summary: This funerary inscription records the age at death of Gaius Octavius Nepos, dedicated by his heir and wife, Iulia Valentina.

EDCS-26600973.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Papiriae Quintillae vixit nnis III mensibus XI diebus VII Papirius Quintinus filiae\n
EDCS-26600973.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Papiria Quintilla lived 3 years, 11 months, and 7 days. Papirius Quintinus [dedicated this] for his daughter.
EDCS-26600973.summary: This funerary inscription records the very young age of Papiria Quintilla, dedicated by her father.

EDCS-26600974.summary: This brief funerary inscription records the age at death of Severianus.

EDCS-26600979.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Marcus Aurelius Maximus legulus votum libens animo solvit\n
EDCS-26600979.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest. Marcus Aurelius Maximus, a legionary, gladly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-26600979.summary: This dedication records a votive offering made by Marcus Aurelius Maximus, a legionary, to Jupiter Optimus Maximus.

EDCS-26600980.text_edition: \n Libero Patri sacrum Atrius Maximi votum libens merito solvit\n
EDCS-26600980.translation: Sacred to Liber Pater. Atrius Maximi gladly fulfilled his vow for his deserving effort.
EDCS-26600980.summary: This dedication records a votive offering made by Atrius Maximi to Liber Pater.

EDCS-26600981.text_edition: \n D(is) M(anibus) Avilliae Pietatis domo Aequo vixit annis XXXX Dasas Suttinis coniugi piissimae faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-26600981.translation: To the Divine Shades. Avillia Pietas, from Aequum, lived 40 years. Dasas Suttinis had this made for his most devoted wife.
EDCS-26600981.summary: A tombstone for Avillia Pietas, erected by her husband Dasas Suttinis, likely from the Roman province of Aequum.

EDCS-26600982.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Arria Mamma vixit nnos XXV posuit Beucus servus coniugi bene merenti\n
EDCS-26600982.translation: To the Divine Shades. Arria Mamma lived 25 years. Beucus the slave set this up for his well-deserving wife.
EDCS-26600982.summary: A tombstone for Arria Mamma, erected by her husband Beucus.

EDCS-26600983.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Attio Saturnino vixit annos X Marcus Attius Primitivos sic nepoti posuit\n
EDCS-26600983.translation: To the Divine Shades. Marcus Attius Saturninus lived 10 years. Marcus Attius Primitivos set this up for his grandson.
EDCS-26600983.summary: A tombstone for Marcus Attius Saturninus, erected by his grandfather Marcus Attius Primitivos.

EDCS-26600984.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcus Aurelius Scenobarbus vixit nnos C Marcus Aurelius Si\n
EDCS-26600984.translation: To the Divine Shades. Marcus Aurelius Scenobarbus lived 100 years. Marcus Aurelius Si...
EDCS-26600984.summary: A tombstone for Marcus Aurelius Scenobarbus, who lived to 100 years, with the dedicator partially preserved.

EDCS-26600985.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cassiae Peregrinae Integrae vixit annos XXVII fecit Bisius Scenobarbi Sardias coniugi bene merenti\n
EDCS-26600985.translation: To the Divine Shades. Cassia Peregrina Integra lived 27 years. Bisius Scenobarbi Sardias made this for his well-deserving wife.
EDCS-26600985.summary: A tombstone for Cassia Peregrina Integra, erected by her husband Bisius Scenobarbi Sardias.

EDCS-26600986.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Domitiae Chlone quae vixit annos XXX filia- mat-- bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-26600986.translation: To the Divine Shades. Domitia Chlone, who lived 30 years. Her daughter ... set this up for her well-deserving [mother].
EDCS-26600986.summary: A tombstone for Domitia Chlone, erected by her daughter.

EDCS-26600987.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Maximae Luri vixit nnos XXV Rufus et Tertia fili-- piissim-- \n
EDCS-26600987.translation: To the Divine Shades. Maxima Luri lived 25 years. Rufus and Tertia ... their most devoted [children].
EDCS-26600987.summary: A tombstone for Maxima Luri, erected by her children Rufus and Tertia.

EDCS-26600988.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Plaetoria Maxima vixit annos III Lavius Verzonis neptiae bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-26600988.translation: To the Divine Shades. Plaetoria Maxima lived 3 years. Lavius Verzonis set this up for his well-deserving granddaughter.
EDCS-26600988.summary: A tombstone for Plaetoria Maxima, a child, erected by her grandfather Lavius Verzonis.

EDCS-26600989.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Planio Baezi qui et Magistro vixit annos L Maxima coniugi bene merenti\n
EDCS-26600989.translation: To the Divine Shades. Planio Baezi, also known as Magistro, lived 50 years. Maxima for her well-deserving husband.
EDCS-26600989.summary: A tombstone for Planio Baezi (also Magistro), erected by his wife Maxima.

EDCS-26600990.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Platinonis Verzonis kastello Anso vixit nnos LIIII Rufus Platoris coniugi karissimae pro pietate faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-26600990.translation: To the Divine Shades. Platinonis Verzonis, from Kastello Anso, lived 54 years. Rufus Platoris had this made for his most cherished wife out of devotion.
EDCS-26600990.summary: A tombstone for Platinonis Verzonis, from Kastello Anso, erected by her husband Rufus Platoris.

EDCS-26600991.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Gaio Valerio Viatori annorum XVI Andes Titi et Valeria Terentia filio bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-26600991.translation: To the Divine Shades. Andes Titi and Valeria Terentia set this up for their son Gaius Valerius Viator, aged sixteen, who deserved well of them.
EDCS-26600991.summary: A funerary epitaph for a sixteen-year-old boy named Gaius Valerius Viator, set up by his parents, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600997.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus MOCIO Bov hoc missus veteranus vixit annos Calp MOCVLI Adiutor vixit annos XXIV Terentius filis posuit\n
EDCS-26600997.translation: To the Divine Shades. Moccius Bov, a veteran, lived X years. Calp[urnius?] Moculi Adiutor lived twenty-four years. Terentius, his son, placed this.
EDCS-26600997.summary: A funerary epitaph for a veteran named Moccius Bov and his son Adiutor, who lived 24 years, set up by the latter's son Terentius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600998.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus veteranus ex beneficiario legionis XIII geminae vixit annos LXX ponendum curavit coniunx infelicissima et filii hic situs est\n
EDCS-26600998.translation: To the Divine Shades. A veteran, formerly a beneficiarius of the Legio XIII Gemina, lived seventy years. His most unhappy wife and sons took care to have this placed. He is buried here.
EDCS-26600998.summary: A funerary epitaph for a seventy-year-old veteran of Legio XIII Gemina, erected by his wife and sons, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26601000.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tito Flavio Rufo veterano legionis XI Claudiae piae fidelis domo Amasia vixit annos LX Iulia Maxima coniunx et Flavi Venusta et Maximus et Rufinus filii pro pietate posuerunt hic situs est\n
EDCS-26601000.translation: To the Divine Shades. Titus Flavius Rufus, a veteran of the Legio XI Claudia Pia Fidelis, from Amasia, lived sixty years. His wife Iulia Maxima and his children Flavi Venusta, Maximus, and Rufinus set this up out of piety. He is buried here.
EDCS-26601000.summary: A funerary epitaph for Titus Flavius Rufus, a sixty-year-old veteran from Amasia of Legio XI Claudia Pia Fidelis, set up by his wife and children, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27100064.text_edition: \n Aesculapio et Hygiae Caius Iulius Atianus praefectus equitum ob resti[tut------\n
EDCS-27100064.translation: To Aesculapius and Hygieia. Gaius Iulius Atianus, prefect of horsemen, for the restoration of [his health?]...
EDCS-27100064.summary: A dedication to the gods Aesculapius and Hygieia by Gaius Iulius Atianus, a cavalry prefect, likely commemorating a recovery or restoration, probably from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27100065.text_edition: \n Apollini sacrum Sola Mucatri veteranus alae Frontonianae votum solvit\n
EDCS-27100065.translation: Sacred to Apollo. Sola Mucatri, a veteran of the ala Frontoniana, fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-27100065.summary: A votive inscription to Apollo by Sola Mucatri, a veteran of the ala Frontoniana, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27100067.text_edition: \n Fortunae Reduci Aelius Celer praefectus equitum alae Frontonianae ob restitutionem balinei posuit\n
EDCS-27100067.translation: To Fortuna Redux. Aelius Celer, prefect of horsemen of the ala Frontoniana, set this up for the restoration of the bathhouse.
EDCS-27100067.summary: A dedication to Fortuna Redux by Aelius Celer, a cavalry prefect of the ala Frontoniana, concerning the restoration of a bathhouse, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27100069.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Aurellius sic Vitellianus sesquiplarius pro se et sorore sua votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-27100069.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest. Aurellius Vitellianus, a sesquiplarius, fulfilled his vow for himself and his sister, willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-27100069.summary: A votive inscription to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Aurellius Vitellianus, a sesquiplarius (a rank in the Roman army), for himself and his sister, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27100070.text_edition: \n Libero Patri et Liberae Montanius Viator\n
EDCS-27100070.translation: To Liber Pater and Libera. Montanius Viator.
EDCS-27100070.summary: A dedication to the gods Liber Pater and Libera by Montanius Viator, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27100071.text_edition: \n Marti ala I Tungrorum Frontoniana per Titum Vettulenum Nepotem praefectum equitum votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-27100071.translation: To Mars. The ala I Tungrorum Frontoniana, through Titus Vettulenus Nepos, prefect of horsemen, fulfilled its vow, willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-27100071.summary: A votive inscription to Mars by the ala I Tungrorum Frontoniana, dedicated by its prefect Titus Vettulenus Nepos, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27100073.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurellio sic Antonino Pio Felici Augusto Parthico maximo Britannico maximo pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate XVI consuli IIII optimo maximoque principi ala I Tungrorum Frontoniana Antoniniana numini eius semper ac merito devota\n
EDCS-27100073.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, greatest conqueror of the Parthians, greatest conqueror of the Britons, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 16th time, consul for the 4th time, the best and greatest princeps; to his divine spirit, the First Wing of the Tungri, called Frontoniana Antoniniana, always and deservedly devoted.
EDCS-27100073.summary: This inscription honors Emperor Marcus Aurelius and is dedicated by the Ala I Tungrorum Frontoniana Antoniniana.

EDCS-27200002.text_edition: \n Genio scholae bbeneficiariorum sub Octavio Iuliano legato Augustorum AB APMIHAAN IVTVALN \n
EDCS-27200002.translation: To the genius of the collegium of beneficiarii under Octavius Iulianus, legate of the Emperors, from the auxiliary cohort.
EDCS-27200002.summary: This inscription is dedicated to the genius of the beneficiarii collegium under the legate Octavius Iulianus, likely from the 2nd century AD.

EDCS-27200003.text_edition: \n Herculi invicto pro salute Marci Aureli Antonini Augusti \n
