EDCS-27800819.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Quinto Aurelio Quinti filio Papiria Tertio decurioni et flamini coloniae Sarmizegetusae Quintus Aurelius Saturninus Augustalis coloniae et Proshodus et Logismus liberti et heredes faciendum curaverunt Dis Manibus Quinto Aurelio Quinti filio Papiria Tertio decurioni et flamini coloniae Sarmizegetusae Quintus Aurelius Saturninus Augustalis coloniae et Proshodus et Logismus liberti et heredes faciendum curaverunt Dis Manibus Quinto Aurelio Quinti filio Papiria Tertio decurioni et flamini coloniae Sarmizegetusae Quintus Aurelius Saturninus Augustalis coloniae et Proshodus et Logismus liberti et heredes faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-27800819.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed: For Quintus Aurelius Tertius, son of Quintus, of the Papirian tribe, decurion and priest of the colony of Sarmizegetusa, Quintus Aurelius Saturninus, Augustalis of the colony, and Proshodus and Logismus, freedmen, and his heirs arranged for this to be made. [Repeated text implies a monumental inscription with repeated dedication for Quintus Aurelius Tertius.]
EDCS-27800819.summary: A tombstone inscription for Quintus Aurelius Tertius, a decurion and priest of Sarmizegetusa, erected by his heirs and freedmen, Quintus Aurelius Saturninus and others.

EDCS-27800820.text_edition: \n Marcus Procilius Marci filius Papiria Niceta IIvir et flamen coloniae Sarmizegetusae item sacerdos Laurentium Lavinatium aedem Augustalibus pecunia sua faciendam instituit eandem Marcus Procilius Regulus decurio coloniae equo publico filius et heres eius perfecit dedicavitque\n
EDCS-27800820.translation: Marcus Procilius Niceta, son of Marcus, of the Papirian tribe, twice a chief magistrate and priest of the colony of Sarmizegetusa, also priest of the Laurentes Lavinates, began to build the temple to the Augustales at his own expense; the same was completed and dedicated by Marcus Procilius Regulus, son of Marcus, a decurion of the colony, who held public office, his son and heir.
EDCS-27800820.summary: An inscription recording the building of a temple to the Augustales, initiated by Marcus Procilius Niceta and completed by his son and heir Marcus Procilius Regulus, both officials in Sarmizegetusa.

EDCS-27800821.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publio Pontio Secundino vixit annos X menses III dies XXIII Publio Pontio Blando vixit annos VI dies VII Publius Pontius Pontianus filiis et Valeriae Blandinae coniugi vixit nnos LXV\n
EDCS-27800821.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed: For Publius Pontius Secundinus, who lived 10 years, 3 months, 23 days; for Publius Pontius Blando, who lived 6 years, 7 days; Publius Pontius Pontianus for his sons and for Valeria Blandina, his wife, who lived 65 years.
EDCS-27800821.summary: A tombstone inscription for Publius Pontius Secundinus, Publius Pontius Blando, and Valeria Blandina, giving their ages at death, erected by Publius Pontius Pontianus.

EDCS-28400485.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico Hermadion votum libens posuit\n
EDCS-28400485.translation: To Silvanus Domestricus, Hermadion gladly placed his offering.
EDCS-28400485.summary: A votive inscription dedicated to Silvanus Domestricus by Hermadion.

EDCS-28400486.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Valerio Septimio veteranus legionis V Macedonicae piae constantis vixit nnos LVIII Aelia Iulia coniux et heres pio virginio bene de se merito posuit\n
EDCS-28400486.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed: For Gaius Valerius Septimius, veteran of the Fifth Macedonian Legion, pious and steadfast, who lived 58 years. Aelia Iulia, his wife and heir, placed this in dutiful and loving memory of one who well deserved of her.
EDCS-28400486.summary: A tombstone inscription for Gaius Valerius Septimius, a veteran of the Fifth Macedonian Legion, erected by his wife and heir Aelia Iulia.

EDCS-28400488.text_edition: \n Publius Aelius Attil et Aelius Ati Emeritus Severa coniux faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-28400488.translation: Publius Aelius Attil and Aelius Ati Emeritus had this made, with Severa his wife.
EDCS-28400488.summary: This inscription records the erection of a monument by Publius Aelius Attil and his wife Severa, likely for themselves or a family member, probably in the Imperial period.

EDCS-28400489.text_edition: \n a filia vixit annos II Aelia Aurelia coniugi bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-28400489.translation: Aelia Aurelia placed this for her well-deserving husband, who lived for two years.
EDCS-28400489.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a wife for her husband, indicating a very young age at death, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28400493.text_edition: \n AFI CINNIronius matri piientissimae posuit merenti\n
EDCS-28400493.translation: Cinnironius placed this for his most dutiful mother, who deserved it.
EDCS-28400493.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication by Cinnironius to his devoted mother, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28400494.text_edition: \n ANN Iulia Aristaenete coniugi carissimo memoriam posuit\n
EDCS-28400494.translation: Iulia Aristaenete placed this memorial for her dearest husband.
EDCS-28400494.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication by Iulia Aristaenete for her beloved husband, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28400496.text_edition: \n I A IIIIA coniunx bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-28400496.translation: His wife placed this for the well-deserving [husband], who lived [---].
EDCS-28400496.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication by a wife for her husband, though the name and lifespan are incomplete, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28400499.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Titi Flavi Paulli vixit annos IIII dies XIIII Titus Flavius Pin\n
EDCS-28400499.translation: To the Divine Shades. Titus Flavius Paullus lived for four years and fourteen days. Titus Flavius Pin [set this up].
EDCS-28400499.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a child named Titus Flavius Paullus, set up by Titus Flavius Pin, indicating a short lifespan, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28400506.summary: This is a dedication to the god Apollo, likely part of a temple or altar, possibly from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28400507.text_edition: \n Lucia vixit annos L Flavia bene merenti posuit \n
EDCS-28400507.translation: Lucia lived for fifty years. Flavia placed this for the well-deserving [Lucia].
EDCS-28400507.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Flavia for Lucia, who lived to be fifty, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28400508.text_edition: \n Aelius Valerianus cum Valeriano filio beneficiario consularis voto libens merito posuit\n
EDCS-28400508.translation: Aelius Valerianus, with Valerianus his son, a beneficiary of the consular governor, gladly and deservedly placed this in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-28400508.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Aelius Valerianus and his son Valerianus, who held a position assisting a consular governor, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28400509.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Mavida Epicadi filia vixit annis XXXXV maritus conpari posuit hic sita est\n
EDCS-28400509.translation: To the Divine Shades. Mavida, daughter of Epicadus, lived for forty-five years. Her husband placed this for his partner; she rests here.
EDCS-28400509.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Mavida, daughter of Epicadus, erected by her husband, indicating her age and place of burial, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28400512.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Hermes.

EDCS-28400513.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Titus Silius Ingenuus pro salute sua suorum votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28400513.translation: Titus Silius Ingenuus gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus for the health of himself and his family.
EDCS-28400513.summary: This is a votive inscription by Titus Silius Ingenuus to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, dated to the Imperial period.

EDCS-28400516.summary: This very short inscription appears to record the name of Gaius Iulius, likely on a tombstone or a dedication, from the Imperial period.

EDCS-28400519.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ulpius Te eques alae Sstipendiorum XV vixit annos XXX II V MAC F fratri pientissimo bene merenti\n
EDCS-28400519.translation: To the Divine Spirits: Ulpius Te, a cavalryman of the auxiliary wing, who served for 15 years, lived for 30 years and 2 months. His brother, F., dedicated this, a most devoted gift to his well-deserving brother.
EDCS-28400519.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Ulpius Te, a Roman cavalryman, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-28400523.text_edition: \n Apollini Plator Panentis ex voto castellanis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28400523.translation: To Apollo Plator, from the district of Panentis: the castellan fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-28400523.summary: This is a votive inscription by a castellan to Apollo Plator, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28400524.text_edition: \n Dianae sacrum Celsenius Adiutor magister collegii donum dedit\n
EDCS-28400524.translation: Sacred to Diana: Celsenius Adiutor, the magistrate of the association, gave this offering.
EDCS-28400524.summary: This is a dedication to Diana by Celsenius Adiutor, who was the magistrate of a collegium, probably from the Imperial period.

EDCS-28400525.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Implaius Linsantis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28400525.translation: Implaius Linsantis fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly to Jupiter Optimus Maximus.
EDCS-28400525.summary: This votive inscription by Implaius Linsantis to Jupiter Optimus Maximus is likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28400526.text_edition: \n Iano Gemino Aelius Baebius et Beusas Platoris votum solverunt libentes merito\n
EDCS-28400526.translation: Aelius Baebius and Beusas, son of Plator, willingly and deservedly fulfilled their vows to Janus Geminus.
EDCS-28400526.summary: This is a joint votive inscription to Janus Geminus by Aelius Baebius and Beusas Platoris, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28400527.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo sacrum Verzo Pantonis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28400527.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Optimus Maximus: Verzo Pantonis fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-28400527.summary: This votive inscription by Verzo Pantonis to Jupiter Optimus Maximus is likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28400529.text_edition: \n Silvano Pla Baotius et collegium donum dederunt\n
EDCS-28400529.translation: Pla Baotius and the association gave this offering to Silvanus.
EDCS-28400529.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Silvanus by Pla Baotius and a collegium, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28400531.summary: This short funerary or dedicatory inscription records the action of T. Revseno, likely placing a monument or marker, from an unspecified period.

EDCS-28400533.translation: To the Eternal God Commacenus, Dolichenus [dedicated this].
EDCS-28400533.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the syncretic deity Jupiter Dolichenus, possibly from the 2nd or 3rd century CE, given the deity's popularity during that period.

EDCS-28400534.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Iunoni Reginae Minervae pro salute et victoria et incolumitate Marci Aureli Antonini Pii Felicis Augusti et Iuliae Augustae matris eius et castrorum senatus et patriae Aelius Sostratus procurator\n
EDCS-28400534.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Juno Regina, and Minerva, for the safety, victory, and well-being of Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, and Julia Augusta, mother of him and the camp, the senate, and the fatherland, Aelius Sostratus, procurator, [dedicated this].
EDCS-28400534.summary: This lengthy official dedication to the Capitoline Triad for the welfare of Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus and his mother was set up by Aelius Sostratus, a procurator, likely during Marcus Aurelius's reign (161-180 CE).

EDCS-28400535.text_edition: \n Isidi pro salute Marci Iuli Apollinaris Verus Augusti nostri Romanus Augusti nostri vernae vilicus posuit sic\n
EDCS-28400535.translation: For Isis, for the well-being of Marcus Julius Apollinaris Verus, freedman of our Augustus, Romanus, freedman of our Augustus, the vilicus, placed this, thus:
EDCS-28400535.summary: This dedication to Isis was set up by Romanus, a freedman and steward (vilicus), for the well-being of Marcus Julius Apollinaris Verus, a freedman of the emperor, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28400536.text_edition: \n Saturno Marcus Antonius Saturninus decurio coloniae Saturno Marcus Antonius Saturninus decurio coloniae votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28400536.translation: To Saturn, Marcus Antonius Saturninus, decurion of the colony, fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-28400536.summary: This inscription records Marcus Antonius Saturninus, a decurion in a colony, fulfilling a vow to Saturn, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE, a period when such dedications were common.

EDCS-28400537.text_edition: \n V Marcus Antonius Saturninus decurio coloniae\n
EDCS-28400537.translation: V. Marcus Antonius Saturninus, decurion of the colony.
EDCS-28400537.summary: This fragmentary inscription names Marcus Antonius Saturninus, a decurion of a colony, possibly an epitaph or a record of a public office, from an indeterminate date.

EDCS-28400538.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico sacrum Marcus Opellius Firminus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28400538.translation: Sacred to Silvanus Domesticos. Marcus Opellius Firminus fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-28400538.summary: This votive inscription to Silvanus Domesticos was set up by Marcus Opellius Firminus, likely during the imperial period when the cult of Silvanus was widespread.

EDCS-28400540.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Calventia Asella domo verna vixit annos XXXV posuit Calventia Marcellina patrona libertae bene meritae\n
EDCS-28400540.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Calventia Asella, from Verna, lived 35 years. Calventia Marcellina, patroness, placed this for her well-deserving freedwoman.
EDCS-28400540.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Calventia Asella at age 35 and was erected by her patroness Calventia Marcellina, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28400541.text_edition: \n Claudiae Piae domo Lapiaerom vixit nnos XXXXVII et Claudio Firmino filio eius vixit nnos XIIII menses IIII dies X posuit Capito Triti libertus coniugi et filio piissimis bene merentibus hic siti sunt\n
EDCS-28400541.translation: To Claudia Pia, from Lapia, lived 47 years, and to her son Claudius Firminus, who lived 14 years, 4 months, and 10 days. Capito Triti, her freedman, placed this for his most devoted, well-deserving wife and son. They lie here.
EDCS-28400541.summary: This detailed funerary epitaph records the lives of Claudia Pia and her son Claudius Firminus and was erected by Capito Triti, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28400543.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo cohors II Flavia Commagenorum cui praeest Caius Pomponius Cassianus praefectus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28400543.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus. The II Flavia Commagenorum cohort, commanded by Caius Pomponius Cassianus, the prefect, fulfilled its vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-28400543.summary: This votive inscription records the fulfillment of a vow by the II Flavia Commagenorum cohort under the command of Prefect Caius Pomponius Cassianus, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28400545.text_edition: \n Mercurio sacrum Iulius Arcanus praefectus cohortis II Flaviae Commagenorum votum solvit laetus libens merito Mercurio sacrum Iulius Arcanus praefectus cohortis II Flaviae Commagenorum votum solvit laetus libens merito\n
EDCS-28400545.translation: Sacred to Mercury. Julius Arcanus, prefect of the II Flavian Cohort of Commageni, joyfully, willingly, and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-28400545.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled to the god Mercury by Julius Arcanus, prefect of a cohort, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28400546.text_edition: \n Deo Mercurio Marcus Antonius Sabinianus princeps votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28400546.translation: To the god Mercury. Marcus Antonius Sabinianus, a princeps, joyfully and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-28400546.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled to the god Mercury by Marcus Antonius Sabinianus, identified as a princeps, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28400547.text_edition: \n Deae Nemesi Augustae Aurelia Epictesis pro sal salute sua et suorum ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-28400547.translation: Aurelia Epictesis dedicated this to the August Goddess Nemesis for the health of herself and her family, in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-28400547.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the goddess Nemesis by Aurelia Epictesis for her own and her family's well-being, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28400548.text_edition: \n Nymphis Caius Iulius Iulianus |(centurio) legionis XIII geminae votum solvit\n
EDCS-28400548.translation: To the Nymphs. Caius Julius Julianus, a centurion of the Legio XIII Gemina, fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-28400548.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled to the Nymphs by Caius Julius Julianus, a centurion of the Legio XIII Gemina, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28400551.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico et Mercurio sacrum Aurelius Secundus\n
EDCS-28400551.translation: Sacred to Silvanus Domestus and Mercury. Aurelius Secundus [fulfilled his vow].
EDCS-28400551.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Silvanus Domestus and Mercury by Aurelius Secundus, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28400552.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico Marcus Veracius Euhodus votum\n
EDCS-28400552.translation: To Silvanus Domestus. Marcus Veracius Euhodus [fulfilled his vow].
EDCS-28400552.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled to Silvanus Domestus by Marcus Veracius Euhodus, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28400554.text_edition: \n Deae Syriae Marcus Ulpius Phoebus libens posuit\n
EDCS-28400554.translation: Marcus Ulpius Phoebus joyfully dedicated this to the Syrian Goddess.
EDCS-28400554.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the Syrian Goddess by Marcus Ulpius Phoebus, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28400555.summary: This inscription appears to be a simple dedication or memorial by Gaius? Pomponius Cassianus, with no further context provided.

EDCS-28400558.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelia Hygia vixit annis XVIII Aelius Valentinus coloniae Apulensis flamen libertae et coniugi gratae quam tempus durum rapuit familiamquae simul Dacia te voluit possedit Micia secum have puella multum adque in aevum vale\n
EDCS-28400558.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Aelia Hygia lived 18 years. Aelius Valentinus of the colony of Apula, priest, for his freedwoman and grateful wife, whom harsh fate snatched away, and for the family Micia took with her, farewell, dear girl, and may you live forever.
EDCS-28400558.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Aelia Hygia, who died at 18, set up by Aelius Valentinus, a local priest, for his freedwoman wife, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28400559.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Antonia Quaeta vixit annos XXXIII et Antonia Valentina filia vixit annos XII Antonius Valentinus filius matri et sorori bene merentibus posuit\n
EDCS-28400559.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Antonia Quaeta lived 33 years, and her daughter Antonia Valentina lived 12 years. Antonius Valentinus set this up for his deserving mother and sister.
EDCS-28400559.summary: This tombstone inscription records the deaths of Antonia Quaeta (33) and her daughter Antonia Valentina (12), with the monument erected by Antonius Valentinus, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28400560.text_edition: \n Dis Inferis Manibus Censorinus imaginifer alae Campagonum stipendiorum XX vixitII EVT et Valeria Cara filia VIAVI ET Augus eius \n
EDCS-28400560.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Censorinus, standard-bearer of the Campagonian cavalry wing, with 20 years of service, lived 20 years. And Valeria Cara, his daughter, [dedicated this]...
EDCS-28400560.summary: This funerary inscription records Censorinus, a standard-bearer in the cavalry, and his daughter Valeria Cara, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28400561.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ulpius Mettius veteranus ex decurione alae Claudiae \n
EDCS-28400561.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Ulpius Mettius, a veteran from the cavalry wing Claudia.
EDCS-28400561.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Ulpius Mettius, a veteran cavalry officer, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28400562.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus is Dassi numeri Maurorum Miciensium vixit hic situs est\n
EDCS-28400562.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Dassis, of the Miciensian Mauri cohort, lived; he is buried here.
EDCS-28400562.summary: This funerary inscription records Dassis, a soldier from a cohort of Mauri, who is buried here, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28400564.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lusiae Priscae vixit annos XVII Ulpius Romulus coniugi bene merenti hic sita est\n
EDCS-28400564.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Lusiae Priscae, lived 17 years. Ulpius Romulus, to his well-deserving wife, had her buried here.
EDCS-28400564.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of Lusiae Priscae, who died at 17, by her husband Ulpius Romulus, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28400566.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aureli- Mas Aureli- annorum XXV \n
EDCS-28400566.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Aurelius Mas, aged 25.
EDCS-28400566.summary: This funerary inscription records Aurelius Mas, who died at the age of 25, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28400570.text_edition: \n Caius Iulius Tato deo aeterno votum posuit\n
EDCS-28400570.translation: Gaius Julius Tato dedicated a vow to the eternal god.
EDCS-28400570.summary: This votive inscription records Gaius Julius Tato fulfilling a vow to an unspecified eternal god, likely dating to the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-28400571.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Caius Romanius Summus decurio coloniae votum solvit merito\n
EDCS-28400571.translation: Gaius Romanius Summus, decurion of the colony, rightfully paid a vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus.
EDCS-28400571.summary: This votive inscription records Gaius Romanius Summus, a decurion of the colony, fulfilling a vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, likely dating to the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-28400572.text_edition: \n Nymphis Marcus Statius Priscus legatus Augusti pro praetore votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28400572.translation: Marcus Statius Priscus, legate of the emperor with pro-praetorian authority, willingly and rightfully paid a vow to the Nymphs.
EDCS-28400572.summary: This votive inscription records Marcus Statius Priscus, a high-ranking imperial legate, fulfilling a vow to the Nymphs, likely dating to the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-28400573.text_edition: \n Genio loci pro salute Imperatoris legionis V Macedonicae CVRV curante Iulio Euangeliano praefecto\n
EDCS-28400573.translation: For the welfare of the Emperor and the Fifth Macedonian Legion. Curated by Julius Euangelianus, prefect.
EDCS-28400573.summary: This inscription records a dedication for the safety of the emperor and the Fifth Macedonian Legion, overseen by Julius Euangelianus, a prefect, likely dating to the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-28400577.text_edition: \n ATIIICOES faciendum curaverunt Aurelia Italica vixit annos LX \n
EDCS-28400577.translation: Aurelia Italica lived 60 years. They took care to have this made.
EDCS-28400577.summary: This funerary inscription records Aurelia Italica, who lived 60 years, and mentions that 'they' took care of its construction, likely dating to the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-28400578.text_edition: \n Firmius Florentinus decurio alae Bosporanorum donum dedit\n
EDCS-28400578.translation: Firmius Florentinus, a decurion of the ala Bosporanorum, made this offering.
EDCS-28400578.summary: This is a military dedication, likely from a decurion of the Bosporan cavalry, offering a gift, date uncertain but likely imperial period.

EDCS-28400580.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo A Helico votum libens solvit merito\n
EDCS-28400580.translation: A Helico willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Jupiter Best and Greatest.
EDCS-28400580.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by A. Helico, recording the fulfillment of a vow, date uncertain but likely imperial period.

EDCS-28400582.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Ulpio Tertio decurioni coloniae vixit annos XXI Ulpia Opiava filio piissimo posuit\n
EDCS-28400582.translation: To the Divine Manes. Marcus Ulpius Tertius, a decurion of the colony, lived 21 years. Ulpia Opiava set this up for her most dutiful son.
EDCS-28400582.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Marcus Ulpius Tertius, a decurion of a colony, erected by his mother, Ulpia Opiava, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-28400583.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Aurelio Daedalo vixit annis LX Valeria Cara vixit annis XXXV coniugi optimo Aurelia Tetula vixit annis L Aurelia Augusta vixit annis XXX Caius Aurelius Carus Caius Aurelius Valerianus veteranus parentibus sororibus fecerunt Flavia Crispina Vetilla Maxima Butes eorum\n
EDCS-28400583.translation: To the Divine Manes. Caius Aurelius Daedalus lived 60 years. Valeria Cara lived 35 years. Aurelia Tetula lived 50 years. Caius Aurelius Carus and Caius Aurelius Valerianus, veterans, made this for their parents and sisters; Flavia Crispina, Vetilla Maxima, Butes, and their family.
EDCS-28400583.summary: This is a complex funerary epitaph for multiple family members, including Caius Aurelius Daedalus, his wife Valeria Cara, and their children, with veterans Caius Aurelius Carus and Caius Aurelius Valerianus commissioning the monument, date uncertain but likely imperial period.

EDCS-28400584.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulius Maximus vixit annos XXV Herennia Valentina coniugi carissimo\n
EDCS-28400584.translation: To the Divine Manes. Julius Maximus lived 25 years. Herennia Valentina [set this up] for her dearest husband.
EDCS-28400584.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Julius Maximus, erected by his wife Herennia Valentina, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-28400585.summary: This is a brief epigraph identifying Diogenes as a stonecutter, likely his occupational marker or nameplate, date uncertain.

EDCS-28400586.text_edition: \n Aesculapio et Hygiae Publius Aelius Theimes\n
EDCS-28400586.translation: To Aesculapius and Hygieia, Publius Aelius Theimes [dedicated this].
EDCS-28400586.summary: This is a dedication to the healing gods Aesculapius and Hygieia by Publius Aelius Theimes, date uncertain but likely imperial period.

EDCS-28400589.text_edition: \n Aesculapio et Hygiae Quintus Axius Aelianus iunior votum pro patris incolumitate susceptum cum gratulatione libens solvit Ioni Ionius\n
EDCS-28400589.translation: Quintus Axius Aelianus the younger willingly and with gratitude fulfilled his vow for his father's well-being, made to Aesculapius and Hygieia. [Dedicated by] Ioni Ionius.
EDCS-28400589.summary: This is a votive inscription to Aesculapius and Hygieia by Quintus Axius Aelianus the younger, fulfilling a vow for his father's health, possibly with a dedication by Ioni Ionius, date uncertain but likely imperial period.

EDCS-28400590.text_edition: \n Deo Aeterno Aurelius Marinus Marini filius vexillarius collegii fabrorum pro salute sua et suorum ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-28400590.translation: For the Eternal God, Aurelius Marinus, son of Marinus, a standard-bearer of the guild of smiths, set this up according to his vow for his own health and that of his family.
EDCS-28400590.summary: This is a votive inscription for the 'Eternal God' (likely a syncretic deity) by Aurelius Marinus, a standard-bearer of the smiths' guild, for his and his family's well-being, date uncertain but likely imperial period.

EDCS-28400591.text_edition: \n Deo Aeterno Herennius Gemellinus vir egregius procurator Auggustorum nnnostrorum pro Ael(ia) Saturnina coniuge et Herenniis Urso et Gemellino et Supero Saturnino votum libens solvit\n
EDCS-28400591.translation: Herennius Gemellinus, an outstanding man and procurator of our Emperors, willingly fulfilled his vow for Aelia Saturnina his wife, and for Herennius Urso, Gemellinus, and Superus Saturninus.
EDCS-28400591.summary: This is a votive inscription by Herennius Gemellinus, a high-ranking imperial official (procurator), dedicating a vow for his wife and children, likely from the later imperial period.

EDCS-28400592.text_edition: \n Dianae Augustae sacrum Ianuarius Auggustorum libertus nummularius ex voto locus datus decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-28400592.translation: Sacred to Diana Augusta. Ianuarius, freedman of the emperors and a money changer, dedicated this from a vow, a site granted by decree of the town councillors.
EDCS-28400592.summary: This is a dedication to Diana Augusta by a freedman money changer, likely from a Roman colony, around the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-28400593.text_edition: \n Eponabus et Campestribus sacrum Marcus Calventius Viator |(centurio) legionis IIII Flaviae Felicis exerciator equitum singularium Cai Avidi Nigrini legati Augusti pro praetore votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28400593.translation: Sacred to Epona and the Campestres. Marcus Calventius Viator, centurion of the IV Flavia Felix legion, trainer of the elite cavalry of the governor Caius Avidius Niger, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-28400593.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the goddesses Epona and Campestres by a centurion serving as a cavalry trainer under a governor, likely in the late 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-28400594.text_edition: \n Genio decuriae XIII collegii fabrum Marcus Ulpius Sa\n
EDCS-28400594.translation: To the Genius of the thirteenth cohort of the guild of builders. Marcus Ulpius Sa...
EDCS-28400594.summary: This is a partial dedication to the Genius of a specific cohort within a builders' guild, likely from the Roman Empire period.

EDCS-28400595.text_edition: \n Genio libertorum et servorum Publius Publicius Anthus et Publicius Cletus donum dederunt dedicaverunt\n
EDCS-28400595.translation: To the Genius of the freedmen and slaves. Publius Publicius Anthus and Publicius Cletus gave and dedicated this gift.
EDCS-28400595.summary: This inscription records a gift dedicated to the Genius of freedmen and slaves by two individuals, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28400596.text_edition: \n Deae Reginae Marcus Cominius Quintus equo publico pontifex et quinquennalis coloniae et Antonia Valentina eius pro salute Claudiae Valentinae templum a solo fecerunt\n
EDCS-28400596.translation: To the goddess Regina. Marcus Cominius Quintus, priest and quinquennial of the colony, and his wife Antonia Valentina, built this temple from the ground up for the health of Claudia Valentina.
EDCS-28400596.summary: This inscription records the construction of a temple to the goddess Regina by a prominent local official and his wife for the well-being of another individual, likely in a Roman colony during the Imperial period.

EDCS-28400597.text_edition: \n Deae Reginae Aelia Primitiva ex voto pro Aelio Metrodoro patrono suo votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28400597.translation: To the goddess Regina. Aelia Primitiva willingly and deservedly fulfilled her vow for her patron Aelius Metrodorus.
EDCS-28400597.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Aelia Primitiva to the goddess Regina for the well-being of her patron, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28400598.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Antonius Priscus ex voto\n
EDCS-28400598.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest. Antonius Priscus, from a vow.
EDCS-28400598.summary: This is a simple votive dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Antonius Priscus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28400599.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Aurelius Valens Flavius Zora decuriones collegii fabrum ex voto posuerunt\n
EDCS-28400599.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest. Aurelius Valens and Flavius Zora, town councillors of the guild of builders, set this up from a vow.
EDCS-28400599.summary: This inscription records a votive dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by two town councillors who were members of a builders' guild, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28400600.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest. Aurelius Caius...
EDCS-28400600.summary: This is a partial dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Aurelius Caius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28400601.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Aeterno pro salute Ianuari Augusti ex arcario et Vitiae Threptes eius Flavius Bellicus ex voto\n
EDCS-28400601.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, the Eternal. Flavius Bellicus, from a vow, for the health of Ianuarius Augustus, from the public treasury, and his wife Vitia Threptes.
EDCS-28400601.summary: This inscription records a votive dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Flavius Bellicus, funded from the public treasury, for the health of Ianuarius Augustus and his wife, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28400602.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Aeterno Quintus Attius Anthimus Augustalis coloniae votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28400602.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Aeternus, Quintus Attius Anthimus, an Augustalis of the colony, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-28400602.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Jupiter by Quintus Attius Anthimus, an Augustalis, likely in the late 1st or early 2nd century CE.

EDCS-28400603.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo aeterno Titus Flavius Aper scriba coloniae ex voto\n
EDCS-28400603.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Aeternus, Titus Flavius Aper, scribe of the colony, fulfills his vow.
EDCS-28400603.summary: This inscription records a votive offering to Jupiter by Titus Flavius Aper, a scribe of the colony, probably dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-28400604.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno Gaius Gaianus et Proculus Apollofanes Suri negotiatores pro salute sua ex voto\n
EDCS-28400604.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus, Gaius Gaianus and Proculus Apollofanes, merchants of Suri, for their own health, fulfill their vow.
EDCS-28400604.summary: This inscription records a vow made by two merchants, Gaius Gaianus and Proculus Apollofanes, to Jupiter Dolichenus for their well-being, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-28400605.text_edition: \n Aurelius Annianus Deo Libero ex suo dedit\n
EDCS-28400605.translation: Aurelius Annianus dedicated this to the god Liber from his own resources.
EDCS-28400605.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Aurelius Annianus to the god Liber, possibly from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28400606.translation: Titus Flavius Aper fulfills his vow.
EDCS-28400606.summary: This brief inscription records Titus Flavius Aper fulfilling a vow, potentially related to inscription EDCS-28400603.

EDCS-28400607.text_edition: \n Minervae Augustae Caius Sempronius Urbanus procurator Augusti\n
EDCS-28400607.translation: To Minerva Augusta, Gaius Sempronius Urbanus, procurator of Augustus.
EDCS-28400607.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Minerva Augusta by Gaius Sempronius Urbanus, an imperial procurator, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-28400609.text_edition: \n Invicto deo Serapidi et Isidi frugiferae pro salute atque incolumitate Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Aureli Antonini Pii Felicis Augusti Parthici maximi pontificis maximi Brittannici maximi VG FS \n
EDCS-28400609.translation: To the unconquered god Serapis and the fruitful Isis, for the health and safety of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, greatest conqueror of Parthia, chief priest, greatest conqueror of Britain, may the gods protect.
EDCS-28400609.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Serapis and Isis for the well-being of Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, likely dating to his reign (161-180 CE).

EDCS-28400610.text_edition: \n Silvano Quartus milis sic legionis XIII geminae votum reddedit\n
EDCS-28400610.translation: Quartus, a soldier of the 13th Gemina legion, rendered his vow.
EDCS-28400610.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Quartus, a soldier of the Legio XIII Gemina, probably from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-28400612.translation: Severus, freedman of Augustus, to Cautopates.
EDCS-28400612.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Cautopates by Severus, an imperial freedman, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28400613.summary: This short inscription records Aelius Nepos fulfilling a vow, potentially personal or connected to a larger cultic practice.

EDCS-28400614.translation: Long, a transport worker, dedicated this in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-28400614.summary: This is a votive dedication by a man named Long, identified as a 'salariarius' (likely a transport worker or porter), possibly dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28400615.translation: Aurelius Valentinus dedicated this in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-28400615.summary: This is a votive dedication by Aurelius Valentinus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28400618.text_edition: \n Cassius Maximus Augustalis coloniae Sarmizegetusae Marcus Ulpius Gaius pro salute sua ex voto posuerunt\n
EDCS-28400618.translation: Cassius Maximus, Augustalis of the colony of Sarmizegetusa, and Marcus Ulpius Gaius dedicated this for their own well-being in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-28400618.summary: This is a joint votive dedication by Cassius Maximus and Marcus Ulpius Gaius, both officials ('Augustalis') in the colony of Sarmizegetusa, for their health.

EDCS-28400620.text_edition: \n Deo Invicto Mithrae Iulius M Priscus\n
EDCS-28400620.translation: To the Unconquered God Mithras, Iulius M. Priscus dedicated this.
EDCS-28400620.summary: This is a dedication to the god Mithras by Iulius M. Priscus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28400621.text_edition: \n Soli Invicto Mithrae Caius Spedius Valerianus Augustalis coloniae donum dedit\n
EDCS-28400621.translation: Caius Spedius Valerianus, Augustalis of the colony, gave this gift to the Unconquered Sun Mithras.
EDCS-28400621.summary: This is a dedication of a gift by Caius Spedius Valerianus, an 'Augustalis' of the colony, to the Unconquered Sun Mithras.

EDCS-28400623.text_edition: \n Soli Invicto Mithrae Valerius V votum libens posuit\n
EDCS-28400623.translation: Valerius V willingly dedicated this vow to the Unconquered Sun Mithras.
EDCS-28400623.summary: This is a votive dedication by Valerius V to the Unconquered Sun Mithras, indicating a willing fulfillment of a vow.

EDCS-28400624.text_edition: \n T Gaius us ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-28400624.translation: T. Gaius dedicated this in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-28400624.summary: This is a votive dedication by T. Gaius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28400625.text_edition: \n Nabarze deo pro salute Ampliati Augusti nostri dispensatoris et sua suorumque omnium Protas vikarius sic eius\n
EDCS-28400625.translation: Protas, the deputy official (vicarius) of Ampliatus, steward (dispensator) of our Emperor Augustus, dedicated this to the god Nabarze for the well-being of Ampliatus, himself, and all their families, as seen in a vision.
EDCS-28400625.summary: This is a dedication to the god Nabarze by Protas, a deputy official, for the health of his superior Ampliatus and their families, possibly related to a vision.

EDCS-28400626.text_edition: \n Soli Invicto Mithrae Carpion Augusti libertus tabularius votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28400626.translation: Carpion, freedman of the Emperor and a clerk ('tabularius'), willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to the Unconquered Sun Mithras.
EDCS-28400626.summary: This is a votive dedication by Carpion, an imperial freedman and clerk, to the Unconquered Sun Mithras.

EDCS-28400627.text_edition: \n Flavius Trofimus sic ex viso posuit\n
EDCS-28400627.translation: Flavius Trofimus dedicated this as seen in a vision.
EDCS-28400627.summary: This is a dedication by Flavius Trofimus, recorded as being made after a vision.

EDCS-28400628.text_edition: \n MMarcii Ulpii Victorinus et Maius deccuriones coloniae Sarmizegetusae metropolis votum libenter posuerunt\n
EDCS-28400628.translation: The decurions Marcus Marcius Victorinus and Marcus Ulpius Maius of the colony of Sarmizegetusa metropolis willingly set up this vow.
EDCS-28400628.summary: This inscription records a vow willingly set up by two decurions of the colony of Sarmizegetusa Metropolis, likely from the 2nd-3rd century CE.

EDCS-28400631.text_edition: \n Philotimus pro se et suis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28400631.translation: Philotimus paid his vow willingly and deservedly for himself and his family.
EDCS-28400631.summary: This inscription records a vow paid by Philotimus for himself and his family, indicating a personal dedication, likely from the 2nd-3rd century CE.

EDCS-28400638.text_edition: \n Soli Invicto Lucius Domitius Primanus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28400638.translation: Lucius Domitius Primanus paid his vow willingly and deservedly to the Unconquered Sun.
EDCS-28400638.summary: This inscription records a vow paid by Lucius Domitius Primanus to the god Sol Invictus, likely from the 2nd-3rd century CE.

EDCS-28400639.translation: Lucius Valerius Eutr... set up (this vow?).
EDCS-28400639.summary: This inscription records a partial dedication by Lucius Valerius Eutr..., possibly a vow, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28400640.text_edition: \n Diis Patriis Malagbel et Bebellahamon et Benefal et Manavat Publius Aelius Theimes IIviralis coloniae templum fecit solo et inpendio suo pro se suisque omnibus ob pietate ipsorum circa se iussus ab ipsis fecit et culinam subiunxit\n
EDCS-28400640.translation: To the Father Gods Malagbel, Bebellahamon, Benefal, and Manavat, Publius Aelius Theimes, duovir of the colony, built this temple at his own expense and on his own land, because of their devotion towards him, having been ordered by them, and added a kitchen.
EDCS-28400640.summary: This inscription records the construction of a temple and an adjacent kitchen by Publius Aelius Theimes, a duovir of the colony, dedicated to several local deities and built at his own expense, likely from the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28400641.text_edition: \n Deo Sancto Malagbelo pro salute Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Aureli Severi Alexandri Pii Felicis Augusti et Iuliae Mamaeae Augustae matri Augusti nostri et castrorum Primitivos sic Augusti libertus tabularius provinciae Daciae Apulensis posuit\n
EDCS-28400641.translation: For the health of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus, and Julia Mamaea Augusta, mother of our Augustus, and of the Praetorian Guard, Primitivos, freedman of Augustus and registrar of the province of Dacia Apulensis, set up this (dedication) to the Holy God Malagbel.
EDCS-28400641.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Primitivos, a freedman and registrar, to the god Malagbel for the well-being of Emperor Alexander Severus and his mother Julia Mamaea, dating to Alexander Severus' reign (222-235 CE).

EDCS-28400645.text_edition: \n Tiberius Claudius Ianuarius Augustalis coloniae patronus decuriae I picturam porticus et accubitum item Claudius Verus filius eius ob honorem dupli proporticum et culinam et frontalem ex suo fecerunt\n
EDCS-28400645.translation: Tiberius Claudius Ianuarius, Augustalis and patron of the colony, (funded) the pictorial decoration of the portico and the dining area for the first decuria. Likewise, Claudius Verus, his son, for the honor of the second decuria, (funded) the portico and the dining area and the facade, at their own expense.
EDCS-28400645.summary: This inscription records civic contributions by Tiberius Claudius Ianuarius and his son Claudius Verus to decorate a portico and dining area for different decuriae within a colony, likely in the 2nd-3rd century CE.

EDCS-28400646.text_edition: \n Claudianus ex Khara sic eorum numini dicatus votum posuit\n
EDCS-28400646.translation: Claudianus from Khara, thus dedicated to their divinity, set up his vow.
EDCS-28400646.summary: This inscription records a vow set up by Claudianus from Khara, who dedicated himself to local deities, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28400648.text_edition: \n ------ pro sal]ute tinae et Prociliorum Flores sic Ingenui filiorum Marcus Procilius Primus libertus eius ex voto\n
EDCS-28400648.translation: ------ for the health] of Tina and the Procilian family, Marcus Procilius Primus, her freedman, (set this up) in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-28400648.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Marcus Procilius Primus, a freedman, for the health of Tina and members of the Procilius family, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28400656.text_edition: \n Publio Licinio Cornelio Valeriano nobilissimo Caesari filio Imperatoris Caesaris Publi Licini Egnati Gallieni Pii Felicis invicti Augusti colonia Ulpia Traiana Augusta Dacica Sarmizegetusa metropolis publice\n
EDCS-28400656.translation: The colony Ulpia Traiana Augusta Dacica Sarmizegetusa metropolis, publicly (dedicated this) to Publius Licinius Cornelius Valerianus, most noble Caesar, son of the Emperor Caesar Publius Licinius Egnatius Gallienus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus.
EDCS-28400656.summary: This inscription records a public dedication by the colony of Sarmizegetusa to Publius Licinius Cornelius Valerianus, son of Emperor Gallienus, likely during Gallienus' reign (253-260 CE).

EDCS-28400658.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelio Cimioni qui vixit nnos menses VI dies XI Synda scriba tabularii filio dulcissimo et pientissimo posuit\n
EDCS-28400658.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Synda, scribe of the record office, set this up for her most beloved and dutiful son, Aurelius Cimionius, who lived 36 years, 6 months, and 11 days.
EDCS-28400658.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aurelius Cimionius, set up by his mother Synda, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-28400660.text_edition: \n I dies XXIIII VIEs libertus Auggustorum et Dubitata DDN\n
EDCS-28400660.translation: For 24 days. Vies, freedman of the Emperors, and Dubitata, in accordance with their vow, dedicated this.
EDCS-28400660.summary: A dedication by Vies and Dubitata, possibly related to a public work or offering, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-28400661.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Iulio Euchari familia consularis \n
EDCS-28400661.translation: To the Divine Spirits. For Gaius Julius Eucharius, of senatorial family.
EDCS-28400661.summary: A funerary inscription for Gaius Julius Eucharius, noting his senatorial status, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-28400663.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aureliae Donatae vixit nnos LV Marcus Ulpius Marteialis sic veteranus et decurio coloniae Sarmizegetusae metropolis coniugi rarissimae\n
EDCS-28400663.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Marcus Ulpius Martialis, a veteran and decurion of the capital colony of Sarmizegetusa, set this up for his most rare wife, Aurelia Donata, who lived 55 years.
EDCS-28400663.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aurelia Donata, set up by her husband, a veteran and local official in Dacia Superior, dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28400665.text_edition: \n Marcus Iulius Papiria Iustus decurio coloniae ob honorem pontificatus campum cum suis aditibus clusit sic et statuam posuit\n
EDCS-28400665.translation: Marcus Julius Iustus Papirius, decurion of the colony, enclosed the field with its entrances in honor of his pontificate and also erected a statue.
EDCS-28400665.summary: An honorary inscription for Marcus Julius Iustus Papirius, a local official who dedicated a field and statue in honor of his priestly office, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-28400666.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Pomponiae Severae filiae annorumet Anclareniae Marcellinae privignae annorum XVIII Marcus Pomponius Severus coniugi carissimae cum filiis\n
EDCS-28400666.translation: To the Divine Spirits. For Pomponia Severa, his daughter, and for Anclarenia Marcellina, his stepdaughter, aged 18. Marcus Pomponius Severus, with his sons, [set this up] for his dearest wife.
EDCS-28400666.summary: A funerary epitaph for Pomponia Severa and her stepdaughter Anclarenia Marcellina, set up by Marcus Pomponius Severus for his wife, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-28400667.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Suronio Adrasto Augustali coloniae vixit annos L et Serviliae Primitivae coniugi vixit annos XL Adrastus Marcus Titianus LII\n
EDCS-28400667.translation: To the Divine Spirits. For Marcus Suronius Adrastus, Augustalis of the colony, who lived 50 years, and for Servilia Primitiva, his wife, who lived 40 years. Adrastus Marcus Titianus [set this up].
EDCS-28400667.summary: A funerary epitaph for Marcus Suronius Adrastus and his wife Servilia Primitiva, set up by their son, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-28400669.text_edition: \n Ave viator Dis Manibus Antoniae F SEC\n
EDCS-28400669.translation: Hail, traveler. To the Divine Spirits. For Antonia FSEC.
EDCS-28400669.summary: A funerary epitaph for Antonia FSEC, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-28400670.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelio Volusiano TVMIA Catu POM TZI O vixit annos XVI cum pietate sua ECIA CATES Timocrates Augusti libertus et Aurelia Ireni mater filio posuerunt\n
EDCS-28400670.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Timocrates, freedman of Augustus, and Aurelia Irena set this up for their son Aurelius Volusianus, who lived 16 years with his piety.
EDCS-28400670.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aurelius Volusianus, set up by his parents, one of whom was a freedman of the emperor, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-28400671.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ulpiae Dubitatae vixit nnos LX et Ulpio Gemellino vixit nnos LX parentibus et Iustiae Ingenuae vixit annos XXX coniugi et Iustiae Sextiliae filiae Ulpii Dubitatus et Gemellinus \n
EDCS-28400671.translation: To the Divine Spirits. For Ulpia Dubitata, who lived 60 years, and for Ulpius Gemellinus, who lived 60 years, parents, and for Iustia Ingenua, who lived 30 years, wife, and for Iustia Sextilia, daughter. Ulpius Dubitatus and Gemellinus [set this up].
EDCS-28400671.summary: A funerary epitaph for Ulpia Dubitata, Ulpius Gemellinus, Iustia Ingenua, and Iustia Sextilia, set up by Ulpius Dubitatus and Gemellinus, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-28400673.summary: This fragmented inscription likely records the name and priestly office of Valerius, possibly from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-28400674.text_edition: \n vixit nnos XVIII Ulpius C coniugi\n
EDCS-28400674.translation: Ulpius C. lived 18 years for his wife.
EDCS-28400674.summary: This epitaph records the lifespan of Ulpius C., who died at 18, dedicated by his wife.

EDCS-28400678.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno Iulius Valentinus flamen municipii Tibiscensium pro salutem sic suam sic suorumque omnium contubernalium votum libens merito posuit\n
EDCS-28400678.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus, Iulius Valentinus, priest of the municipality of Tibiscensium, willingly and deservedly set up this votive offering for the safety of himself and all his comrades.
EDCS-28400678.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Dolichenus by Iulius Valentinus, a priest of the municipality of Tibiscensium, for the well-being of himself and his companions, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28400682.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus MAR et heres mearitis suis MARTI I BIAIV vixit annos XXXII menses II rissimo bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-28400682.translation: To the Divine Shades. For his deserving wife, MARTI I BIAIV lived 32 years, 2 months. He dedicated this to his most deserving wife.
EDCS-28400682.summary: This epitaph records the lifespan of a woman named MARTI I BIAIV, who lived 32 years and 2 months, and was dedicated by her husband, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28400683.text_edition: \n Aurelio Aciliano et HE FIL NIN vixit annos XVIIII\n
EDCS-28400683.translation: For Aurelius Acilianus and his son... lived 19 years.
EDCS-28400683.summary: This epitaph records the age of death of a young boy, 19 years old, and is likely dedicated to Aurelius Acilianus and his son.

EDCS-28400684.text_edition: \n Decimus Baebatius Chrysantus Augustalis coloniae Zermicegetusae sic vixit nnos LXVI hic situs est proscriptus per Baebatiam Gamicen coniugi et CO et comheredibus IIII\n
EDCS-28400684.translation: Decimus Baebatius Chrysantus, an Augustalis of the colony of Zermicegetusa, lived 66 years. He is buried here, assigned by Baebatia Gamicen, to his wife and... and four co-heirs.
EDCS-28400684.summary: This epitaph records the death and burial of Decimus Baebatius Chrysantus, an Augustalis of Zermicegetusa, who lived 66 years, and was interred by his wife and co-heirs, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28400685.text_edition: \n Aeliae Lepidae vixit annis LXX Aelius Abascantus coniugi pientissimae posuit PVMIA\n
EDCS-28400685.translation: Aelius Abascantus placed this for his most devoted wife, Aelia Lepida, who lived 70 years.
EDCS-28400685.summary: This epitaph records the lifespan of Aelia Lepida, who lived 70 years, and was dedicated by her husband, Aelius Abascantus, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-28400686.text_edition: \n Dianae Augustae aram Lucius Samognatius Tertius Trever votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28400686.translation: Lucius Samognatius Tertius from Trier willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to the August Diana.
EDCS-28400686.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Diana Augusta by Lucius Samognatius Tertius from Trier, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28400687.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Lucius Sam(ognatius Tertius ex viso votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28400687.translation: Lucius Samognatius Tertius, by vision, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus.
EDCS-28400687.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Lucius Samognatius Tertius, who claims to have had a divine vision, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28400688.text_edition: \n Matri Deum Magnae Abuccia Claudiana votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28400688.translation: Abuccia Claudiana willingly and deservedly fulfilled her vow to the Great Mother of the Gods.
EDCS-28400688.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to the Great Mother of the Gods (Magna Mater) by Abuccia Claudiana, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28400689.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus, son of the deified Hadrian, grandson of the deified Trajan the Parthian, great-grandson of the deified Nerva.
EDCS-28400689.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Antoninus Pius, listing his imperial titles and ancestry, likely from the mid-2nd century CE.

EDCS-28400692.text_edition: \n Turmasgada Maximus Maximinus et Iulianus Maximinus ex voto posuerunt\n
EDCS-28400692.translation: Turmasgada Maximus Maximinus and Iulianus Maximinus erected this in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-28400692.summary: This is a votive dedication by two individuals, Turmasgada Maximus Maximinus and Iulianus Maximinus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28400694.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Iulius Philippus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus tribunicia potestate V cons(ul) III pater patriae proconsul et Marcus Iulius Philippus iunior imperator cons(ul) II proconsul princeps iuventutis pater patriae Pius Felix Augustus et Marcia Otacilia Severa sanctissima Augusta nostra restitutores orbis totius ob tutelam civitatis coloniae suae Romulae circitum muri manu militari a solo fecerunt\n
EDCS-28400694.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Iulius Philippus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, with tribunician power for the 5th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the fatherland, proconsul, and Marcus Iulius Philippus the Younger, emperor, consul for the 2nd time, proconsul, prince of the youth, father of the fatherland, Pius Felix Augustus, and Marcia Otacilia Severa, our most holy Augusta, restorers of the entire world, built the circuit of the wall for the defense of their city, the colony of Romula, from the ground up by military force.
EDCS-28400694.summary: This inscription records the rebuilding of a city wall by Emperors Philip the Elder and Philip the Younger, along with Empress Otacilia Severa, likely in the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-28400695.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelius Germanus veteranus vixit annis LXX Aelius Germanus decurio municipii fratri bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-28400695.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aelius Germanus, a veteran, lived for 70 years. Aelius Germanus, a decurion of the municipium, erected this for his well-deserving brother.
EDCS-28400695.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Aelius Germanus, a veteran and brother of a decurion, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28400696.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelio Valeriano veterano ex duplicario vixit annos LX et Aelio Titiano veterano vixit annos LXX et Aelio Valeriano filio vixit annos L Aelius Candidinus tesserarius legionis XIII geminae et Aelius Veteranus filii posuerunt\n
EDCS-28400696.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aelius Valerianus, a veteran and former duplicarius, lived for 60 years, and Aelius Titianus, a veteran, lived for 70 years, and Aelius Valerianus, their son, lived for 50 years. Aelius Candidinus, a tesserarius of the Legio XIII Gemina, and Aelius Veteranus, his sons, erected this.
EDCS-28400696.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for three members of the Aelius family, including two veterans and a son, erected by two other sons, one a soldier, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28400698.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Titus Patulcius Severus vixit annos \n
EDCS-28400698.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Titus Patulcius Severus lived for...
EDCS-28400698.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph for Titus Patulcius Severus, indicating his name and lifespan, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28400699.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelius Valens qui et Esbenus vixit annos LXXX Sira coniux et Fortunatus libertus patrono bene merenti faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-28400699.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aelius Valens, also known as Esbenus, lived for 80 years. His wife Sira and his freedman Fortunatus arranged for this to be made for their well-deserving patron.
EDCS-28400699.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Aelius Valens (Esbenus), set up by his wife and freedman, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28400704.text_edition: \n legionis VII Claudiae piae fidelis Philippianae |(centuriae) VIIII hastati prioris signifer Aurelius Probus optio Publius Aelius Victorinus \n
EDCS-28400704.translation: From the Legio VII Claudia Pia Fidelis Philippiana: from the 9th century, hastatus prior; standard-bearer Aurelius Probus; optio Publius Aelius Victorinus.
EDCS-28400704.summary: This inscription likely names soldiers of the Legio VII Claudia, specifying their rank and possibly their century, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28400705.text_edition: \n |(centurio) Ferox militavit annos XXV eques annos XVIII vixit annos VIIL Marcus Pompeius Proculus frater beneficiarius Tironis legati heres bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-28400705.translation: Centurion Ferox served for 25 years, as an equestrian for 18 years, and lived for 78 years. Marcus Pompeius Proculus, brother, beneficiarius of the legate, heir, erected this for the well-deserving.
EDCS-28400705.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a centurion named Ferox, detailing his service and lifespan, erected by his brother and heir, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28400718.text_edition: \n Imperatore Caesare Caio Vivio sic Traboniano sic Gallo Pio Felici Augusto patre patriae et Imperatore Caesare Caio Vivio sic Afinio Gallo Veldominiano sic Volusiano Augusto patre patriae ab Apulo milia passuum XLV\n
EDCS-28400718.translation: Under Emperor Caesar Gaius Vivius Trabonianus Gallus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, father of the fatherland, and Emperor Caesar Gaius Vivius Afinius Gallus Velduminianus Volusianus Augustus, father of the fatherland, 45 miles from Apulum.
EDCS-28400718.summary: This inscription records a distance marker from Apulum, mentioning the joint reign of Emperors Trebonianus Gallus and Volusianus, from the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-28400721.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo D<o=V>licheno Publius Caius Valerianus |(centurio) legionis X <f=E>r<e=A>tensis RESRNERIDONIS votum solvit libens merito \n
EDCS-28400721.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, and to Solichenus, Publius Caius Valerianus, centurion of the Tenth Legion Gemina, Fullo, fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-28400721.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter and Solichenus made by a centurion of the Tenth Legion, likely a soldier's votive offering.

EDCS-28400722.text_edition: \n Publius Aelius Paulinus templum instituit pro se suorumque salute Genio sancto scholae decurionum\n
EDCS-28400722.translation: Publius Aelius Paulinus established this temple for the health of himself and his family, to the sacred Genius of the college of decurions.
EDCS-28400722.summary: This records the construction of a temple by Publius Aelius Paulinus, dedicated to the Genius of the decurions for the well-being of his family.

EDCS-28400723.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Iunoni Re<g=C>inae Valerius Avitus veteranus votum solvit libens merito \n
EDCS-28400723.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, and to Juno Regina, Valerius Avitus, a veteran, fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-28400723.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter and Juno Regina made by a veteran, likely a soldier's votive offering.

EDCS-28400724.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico Aul(us Cutius Secundanus pro se et suis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28400724.translation: To Silvanus Domesticus, Aulus Cutius Secundanus fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly for himself and his family.
EDCS-28400724.summary: This is a dedication to Silvanus Domesticus made by Aulus Cutius Secundanus for the welfare of his family, likely a soldier's votive offering.

EDCS-28400725.text_edition: \n Gaius Iulius V stipendiorum XV stipendiorum XV et Iulius \n
EDCS-28400725.translation: Gaius Julius V with 15 years of service, 15 years of service, and Julius
EDCS-28400725.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription mentioning Gaius Julius V and Julius, possibly recording military service or honors.

EDCS-28400726.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription mentioning the deity Dolichenus, likely part of a longer dedication.

EDCS-28400728.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Blasa eques stipendiorum Aureli-- Tsinta vixit XXXI Aureli-- Aeli \n
EDCS-28400728.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld, Blasa, an equestrian of Aurelian descent with 31 years of service, died at the age of 31. Aurelius [son of?] Tsinta, Aurelius [son of?] Aelius.
EDCS-28400728.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Blasa, an equestrian who served for 31 years, mentioning her age at death and the names of relatives.

EDCS-28400730.text_edition: \n pro sal(ute) Cai Iuli Castini legati Augusti pro praetore Lucius Antonius Marinianus praefectus cohortis V Lingonum Antoninianae cum Lucio Antonio Marino filio suo Iulio Antonino fratre ex tribuno \n
EDCS-28400730.translation: For the health of Gaius Julius Castinus, legate of the emperor with praetorian power, Lucius Antonius Marinianus, prefect of the Fifth Cohort of Lingones Antoniniana, with his son Lucius Antonius Marinus and his brother Julius Antoninus, formerly a tribune.
EDCS-28400730.summary: This records a dedication for the health of an imperial legate by a cohort prefect, his son, and his brother, likely from the late 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28400731.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Eustinae Afri vixit XXX Aurelius Iustinus |(centurio bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-28400731.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld, for Eustina Afri, who lived 30 years, Aurelius Iustinus, centurion, erected [this] for her who deserved well.
EDCS-28400731.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Aurelius Iustinus, a centurion, for Eustina Afri, his deceased wife or relative.

EDCS-28400734.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Antonio Eroti patri vixit annos LIIIIVIII posuerunt Antonia Iulia et Antonius Eros filii bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-28400734.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld, for Antonius Eros, father, who lived 53 (or 58) years, his children Antonia Iulia and Antonius Eros erected [this] for him who deserved well.
EDCS-28400734.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by children for their father, Antonius Eros, who lived to be 53 or 58 years old.

EDCS-28400735.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aureli Masinni duplicari alae |(miliariae) vixit annis XXXVI Iulia Severa coniux et Flavius Saturninus heres ob merita posuerunt faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-28400735.translation: To the departed spirits. Aurelius Masinnus, a duplicarius of an auxiliary cavalry unit, lived 36 years. Iulia Severa, his wife, and Flavius Saturninus, his heir, erected this monument in his honor and took care to have it made.
EDCS-28400735.summary: This is a tombstone for Aurelius Masinnus, set up by his wife and heir.

EDCS-28400736.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno pro salute ddominorum nnostrorum Marci Aureli Antonini Pii Augusti nostri Publius Iulius Firminus beneficiarius consularis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28400736.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, the supreme protector of the eastern frontier. For the safety of our lords Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Augustus, Publius Iulius Firminus, an officer's orderly, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-28400736.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Dolichenus for the well-being of the emperors, by Publius Iulius Firminus.

EDCS-28400739.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo P(ublius) Candidius Patruinus praefectus cohortis I Aeliae Gaesatorum votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28400739.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest. Publius Candidius Patruinus, prefect of the I Aelia Gaesatorum cohort, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-28400739.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Publius Candidius Patruinus, prefect of a cohort.

EDCS-28400741.text_edition: \n Bono Puero Firminius Bellicus ve<t=I>(eranus) ex custode a(rmorum) votum solvit libens merito \n
EDCS-28400741.translation: To the Good Boy. Firminius Bellicus, a veteran and former armoury custodian, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-28400741.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to the 'Good Boy' by Firminius Bellicus, a veteran.

EDCS-28400742.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Quintus Cominius Pius vixit annum I et dies IIII PPVD ES pater\n
EDCS-28400742.translation: To the departed spirits. Quintus Cominius Pius lived 1 year and 4 days. His father, PPVD ES, [erected this monument].
EDCS-28400742.summary: This is a tombstone for Quintus Cominius Pius, who died as an infant, erected by his father.

EDCS-28400744.text_edition: \n Dianae Reginae sacrum pro salute et incolumitate Publi D Nepotiani praefecti cohortis IIII Hispanorum domo Roma et Gestiae Valentinae eius\n
EDCS-28400744.translation: Sacred to Diana Regina for the health and safety of Publius Nepotianus, prefect of the IIII Hispana cohort, from Rome, and his wife Gestia Valentina.
EDCS-28400744.summary: This is a dedication to Diana Regina for the safety of Publius Nepotianus, a cohort prefect, and his wife.

EDCS-28400746.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo pro salute Cai Iuli Constantis pontificis Caius Iulius Marcus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28400746.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest. For the safety of Caius Iulius Constans, the priest, Caius Iulius Marcus gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-28400746.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus for the well-being of Caius Iulius Constans, a priest, by Caius Iulius Marcus.

EDCS-28400747.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno pro salute Aeli Lycini procuratoris Auggustorum \n
EDCS-28400747.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, the supreme protector of the eastern frontier. For the safety of Aelius Lycinus, procurator of the emperors.
EDCS-28400747.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Dolichenus for the safety of Aelius Lycinus, an imperial procurator.

EDCS-28400748.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno Ulpii Paternus et Iustinus \n
EDCS-28400748.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, the supreme protector of the eastern frontier. Ulpius Paternus and Iustinus [fulfilled their vows].
EDCS-28400748.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Dolichenus by Ulpius Paternus and Iustinus.

EDCS-28400749.text_edition: \n Deo Soli Invicto pro salute sua et suorum Marcus Cocceius Genialis vir egregius procurator Auggustorum nnostrorum provinciae Daciae Porolissensis votum libens merito posuit\n
EDCS-28400749.translation: To the Unconquered Sun God. For the safety of himself and his family, Marcus Cocceius Genialis, an outstanding man and procurator of our emperors in the province of Dacia Porolissensis, gladly and deservedly set this up.
EDCS-28400749.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Sol Invictus by Marcus Cocceius Genialis, an imperial procurator in Dacia Porolissensis.

EDCS-28400750.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Victorio <G=C>allicano vixit annos XX Marcus Victorius Sep INI \n
EDCS-28400750.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Marcus Victor Gallicanus lived 20 years. Marcus Victorius Sep... had it placed.
EDCS-28400750.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Marcus Victor Gallicanus, who died at the age of 20, set up by Marcus Victorius Sep.

EDCS-28400751.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Victorina vixit annis XVI posuit Quieta mater bene merenti ponendum curavit\n
EDCS-28400751.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Victorina lived 16 years. Her mother, Quieta, placed this monument for her deserving daughter, having seen to its erection.
EDCS-28400751.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Victorina, who died at 16, set up by her mother Quieta.

EDCS-28400752.summary: This fragmented inscription likely records a dedication or funerary notice to someone named Zoilus, possibly by Marcia Longina.

EDCS-28400754.text_edition: \n Dianae Invictae fecit et posuit Cresce[ns ------\n
EDCS-28400754.translation: For Invicta Diana, Crescens made and placed [this]...
EDCS-28400754.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Crescens to the goddess Diana Invicta.

EDCS-28400755.text_edition: \n Aelius Gorgias domino et dominae votum posuit\n
EDCS-28400755.translation: Aelius Gorgias placed a vow for his master and mistress.
EDCS-28400755.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Aelius Gorgias for his patrons.

EDCS-28400756.text_edition: \n Iulius Maximianus |(centurio) Genio |(centuriae) votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28400756.translation: Julius Maximianus, centurion of the [first] century, willingly fulfilled his vow to the genius of his century.
EDCS-28400756.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Julius Maximianus, a centurion, to the genius of his military unit.

EDCS-28400757.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Aelius Fronto VNM CV \n
EDCS-28400757.translation: For Jupiter, Best and Greatest. Aelius Fronto willingly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-28400757.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Aelius Fronto to Jupiter Optimus Maximus.

EDCS-28400758.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Publius Aelius Mucianus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28400758.translation: For Jupiter, Best and Greatest. Publius Aelius Mucianus willingly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-28400758.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Publius Aelius Mucianus to Jupiter Optimus Maximus.

EDCS-28400759.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Aurelius Quietus miles voto posuit\n
EDCS-28400759.translation: For Jupiter, Best and Greatest. Aurelius Quietus, a soldier, placed [this] in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-28400759.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by a soldier named Aurelius Quietus, dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus.

EDCS-28400761.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo sacrum Co<n=M>cessus votum libens solvit \n
EDCS-28400761.translation: Sacred to Jupiter, Best and Greatest. Concessus willingly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-28400761.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Concessus, dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus.

EDCS-28400762.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Marcus Ulpius Magnus IIIIviralis posuit votum merito\n
EDCS-28400762.translation: Marcus Ulpius Magnus, a municipal magistrate, set this up to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, fulfilling a deserved vow.
EDCS-28400762.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by a local official, Marcus Ulpius Magnus.

EDCS-28400764.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Balmarcodi et Iunoni arius veteranus legionis \n
EDCS-28400764.translation: Arius, a veteran of the [legion name], set this up to Jupiter Optimus Maximus and Juno.
EDCS-28400764.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus and Juno by a veteran named Arius.

EDCS-28400765.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Her<c=Q>uli Invicto et Libero Patri Au<r=P>(elius) Gaius et Aurelius Ingenuus sacerdotes ex voto posuerunt \n
EDCS-28400765.translation: Aurelius Gaius and Aurelius Ingenuus, priests, set this up according to their vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Hercules Invictus, and Liber Pater.
EDCS-28400765.summary: This is a votive inscription set up by two priests, Aurelius Gaius and Aurelius Ingenuus, to three deities: Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Hercules Invictus, and Liber Pater.

EDCS-28400766.translation: Annius Saturninus, fulfilling a vow to the free Liber Pater.
EDCS-28400766.summary: This inscription records Annius Saturninus fulfilling a vow to Liber Pater.

EDCS-28400767.text_edition: \n A<u=Y>relius Timotheus signum Liberi votum libens meritomerito \n
EDCS-28400767.translation: Aurelius Timotheus willingly and deservedly set up this statue of Liber, fulfilling his vow.
EDCS-28400767.summary: Aurelius Timotheus erected a statue of Liber in fulfillment of a vow.

EDCS-28400768.text_edition: \n Libero Patri et Liberae Marcus Ulpius Firminus immunis librarius posuit\n
EDCS-28400768.translation: Marcus Ulpius Firminus, an exempt clerk, set this up to Liber Pater and Libera.
EDCS-28400768.summary: Marcus Ulpius Firminus, an exempt clerk, erected this monument to Liber Pater and Libera.

EDCS-28400769.text_edition: \n Soli Invicto Mythrae sic Aurelius Victorinus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28400769.translation: Aurelius Victorinus willingly and deservedly paid his vow to Sol Invictus Mithras.
EDCS-28400769.summary: Aurelius Victorinus fulfilled a vow to the unconquered sun god Mithras.

EDCS-28400770.text_edition: \n Deo Invicto votum posuit Flavius Marcellinus tesserarius\n
EDCS-28400770.translation: Flavius Marcellinus, a standard-bearer, set this up as a vow to the unconquered god.
EDCS-28400770.summary: This inscription records Flavius Marcellinus, a standard-bearer, dedicating a votive offering to an unconquered god.

EDCS-28400771.summary: Iulius Callinicus dedicated this to holy Victory.

EDCS-28701079.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Victoriae AIIFR filiae vixit annos XX PATONIA patronus liber libertae bene merenti\n
EDCS-28701079.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Patonia, patron, for her well-deserving freedwoman Victoria, daughter of AIIFR, who lived 20 years.
EDCS-28701079.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated by a patron, Patonia, to her freedwoman Victoria, who died at the age of 20.

EDCS-28701080.text_edition: \n numini Asclepii pro se Marcus Aurelius Vitalianus libens merito\n
EDCS-28701080.translation: For the divinity of Asclepius, Marcus Aurelius Vitalianus, willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-28701080.summary: This is a dedication to the god Asclepius by Marcus Aurelius Vitalianus, likely a private votive offering.

EDCS-28701081.text_edition: \n XXXV Aelia Proba vixit annos XI Publius Aelius Ingenuus vixit Aelius Probus flame<n=M> municipii pientissimis et sibi vixit \n
EDCS-28701081.translation: Aelia Proba lived 35 years. Publius Aelius Ingenuus lived [fewer than X] years. Aelius Probus, flame of the municipality, for the most dutiful and for himself lived.
EDCS-28701081.summary: This is an epitaph recording the ages of Aelia Proba and Publius Aelius Ingenuus, with a mention of Aelius Probus, likely related to municipal administration, and a statement about their virtuous lives.

EDCS-28701082.text_edition: \n Iulius Alexander veteranus ex beneficiario consularis et nnius Martinianus |centurio le<g=C>(ionis) ex voto posuerunt \n
EDCS-28701082.translation: Julius Alexander, a veteran, formerly a subordinate of the governor, and Martinianus, a centurion, erected [this] in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-28701082.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument by Julius Alexander, a veteran and former subordinate of a governor, and Martinianus, a centurion, who fulfilled a vow.

EDCS-29000015.translation: Tilianus, soldier of the Legio XIII Gemina Antoniniana, scribe of our governor, and Aelia Paulina and Ulpia Dubitata, his children, erected [this] for their most dutiful parents.
EDCS-29000015.summary: This is a dedication by children, Tilianus, Aelia Paulina, and Ulpia Dubitata, in honor of their dutiful parents, with Tilianus identified as a soldier and scribe.

EDCS-29000016.text_edition: \n Silv<a=V>no sacrum pro salute Claudi Rufini Myro eiusdem \n
EDCS-29000016.translation: Sacred to Silvanus, for the health of Claudius Rufinus. Myro, of the same [household/group].
EDCS-29000016.summary: This is a dedication to the god Silvanus for the health of Claudius Rufinus, made by Myro.

EDCS-29000021.text_edition: \n IA LAN vixit annos Aeli-- Alan \n
EDCS-29000021.translation: IA LAN lived X years. Aelius Alan.
EDCS-29000021.summary: This is a fragment of an epitaph recording the lifespan of a person named IA LAN and mentioning Aelius Alan.

EDCS-29000023.text_edition: \n Marti Gradivo sacrum cohors I sagittariorum |(milliaria) Gordiana\n
EDCS-29000023.translation: Sacred to Mars Gradivus. Cohors I sagittariorum (milliaria) Gordiana.
EDCS-29000023.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Mars Gradivus by the first cohort of sagittarii (archers), a milliary unit named Gordiana.

EDCS-29000024.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulia Philumene vixit annis XXX hic sita est Philetus Iuli Rufini coniugi bene merenti \n
EDCS-29000024.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Iulia Philumene lived 30 years, here she is laid. Philetus for his well-deserving wife Iulia Rufini.
