EDCS-45100332.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Iulia Iustina lived 42 years. Cassius Martialis, a military adjutant of a provincial governor, to his dearest wife, Iustina. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-45100332.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated by Cassius Martialis to his wife Iulia Iustina, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-45100335.summary: An inscription likely recording a dedication or memorial for Aelius, possibly made during his lifetime.

EDCS-45100336.summary: A fragmentary inscription recording the name Aelia and her age at death, likely a tombstone.

EDCS-45100337.summary: A very fragmentary inscription that may mention two female names, Aelia and possibly Iunia.

EDCS-45100361.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest. Aelius Rufus...
EDCS-45100361.summary: A fragmentary inscription beginning a dedication to Jupiter, initiated by Aelius Rufus.

EDCS-45100362.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Aelius Victor - votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-45100362.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest. Aelius Victor gladly fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-45100362.summary: A votive inscription by Aelius Victor to Jupiter, fulfilling a vow.

EDCS-45100363.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo F Aurelius C us\n
EDCS-45100363.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest. F. Aurelius C[...]
EDCS-45100363.summary: A fragmentary inscription beginning a dedication to Jupiter, possibly by Aurelius C[...].

EDCS-45100365.text_edition: \n Deae Nemesi sacrum Aurelius Rufinus beneficiarius consularis legionis XIII geminae Severianae votum libens posuit Maximo et Albino consulibus\n
EDCS-45100365.translation: Sacred to the goddess Nemesis. Aurelius Rufinus, a military adjutant of the XIII Gemina Severiana legion, willingly set this up, when Maximinus and Albinus were consuls.
EDCS-45100365.summary: A votive inscription to the goddess Nemesis by Aurelius Rufinus, a military adjutant, dated to 236 CE.

EDCS-45100369.text_edition: \n dis deabusque omnibus sacratum Publius Aelius Sextilianus beneficiarius consularis legionis V M[acedonicae ------\n
EDCS-45100369.translation: Dedicated to all gods and goddesses. Publius Aelius Sextilianus, a military adjutant of the V Macedonica legion...
EDCS-45100369.summary: A fragmentary inscription beginning a dedication to all gods and goddesses by Publius Aelius Sextilianus, a military adjutant.

EDCS-45100371.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelius Sura vixit annos L Aureli- \n
EDCS-45100371.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Aelius Sura lived 50 years. Aureli-...
EDCS-45100371.summary: A fragmentary funerary epitaph for Aelius Sura, who lived 50 years.

EDCS-45100372.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelius Viator vixit annos XXV Aelius Iunianus vixit annos XVI CN SAI AVR R RAN veteranus cohortis I Britannicae vivus posuit filiis piisimis\n
EDCS-45100372.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aelius Viator lived 25 years. Aelius Iunianus lived 16 years. CN. SAI. AVR. R. RAN., a veteran of the first British cohort, erected this in his lifetime for his most dutiful sons.
EDCS-45100372.summary: A funerary epitaph for two sons, Aelius Viator and Aelius Iunianus, erected by a veteran of the Roman army.

EDCS-45100377.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari divi Traiani Parthici filio divi Nervae nepoti Traiano Hadriano Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate XV consuli III patri patriae ala I Tungrorum Frontoniana Antoniniana\n
EDCS-45100377.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrian Augustus, son of the divine Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the divine Nerva, Pontifex Maximus, in his fifteenth year of tribunician power, Consul for the third time, Father of the Fatherland. [Erected by] the First Wing of the Tungrians, the Frontonian unit, Antoninian.
EDCS-45100377.summary: An inscription dedicated to Emperor Hadrian, likely erected by the ala I Tungrorum Frontoniana, possibly commemorating a visit or event.

EDCS-45100378.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelius Maximinus vixit nnos VL et Aeli Maximus vixit nnos VII Ursinus vixit nnos IIII Serena Licconis coniugi et filiis bene merentibus posuit\n
EDCS-45100378.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aelius Maximinus lived 40 years and Aelius Maximus lived 7 years. Ursinus lived 4 years. Posuit erected this for his deserving wife Serena, daughter of Licco, and his children.
EDCS-45100378.summary: A funerary epitaph for Serena, her husband Aelius Maximinus, and their children Aelius Maximus and Ursinus, erected by a husband.

EDCS-45100379.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Brisanus vixit annis L Aurelius Mucatius et Aurelius Maximianus filii ed sic heredes pro pietate posuerunt\n
EDCS-45100379.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aurelius Brisanus lived 50 years. His sons Aurelius Mucatius and Aurelius Maximianus, as heirs, erected this out of duty.
EDCS-45100379.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aurelius Brisanus, erected by his sons Aurelius Mucatius and Aurelius Maximianus.

EDCS-45900188.summary: A very fragmentary inscription, possibly a name or a dedication.

EDCS-46400280.text_edition: \n Publio Aelio Publi filio Papiria Marcello viro egregio primo pilo ex praefecto legionis VII Claudiae et I adiutricis sub principe peregrinorum |(centurioni) frumentario sacerdoti Laurentium Lavi<natium patrono et decurioni colo>niae patrono rerum publicarum Fulginiatium Foroflaminensium itemque Iguvinorum \n
EDCS-46400280.translation: To Publius Aelius Marcellus, son of Publius, of the Papiria tribe, an outstanding man, former chief centurion of the VII Claudia and I Adiutrix legions, formerly prefect of peregrini, military tribune, frumentarius, priest of the Laurentes Lavinates, patron and decurion of the colony, responsible for the public affairs of Fulginates, Foroflaminenses, and also Iguvines.
EDCS-46400280.summary: A funerary epitaph celebrating the distinguished career and civic roles of Publius Aelius Marcellus, a high-ranking military officer and local patron.

EDCS-46400873.text_edition: \n Ulpius Claudius beneficiarius tr<i=T>buni Pullius? Aurelius READSB Claudius Florus strator legati Tertius Iust Aurelius Secc speculator Statius Alexander tu<b=R>(icen) Aurelius Licinius oiptio fabricae consularis Aelius Proc Aurelius Val strator legati Aelius Ma Aurelius Pri custos armorum Ulpius Bu immunis Pere M Aelius Cl tesserarius Laberius S beneficiarius consularis Helvius Iu consularis Aurelius V n T m Ulpius Bassinus dupliarius Valerianus duplicarius Florus duplicarius Inus Modestus Valerianus duplicarius COI ato duplicarius Genialis duplicarius Valentinus duplicarius er Val<en=IV>s Ingenuus Maximus <I=L>ucundus Iulius Aelianus Te<r=I>(tius Marti Ian duplarius Pu Licini \n
EDCS-46400873.translation: Ulpius Claudius, staff-sergeant of the tribune. Pullius? Aurelius, standard-bearer. Claudius Florus, groom of the legate. Tertius Iustus, Aurelius Secc, speculator. Statius Alexander, trumpeter. Aurelius Licinius, optio of the consular workshop. Aelius Proc, Aurelius Val, groom of the legate. Aelius Ma, Aurelius Pri, armour keeper. Ulpius Bu, immune. Pere M, Aelius Cl, tesserarius. Laberius S, staff-sergeant of the consul. Helvius Iu, consular. Aurelius V, T. M. Ulpius Bassinus, duplicarius. Valerianus, duplicarius. Florus, duplicarius. Inus Modestus, duplicarius. (Names ending in) duplicarius. Genialis, duplicarius. Valentinus, duplicarius. er Valens, Ingenuus, Maximus, Lucundus, Julius Aelianus, Tertius Martius, Ian, duplicarius. Pu Licinius.
EDCS-46400873.summary: A list of military officials, mostly of lower rank and specialists, from a military unit, likely detailing personnel serving in a specific context or workshop.

EDCS-46400919.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Dasas Liccai Delmata kastello Starvae vixit annos XXXV posuerunt Beucus Sarius et Da i heredes bene merenti\n
EDCS-46400919.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Dasas, son of Liccai, a Delmatian from the fortress of Starva, lived 35 years. Beucus and Sarius and Da i, his heirs, erected this for him, who deserved well.
EDCS-46400919.summary: A funerary epitaph for Dasas, a Delmatian soldier from the fortress of Starva, erected by his heirs.

EDCS-46400920.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Panenti Bizonis filio Delmatae kastello Starvae vixit annos XXV Plator Scenobarbi nepoti bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-46400920.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. To Panentis, son of Bizo, a Delmatian from the fortress of Starva, who lived 25 years. Plator, grandson of Scenobarbus, erected this for him, who deserved well.
EDCS-46400920.summary: A funerary epitaph for Panentis, a Delmatian soldier from the fortress of Starva, erected by Plator.

EDCS-46700087.text_edition: \n Deo Forti pro salute Impersacerdotalis Daciae Aurelius Quintianus Aurelius Augustianus Iulius Rufinus Aurelius Surus Aurelius Marc ex custode armorum ex speculatore ex duplicario ex optione ex optione ex duplicario Aurelius Victorinus --- Aurelius Farnax Aurelius Lucilius ex optione Aurelius Gaianus Aurelius Mucianus ex custode armorum Aurelius Maximus ex custode armorum Aelius Cassius IL Aurelius Lucius ex custode armorum Aurelius Agrippa ex custode armorum ius ex duplicario nes nus ex duplicario us s us ex duplicario ex duplicario Aurelius Vitalis ex duplicario Aurelius Celsus ex duplicario Aurelius ValerianusEM G N Aurelius Sabinus Septimius Crisi CAD Aelius Lu OVP ex optione Aurelius Iulianus Aurelius Statilius ex duplicario Aurelius Valens Aurelius Valens Septimus Alexander ex duplicario Valerius Valens Aurelius Flavianus ex immuni Aurelius Viatorinus Aurelius Crispus Aurelius Verus Aurelius Maximus ex armorum custode Aurelius Aelius Valerius ex custode armorum Aurelius Domnio ex immuni Aurelius Valerius Aurelius Anici Aurelius Firmidius Aelius Aufidius Aurelius Victorinus ex equite Aurelius Verus ex equite Aurelius Firmus ex duplicario Aurelius Maximinus Aurelius Sedatus ex immuni librario Aurelius Crescens Aurelius Iulius ex BETRI Aurelius Quintillianus Petronius Marcianus ex librario Aurelius Valentinus ex tubicine Aelius Maximianus ex tesserario SFI Volusius us veteranus ex beneficiario legionis MRA ex suo posuit\n
EDCS-46700087.translation: To the Strong God, for the health of the Emperor. Priests of Dacia: Aurelius Quintianus, Aurelius Augustianus, Julius Rufinus, Aurelius Surus, Aurelius Marc (from armour keeper, from speculator, from duplicarius, from optio, from optio, from duplicarius), Aurelius Victorinus [illegible] Aurelius Farnax, Aurelius Lucilius (from optio), Aurelius Gaianus, Aurelius Mucianus (from armour keeper), Aurelius Maximus (from armour keeper), Aelius Cassius, IL, Aurelius Lucius (from armour keeper), Aurelius Agrippa (from armour keeper), ius (from duplicarius), nes nus (from duplicarius), us s us (from duplicarius), ex duplicario, Aurelius Vitalis (from duplicarius), Aurelius Celsus (from duplicarius), Aurelius Valerianus, EM G N, Aurelius Sabinus, Septimius Crisi, CAD, Aelius Lu, OVP, (from optio), Aurelius Iulianus, Aurelius Statilius (from duplicarius), Aurelius Valens, Aurelius Valens, Septimus Alexander (from duplicarius), Valerius Valens, Aurelius Flavianus (from immune), Aurelius Viatorinus, Aurelius Crispus, Aurelius Verus, Aurelius Maximus (from armour keeper), Aurelius, Aelius Valerius (from armour keeper), Aurelius Domnio (from immune), Aurelius Valerius, Aurelius Anici, Aurelius Firmidius, Aelius Aufidius, Aurelius Victorinus (from cavalryman), Aurelius Verus (from cavalryman), Aurelius Firmus (from duplicarius), Aurelius Maximinus, Aurelius Sedatus (from immune, clerk), Aurelius Crescens, Aurelius Julius (from BETRI), Aurelius Quintillianus, Petronius Marcianus (from clerk), Aurelius Valentinus (from trumpeter), Aelius Maximianus (from tesserarius), SFI, Volusius, veteran, from staff-sergeant of legion MRA, erected this from his own means.
EDCS-46700087.summary: A dedication to the god Fortis for the emperor's health, listing numerous military personnel and priests from Dacia, likely associated with a religious cult or military chapel.

EDCS-46700089.text_edition: \n Lucius Aninius Luci filius Papiria Firminus Tridente equo publico ex CCCtrecenario primus pilus legionis XIII geminae tetrastylum fecit et aquilam argenteam posuit\n
EDCS-46700089.translation: Lucius Aninius Firminus, son of Lucius, of the Papiria tribe, from the class of 300, a former centurion (primus pilus) of the XIII Gemina legion, built this four-columned structure and dedicated a silver eagle.
EDCS-46700089.summary: This inscription records the building of a tetrastyle and dedication of a silver eagle by a Roman military officer of equestrian rank, Lucius Aninius Firminus, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-49500005.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Aurelius Iulianus ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-49500005.translation: Aurelius Iulianus dedicated this in fulfillment of a vow to Jupiter Best and Greatest.
EDCS-49500005.summary: This is a votive dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Aurelius Iulianus, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-51000089.translation: To the Unconquered Sun God Mithras.
EDCS-51000089.summary: This is a short dedication to the Unconquered Sun God Mithras, common in the Roman Empire, especially during the 2nd to 4th centuries CE.

EDCS-55702024.text_edition: \n Dianae sacrum et omnibus Dis posuit kastellum Maniatium Nevato Ladi\n
EDCS-55702024.translation: Sacred to Diana and all the Gods, Nevato Ladi dedicated this fort of Maniatium.
EDCS-55702024.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a fort named Maniatium by Nevato Ladi to Diana and all the gods, likely from a frontier region in the Roman Empire.

EDCS-55702025.text_edition: \n Asclepio Augusto sacrum Fronto Plarentis dono dedit collegio\n
EDCS-55702025.translation: Fronto, son of Plarentis, gave this as a gift to Asclepius Augustus for the guild.
EDCS-55702025.summary: This inscription records a gift made by Fronto to Asclepius Augustus, dedicated to a collegium (guild or association), likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-56900095.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription beginning with the titles of an emperor, the rest of whose name and dedication are lost, dating potentially from the imperial period.

EDCS-57100063.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Firmus vixit nnos XXX Aurelius Iulianus frater bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-57100063.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aurelius Firmus lived for 30 years. Aurelius Iulianus, his brother, dedicated this to the well-deserving.
EDCS-57100063.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the death of Aurelius Firmus at age 30, with the monument erected by his brother Aurelius Iulianus, likely from the 2nd to 3rd century CE.

EDCS-57200151.text_edition: \n Herculi Aurelius Dubitatus votum solvit libens\n
EDCS-57200151.translation: Aurelius Dubitatus gladly paid his vow to Hercules.
EDCS-57200151.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Aurelius Dubitatus to Hercules, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-59500070.text_edition: \n Deo Soli Invicto Mithraethraeacrum MAA NA \n
EDCS-59500070.translation: To the Unconquered Sun God Mithras. [---]
EDCS-59500070.summary: This is a dedication to the Unconquered Sun God Mithras, with a lacuna indicating missing text, probably from the 2nd to 4th century CE.

EDCS-59500074.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription that appears to be simply the name of an individual, Statinius Sabinus, potentially a tombstone or dedication by that person, likely from the 1st to 3rd century CE.

EDCS-59500075.text_edition: \n Dis Maioribus Domno et Domnae Caius Postumius Pansa |(centurio) legionis III Italicae votum solvit\n
EDCS-59500075.translation: Caius Postumius Pansa, centurion of the III Italica legion, discharged his vow to the ancestral gods, Lord and Lady.
EDCS-59500075.summary: A vow made by Caius Postumius Pansa, a centurion, to the household gods is recorded.

EDCS-62800026.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus and Juno Regina, Publius Aelius Stadylo, decurion and aedile, for a vow.
EDCS-62800026.summary: A dedication to Jupiter and Juno made by Publius Aelius Stadylo, an official, fulfilling a vow.

EDCS-64100113.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus and Silvanus.
EDCS-64100113.summary: A dedication to the gods Jupiter and Silvanus.

EDCS-64100116.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Crescens Maximi vixit nnos XXXV Iulius filius eius vixit annos IIII Iulia Saturnina fecit coniugi carissimo VI \n
EDCS-64100116.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Crescens, son of Maximus, lived 35 years. Julius, his son, lived 4 years. Iulia Saturnina made this for her dearest husband.
EDCS-64100116.summary: A tombstone inscription recording the lives of Crescens and his son Julius, dedicated by Iulia Saturnina.

EDCS-64100119.text_edition: \n Cultores dei Solis Malagbeli --- Proculus a exstruxerunt quorum nomina subscripta sunt Spedius V coloniae Marcus Marcus Aurelius Iu Spedius anus Spedius? Augustalis coloniae Pomponi(us) Avitus Valerius nus Spedius Primanus quiqui Rex Aurelius Aquilinus Aurelius Zopyrus Cominius Maximinus Aurelius Viator Aurelius Marcianus Antonius Barbas Aelius Geminus Marcus Valentinus Antipater Crescentio Eutyches Valerius Rufus Aurelius Vitalianus Esurio Domitius I\n
EDCS-64100119.translation: The devotees of the god Sol Malagbel. [---] Proculus and others erected this; their names are listed below: Spedius V (of the) colony, Marcus, Marcus Aurelius Iu, Spedius anus, Spedius? Augustalis (of the) colony, Pomponius Avitus, Valerius nus, Spedius Primanus, Quiqui Rex, Aurelius Aquilinus, Aurelius Zopyrus, Cominius Maximinus, Aurelius Viator, Aurelius Marcianus, Antonius Barbas, Aelius Geminus, Marcus Valentinus, Antipater, Crescentio, Eutyches, Valerius Rufus, Aurelius Vitalianus, Esurio, Domitius I.
EDCS-64100119.summary: A list of subscribers who erected a monument to the god Sol Malagbel, likely from the late Roman period.

EDCS-64100120.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aemilio Proculo Lucilia Marcia coniugi bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-64100120.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Lucilia Marcia placed this for her well-deserving husband, Aemilius Proculus.
EDCS-64100120.summary: A tombstone inscription by Lucilia Marcia for her husband Aemilius Proculus.

EDCS-64900620.text_edition: \n Nemesi Reginae sacrum pro salute Impperatorum nnostrorum Marcus Vibius Verinus evocatus legionis XIII geminae et Quintus Valerius Felicianus salariar<i>us fecerunt\n
EDCS-64900620.translation: Sacred to Nemesis Regina for the safety of our Emperors. Marcus Vibius Verinus, an evocatus of the XIII Gemina legion, and Quintus Valerius Felicianus, a salaryman, made this.
EDCS-64900620.summary: A dedication to Nemesis Regina for the emperors' safety, made by two soldiers from the XIII Gemina legion.

EDCS-64900621.text_edition: \n Marcus Vibius Verinus evocatus Quintus Valerius Felicianus salariarius\n
EDCS-64900621.translation: Marcus Vibius Verinus, evocatus; Quintus Valerius Felicianus, salaryman.
EDCS-64900621.summary: The names of two soldiers, Marcus Vibius Verinus and Quintus Valerius Felicianus, likely from the XIII Gemina legion.

EDCS-65000086.summary: An inscription likely indicating a member or belonging of the XIII Gemina legion named Aurelius Gaius.

EDCS-65000087.summary: An inscription indicating a member or belonging of the XIII legion named Aurelius Cam[---].

EDCS-65000099.translation: The singular infantrymen of the Batavians.
EDCS-65000099.summary: This inscription refers to the elite infantrymen of the Batavian auxiliary cavalry unit, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-65000100.summary: This inscription likely refers to the elite infantrymen attached to a Roman auxiliary unit, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-65000101.summary: This inscription refers to the Batavian auxiliary unit, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-65000193.summary: This inscription names the Thirteenth Legion Gemina, a Roman legion, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-65600129.text_edition: \n Aesculapio et Hygiae pro salute domini nostri Imperatoris Gallieni Augusti M(arcus) Publicianus Rhenus praefectus alae I Batavorum agens vice praefecti legionis\n
EDCS-65600129.translation: To Aesculapius and Hygieia for the health of our lord Emperor Gallienus Augustus, Marcus Publicianus Rhenus, prefect of the I Batavorum ala, acting in place of the legionary prefect.
EDCS-65600129.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the gods Aesculapius and Hygieia for the emperor's health, made by Marcus Publicianus Rhenus, a prefect of an auxiliary cavalry unit, likely in the mid-3rd century AD.

EDCS-65600130.text_edition: \n Fortunae pro salute domini nostri Imperatoris Gallieni Aemiliani Augusti M(arcus) Publicianus R praefectus alae Batavroum |(milliariae) agens vice praefecit legionis\n
EDCS-65600130.translation: To Fortune for the health of our lord Emperor Gallienus Aemilianus Augustus, Marcus Publicianus R, prefect of the milliary Batavorum ala, acting in place of the legionary prefect.
EDCS-65600130.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the goddess Fortune for the emperor's health, made by Marcus Publicianus R, prefect of the milliary Batavorum cavalry unit, likely during the reign of Gallienus (mid-3rd century AD).

EDCS-80600002.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-80600002.translation: Emperor Caesar Traianus Hadrianus Augustus, son of the deified Trajan the Parthian, grandson of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 15th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the country, proconsul, granted citizenship and the right of marriage to the cavalrymen and infantry who served for twenty-five or more years and were honorably discharged in one cavalry wing and eight cohorts, namely the II Pannoniorum and I Hispanorum Pia Fidelis, and the I Batavorum Miliaria Civium Romanorum Pia Fidelis, and the I Cannanefatium, and the II Britannorum Miliaria Civium Romanorum Pia Fidelis, and the II Augusta Nerviana Pacensis Miliaria Brittonum, and the II Hispanorum, and the V Lingonum, and the VI Thracum, and who are stationed in Dacia Porolissensis under Flavius Italicus. He granted this to them, their children, and their descendants. With the wives they then had when citizenship was granted to them, or if they were unmarried, with those they would later marry, provided they married only one woman each. This was inscribed and verified from a bronze tablet which is fixed in Rome on the wall behind the Temple of the deified Augustus, near the Temple of Minerva. Emperor Caesar Traianus Hadrianus Augustus, son of the deified Trajan the Parthian, grandson of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 15th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the country, proconsul, granted citizenship and the right of marriage to the cavalrymen and infantry who served for twenty-five or more years and were honorably discharged in one cavalry wing and eight cohorts, namely the II Pannoniorum and I Hispanorum Pia Fidelis, and the I Batavorum Miliaria Civium Romanorum Pia Fidelis, and the I Cannanefatium, and the II Britannorum Miliaria Civium Romanorum Pia Fidelis, and the II Augusta Nerviana Pacensis Miliaria Brittonum, and the II Hispanorum, and the V Lingonum, and the VI Thracum, and who are stationed in Dacia Porolissensis under Flavius Italicus. He granted this to them, their children, and their descendants. With the wives they then had when citizenship was granted to them, or if they were unmarried, with those they would later marry, provided they married only one woman each.
EDCS-80600002.summary: A formal grant of citizenship and the right to marry issued by Emperor Hadrian in AD 132 to veterans of several auxiliary units serving in Dacia Porolissensis, with a copy preserved on a bronze tablet in Rome.

EDCS-81100029.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus. Marcus Ulpius Vitalis, aide of a consular governor, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.

EDCS-81200277.translation: [He] Romanicus handed over and, having become a domesticus, served as protector for 8 years, promoted to Sucidava, he served for 1 year in the cavalry troop of the equites stabilesiani, who lived for 45 years and 10 months and 10 days, completed his munera by fate and passed away in his affairs; Flavius Mellites, his fellow citizens, erected this monument to him for his well-deserved merit. Hail, traveller!
EDCS-81200277.summary: A funerary monument erected by fellow citizens for Romanicus, a soldier who served as a protector and in cavalry units, and died at the age of 45.

EDCS-11201129.translation: To the unconquered Mithras, Aelius Marcianus and Aelius Aeternalis set this up according to their vow for themselves and their families

EDCS-11201133.translation: Herennianus set this up in accordance with a vision

EDCS-11201153.translation: Under the consulship of Laelianus and Pastorus, on the 10th day before the Kalends of November [October 23rd], I, Adiutor, scribe of Macarius, wrote at his request in his presence, because he said he did not know how to write, that he has hired and hires out his services to Socration, son of Socrates, for one year from the following Ides [of November] for seventy denarii, to be paid at his own times. He must provide healthy and able services, and if the contractor hinders him from working, he must [leave or cease...]. Done at Alburnus Maior.
EDCS-11201153.summary: A contract recording the hiring of services for one year, dated to October 23rd, during the consulship of Laelianus and Pastorus.

EDCS-11201155.translation: I, Marcus, wrote through Restitutus, surnamed Senior, because he said he did not know how to write, acknowledging that he has hired out and hires out his services for gold work, whatever work may be required, from this day until the coming Ides of November to Titus Beusantis, also known as Bradua, for one hundred and five denarii, of which he has already received twenty-five denarii in advance, and he will receive the remaining payment over time. He must provide healthy and able services to the aforementioned contractor. If he ceases work against the contractor's will...
EDCS-11201155.summary: A contract for hiring services for unspecified 'gold work', dated to the Ides of November, with a payment of 105 denarii.

EDCS-11201163.translation: Under the consulship, on the 12th day before the Kalends of November [October 21st], it was agreed in the presence of witnesses that [something] was presented to Cerialis, son of T. Flavius Valens.
EDCS-11201163.summary: A legal or administrative note recording an agreement or presentation made to Cerialis on October 21st.

EDCS-11201164.translation: Aepicadi acknowledged Terentius, acknowledged Dasantis, son of Loni, who is also Saturninus, acknowledged Scenobarbus, son of Sextus, acknowledged Imalus, son of Baton, acknowledged Annaeus himself.
EDCS-11201164.summary: A list of acknowledgments of debt or legal recognition by several individuals, possibly related to property or inheritance.

EDCS-12300241.translation: The Emperor Caesar Domitianus Augustus Germanicus, son of the deified Vespasian, Pontifex Maximus, with tribunician power for the 11th time, Imperator for the 21st time, Censor for life, Consul for the 16th time, Father of the Fatherland, granted citizenship and the right of marriage with the wives they then had (when citizenship was granted) or, if they were unmarried, with those they would later marry, to those serving in the Classis Flavia Moesica which is under Sextus Octavius Fronton, who have completed twenty or more years of service, and also to those discharged with an honorable dismissal, to themselves and their children and descendants. This record and verification was made on the 14th day before the Kalends of July, when Tiberius Iulius Celsus Polemaeanus and Lucius Stertinius Avitus were consuls, from the bronze tablet fixed in Rome on the wall behind the temple of the deified Augustus, near the temple of Minerva, belonging to Aulus Lappius Pollianus, Caius Iulius Helenus, Marcus Caelius Fortis, Cnaeus Matius Barbarus, Quintus Orfius Cupitus, Lucius Pullus Speratus, Lucius Pullus Verecundus.
EDCS-12300241.summary: An imperial grant of citizenship and marriage rights issued by Emperor Domitian to veterans of the Classis Flavia Moesica who had served honorably, inscribed on a bronze tablet.

EDCS-13600234.translation: Caius Iulius Fabius, optio of the First Italian Legion, of the centuria of Carisius Clementis, willingly paid his vow to Hercules the Unconquered, the All-Powerful.

EDCS-28400679.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aelius Guras, optio from the ranks of the Palmyrenes, lived 42 years and served for 21 years. Aelius Habibis, priest and heir, erected this for him as one who deserved well.
EDCS-28400679.summary: This is a funerary inscription from the Roman army, recording the details of Aelius Guras, a Palmyrene optio who served for 21 years and lived to 42, with his monument erected by his heir, Aelius Habibis.

EDCS-28400680.translation: Gaddes Aninas, a veteran, lived 50 years. His wife Iulia Marcia Erapoles I placed this for TICIA, who lived 30 years.

EDCS-28400690.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Caius Quintinius Victor junior lived 8 years. His loving father Caius Quintinius Victor set this up for his dear son.

EDCS-28400702.translation: Philistio has paid his vow willingly and deservedly at the Four Ways intersection.

EDCS-28400708.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Iulia Saturnina lived 16 years. Her mother Iulia Procula and Iulius Diomedes [set this up].

EDCS-32500278.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Brisenus, standard-bearer of the cavalry wing II Pannoniorum, who lived for 38 years, his heir arranged for this to be set up.
EDCS-32500278.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Brisenus, a standard-bearer in the ala II Pannoniorum, set up by his heir.

EDCS-32500280.translation: [He/She lived] 70 years. Lupe, Montanus, and Silvester, and NIVEN [arranged this monument].
EDCS-32500280.summary: This inscription records the age of a deceased person and names multiple individuals who arranged the monument.

EDCS-32500281.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aelius Ana, a veteran of the Pannonian cavalry wing and standard-bearer, who lived for 63 years, for his brother who deserved well of him.
EDCS-32500281.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aelius Ana, a veteran standard-bearer of the Pannonian cavalry, set up by his brother.

EDCS-32500286.translation: Flavis Mesicos, an armed soldier, Ernium Anciius.
EDCS-32500286.summary: This inscription appears to name two individuals, likely soldiers, with their ethnic or descriptive names.

EDCS-33500317.translation: To Jupiter the Stabilizer, to Hercules the Victorious. Emperor Caesar Trajanus Hadrianus Augustus, having conquered Decebalus and subdued Dacia, fulfilled his vow. Behold, Father Romulus, rejoice, Quirites, this is your glory.
EDCS-33500317.summary: This is a monumental inscription from the reign of Emperor Hadrian celebrating his Dacian victories and fulfilling a vow.

EDCS-34100582.translation: Flavius Calliopius, our lord, approved and signed at Thessalonica, curator of the sacred treasury, approved and signed the gold obryzum in the strongbox at Thessalonica, our lord.
EDCS-34100582.summary: This inscription records the approval and signing of gold obryzum in a treasury strongbox by Flavius Calliopius, curator of the sacred treasury, likely during the late Roman period.

EDCS-36800123.summary: This inscription records that King Thiamarcus was the maker of the object, likely an artifact or monument.

EDCS-36800127.translation: Abrassax, Ablanathanabla, grace, life, health. Iao, Maria.
EDCS-36800127.summary: This is a magical or devotional inscription invoking mystical names and deities, possibly for protection or good fortune, dating from the Roman or late antique period.

EDCS-36900303.translation: Our ancestor dedicated it. Flavius Ourimos, steward of Flavius Makedon, by command to the unconquered god Mithras, dedicated it into a hidden grove to pray purely to the Euphrates. Phoebus of Nikomedeia made it.
EDCS-36900303.summary: This inscription records the dedication of an object to the unconquered god Mithras in a hidden grove, made by Flavius Ourimos and crafted by Phoebus of Nikomedeia, likely in the Roman period.

EDCS-36900304.translation: Flavius Ourimos to the unconquered god Rithras. ORO
EDCS-36900304.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Flavius Ourimos to the unconquered god Rithras, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-36900305.translation: Ourimos to the god S. Autē. DXANO
EDCS-36900305.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Ourimos to a deity, possibly S. Autē, with unidentified letters, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-36900306.translation: Flavius Ourimos dedicated it to the god Mithras.

EDCS-38800043.translation: To Asclepius and Hygieia, the philanthropic gods, Aelius Aelianus the younger, a thankful offering. Ionius.
EDCS-38800043.summary: This inscription is a votive offering from Aelius Aelianus the younger to the gods Asclepius and Hygieia, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-38800045.translation: To the Most High, responsive God, I, Aelia Cassia, gave thanks and dedicated it.

EDCS-44800154.translation: Romulus, commander of the Fourth Cohort.
EDCS-44800154.summary: This inscription identifies Romulus as the commander of the Fourth Cohort, indicating a military context, likely dating from the 1st to 3rd centuries AD.

EDCS-44800160.summary: This inscription appears to be an ownership mark or a dedication by Gnaeus Domitius Evarestus, possibly on a building or object, dating from the 1st to 3rd centuries AD.

EDCS-44800196.translation: To the god Grannus Apollo, always and everywhere attentive, Aelius the younger, a native of Ionia, [set this up] in thanks.

EDCS-53700771.translation: From Publius Cornelius Colonus: 'chelidonium' (an ointment) with balm for dark circles, from Publius Cornelius Colonus: 'dioxus' for roughness and chapped cheeks, from Publius Cornelius Colonus: 'diapsoricum' with balm for clarity.

EDCS-66600393.translation: Under Emperor Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of the fatherland, when Furius Saturninus, governor and legate of the Thirteenth Legion Gemina, Eggius Proculus, trainer of horsemen of the same legion, set up an altar and statue of Mars.
EDCS-66600393.summary: This is a dedication to Mars by Eggius Proculus, an officer in the Thirteenth Legion Gemina, during the reign of Emperor Antoninus Pius and the governorship of Furius Saturninus.

EDCS-66600395.translation: To the departed spirits. Marcus Granius Stabilianus of the Thirteenth Legion Gemina, first rear-spearman, lived 47 years, and Marcus Granius Stabilianus, his son, lived 3 years, and Grania Sabina, his daughter, lived 6 years. Attilia Paulina set this up for her most holy and dutiful husband and children, who deserved well.
EDCS-66600395.summary: This is a family tombstone set up by Attilia Paulina for her husband, Marcus Granius Stabilianus (a soldier in the Thirteenth Legion Gemina), and their two children, with an unknown approximate date.

EDCS-66600403.translation: Valerius Valerianus set this up for the college.
EDCS-66600403.summary: This inscription records Valerius Valerianus setting up a dedication for a college or association, with an unknown approximate date.

EDCS-66700022.translation: To the departed spirits. Aelia Muciane lived 27 years. Aelius Orus lived [---] Firmus, son of Aelius.
EDCS-66700022.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aelia Muciane and Aelius Orus, with an unknown approximate date.

EDCS-67400425.summary: This inscription mentions 'Prisci the duplicarii', likely referring to soldiers holding a specific rank or role, with an unknown approximate date.

EDCS-67400427.translation: Albanus Dicatulli gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to the sacred deity.
EDCS-67400427.summary: A votive inscription from Albanus Dicatulli, fulfilling a vow.

EDCS-67400441.translation: The Fifth Macedonian Legion, from Balus.

EDCS-67400442.summary: This inscription likely names Victorinus, a soldier belonging to the Fifth Macedonian Legion.

EDCS-67400451.summary: A short inscription wishing prosperity to someone named Valeri, possibly marking a tomb or a gift.

EDCS-69000047.translation: On the fifteenth day before the Kalends of July, in the consulship of Iu... and Paullus.
EDCS-69000047.summary: This is a dating formula specifying a date within the consulship of Iu... and Paullus.

EDCS-69100021.translation: Aurelius Cam[pus?] of the Thirteenth Legion Gemina.
EDCS-69100021.summary: This inscription mentions Aurelius Cam[pus?], a member of the Thirteenth Legion Gemina.

EDCS-69200144.translation: To the departed spirits. Roem[etalces(?)], a cavalryman of the Second Pannonian Wing, lived for 38 years. Brisenus, the standard-bearer, took care to have this erected.
EDCS-69200144.summary: A funerary inscription for Roem[etalces?], a cavalryman who lived 38 years, with his heir Brisenus arranging the monument, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-69200150.summary: This inscription likely refers to a centuria (a unit of Roman soldiers) and a person named Crespus, possibly a centurion or a member of that unit.

EDCS-69200152.translation: Aurelius Mucatra, a veteran of the aforementioned Fifth Macedonian Legion, lived for 50 years; his heir arranged for this to be made.
EDCS-69200152.summary: A funerary inscription for Aurelius Mucatra, a veteran of the Fifth Macedonian Legion who lived 50 years, with his heir arranging the monument.

EDCS-69200202.translation: Aurelius Godes, manager of the potteries, of the Legio XIII Gemina.
EDCS-69200202.summary: This is a potter's mark by Aurelius Godes, manager of the kilns for Legio XIII Gemina.

EDCS-69200639.translation: Helius gladly and deservedly paid his vow to Silvanus Domesticus.
EDCS-69200639.summary: This is a dedication by Helius to the god Silvanus Domesticus.

EDCS-69200642.translation: Augustus and his assistant of the office.
EDCS-69200642.summary: This is a dedication or record related to Augustus and an office assistant.

EDCS-70100049.translation: L. Ioa[---]lius V., from the base of Do[---].
EDCS-70100049.summary: This inscription is too fragmented to provide a meaningful summary.

EDCS-70800229.translation: Gaius Calpurnius Priscinus, a consular legate's aide, gladly and deservedly paid his vow to Jupiter Best and Greatest.
EDCS-70800229.summary: This is a votive dedication by Caius Calpurnius Priscinus, an aide to a consular legate, to Jupiter.

EDCS-71200587.translation: The Getic springs were created by the divine Nymph, near which lies the beautiful land of Germisara, where the British soldier leads the annual rites, fulfilling and discharging his former vows over time, and again undertakes them himself. Where the crested peak falls in a highest vortex, from here he enters the flowing good waters, and from there the pumiceous spring and the healing liquid arise. The praepositus (commander) gives these gifts, and the garden that he had vowed, to be surrounded by the vine, graced by your sweet gift, surrounded by grapes, I beg you, Odrysian Nymph, to keep them hidden as a gift, having been appeased. The Nymphs of Germisarae fashioned this flowing stream through the providence of the god Asclepius. The gifts which mortals celebrate, men who, having benefited from the therapy, having come lame, walk upright, and having made vows, returned again, bringing gifts for the panacea, and gave them as thank-offerings in the sacred enclosure, and returned on their way. If you extend your favor, leader of the Britons, he dedicated this hymn to you, in the most glorious reign of Candidus. Gaius Sentius Iustinus, centurion of the Legio V Macedonica, acting through the territory of Lucania.
EDCS-71200587.summary: This lengthy bilingual inscription records the creation and healing properties of springs near Germisara, dedicated to nymphs, Asclepius, and Artemis, with a dedication by Gaius Sentius Iustinus, a centurion of Legio V Macedonica, during the reign of Candidus.

EDCS-71200606.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Ingenuus, son of Nis, lived 55 years. Tauri, daughter of [---]tor, lived [---] years. His sons and heirs in Gallica[n ---].
EDCS-71200606.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Ingenuus and Tauri, providing their names, parentage, and ages at death, with a mention of their heirs.

EDCS-72000025.summary: This is a simple inscription identifying Marcellus by his trade as a tiler.

EDCS-72200044.summary: This inscription likely refers to a centuria (a unit of Roman soldiers) named after Faustinus, with some unclear additional text.

EDCS-72200045.summary: This inscription refers to a centuria (a unit of Roman soldiers) named after Antoninus, with an incomplete mention of Materni.

EDCS-72800148.summary: This inscription is a label or ownership mark, likely for a seal, dating from the Roman period.

EDCS-72800152.translation: Dioscorus, son of Marcus, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to the unconquered Mithras.
EDCS-72800152.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to the god Mithras by Dioscorus, likely dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-75300268.summary: This inscription simply records the name of Claudius Ianuarius, possibly on a tombstone or a personal object.

EDCS-76400075.translation: To the god Magusanus, Lucius M. Adrianus.
EDCS-76400075.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription to the Germanic deity Magusanus by Lucius M. Adrianus, probably from the Roman military presence in Germania Inferior.

EDCS-77700106.translation: The names of Vitalis Gemini, Maraes, Aelianeas, Iuliaes, Valeriae, Crescentis, Armationis, Filumeni, Ceriani, N. RIN, Faustulonis, Sabini, Flaviaes Iuliaes, Auriolaes, Iuliaes, NAV i, Primi, Valeri, Secundini, Fausti.
EDCS-77700106.summary: This inscription lists numerous personal names, possibly belonging to a group, freedmen, or slaves, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-78300218.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, to the Genius of the century, Marcus Attius Valens, optio.
EDCS-78300218.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Jupiter and the Genius of his century by Marcus Attius Valens, an optio (officer) in a Roman legion, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-78300219.summary: This inscription simply names the IIII Flavia Felix Legion, likely marking a building, equipment, or territory associated with the legion during the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-78800010.translation: Marcus Aurelius Sila, actarius of the equites singulares, willingly fulfilled his vow for himself and his family.
EDCS-78800010.summary: This is a votive inscription by Marcus Aurelius Sila, an actarius (a type of clerk or administrator) serving in the elite cavalry unit equites singulares, likely dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-79600009.summary: This inscription appears to be an ownership mark or a funerary dedication for Marci Sergi Donati, dating to the Roman period.

EDCS-80700051.summary: This inscription names the V Macedonica Legion and Victorinus, possibly indicating a member of the legion or a dedication made by the legion, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-81300034.translation: Alexander willingly paid his vow to Silvanus Domesticus, as was deserved.
EDCS-81300034.summary: A private dedication to Silvanus Domesticus by Alexander, likely from the Roman imperial period.
